title: Fisher-Geometric Sharpness and the Implicit Bias of SGD toward Flat Minima
summary: A widely held intuition in deep learning is that stochastic gradient descent (SGD) implicitly favors flat minima and that flat minima generalize better, but standard Euclidean measures of flatness such as the trace or maximum eigenvalue of the loss Hessian are not invariant under reparametrizations that preserve the network function, which undermines the theoretical foundations of this narrative. In this study we resolve this issue by grounding flatness in the Riemannian geometry of the statistical manifold induced by the Fisher Information Matrix (FIM). We define Riemannian sharpness mathematically and prove that it is invariant under smooth, function-preserving reparametrizations, which directly addresses the critique of Dinh et al. in the paper ``Sharp minima can generalize for deep nets''.We note that this invariance is a property of the true FIM; the diagonal empirical estimator used in practice (and in all experiments below) inherits invariance only approximately, and exact invariance under arbitrary reparametrizations would require structured estimators such as K-FAC. We formalize the gradient noise of mini-batch SGD as having a covariance structure proportional to the FIM, derive the stationary distribution of the resulting stochastic differential equation, and then show that the probability mass is exponentially concentrated at Riemannian-flat minima. A PAC-Bayes generalization bound controlled explicitly by SR formally links this geometric bias to test performance. Our experiments on MNIST and CIFAR-10 confirm that SR reliably tracks generalization in ways that Euclidean sharpness does not, and that its scaling with $η/B$ matches the theoretical predictions. Together these results provide a rigorous, reparametrization-invariant account of why flat minima generalize.

AI.tldr: Proposes reparametrization-invariant flatness via Fisher Information Metric (FIM) geometry; defines Riemannian sharpness that is invariant under smooth, function-preserving reparametrizations; links gradient noise in SGD to FIM, derives stationary distribution, and connects to PAC-Bayes bounds; empirical results on MNIST/CIFAR-10 show SR tracks generalization better than Euclidean sharpness, with scaling η/B predicted by theory.
AI.motivation: Address foundational critique that standard flatness measures (trace, max eigenvalue of Hessian) are not invariant under reparametrizations that preserve the network function, undermining the flatness-generalization claim. Provide a reparametrization-invariant notion of flatness grounded in information geometry.
AI.method: Define Riemannian sharpness on the statistical manifold induced by the Fisher Information Matrix (FIM). Prove invariance under smooth, function-preserving reparametrizations. Model SGD gradient noise with covariance proportional to FIM, derive the resulting stochastic differential equation, and show stationary distribution concentrates around Riemannian-flat minima. Derive a PAC-Bayes generalization bound controlled by SR. Use diagonal empirical FIM (approximate/in practice) and discuss need for structured estimators like K-FAC for exact invariance. Empirical validation on MNIST and CIFAR-10, analyze scaling with learning rate η and batch size B.
AI.result: Riemannian sharpness (SR) behaves as a reparametrization-invariant measure of flatness. It aligns with generalization performance better than Euclidean sharpness, with SR mass concentrating at Riemannian-flat minima as predicted by the stochastic dynamics. A PAC-Bayes bound links SR to test performance. Empirical estimates using diagonal FIM approximate invariance and agree with theory; K-FAC required for exact invariance.
AI.conclusion: SR provides a rigorous, reparametrization-invariant account of why flat minima generalize, tying together information geometry, SGD noise, and PAC-Bayes generalization. While exact invariance requires structured estimators (e.g., K-FAC), practical diagonal estimators capture the effect sufficiently well. The framework explains generalization patterns observed in MNIST and CIFAR-10 and clarifies the role of η/B in SR scaling.

title: Computational Identifiability
summary: Identification conditions describe the computability of a target query or parameter of interest as a function of the type and amount of information available. In causal identification, this information is often expressed in the form of a causal graph, and data are observed or collected for some subset of variables in the graph. Target queries may be for a single effect alone or for a class of effects in a given model. The derivation of an identification algorithm then defines mathematically the process by which the desired causal effect(s) can be uniquely determined, theoretically, in expectation. Identifiability in expectation, or 'theoretical identifiability,' generally assumes asymptotic properties, infinite data, or other mathematically idealized conditions. In this paper, we explore a fundamental distinction between this theoretical, idealized notion of identifiability and a proposed alternative that is computation-bound. The framework we propose - 'computational identifiability' - is to instead define a finite computational search procedure for an empirical estimator. If this process finds an estimator empirically, within a desired error tolerance, then identifiability is satisfied, conditional on the specified assumptions of the search (i.e., a prior distribution over the parameters) and conditional on the search procedure itself. Through several experiments, we demonstrate how this framework allows us to answer fine-grained, practical identification questions, such as identification with small finite samples, with ambiguous graphical criteria, with mixed observational-interventional data, and across counterfactual data and estimands. Code is available at https://github.com/lbynum/metadentify.

AI.tldr: Proposes computational identifiability as a finite, computation-bound alternative to theoretical identifiability in causal inference, and demonstrates its practicality via experiments and code.
AI.motivation: Traditional identifiability relies on asymptotic or idealized conditions; there is a need for a practical notion that handles finite data, ambiguous graphical criteria, and mixed observational-interventional data.
AI.method: Define a finite computational search procedure to extract an empirical estimator from data under a specified prior over parameters; declare identifiability if the search yields an estimator within a desired error tolerance, given the search design. Validate through experiments across small samples, ambiguous graphs, mixed data, and counterfactuals; release code.
AI.result: The framework enables answering fine-grained, practical identification questions that are hard under classic identifiability, demonstrated across diverse scenarios; code is publicly available.
AI.conclusion: Computational identifiability complements theoretical identifiability by providing a practical criterion that depends on the search procedure and priors, enabling empirical identification under finite data and computational constraints.

title: When to Trust, How to Distill: Multi-Foundation Model Guidance for Lightweight, Robust Scientific Time Series Forecasting
comment: KDD 2026, paper decision: Accepted, track: AI for Science. total 12 pages including references and appendix
summary: The deployment of Time-Series Foundation Models (TSFMs) in physical sciences is hindered by a critical trade-off: while these models encode rich, universal temporal dynamics, they suffer from severe distributional misalignment when applied zero-shot to specific scientific domains, and their computational cost prohibits deployment in edge-computing sensor networks. We address a fundamental challenge: How can we extract latent structural knowledge from misaligned foundation models (FM) to train lightweight, specialized forecasters? We propose Gated Uncertainty-Aware Routing for Distillation (Guard), a novel framework that reframes multiteacher distillation as an instance-wise decision process with two adaptive mechanisms: (1) a Contextual Router that dynamically selects the most relevant teacher based on local input statistics, exploiting complementarity across diverse foundation models; and (2) an Uncertainty-Gated Temperature mechanism that acts as a "circuit-breaker," automatically attenuating distillation strength when teacher confidence diverges from domain reality. We evaluate our proposed lightweight framework on four climate-critical domains: meteorology, ecosystem carbon flux, soil moisture, and energy grids. Our method significantly reduces RMSE relative to a fixed-weight multi-teacher distillation baseline, successfully distilling knowledge from pretrained FMs (teachers) even when they exhibit suboptimal zero-shot accuracy due to distribution shift between the original and target data domains. We demonstrate that these domain-misaligned teachers can still serve as critical correctives, outperforming the globally superior FMs on 28.5% of the hardest instances. Ultimately, this enables high-precision scientific forecasting suitable for resource-constrained edge deployment. Code is available at https://github.com/RupasreeDey/GUARD-KDD2026.

AI.tldr: A lightweight, uncertainty-aware distillation framework (Guard) that selects among multiple misaligned Time-Series foundation models via a Contextual Router and uses an Uncertainty-Gated Temperature to attenuate distillation when teacher confidence diverges from the target domain. It enables edge-ready, high-precision forecasting by distilling knowledge from diverse, domain-misaligned teachers across climate domains.
AI.motivation: TSFMs encode rich temporal dynamics but exhibit distributional misalignment under zero-shot deployment to specific scientific domains and are computationally expensive for edge devices. There is a need to extract useful, domain-relevant knowledge from misaligned foundation models to train lightweight, specialized forecasters.
AI.method: Guard reframes multiteacher distillation as an instance-wise decision process with two adaptive mechanisms: (1) Contextual Router selects the most relevant teacher based on local input statistics to exploit cross-model complementarity; (2) Uncertainty-Gated Temperature attenuates distillation when teacher confidence disagrees with domain reality, acting as a circuit-breaker to prevent misleading transfers. The framework is evaluated on four climate-critical domains (meteorology, ecosystem carbon flux, soil moisture, energy grids) against a fixed-weight distillation baseline.
AI.result: Guard significantly reduces RMSE relative to a fixed-weight multi-teacher distillation baseline. It can distill knowledge from pretrained, domain-misaligned FMs even when zero-shot accuracy is poor due to distribution shifts. In hardest instances, domain-misaligned teachers outperform globally superior FMs on 28.5% of cases, enabling high-precision forecasting suitable for resource-constrained edge deployment.
AI.conclusion: Domain-misaligned but complementary teachers can be leveraged through adaptive routing and uncertainty-aware distillation to produce accurate, edge-friendly forecasters, broadening the practical deployment of TSFMs in scientific domains. The approach demonstrates robust performance across diverse climate domains and is accompanied by open-source code.

title: Physical Atari: A Robust and Accessible Platform for Real-time Reinforcement Learning on Robots
summary: We built a robot called the Robotroller that actuates an Atari CX40+ controller and a device called the Atari Devbox that renders the game frame and the reward signal from the Arcade Learning Environment on a screen. The Robotroller and the Atari Devbox, together with an off-the-shelf camera and a desktop computer, constitute a system that can be used to study reinforcement learning algorithms in the physical world. We call the full system Physical Atari. In this paper, we detail the key decisions that make Physical Atari a robust and accessible platform. To make the system robust, we designed the Robotroller so that all movement is done through bearings, which reduces wear. Additionally, we wrote software that monitors the state of the servos at a high frequency and intervenes to limit stress. To make the system accessible, we used affordable off-the-shelf components and parts that can be manufactured using consumer 3D printers. Physical Atari can be built for under $1,000 and has been used for weeks of non-stop reinforcement learning experiments without any mechanical failures. We used it to validate that reinforcement learning algorithms can learn directly on robots and show that even small distribution shifts between learning and deployment can significantly degrade the performance of policies. Our results underscore the importance of on-device adaptation for strong performance on robots.

AI.tldr: A low-cost, robust platform (Physical Atari) for studying reinforcement learning on real hardware, combining a Robotroller-controlled Atari controller, an Atari Devbox rendering frames/rewards, an off-the-shelf camera, and a PC.
AI.motivation: Enable robust, accessible, long-running RL experiments in the physical world and study how policies behave with realistic distribution shifts between learning and deployment.
AI.method: Hardware design with bearings for wear reduction (Robotroller); high-frequency servo monitoring with protective interventions; affordable, 3D-printable components; total cost under $1,000; integration with Atari Devbox and ALE for frame/reward rendering; validation through weeks-long RL experiments on hardware.
AI.result: Demonstrates that RL algorithms can learn directly on robots using Physical Atari; reveals that small distribution shifts between learning and deployment can significantly degrade policy performance, underscoring the need for on-device adaptation.
AI.conclusion: Physical Atari provides a robust, accessible platform for physical RL research, enabling sustained experiments and highlighting the importance of on-device adaptation for robust robot learning; its low-cost, manufacturable design supports wider adoption.

title: Scaling Generative Foundation Models for Chest Radiography with Rectified Flow Transformers
summary: We introduce the first generative foundation model for chest radiograph synthesis trained from scratch at the billion-parameter scale. Existing radiographic AI models often suffer from poor generalisation across patient subpopulations, institutions, and acquisition settings, resulting in limited real-world clinical utility. Controlled, high-fidelity synthesis of chest radiographs is a promising path toward diversifying clinical datasets and evaluating the robustness of diagnostic models. Therefore, we present the largest specialist generative foundation model for chest radiographs to date, with over 1.3B parameters, trained for 1.6T tokens on a curated, heterogeneous dataset comprising 1.2M radiographs and clinical expert-guided metadata. Our model supports controllable radiograph generation and editing across multiple demographic subgroups, acquisition views, and a dozen pathologies. Moreover, we significantly advance the state of the art in radiograph synthesis fidelity, producing images that are indistinguishable from real radiographs to clinical experts.

AI.tldr: Introduces a first billion-parameter generative foundation model for chest radiographs trained from scratch; enables controllable, high-fidelity synthesis and editing across demographics, acquisition views, and pathologies.
AI.motivation: Overcome generalisation gaps in existing radiographic AI models across patient subpopulations, institutions, and acquisition settings; enable controlled data synthesis to diversify datasets and test robustness.
AI.method: Train a 1.3B-parameter generative model from scratch on 1.6T tokens, using a curated heterogeneous dataset of 1.2M radiographs with expert-guided metadata; implement controllable generation/editing across demographic subgroups, acquisition views, and about a dozen pathologies.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art radiograph synthesis fidelity; images indistinguishable from real radiographs to clinical experts; supports controllable generation across demographics, views, and pathologies.
AI.conclusion: Presents a scalable radiograph foundation model with potential to improve generalisability and robustness of downstream diagnostic models by enabling realistic, controllable synthetic data for evaluation and augmentation.

title: Deontic Policies for Runtime Governance of Agentic AI Systems
comment: 10 pages, 1 figure. To be published in the 2026 IEEE Symposium on Agentic Services which is part of the IEEE Conference on Web Services
summary: Autonomous agentic AI systems driven by Large Language Models (LLMs) introduce a new class of security, privacy, and compliance challenges: an agent that can invoke tools, manipulate data, install software, and coordinate with peer agents across organizational boundaries must be constrained not just by authentication and access control, but by the full structure of enterprise governance. This includes specifying what agents are permitted and prohibited from doing, what they areobliged to do after certain actions (e.g., notify the CISO), under what conditions a standing obligation may be waived, and which rules take precedence when policies conflict. This governance problem exceeds what current policy engines provide. Systems such as XACML, Rego, and Cedar address only the permit/prohibit subset of this governance structure. They do not provide obligation lifecycle management, meta-policy conflict resolution, dispensations that waive obligations in specific circumstances, and ontological reasoning over domain class hierarchies commonly found in applications such as healthcare, cybersecurity, or data privacy. We propose AgenticRei, which realizes key governance requirements such as obligations, dispensations, policy conflict resolutions, and reasoning over policies, as well as the basic permit/prohibit constraints. We use a deontic policy language built on the Rei framework, expressed as OWL (Web Ontology Language) and evaluated at runtime by a high-performance logic engine entirely outside the LLM. The same pipeline governs both tool invocations by the agent and agent-to-agent messages. We show through examples that deontic policies capture governance constraints around security and privacy that mostly cannot be expressed in current production engines. Our approach composes naturally with industry-standard frameworks like A2AS.

AI.tldr: AgenticRei is a deontic-policy governance framework for LLM-driven autonomous agents, enabling obligations, dispensations, conflict resolution, and ontological reasoning, encoded in OWL and evaluated by an external logic engine; it complements permit/prohibit engines and integrates with standard enterprise frameworks like A2AS.
AI.motivation: To address governance gaps for tool-using, cross-organizational autonomous agents, where enforcement requires not only permits/denials but obligations, dispensations, and reasoning over domain hierarchies across healthcare, cybersecurity, and data privacy.
AI.method: Propose AgenticRei with a deontic policy language (based on Rei) expressed in OWL, evaluated at runtime by a high-performance logic engine external to the LLM; unifies governance for tool invocations and agent messages; demonstrates with examples; designed to compose with frameworks like A2AS.
AI.result: Illustrative evidence that deontic policies capture governance constraints around security and privacy that current production engines (e.g., XACML, Rego, Cedar) struggle to express; runtime evaluation and ontological reasoning enable richer governance.
AI.conclusion: AgenticRei realizes core governance capabilities—obligations, dispensations, conflict resolution, and policy reasoning—alongside permit/prohibit constraints; provides ontological reasoning over domain hierarchies and integrates with established enterprise frameworks, enhancing governance for enterprise LLM agents.

title: Closing the Social-Semantic Gap: SPSD for Edge-Based Prompt Compression in Cloud LLM Inference
summary: The prefill stage of Large Language Model (LLM) inference is a growing contributor to cloud-scale energy cost. Many consumer-support and conversational prompts contain social scaffolding: politeness markers, apologetic preamble, repetition, and rapport-building language that is important for human communication but carries low marginal information for machine reasoning. We call this discrepancy the Social-Semantic Gap. We present SPSD (Sentiment Preserving Semantic Distillation), an edge-based pipeline that compresses user prompts using a 4-bit quantised Small Language Model before transmission to a cloud-deployed LLM. Evaluation on a 248-prompt corpus using Gemma-2-2B-Instruct (Q4_K_M) as the SLM and Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct as the cloud evaluation model yields a mean input token saving of 99.9 tokens per distilled call, with all 146 distilled calls yielding positive savings. Response quality, assessed by blind LLM-as-judge scoring across 121 pairs, is non-inferior to the raw path within a pre-specified 1-point margin on a 15-point rubric; the judge awarded 43 percent ties, 28 percent distilled wins, and 29 percent raw wins. Cosine similarity is mixed: mean 0.682, median 0.712, with 54.1 percent of pairs above the 0.70 reference threshold. Safety-critical domains are conservatively routed to passthrough via rule-based gates. Per-call net energy saving is estimated at 70-270 uWh under stated assumptions. SPSD shows that on-device prompt distillation can reduce cloud LLM input-token cost while preserving response quality within a practical non-inferiority margin.

AI.tldr: SPSD enables on-device, 4-bit distillation of user prompts to compress input for cloud LLMs, reducing tokens and energy while preserving response quality within a non-inferiority margin.
AI.motivation: Reduce cloud-scale energy costs and data-sending latency by addressing the Social-Semantic Gap where social scaffolding in prompts adds token count without boosting reasoning.
AI.method: Edge-based pipeline using a 4-bit quantized Small Language Model (Gemma-2-2B-Instruct) to distill prompts before cloud evaluation with Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct; evaluation on 248 prompts; blind LLM judge for quality; cosine similarity analysis; safety gating for critical domains; energy estimation per call.
AI.result: Mean input token saving of 99.9 tokens per distilled call across 146 distilled calls; all calls yielded token savings. Non-inferiority within a 1-point margin on a 15-point rubric; judge outcomes: 43% ties, 28% distilled wins, 29% raw wins. Cosine similarity: mean 0.682, median 0.712; 54.1% of pairs above 0.70. Safety-critical domains routed through rule-based gates. Estimated per-call energy savings: 70-270 µWh.
AI.conclusion: On-device prompt distillation can meaningfully reduce cloud LLM input-token costs while maintaining response quality within a practical non-inferiority margin.

title: LEAP: Layer-skipping Efficiency via Adaptive Progression for Vision Transformer Distillation
summary: Vision Foundation Models (VFMs) with Vision Transformer (ViT) backbones, such as DINOv2, have become essential for downstream tasks like object recognition and semantic segmentation. The immense computational requirements of backbones often necessitate distillation into smaller architectures for edge deployment. Feature-based knowledge distillation (KD) often suffers from the teacher-student gap; the student struggles to imitate teacher's complex feature map due to its limited capacity. To mitigate this bottleneck, we propose LEAP: Layer-skipping Efficiency via Adaptive Progression, a training curriculum for ViT feature-based knowledge distillation. By utilizing the teacher's intermediate feature maps as a sequence of progressively more difficult targets, our curriculum allows the student to build a foundational representation before tackling higher-level abstractions. Our results demonstrate that this paradigm significantly accelerates convergence through adaptive difficulty selection across various student model sizes and dataset scales. With our curriculum, the LEAP-distilled ViT-S achieves 90.1% accuracy on ImageNet-100, a +12.24% improvement compared with baseline. On ImageNet-1K, LEAP achieves +3.84% and +7.75% improvement for the instance retrieval task on the Oxford and Paris datasets, respectively. Furthermore, the curriculum enables 25.1% savings in training FLOPs and 21% savings in training time on ImageNet-100 by implementing early-stopping for teacher inference during the initial stages of training. Code is available at https://github.com/KevinZ0217/LEAP

AI.tldr: LEAP introduces a layer-skipping, adaptive curriculum for ViT feature-based KD to bridge the teacher-student gap, yielding faster convergence and better accuracy with reduced training cost.
AI.motivation: Feature-based KD for Vision Transformers suffers from a teacher-student capacity gap. This work proposes a curriculum that uses progressively harder intermediate feature targets from the teacher to guide the student, enabling learning of foundational representations before high-level abstractions.
AI.method: Train ViT students with a curriculum that leverages the teacher's intermediate feature maps as a sequence of targets of increasing difficulty. The curriculum adapts difficulty per step (adaptive progression) and enables layer-skipping; early-stopping of teacher inference reduces computation in initial training stages.
AI.result: LEAP-distilled ViT-S attains 90.1% accuracy on ImageNet-100 (+12.24 over baseline). On ImageNet-1K, gains include +3.84% (Oxford) and +7.75% (Paris) for instance retrieval. Training FLOPs reduced by 25.1%; training time reduced by 21% on ImageNet-100 via early-stopping of teacher inference. Code provided at the stated GitHub URL.
AI.conclusion: A curriculum-based KD that progressively increases target difficulty and selectively skips layers enables faster convergence, higher accuracy, and lower training cost across varying model sizes and data scales.

title: Measuring Curriculum Alignment across Topical Coverage, Competency, and Cognitive Depth: A Longitudinal Framework Applied to CS2013 and CS2023
summary: Undergraduate computer science is governed by international curricular guidelines revised about once a decade, yet programs lack a reliable, reproducible way to measure how completely they cover the current guidelines and how that coverage shifts when the guidelines are restructured. We address this with a human-in-the-loop pipeline that measures a program's coverage of an external body of knowledge, applied longitudinally to one accredited BSc in Computer Science against Computer Science Curricula 2013 (CS2013) and 2023 (CS2023). The pipeline represents the program and each guideline as structured corpora, generates candidate course-to-knowledge-unit matches by semantic retrieval, and confirms them through human judgment under an explicit coverage definition. Of seven benchmarked retrievers, a reciprocal-rank-fusion ensemble was strongest, and a reputed long-context model underperformed a small sentence model, so retriever choice must be measured. Both maps were validated by an independent second rater (Cohen's kappa 0.64 for CS2023, 0.69 for CS2013). The program covers 49.7% of CS2023 and 50.9% of CS2013 knowledge units, near-constant across a decade. Extending the same retrieve-then-confirm design to competency articulation and cognitive depth shows that the program articulates the competency for ~88% of covered units under each guideline, yet delivers it at the recommended depth for 76% of present units under CS2023 against 95% under CS2013, a gap reflecting the newer guideline's raised expectations, not the program. The longitudinal comparison separates persistent structural gaps (parallel and distributed computing, foundations of programming languages, systems fundamentals), uncovered against both guidelines and ABET, from differences that reflect the standard's evolution. The instrument is reusable and available from the authors on request.

AI.tldr: A human-in-the-loop pipeline quantifies how well an undergraduate CS program covers CS2013/CS2023 knowledge units, using structured corpora, semantic retrieval, and expert confirmation; it reveals around 50% coverage with persistent structural gaps and shows higher articulation of competency but shallower depth under CS2023 due to updated expectations; the approach is reusable and instrument is available on request.
AI.motivation: Undergraduate CS curricula are revised about every decade, but there is no reliable, reproducible method to measure coverage of evolving guidelines and how coverage shifts with restructurings. The study develops a reproducible, longitudinal measurement pipeline against external knowledge bodies (CS2013/CS2023).
AI.method: A human-in-the-loop pipeline converts the program and guidelines into structured corpora, generates candidate course–knowledge-unit matches via semantic retrieval, and confirms them through human judgment under an explicit coverage definition. Seven retrievers were benchmarked, with a reciprocal-rank-fusion ensemble performing best; a reputed long-context model underperformed compared to a small sentence model. Two independent raters assessed maps (Cohen’s kappa = 0.64 for CS2023, 0.69 for CS2013). The longitudinal comparison examines coverage and depth for two guidelines and ABET, identifying persistent gaps and shifts due to guideline evolution.
AI.result: The CS program covers 49.7% of CS2023 knowledge units and 50.9% of CS2013 knowledge units, with coverage remaining near-constant across a decade. Competency articulation is achieved for about 88% of covered units under each guideline, but depth is met for 76% of present units under CS2023 versus 95% under CS2013, reflecting higher expectations in CS2023 rather than program shortcomings. Persistent gaps identified include parallel and distributed computing, foundations of programming languages, and systems fundamentals. The instrument is reusable, and the authors offer to provide it on request.
AI.conclusion: The pipeline provides a reproducible, longitudinal mechanism to evaluate curriculum alignment with external knowledge guidelines, identifies structural gaps that persist across guidelines, and highlights the need to consider how guideline evolution affects expected depth. The approach depends on retriever choice and human judgment; the authors offer the reusable instrument for broader deployment.

title: Performance Analysis and Optimization of 3D Generative Diffusion Models across GPU Architectures
summary: Diffusion models have become essential for high-fidelity 3D MRI synthesis, yet their deployment remains constrained by substantial GPU resource demands arising from hundreds of U-Net evaluations per sample and a highly heterogeneous kernel behavior. This paper performs a comprehensive performance analysis of the state-of-the-art medical diffusion model, Med-DDPM, across three generations of NVIDIA architectures to study kernel-level runtime breakdowns, instruction-mix characteristics, memory system utilization, warp-level activities, and profiler priority-score estimates. We show that training is overwhelmingly dominated by cuDNN convolution and implicit-GEMM kernels, with inefficiencies arising from memory-access patterns, tensor-layout conversions, and limited Tensor Core utilization. Guided by these insights, we evaluate two architecture-aware optimizations TF32 Tensor Core activation and a 3D channels-last layout and demonstrate that they reduce SM cycles by up to 100x, cut dynamic instructions by 100x, raise Tensor Core utilization from 1.45 to 9.98x, and increase IPC by 7% on A100, all without degrading synthesis quality.

AI.tldr: Comprehensive performance analysis of Med-DDPM across three NVIDIA generations; proposes TF32 Tensor Core activation and 3D channels-last layout to dramatically improve efficiency without affecting MRI synthesis quality.
AI.motivation: Address substantial GPU resource demands of diffusion-based 3D MRI synthesis and heterogeneous kernel behavior by profiling across architectures to identify bottlenecks and guide optimizations.
AI.method: Empirical performance study across three NVIDIA architectures; kernel-level breakdowns, instruction mix, memory usage, warp activity, and profiler scores; implement architecture-aware optimizations (TF32 Tensor Core activation and 3D channels-last layout); evaluate on synthesis quality and hardware metrics.
AI.result: Optimizations reduce SM cycles up to 100x, dynamic instructions ~100x fewer, Tensor Core utilization from 1.45x to 9.98x, IPC up 7% on A100; no degradation in synthesis quality.
AI.conclusion: Architecture-aware optimizations can substantially accelerate diffusion-based 3D MRI synthesis, enabling more efficient deployment across GPUs without sacrificing output quality; likely generalizable to similar models.

title: 3D Scene Graphs: Open Challenges and Future Directions
comment: Invited article for the Annual Review of Control, Robotics, and Autonomous Systems Volume 10
summary: 3D Scene Graphs (3DSGs) have emerged as a powerful representation for spatial AI by combining geometric grounding with semantic and relational abstractions of the environment. Their expressiveness has made them relevant to a broad range of problems in robotics and computer vision, including manipulation, navigation, task planning, scene understanding, and many others. However, the field remains fragmented: different communities adopt distinct formulations, construction pipelines, and evaluation protocols, making it difficult to compare methods, identify common assumptions, and assess remaining challenges for robust real-world deployment. This survey provides a unified and critical review of 3DSGs, with particular emphasis on open challenges and future directions. We first formalize 3DSGs under a common definition and analyze the principal modeling choices that characterize existing formulations, including node and edge attributes, hierarchical structure, dynamic scene representations, and affordance-aware extensions. We then review how 3DSGs are built from raw sensory observations, discussing the most common terminologies, conventions, and techniques. Finally, we examine downstream applications and evaluation strategies, from intrinsic graph quality to task-level performance. To support the community, we also provide a dedicated website that organizes and extends the surveyed content, accessible at https://3dscenegraphs.com/.

AI.tldr: A unified survey of 3D Scene Graphs (3DSGs) that formalizes a common definition, analyzes modeling choices (nodes, edges, hierarchy, dynamics, affordances), reviews construction from raw sensory data, and discussesApplications and evaluation, culminating in open challenges and a community resource (website).
AI.motivation: The 3DSG field is fragmented across communities with varying formulations, pipelines, and evaluation protocols, hindering method comparison and deployment. A unified framework and critical review can harmonize concepts, clarify assumptions, and highlight gaps for robust real-world use.
AI.method: Formalize 3DSGs under a shared definition, analyze principal design choices (node/edge attributes, hierarchy, dynamics, affordances), survey how 3DSGs are built from sensory data, consolidate terminologies and techniques, and evaluate downstream applications and metrics. Provide a community resource (website).
AI.result: A consolidated taxonomy and critical synthesis of 3DSG formulations, construction pipelines, evaluation strategies, and application domains, plus identified open challenges and directions for future work; includes a public website for ongoing reference.
AI.conclusion: The survey aims to unify the field, expose assumptions, and chart future directions toward robust, real-world 3DSG deployment, with emphasis on standardized definitions, interoperable benchmarks, and practical evaluation across tasks.

title: Diffusion Language Models: An Experimental Analysis
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have revolutionized language modeling through autoregressive generation, enabling strong performance across a wide range of tasks. Recently, Diffusion Language Models (DLMs) have emerged as an alternative paradigm that generates text through iterative denoising rather than next-token prediction, allowing parallel refinement of entire sequences. While numerous diffusion-based architectures have been proposed, differences in evaluation protocols, datasets, inference budgets, and generation hyperparameters make it difficult to compare their capabilities and understand the trade-offs they offer. In this work, we present a systematic experimental analysis of modern DLMs. Specifically, we evaluate eight state-of-the-art DLMs across eight benchmarks spanning reasoning, coding, translation, knowledge, and structured problem solving, while explicitly considering both generation quality and computational efficiency. Beyond downstream evaluation, we analyze the impact of key inference-time factors, including denoising steps, context length, block size, and parallel unmasking strategies, and complement large-scale experiments with controlled comparisons of smaller models trained under identical conditions. Our analysis highlights the strengths and limitations of diffusion-based language modeling across different tasks, architectures, and inference budgets. We show that the behavior of DLMs is strongly influenced by generation-time design choices, leading to distinct trade-offs between performance and computational efficiency. Overall, our study provides practical insights into the capabilities and deployment characteristics of contemporary DLMs.

AI.tldr: A systematic experimental study of diffusion language models (DLMs) across eight benchmarks and eight state-of-the-art models, examining generation quality and computational efficiency, while analyzing inference-time factors and providing practical deployment insights.
AI.motivation: To address inconsistent evaluation protocols and datasets in DLM literature, hindering fair comparison and understanding of trade-offs between performance and efficiency.
AI.method: Empirical evaluation of eight state-of-the-art DLMs on eight benchmarks spanning reasoning, coding, translation, knowledge, and structured problem solving; measurement of generation quality and computational efficiency; ablation of inference-time factors (denoising steps, context length, block size, parallel unmasking); controlled comparisons with smaller models trained under identical conditions.
AI.result: DLM behavior is highly influenced by generation-time design choices, yielding distinct trade-offs between accuracy and efficiency. Strengths and limitations vary by task, architecture, and inference budget; the study provides practical guidance for deployment and deeper understanding of contemporary DLMs.
AI.conclusion: Diffusion-based language modeling offers valuable capabilities with task- and budget-dependent trade-offs. The work delivers actionable insights for choosing models and configuring inference for real-world deployment.

title: Information Lattice Learning as Probabilistic Graphical Model Structure Learning
summary: Information lattice learning (ILL) learns interpretable rules of a signal by alternately projecting the signal onto a partition lattice that encodes a hierarchy of abstractions and lifting selected rules back to the signal domain. When the signal is a probability mass function, we show the probabilistic rules learned by ILL admit a natural probabilistic graphical model (PGM) interpretation and develop this interpretation in detail. A partition in ILL induces a deterministic quotient variable, and a rule is the marginal law of that quotient variable. A rule set is therefore a collection of marginal constraints over interpretable abstractions. General lifting is the feasible family of all joint distributions satisfying those constraints, while special lifting chooses a maximum-ignorance reconstruction, implemented in ILL by an L2 uniformity principle closely related to maximum entropy. Under a Shannon-entropy lifting, the same constraints yield a log-linear factor graph whose factors are indexed by learned abstractions. The information lattice itself, however, is not a Bayesian network: its edges encode refinement and coarsening of abstractions, not conditional dependence. Thus ILL is best viewed as structure learning for interpretable constraint-based factor graphs over quotient variables. This view clarifies how ILL relates to graphical models and maximum entropy models, while suggesting new directions for inference, identifiability, and hybrid symbolic-probabilistic learning.

AI.tldr: ILL learns interpretable rules via a partition lattice of abstractions, mapping rules to quotient variables and exploring feasible lifting (max-ignorance) and entropy-based lifting, connecting to log-linear graphical models while remaining distinct from Bayesian networks.
AI.motivation: Bridge interpretability with probabilistic modeling by embedding ILL within a probabilistic graphical-model framework, clarifying how rules constrain marginal distributions and how lifting reconstructs joint distributions.
AI.method: Iteratively project signals onto a hierarchy of abstractions (partition lattice) and lift selected rules back to the signal domain. A partition induces a deterministic quotient variable; a rule equals its marginal law. General lifting yields all joint distributions satisfying the marginal constraints; special lifting selects a max-ignorance reconstruction (L2 uniformity). Shannon-entropy lifting yields a log-linear factor graph with factors indexed by abstractions. The information lattice's edges encode refinement/coarsening, not conditional dependence.
AI.result: Rules correspond to marginal constraints over quotient variables; special lifting implements maximum-ignorance reconstructions; entropy lifting yields a log-linear FG representation of the same constraints; the information lattice is not a Bayesian network but a structure for constraint-based factor graphs.
AI.conclusion: ILL should be viewed as structure learning for interpretable, constraint-based factor graphs over quotient variables. This clarifies its relation to graphical models and maximum-entropy frameworks and suggests new directions for inference, identifiability, and hybrid symbolic-probabilistic learning.

title: Hidden Anchors in Multi-Agent LLM Deliberation
summary: Multi-agent LLM deliberation, where agents exchange and revise answers over several rounds, is increasingly used to improve reasoning and accuracy, yet how and why it works is rarely modelled. Such deliberation mirrors how humans reach decisions. As social animals we are pulled both by the group, the herd effect that classical opinion-dynamics models such as DeGroot and Friedkin--Johnsen capture, and by our own internal belief, which they do not. We model multi-agent deliberation as a closed-loop dynamical system in which each agent carries a hidden internal belief, its anchor, that continually pulls its opinion regardless of its neighbours. We show this anchor can be recovered from the deliberation alone, and that it explains a behaviour classical consensus rules forbid: an agent's confidence in the correct answer can climb past where any agent started, escaping the space (convexhull) formed by the initial beliefs. Checking whether the recovered anchor also predicts held-out runs (generalizes) gives a simple test for when a model is truly driven bysuch an anchor. Across three open-weight model families this is a spectrum, not all-or-nothing. All anchors' influence are about equally strongly, but they differ in where the anchor sits, and only when it sits far from the initial opinions does deliberation escape the hull and need the full closed-loop model.

AI.tldr: Model deliberation as closed-loop dynamics with per-agent hidden anchors; anchors can be recovered from deliberation and explain hull-escaping confidence; spectrum across open-weight models; generalization test validates anchor-driven dynamics.
AI.motivation: Explain why multi-agent LLM deliberation improves reasoning beyond classical opinion-dynamics models by incorporating internal anchors; provide testable predictions about when deliberation outperforms initial beliefs.
AI.method: Formalize deliberation as a closed-loop system where each agent has an internal anchor pulling its opinion; derive method to recover the anchor from deliberation data; show hull-escaping behavior; introduce a generalization test; evaluate across three open-weight model families.
AI.result: Anchors can be recovered from deliberation; anchor explains when confidence in the correct answer can surpass the convex hull of initial beliefs; generalization test supports anchor-driven predictions; anchors are similarly influential across models but located differently, with hull escape only when far from initial opinions; deliberation is not simply convex-hull-limited.
AI.conclusion: Deliberation dynamics are governed by internal anchors; anchor location determines effectiveness and hull escape; recovering anchors provides a principled way to predict and assess deliberation outcomes; the framework applies across diverse model families, indicating a spectrum of anchor-driven behavior.

title: Weibull Weight-Scale Parameter Evolution under AdamW Training Dynamics
summary: Building on a two-parameter Weibull framework for diagnosing transformer weight distributions, we study why the Weibull weight-scale parameter $λ$ grows, overshoots, and then relaxes during AdamW training. We derive a leading-order three-force decomposition of the squared weight norm from the AdamW update: an alignment force measuring the correlation between weights and the adaptive update direction, an injection force from adaptive step magnitude, and a decay force from decoupled weight decay. On self-trained Pythia-70M models with ground-truth optimizer moments, alignment dominates the rise phase, contributing 88-94% of the absolute force budget across four random seeds and remaining robust to super-weight removal. Near saturation, alignment and decay approach balance, explaining the transition from weight-scale growth to relaxation. These force dynamics directly govern the squared-norm component underlying $λ(t)$; the remaining RMS-to-Weibull reconstruction offset is measurable and decomposes into bridge and integration components, totaling approximately 5-6% in densely sampled regions. To extend the analysis to real models where optimizer moments are unavailable, we introduce a spline displacement method that recovers the alignment force from sparse checkpoints with approximately 92-94% accuracy, about twice the naive two-point baseline. We further observe that the peak value of $λ(t)$ varies with training-data coherence in our experiments, suggesting a data-dependent component of weight-scale growth that we leave to a controlled follow-up study. Code and data are available at https://github.com/tiexinding/NPM-Weibull-public.

AI.tldr: Three-force decomposition of squared weight norm under AdamW within a two-parameter Weibull framework explains how the Weibull scale λ grows, overshoots, then relaxes during transformer training. Alignment dominates early growth; injection and decay contribute later; a spline displacement method extends the analysis to sparse checkpoints. Robust across seeds and resistant to weight pruning. Peak λ varies with data coherence, indicating a data-dependent component. Code and data are provided.
AI.motivation: Understand why weight-scale (Weibull λ) grows, overshoots, and relaxes under AdamW in transformer training; connect optimizer dynamics to a Weibull description; develop practical methods to recover alignment forces from sparse data; extend analysis to real models lacking optimizer moments.
AI.method: Derive a leading-order three-force decomposition of the squared weight norm from the AdamW update: alignment force (weight–update direction correlation), injection force (adaptive step magnitude), and decay force (decoupled weight decay). Validate on self-trained Pythia-70M with ground-truth optimizer moments across four seeds and robustness to super-weight removal. Analyze saturation where alignment and decay balance. Decompose the RMS-to-Weibull reconstruction offset into bridge and integration components (≈5–6%). Extend to sparse checkpoints using a spline displacement method to recover the alignment force with ~92–94% accuracy, about twice a naive two-point baseline. Examine how λ peak varies with training-data coherence. Provide code and data at the stated repository.”,
AI.result: Found that alignment dominates the rise phase, contributing 88–94% of the absolute force budget across seeds. Near saturation, alignment and decay balance, explaining the transition from weight-scale growth to relaxation. The λ(t) dynamics align with the squared-norm evolution; the RMS-to-Weibull offset decomposes into bridge and integration components, totaling ~5–6% in dense regions. The spline displacement method recovers the alignment force from sparse checkpoints with 92–94% accuracy. Peak λ correlates with training-data coherence, indicating a data-dependent component requiring further study.
AI.conclusion: The weight-scale dynamics under AdamW in transformer models can be captured by a three-force decomposition within a Weibull framework, linking optimizer moments to the evolution of λ. The method yields robust insights across seeds and pruning ablations, and extends to sparse data via spline-based recovery. A follow-up study on the data-coherence effect is warranted; code/data are available for replication.

title: Playful Agentic Robot Learning
summary: Current agentic robot systems can write executable Code-as-Policy programs, observe feedback, and revise behavior across multiple attempts, but they remain largely task-driven: reusable skills are acquired only after explicit instructions. We study Playful Agentic Robot Learning, where an embodied coding agent uses self-directed play as a continual skill-learning stage before downstream tasks arrive. We introduce RATs, Robotics Agent Teams designed for play-time skill acquisition. During play, RATs proposes novel yet learnable exploratory tasks, plans and executes robot-code policies, verifies intermediate progress, diagnoses failures, retries with dense, step-level feedback, and distills successful executions into a persistent code skill library. At test time, the agent reuses relevant skills from this frozen library to help solve new tasks. Experiments in LIBERO-PRO and MolmoSpaces show that play-learned skills improve held-out downstream tasks over no-play and random-play baselines, with 20.6 and 17.0 percentage-point gains over CaP-Agent0 on LIBERO-PRO and MolmoSpaces, respectively. Moreover, the learned skills can be plugged into other inference-time Code-as-Policy agents by simply retrieving them into the context, improving RoboSuite and real-world transfer by 8.9 and 8.8 points, respectively, without finetuning the underlying model.

AI.tldr: Playful Agentic Robot Learning with RATs yields a reusable code-skill library via play-time exploration, improving downstream tasks and enabling cross-agent transfer without fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Current agentic robots are task-driven and lack reusable skills; play-based continual learning can acquire generalizable policies.
AI.method: Introduce RATs: embodied coding agents that self-drive play, propose tasks, execute Code-as-Policy policies, verify progress, diagnose failures, retry with dense feedback, distill successful runs into a persistent library; test on LIBERO-PRO and MolmoSpaces; retrieve skills to boost other agents.
AI.result: Significant gains: +20.6pp on LIBERO-PRO and +17.0pp on MolmoSpaces for held-out tasks vs CaP-Agent0; plugging retrieved skills into other inference-time Code-as-Policy agents yields +8.9pp (RoboSuite) and +8.8pp (real-world) without finetuning.
AI.conclusion: Skill libraries learned from play generalize to new tasks and other agents, offering a scalable path to reusable robot policies.

title: PerceptionDLM: Parallel Region Perception with Multimodal Diffusion Language Models
summary: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have achieved remarkable progress in visual understanding tasks. However, most existing MLLMs rely on autoregressive generation, which limits their efficiency for perception tasks that require captioning multiple regions. In this work, we propose PerceptionDLM, a multimodal diffusion language model optimized for efficient parallel region perception. Built upon PerceptionDLM-Base, a strong foundational baseline that achieves state-of-the-art performance among open-source diffusion MLLMs, our architecture fully leverages the parallel decoding nature of DLMs. Specifically, we introduce efficient prompting and structured attention masking to enable simultaneous perception of multiple masked regions, allowing the model to generate region descriptions in parallel at both the sequence and token levels. This design significantly improves inference efficiency compared with existing approaches that process regions sequentially. To systematically evaluate the parallelism property of visual perception capability for DLMs, we construct a new Parallel Detailed Localized Captioning Benchmark (ParaDLC-Bench) by scaling the DLC-Bench to include multiple region masks per image, enabling joint evaluation of both caption quality and inference efficiency. Experiments demonstrate that PerceptionDLM maintains competitive performance in region captioning while achieving substantial speed improvements for multi-region perception tasks. Our results highlight the potential of multimodal diffusion language models for efficient, parallel visual perception. To the best of our knowledge, we are the first to achieve parallel region caption and perception by leveraging the advantages of diffusion language models. Code, models, and datasets are released.

AI.tldr: Proposes PerceptionDLM, a diffusion-based multimodal language model enabling parallel region perception and captioning, achieving substantial speedups over sequential-region methods while maintaining competitive region-caption quality; introduces ParaDLC-Bench for evaluating parallel perception.
AI.motivation: Current multimodal LMs relying on autoregressive generation are inefficient for perceiving and captioning many regions; there is a need for parallel processing to speed up multi-region perception without sacrificing quality.
AI.method: Base model PerceptionDLM-Base; adds efficient prompting and structured attention masking to enable simultaneous perception of multiple masked regions, enabling parallel generation of region descriptions at both sequence and token levels; introduces ParaDLC-Bench to evaluate parallel perception and captioning; releases code and datasets.
AI.result: Maintains competitive performance on region captioning while achieving substantial inference speedups for multi-region perception; first method to enable parallel region captioning and perception using diffusion LLMs; new benchmark supports evaluation of parallelism.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates the viability of diffusion language models for efficient, parallel multimodal perception; offers practical routing and benchmark tools to advance research and applications; full code/models/datasets released.

title: DeXposure-Claw: An Agentic System for DeFi Risk Supervision
summary: Decentralized finance exposes supervisors to fast-moving, networked credit risks. General-purpose LLM agents fit this setting poorly: they over-read weak evidence and recommend high-stakes interventions, while existing evaluations offer no regulator-aligned way to measure the resulting false alarms. We introduce DeXposure-Claw, a forecast-grounded agentic supervision system that routes LLM decisions through structured evidence: (1) DeXposure-FM, a graph time-series foundation model, forecasts future exposure networks; (2) deterministic monitors and stress scenarios then turn those forecasts into typed alerts, attribution signals, and scenario evidence; and (3) data-health and confidence gates constrain escalation before DeXposure-Claw emits auditable supervisory tickets with rationales. We further develop DeXposure-Bench, a six-axis evaluation harness, whose decision axis scores tickets against a regulator-aligned absolute-loss ground truth and an explicit false-intervention rate. Experiments on five years of weekly real data fully support our system. Code is at https://github.com/EVIEHub/DeXposure-Claw.

AI.tldr: Forecast-grounded, regulator-aligned supervisory system for DeFi risk: DeXposure-Claw uses a graph time-series model (DeXposure-FM), deterministic monitors, data-health/confidence gates, and auditable tickets; evaluated with DeXposure-Bench on five years of weekly data; code released.
AI.motivation: General-purpose LLMs mis-handle fast-moving, networked credit risk in decentralized finance, over-reading weak evidence and producing high-stakes interventions. Current evaluations lack regulator-aligned metrics for false alarms, hindering auditable, trustworthy supervision.
AI.method: (1) DeXposure-FM—a graph time-series foundation model forecasting future exposure networks; (2) deterministic monitors and stress scenarios convert forecasts into typed alerts, attribution signals, and scenario evidence; (3) data-health and confidence gates constrain escalation; culminates in auditable supervisory tickets with rationales. DeXposure-Bench—a six-axis evaluation harness scoring tickets against a regulator-aligned absolute-loss ground truth and explicit false-intervention rate.
AI.result: Experiments on five years of weekly real data fully support the system.
AI.conclusion: DeXposure-Claw provides an auditable, evidence-driven, regulator-aligned supervision workflow for DeFi credit risk, with publicly available code; potential applicability to other fast-moving networked-risk domains.

title: Zero-Inflated Gaussian Distributions Enable Parameter-Space Sparsity in Estimation-of-Distribution Algorithms
summary: Estimation-of-distribution algorithms (EDAs) are a powerful class of evolutionary methods for black-box optimization, especially when little is known about the structure of the objective. Whereas classical evolutionary algorithms rely on hand-designed mutation and crossover operators, hard to devise for unknown problem structures, and a source of bias, EDAs sidestep operator design entirely: they fit a probability distribution to the best individuals and sample the next generation from it. EDAs are well established on continuous parameter spaces, but they have not previously been generalized to sparse ones, in which most coefficients of a good solution are exactly zero. Existing sparse black-box optimizers therefore reintroduce exactly what EDAs were designed to avoid: hand-crafted sparsity operators, bi-level schemes alternating between support set and active values, zeroing thresholds, and other baked-in assumptions. We close this gap by proposing multivariate zero-inflated Gaussian (ZIG) distributions as EDA sampling laws. A latent Gaussian model with separate indicator and value dimensions represents sparsity patterns, correlations among active parameters, and the interactions between the two, so sparsity patterns and active values are optimized jointly, hierarchy-free. We show that the latent parameters of this model are identifiable from observed samples, unlike in the missing-data settings where related constructions originate, and introduce practical amortized inversion-based estimators for them. The estimators accurately recover latent correlation structures, and on the Lunar Lander benchmark the resulting ZIG-EDA converges faster and reaches higher final returns than a dense Gaussian EDA, a hand-crafted sparse evolutionary algorithm, and an ad-hoc sparse EDA, while finding controllers with only a small fraction of parameters active.

AI.tldr: Introduces a principled EDA for sparse optimization by using a multivariate zero-inflated Gaussian (ZIG) model; a latent Gaussian framework with separate indicator and value dimensions enables joint sparsity and parameter estimation, yielding identifiable parameters and faster, sparser solutions on Lunar Lander.
AI.motivation: Extend EDAs to sparse, high-dimensional spaces without hand-crafted sparsity operators or bi-level schemes, addressing issues of identifiability and learning efficiency in missing-data-like models.
AI.method: Propose a latent Gaussian ZIG model with distinct indicator (sparsity) and value (active parameters) dimensions; derive amortized inversion-based estimators to recover latent structure; enable hierarchy-free, joint optimization of sparsity patterns and values; sample from ZIG-EDA distributions.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on Lunar Lander shows ZIG-EDA converges faster and achieves higher final rewards than dense Gaussian EDA, hand-crafted sparse EA, and ad-hoc sparse EDA; finds controllers with only a small fraction of parameters active; latent parameters are identifiable.
AI.conclusion: ZIG-EDA provides a principled, identifiable, hierarchy-free approach to sparse EDAs, enabling joint optimization of sparsity and values and delivering superior performance with sparse solutions.

title: Mix-QVLA: Task-Evidence-Aware Mixed-Precision Quantization of Vision-Language-Action Models
summary: We propose Mix-QVLA, a task-evidence-aware mixed-precision PTQ framework for VLA models. Mix-QVLA anchors each quantized variant to the full-precision action-token reference decision and evaluates whether quantization preserves task-relevant evidence across key VLA functional boundaries. It computes normalized gradient-weighted task-evidence maps from boundary activations and compares full-precision and quantized maps using evidence-mass and attribution-distribution distortion, capturing changes in both the strength and allocation of decision-supporting evidence. A soft-bottleneck objective aggregates boundary-level degradation into layer-wise sensitivity scores. Mix-QVLA further models sensitivity throughout task execution, capturing phase-dependent shifts in layer importance rather than assuming a fixed sensitivity profile. The resulting evidence- and time-aware scores guide mixed-precision bit allocation under model-size and BitOps budgets. Extensive evaluations on OpenVLA-style policies show that Mix-QVLA improves the accuracy-efficiency trade-off of low-bit VLA deployment. On LIBERO, Mix-QVLA reduces OpenVLA-OFT memory from 15.4 GB to 4.1 GB, retains 96.3 average success compared with 97.1 for the BF16 model, and achieves a 1.52x inference speedup.

AI.tldr: Mix-QVLA is a task-evidence-aware mixed-precision post-training quantization framework for VLA models. It anchors quantized variants to full-precision decisions, uses gradient-weighted task-evidence maps across boundary activations, and introduces soft-bottleneck and phase-aware sensitivity for allocation under size/compute budgets. It yields improved accuracy-efficiency and substantial memory/speed gains on OpenVLA-style policies and LIBERO benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Quantization can degrade task-relevant evidence in vision-language alignment; fixed sensitivity assumptions fail to capture phase-dependent importance. There is a need for evidence- and time-aware bit allocation that preserves decision-supporting evidence across model execution.
AI.method: Compute normalized gradient-weighted task-evidence maps from boundary activations, compare full-precision and quantized maps using evidence-mass and attribution-distribution distortion. Use a soft-bottleneck objective to derive layer-wise sensitivity, and model phase-dependent sensitivity to capture shifts in layer importance during task execution. Use evidence- and time-aware scores to guide mixed-precision bit allocation under model-size and BitOps budgets.
AI.result: OpenVLA-style policy evaluations show improved accuracy–efficiency trade-offs for low-bit VLA deployment. In LIBERO, memory usage drops from 15.4 GB to 4.1 GB, average success remains high (96.3 vs 97.1 for BF16), and inference speedup is 1.52×.
AI.conclusion: A dynamic, evidence- and time-aware mixed-precision framework enabling more efficient deployment of VLA models by preserving task-relevant evidence across quantization, with demonstrated gains on standard benchmarks.

title: LLM Doesn't Know What It Doesn't Know: Detecting Epistemic Blind Spots via Cross-Model Attribution Divergence on Clinical Tabular Data
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly applied to structured clinical data, yet whether they can recognize the limits of their own knowledge on such tasks remains unexplored. We study this question through the lens of cross-model attribution divergence with the goal of reducing epistemic uncertainty for structured tasks, comparing Qwen 2.5 7B and XGBoost on a prediction task via attribution divergence analysis. We report four findings. First, LLM verbalized confidence is epistemically vacuous, it outputs a near-constant (0.856-0.937) regardless of whether accuracy is 49% or 75.3%, tracking prompt format rather than prediction quality. Second, the LLM exhibits an inverse difficulty effect: accuracy drops to 64.8% when XGBoost is 99% correct, but matches XGBoost (73.8% vs. 73.1%) when it is moderately uncertain. Third, few-shot examples and SHAP-derived feature evidence are orthogonal, super-additive interventions: they reduce the Attribution Disagreement Score (ADS) from 1.54 to 0.38 and improve accuracy from 49% to 75.3% without training. Fourth, a cross-model calibrator that determined LLM reliability using attribution divergence signals reduces expected calibration error from 0.254 to 0.080, replacing uninformative verbalized confidence with patient-specific reliability estimates, without accessing model internals or requiring repeated inference. We frame these findings as a cold start problem for LLMs on structured data and outline a path toward genuine epistemic self-awareness.

AI.tldr: LLMs show epistemic uncertainty in structured data; attribution-divergence analysis with Qwen 2.5 7B vs XGBoost reveals uninformative verbalized confidence; few-shot prompts and SHAP evidence mitigate attribution disagreement and boost accuracy; cross-model calibrator improves calibration without model internals, suggesting a path to epistemic self-awareness.
AI.motivation: Investigate whether LLMs can recognize the limits of their knowledge on structured tasks and reduce epistemic uncertainty using cross-model attribution signals.
AI.method: Compare Qwen 2.5 7B (LLM) and XGBoost (structured model) on a structured prediction task; analyze attribution divergence; assess verbalized confidence; test few-shot prompts and SHAP-derived feature evidence; measure Attribution Disagreement Score (ADS) and accuracy; develop a cross-model calibrator using attribution-divergence signals to estimate reliability without accessing internals or repeating inferences.
AI.result: LLM verbalized confidence is near-constant across varying accuracy; inverse difficulty effect observed; few-shot and SHAP interventions are orthogonal and additive, reducing ADS from 1.54 to 0.38 and increasing accuracy from 49% to 75.3% without training; cross-model calibrator reduces expected calibration error from 0.254 to 0.080, replacing uninformative confidence with patient-specific reliability estimates.
AI.conclusion: Frames the problem as a cold start for LLMs on structured data and outlines a path toward genuine epistemic self-awareness through cross-model signals and nonintrusive calibration.

title: Human-like autonomy emerges from self-play and a pinch of human data
summary: Self-play reinforcement learning has recently emerged as a way to train driving policies without any human data. It uses cheap, large-scale simulations to substitute expensive, large-scale human driving demonstrations. A key limitation of this approach is that policies trained through pure self-play can learn effective but alien driving conventions incompatible with people. Previous works attempt to mitigate such behavioral misalignments through extensive reward engineering and domain randomization, which are brittle and labor-intensive. Instead of completely discarding human demonstrations, our method treats them as a regularization objective on top of a minimal safe goal-reaching reward. Like the spice in a good stew, we find that a little human data goes a long way: our method uses only 30 minutes of human demonstrations, 2500x fewer than comparable imitation learning approaches. Resulting policies coordinate with held-out human trajectories and complete training in 15 hours on a single consumer-grade GPU. Videos and full source code are available at https://spiced-self-play.com/.

AI.tldr: A data-efficient self-play reinforcement learning framework for driving that uses a small amount of human demonstrations as a regularizer atop a minimal safe goal-reaching objective, achieving alignment with human trajectories with only 30 minutes of data and 15 hours of training on a consumer GPU.
AI.motivation: Pure self-play can yield effective but human-incompatible driving conventions. Previous reward engineering and domain randomization approaches are brittle and labor-intensive. Incorporating a small amount of human data as a regularization term aims to robustly align self-play policies with human behavior while maintaining sample efficiency.
AI.method: Train driving policies with a minimal safe goal-reaching reward from self-play, and augment with a human-demonstration regularization objective. Use only ~30 minutes of human data (about 2500x less than typical imitation learning baselines). Training completes in ~15 hours on a consumer-grade GPU. Full code and videos available.
AI.result: Policies coordinate with held-out human trajectories, showing improved behavioral alignment without extensive human data. Demonstrates high sample efficiency and practical training requirements; scalable to home hardware.
AI.conclusion: A small amount of human data, applied as a regularizer to a self-play objective, significantly improves alignment with human driving behavior and reduces data and compute requirements compared to traditional imitation learning approaches.

title: Language-Instructed Vision Embeddings for Controllable and Generalizable Perception
summary: Vision foundation models are typically trained as static feature extractors, placing the burden of task adaptation onto large downstream models. We propose an alternative paradigm: instead of solely feeding visual features into language models, we use language itself to dynamically guide the vision encoder. Our method, Language-Instructed Vision Embeddings (LIVE), leverages language as high-level guidance to produce task-centric embeddings at inference time, removing the need for task-specific retraining. This enables the encoder to focus on contextually relevant aspects of the input, yielding more controllable and generalizable representations. Empirically, LIVE reduces visual hallucinations (+34 points on MMVP), surpasses vision-language models with orders of magnitude more parameters on visual question answering, and generalizes to unseen instructions and tasks -- offering a direct path toward adaptive, instruction-driven visual intelligence.

AI.tldr: LIVE introduces language-instructed vision embeddings to dynamically guide the vision encoder at inference time, producing task-centric representations without retraining.
AI.motivation: To overcome the limitations of static, pre-trained vision features that require task-specific fine-tuning and can cause hallucinations; leverage natural language as high-level guidance to steer visuals toward task relevance and generalize to unseen tasks.
AI.method: Use language prompts to condition the vision encoder, producing task-centric embeddings at inference time without task-specific retraining; language-guided modulation likely through gating/attention or prompting to align visual features with instructions.
AI.result: Empirically, LIVE reduces visual hallucinations by +34 points on MMVP, outperforms larger vision-language models on VQA, and generalizes to unseen instructions and tasks.
AI.conclusion: Language-conditioned vision embeddings offer a direct path to adaptive, instruction-driven visual intelligence with improved controllability and generalization without retraining.

title: REVEAL++: Differentiable Phenotypic Grouping for Vision-Language Retinal Modeling of Alzheimer's Disease Risk
summary: The retina offers a noninvasive window into neurodegenerative disease, capturing subtle structural patterns associated with a risk of future cognitive decline. Vision-language alignment frameworks such as REVEAL have shown that pairing retinal fundus images with structured clinical risk narratives improves early prediction of Alzheimer's disease (AD). A key design choice in these approaches is the use of phenotypic grouping, where individuals with similar risk profiles are treated as multi-positive pairs during contrastive learning. However, existing methods operationalize phenotypic similarity as a discrete construct, relying on hard group assignments that impose rigid supervision and decouple group formation from representation learning. We propose a continuous formulation of phenotypic structure within contrastive learning. Rather than assigning samples to fixed clusters, we model inter-subject similarity as a differentiable weighting function derived from intra-modality embedding similarities in both retinal images and risk profiles. These weights define soft multi-positive relationships through a continuous aggregation operator, enabling graded supervision that reflects the spectrum nature of disease risk. We further introduce a soft-target contrastive objective that jointly learns cross-modal alignment and phenotypic structure in an end-to-end manner. Evaluated on UK Biobank retinal imaging data for incident AD prediction, the proposed framework consistently outperforms discrete group-based contrastive learning and standard vision-language baselines. By treating phenotypic similarity as a learnable, continuous signal rather than a fixed grouping rule, our approach provides a principled and robust foundation for population-scale neurodegenerative risk modeling from multi-modal retinal and clinical data.

AI.tldr: Continuous phenotypic structure in contrastive learning for retina-based AD risk; soft multi-positive weights derived from intra-modality embeddings to improve cross-modal alignment.
AI.motivation: Limitations of discrete phenotypic grouping in vision-language contrastive learning for AD risk; need to model disease risk as a spectrum and leverage multi-modal data.
AI.method: Compute differentiable similarity weights from image and risk-embedding spaces, apply soft aggregation to produce soft positives; train with soft-target contrastive loss jointly optimizing cross-modal alignment and phenotypic structure end-to-end.
AI.result: On UK Biobank retinal data, improved incident AD prediction over discrete-group contrastive learning and standard vision-language baselines.
AI.conclusion: Treating phenotypic similarity as a learnable continuous signal yields improved robustness and scalability for population-scale neurodegenerative risk modeling from retina and clinical data.

title: ProMUSE: Progressive Multi-modal Uncertainty-guided Staged Evidential Alzheimer Disease Classification
summary: Alzheimer's disease (AD) is a fatal disorder that destroys memory and cognitive skills in the elderly population. Most treatments for AD are effective in the early stage, leading to an increasing demand for early AD diagnosis. AD diagnosis increasingly relies on multimodal data such as clinical assessments, structural Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI), and Positron Emission Tomography (PET) imaging. However, MRI and PET acquisition remain costly and not universally accessible, making full-modality inference impractical in real-world clinical workflows. We propose ProMUSE, a Progressive Multi-modal Uncertainty Guided Staged Evidential Network that adaptively determines when additional modalities are necessary, helping reduce the overall cost of data acquisition while maintaining accuracy. ProMUSE first performs evidential classification using low-cost clinical data and quantifies uncertainty via a Dirichlet-based subjective logic model. When uncertainty exceeds a learned threshold, ProMUSE progressively incorporates MRI or PET features, fusing modality-wise belief and uncertainty through Dempster-Shafer theory to obtain a calibrated multimodal prediction. This staged acquisition strategy enables accurate diagnosis while minimizing reliance on expensive imaging. Experiments on ADNI, AIBL, and OASIS across CN-AD, CN-MCI, and MCI-AD tasks demonstrate that ProMUSE achieves competitive or superior accuracy compared to full-modality baselines while reducing MRI/PET usage by 50-90%, yielding substantial cost savings. These results highlight ProMUSE as a practical, uncertainty-aware, and resource-efficient solution for real-world AD screening.

AI.tldr: ProMUSE performs staged multimodal diagnosis for Alzheimer's disease by starting with low-cost clinical data, using Dirichlet-based uncertainty to decide when to add MRI/PET, and fusing evidence with Dempster-Shafer theory; achieves comparable accuracy to full-modality baselines while greatly reducing expensive imaging.
AI.motivation: The need for early and accurate AD diagnosis is hampered by the cost and limited accessibility of MRI/PET, making full-modality inference impractical in real-world clinics.
AI.method: Evidential classification on clinical data with a Dirichlet-based subjective-logic model to quantify uncertainty; a thresholded, progressive incorporation of MRI or PET features; fusion of modality-wise belief and uncertainty via Dempster-Shafer theory to yield calibrated multimodal predictions.
AI.result: On ADNI, AIBL, and OASIS datasets across CN-AD, CN-MCI, and MCI-AD tasks, ProMUSE matches or exceeds full-modality baselines while reducing MRI/PET usage by 50–90%, enabling cost savings without sacrificing accuracy.
AI.conclusion: ProMUSE offers an uncertainty-aware, resource-efficient solution for real-world AD screening, enabling accurate diagnosis with substantially reduced reliance on expensive imaging.

title: GB-LSR: A Fast Local Spectral Image Representation with a Single Global Bandwidth for Continuous Reconstruction and Super-Resolution
summary: We present GB-LSR (Global-Bandwidth Local Spectral Representation), a fixed-grid local spectral representation for continuous image reconstruction. The image domain is partitioned into non-overlapping square patches, each carrying coefficients for a truncated Fourier basis predicted from shared convolutional-encoder features. A single trainable scalar bandwidth is shared globally across all patches and images, and reconstruction at any continuous coordinate is a fixed-size basis contraction whose cost is independent of image size.
