To bridge the sim-to-real gap, we subsequently transfer the self-play trained policies to real-world sensor data through lightweight perception adaptation. Policies trained in Gigapixel and adapted to real-world sensor data achieve competitive performance on the HUGSIM and NAVSIM-v2 benchmarks without human trajectory supervision. Moreover, scaling self-play training yields proportional gains in policy performance, establishing self-play as a practical and scalable strategy for training end-to-end models.

AI.tldr: Pixel-based self-play with a high-throughput simulator (Gigapixel) and on-policy distillation yields scalable end-to-end driving policies that transfer to real sensor data without human trajectories.
AI.motivation: To overcome limitations of offline, purely supervised training (limited state coverage, no closed-loop feedback) and brittleness to long-tail interactions in end-to-end driving by enabling large-scale self-play directly from pixels.
AI.method: Introduce Gigapixel, a perspective-rendered bounding-box driving simulator with high throughput (50k agent steps/s) for scalable pixel-based self-play. Train policies via self-play DAgger using on-policy distillation from a privileged RL teacher, followed by lightweight perception adaptation to real-world sensor data.
AI.result: Policies trained in Gigapixel and adapted to real sensor data achieve competitive performance on HUGSIM and NAVSIM-v2 benchmarks without human trajectory supervision; scaling self-play yields proportional gains in policy performance, demonstrating practicality and scalability of self-play for end-to-end driving.
AI.conclusion: Self-play is a viable, scalable strategy for end-to-end driving model training. The combination of pixel-based self-play, efficient simulation, and sim-to-real adaptation enables competitive performance without human trajectory data; performance improves proportionally with more self-play.

title: VFACamou: View-Fused Adversarial Camouflage for Environment-Adaptive Physical Evasion
summary: Adversarial camouflage in the physical world remains highly challenging, particularly under UAV reconnaissance where targets undergo continuous geometric changes and extreme illumination variations. Existing methods either optimize 2D digital perturbations that fail to generalize to dynamic viewpoints or produce visually unnatural textures that cannot be deployed in real scenarios. Therefore, we propose an end-to-end framework for adversarial camouflage generation that automatically produces wearable adversarial patterns and maintains stable attack performance in real physical environments with changing viewpoints, poses, and lighting conditions. Our method integrates UV-volume rendering with a diffusion-based texture generator, enabling consistent appearance under varying scales, poses, and lighting conditions. To ensure environmental realism, we propose an illumination color consistency estimator that extracts dominant background attributes and guides a natural texture loss to align the generated UV texture with the surrounding environment. A multi-scale dynamic training strategy further enhances robustness against viewpoint shifts and body deformation. Extensive experiments across multiple mainstream detectors demonstrate that our method achieves strong and stable physical attack performance while maintaining high perceptual naturalness, reducing human detection rates without introducing unnatural artifacts.

AI.tldr: End-to-end adversarial camouflage framework for wearable patterns that remain effective under changing UAV viewpoints and lighting, using UV-volume rendering with a diffusion-based texture generator and illumination-aware losses to ensure natural, robust physical attacks.
AI.motivation: Current 2D perturbations often fail to generalize to dynamic viewpoints and lighting; textiles with unnatural textures hinder real-world deployment; there is a need for robust, perceptually plausible camouflage under varying conditions.
AI.method: Integrates UV-volume rendering with a diffusion-based texture generator; introduces an illumination color consistency estimator to extract dominant background attributes and guide a natural texture loss that aligns the UV texture with the surrounding environment; employs a multi-scale dynamic training strategy to enhance robustness against viewpoint shifts and body deformation.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across multiple mainstream detectors show strong and stable physical attack performance while maintaining high perceptual naturalness, reducing human detection rates without introducing unnatural artifacts.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework enables practical, robust physical adversarial camouflage for UAV scenarios with changing viewpoints and lighting, balancing attack efficacy and perceptual realism.

title: BrainG3N: A Dual-Purpose Tokenizer for Controllable 3D Brain MRI Generation
summary: Three-dimensional (3D) brain MRI is central to clinical neurology and neuro-oncology, where generative models could augment under-represented cohorts, simulate disease trajectories, and support privacy-preserving data sharing. Latent diffusion has been the go-to solution for modeling imaging data, but it places two competing demands on the tokenizer: encoder embeddings must retain the clinical information that downstream tasks act on, and the decoder must reconstruct anatomically faithful volumes. Existing reconstruction-driven tokenizers achieve the second at the expense of the first. To address this, we introduce a fully volumetric masked-autoencoder (MAE) based tokenizer for 3D brain MRI latent diffusion, decoupling encoder and decoder: a frozen 3D MAE encoder produces clinically informative embeddings, while a dedicated CNN decoder reconstructs voxels from a linear projection of those embeddings. We pretrain the encoder on 35,309 volumes from 18 public cohorts spanning four modalities, ten disease categories, and 200+ acquisition sites, and demonstrate its dual utility in two settings. First, on a 23-task linear-probing benchmark, the encoder outperforms or matches SOTA models (i.e., BrainIAC, BrainSegFounder, and MedicalNet) on 21 of 23 tasks. Second, a conditional diffusion transformer (DiT) trained on these clinically informative embeddings supports both conditional generation across six variables and patient-specific longitudinal forecasting. Together these results establish a single 3D brain-MRI embedding space capable of both downstream clinical tasks and controllable generation.

AI.tldr: A volumetric MAE-based tokenizer decouples encoder/decoder for 3D brain MRI latent diffusion, enabling a single embedding space usable for both downstream tasks and controllable generation; pretraining on large, multi-site data yields strong linear-probing performance and supports conditional diffusion-based generation with longitudinal forecasting.
AI.motivation: To resolve the conflict in reconstruction-driven tokenizers between preserving clinically informative embeddings and reconstructing anatomically faithful volumes by decoupling the encoder and decoder using a frozen volumetric MAE encoder and a dedicated CNN decoder.
AI.method: A fully volumetric MAE-based tokenizer for 3D brain MRI: freeze a 3D MAE encoder to produce embeddings; train a CNN decoder to reconstruct voxels from a linear projection of those embeddings. Pretrain the encoder on 35,309 volumes from 18 public cohorts (four modalities, ten disease categories, 200+ acquisition sites). Train a conditional diffusion transformer (DiT) on these embeddings for conditional generation across six variables and patient-specific longitudinal forecasting.
AI.result: Encoder achieves 21/23 tasks on a 23-task linear-probing benchmark, outperforming or matching SOTA models (BrainIAC, BrainSegFounder, MedicalNet). The DiT trained on these embeddings enables controllable generation across six variables and patient-specific longitudinal forecasting.
AI.conclusion: A single 3D brain-MRI embedding space can support both downstream clinical tasks and controllable generation, enabling privacy-preserving data sharing and broader utility across neurology and neuro-oncology.

title: Thermodynamic Signatures of Reasoning: Free-Energy and Spectral-Form-Factor Diagnostics for Hallucination Detection in Large Language Models
summary: Hallucination detection in large language models (LLMs) is deployment-critical, and recent work shows that the spectrum of attention-derived graph Laplacians carries strong signal about reasoning quality. Prior spectral diagnostics, however, summarize the Laplacian spectrum by a handful of eigenvalues or hand-picked scalars, leaving most of its structure unused. We propose Free-Energy Signatures (Fes), a spectral descriptor that treats each layer's attention Laplacian as a Hamiltonian and extracts its thermodynamic potentials partition function, free energy, spectral entropy, heat capacity together with the random-matrix-theory (RMT) spectral form factor. We prove three results: (i)~Lipschitz stability of Fes under attention perturbation; (ii)~an expressiveness result showing that Fes enriches finite spectral summaries and approximates moment-derived spectral functionals under explicit regularity and grid-resolution assumptions; and (iii)~a finite-sample PAC bound on the AUROC of a training-free detector built from Fes. Empirically, across six open-weight LLMs and six benchmarks, a lightweight probe on Fes descriptors achieves the strongest aggregate AUROC among attention-spectral baselines, improving over LapEig by $+6.5$ AUROC points and over GoR-4 by $+2.4$ points on average, while requiring no update to the underlying LLM. In the fully unsupervised setting, an RMT-deviation score achieves mean AUROC $0.71$, providing a label-free but weaker detector. A complementary RMT analysis shows that correct generations exhibit more Wigner-Dyson like spectral statistics, whereas hallucinations exhibit more Poisson-like statistics. The anonymized code and config are provided in the supplementary material.

AI.tldr: Introduces Free-Energy Signatures (Fes) to analyze attention-derived Laplacians in LLMs, providing stable, expressive spectral descriptors with theoretical guarantees and strong empirical detection of reasoning quality and hallucinations; includes training-free AUROC gains and unsupervised RMT-based scoring.
AI.motivation: Address limitations of prior spectral diagnostics that rely on a small subset of Laplacian eigenvalues. By treating each layer's attention Laplacian as a Hamiltonian and extracting thermodynamic potentials plus RMT metrics, Fes aims to capture richer spectral structure linked to reasoning quality and hallucinations in LLMs.
AI.method: Model each layer's attention Laplacian as a Hamiltonian. Compute thermodynamic descriptors (partition function, free energy, spectral entropy, heat capacity) and the RMT spectral form factor. Prove Lipschitz stability to attention perturbations; establish expressiveness bounds under regularity/grid assumptions; derive a finite-sample PAC bound for AUROC of a training-free detector built from Fes. Build a lightweight, training-free detector from Fes descriptors and evaluate across six open-weight LLMs and six benchmarks; also propose an unsupervised RMT deviation score.
AI.result: Fes-based descriptors outperform attention-spectral baselines, achieving the strongest aggregate AUROC among baselines, with +6.5 AUROC over LapEig and +2.4 over GoR-4 on average. The unsupervised RMT deviation score yields mean AUROC 0.71. RMT analysis indicates hallucinations tend toward Poisson-like spectra, while correct generations align with Wigner-Dyson statistics. Code/config are provided in supplementary material.
AI.conclusion: Fes provides a richer, theoretically grounded spectral descriptor for attention graphs that enhances detection of hallucinations without modifying the LLM, offers formal guarantees (stability, expressiveness, PAC bounds), and reveals a link between spectral statistics and generation quality. The approach yields practical gains and a label-free detector option via RMT, with available supplementary code.

title: DF-ExpEnse: Diffusion Filtered Exploration for Sample Efficient Finetuning
summary: A natural recipe for intelligent robotic decision-making is initializing from pretrained generative control policies, which have summarized offline experience, and adapting them to self-collected online experience. We present DF-ExpEnse, an exploration technique that improves the quality of online experience collection, thus increasing finetuning sample-efficiency. DF-ExpEnse leverages the multimodal modeling capabilities of the generative control policy to create an expressive and tractably evaluatable candidate set. It then utilizes an ensemble of critics to identify the action that best balances quality with high exploration interest. In fleet settings, DF-ExpEnse further enables cross-agent communication to facilitate collaborative exploration as a group. DF-ExpEnse can be seamlessly integrated with existing strategies that finetune pretrained generative control policies via reinforcement learning. We experimentally validate consistent sample-efficiency benefits through DF-ExpEnse across a variety of manipulation and locomotion tasks, compared to default finetuning and alternative action selection schemes. Project can be found at https://df-expense.github.io.

AI.tldr: DF-ExpEnse is an exploration technique that improves online data collection quality for finetuning pretrained generative control policies, using multimodal policy candidates and an ensemble of critics to balance action quality and exploration, with cross-agent collaboration in fleets. It yields consistent sample-efficiency gains on manipulation and locomotion tasks and integrates with standard RL fine-tuning methods.
AI.motivation: Increase sample efficiency during fine-tuning of pretrained generative control policies by improving the quality of online experience data and enabling scalable exploration, including cross-agent coordination.
AI.method: Generate expressive candidate actions from the multimodal generative control policy, evaluate them with an ensemble of critics to balance exploitation (quality) and exploration, select actions accordingly, and, in fleet settings, enable cross-agent communication for collaborative exploration. The approach is designed to integrate with existing offline-to-online RL fine-tuning pipelines.
AI.result: Demonstrates consistent gains in sample efficiency across various manipulation and locomotion tasks compared to default finetuning and alternative action selection schemes; shows improved quality and diversity of online experiences; includes a project page for further details.
AI.conclusion: DF-ExpEnse provides a practical, plug-in exploration mechanism to enhance fine-tuning of pretrained generative control policies, with scalable benefits in single-agent and fleet contexts, and compatible with standard reinforcement learning fine-tuning workflows.

title: Occ-VLM: Occupancy Grounded Vision Language Model for Indoor Scene Understanding
summary: Recently, vision-language models (VLMs) have made significant progress in 3D scene understanding, driving advances in applications such as embodied intelligence and robotic vision. However, existing approaches typically either rely directly on explicit 3D inputs (e.g., point clouds or RGB-D sequences), or introduce an additional 3D geometry encoder to derive 3D-aware visual tokens from 2D images. Such designs structurally decouple 3D geometric perception from the rich 2D semantics learned via vision-language pre-training, hindering the development of a unified 3D vision-language representation. In this work, we propose Occ-VLM, a novel framework for 3D scene understanding that operates purely on posed RGB images and employs a single 2D vision encoder. Specifically, Occ-VLM reconstructs 3D scene occupancy as an auxiliary geometric prior, which is utilized to spatially associate foreground 2D tokens with 3D space. These tokens are then decoded by a Large Language Model (LLM) for unified scene understanding. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Occ-VLM achieves both accurate geometric perception and robust vision-language reasoning: it attains state-of-the-art performance on multi-view occupancy prediction, while performing on par with 3D-input VLMs on 3D Visual Question Answering (VQA) and 3D dense captioning benchmarks.

AI.tldr: Occ-VLM enables 3D scene understanding using posed RGB images with a single 2D vision encoder, reconstructing 3D occupancy as a geometric prior to align 2D tokens to 3D space, and decoding with an LLM for unified vision-language reasoning; it achieves state-of-the-art multi-view occupancy prediction and competitive 3D VQA/dense captioning without explicit 3D inputs or 3D encoders.
AI.motivation: To bridge the gap between 2D vision-language pre-training and 3D geometric perception by avoiding separate 3D geometry encoders or explicit 3D inputs, enabling unified 3D vision-language representations.
AI.method: Use posed RGB images with a single 2D vision encoder. Reconstruct 3D scene occupancy as an auxiliary geometric prior to spatially associate foreground 2D tokens with 3D space, then decode with a Large Language Model (LLM) for unified scene understanding.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on multi-view occupancy prediction and performs on par with 3D-input VLMs on 3D VQA and 3D dense captioning benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that accurate geometric perception and robust vision-language reasoning can be achieved in a unified framework without explicit 3D inputs or a separate 3D geometry encoder, advancing unified 3D VLM representations.

title: Denoising Implicit Feedback for Cold-start Recommendation
summary: Implicit feedback is widely used in recommender systems due to its accessibility and generality, yet it usually presents noisy samples (e.g., clickbait, position bias). Meanwhile, recommenders inevitably face the item cold-start problem due to the continuous influx of new items. We identify that cold items are more prone to noisy samples due to the aforementioned factors, and researchers often overlook the significance of denoising implicit feedback for cold items. Previous denoising studies usually identify noisy samples based on heuristic patterns, such as higher loss values, and mitigate noise through sample selection or re-weighting. However, these methods have limited adaptability and are ineffective in cold-start scenarios. To achieve denoising implicit feedback for cold-start recommendation, we propose a model-agnostic denoising method called DIF. First, user preferences for content remain stable, which allows us to infer pseudo-labels indicating whether a user is interested in a cold item through content-similar warm items. Furthermore, to improve pseudo-label accuracy, we model the confidence of pseudo-labels based on the content similarity between the cold item and warm items, and then aggregate multiple pseudo-labels for each sample. Finally, we explicitly estimate the uncertainty of the noisy sample label by considering its relative entropy and the cold-start status of the item, which adaptively guides the role of pseudo-labels to correct the noisy labels at the sample level. DIF's superiority is supported by both theoretical justification and extensive experiments on real-world datasets. The method has been deployed on a billion-user scale short video application Kuaishou and has significantly improved various commercial metrics within cold-start scenarios.

AI.tldr: Proposes DIF, a model-agnostic denoising method for implicit feedback in cold-start items, using content-based pseudo-labels, confident aggregation, and uncertainty-guided labeling; demonstrates theoretical justification and empirical/industrial deployment improvements.
AI.motivation: Implicit feedback is noisy and cold-start items accumulate more noise. Existing denoising methods often rely on heuristic signals and fail to address cold-start scenarios; a content-driven, model-agnostic solution is needed to denoise implicit feedback for new items.
AI.method: DIF creates pseudo-labels for cold items by leveraging content similarity to warm items with stable user preferences; assigns pseudo-label confidence based on content similarity; aggregates multiple pseudo-labels per sample; explicitly estimates label uncertainty via relative entropy and cold-start status to modulate pseudo-label influence at the sample level.
AI.result: The approach is theoretically justified and validated with extensive experiments on real-world datasets; deployed at billion-user scale in Kuaishou, yielding significant improvements in commercial metrics for cold-start scenarios.
AI.conclusion: DIF is effective, scalable, and model-agnostic for denoising implicit feedback in cold-start recommendations, combining content-based pseudo-labeling with uncertainty-aware aggregation and providing practical deployment benefits.

title: FlexLAM: Resolving the Bottleneck Trade-off in Latent Action Learning
summary: Latent actions provide a compact interface between action-free video and downstream decision-making, yet existing Latent Action Models (LAMs) force every transition through a fixed-capacity bottleneck. We identify a bottleneck trade-off: overly tight codes can discard transition cues needed for action alignment, while overly loose codes preserve additional transition variation that must be resolved when alignment labels are scarce or narrowly distributed. FlexLAM replaces this fixed capacity with variable-length latent actions trained by nested dropout, yielding prefix-valid codes that capture compact transition structure first and add detail only when needed, without new architectures or losses. A single FlexLAM matches or surpasses separately trained fixed-capacity LAMs at every evaluated token budget under standard scarce-label supervision and under a low-return single-task alignment stress test, indicating that FlexLAM is not merely adjustable at inference time but learns a better latent-action interface at the same token budgets. The same model supports inference-time token-budget adjustment without retraining, and FlexLAM improves Ego4D transition reconstruction. These results suggest that variable-length latent actions are an architecture-free, drop-in upgrade to the fixed-capacity bottleneck in latent action models, latent-action world models, and video-pretrained action interfaces.

AI.tldr: FlexLAM replaces fixed-capacity latent-action bottlenecks with variable-length latent actions learned by nested dropout, enabling prefix-valid codes and budget-aware inference; it matches or exceeds fixed-capacity LAMs across token budgets and improves Ego4D transition reconstruction, without new architectures or losses.
AI.motivation: Conventional LAMs suffer a bottleneck trade-off: too-tight codes discard transition cues needed for action alignment, while too-loose codes maintain variation that is hard to resolve with scarce or narrowly distributed alignment labels. A flexible, architecture-free latent-action interface is desired.
AI.method: Train variable-length latent actions via nested dropout to produce prefix-valid codes; no new architectures or losses. Evaluate under scarce-label supervision and a low-return single-task alignment stress test; support inference-time token-budget adjustment without retraining; apply to Ego4D.
AI.result: A single FlexLAM matches or surpasses separately trained fixed-capacity LAMs at every token budget under scarce-label supervision and the stress test; supports budget-adjusted inference without retraining; improves Ego4D transition reconstruction.
AI.conclusion: Variable-length latent actions are an architecture-free, drop-in upgrade to fixed-capacity bottlenecks in latent-action models and related video interfaces, enabling better latent-action interfaces at fixed budgets and enabling budget-aware inference.

title: HypOProto: Hyperbolic Ordinal Prototypes for Left Ventricular Filling Pressure Classification
summary: Echocardiography (echo) is a widely used imaging modality for assessing cardiac function, with Left Ventricular Filling Pressure (LVFP) serving as a critical physiological marker for conditions such as heart failure. Standard LVFP classification into normal \emph{vs} elevated categories relies on the Doppler-derived $E/e'$ ratio, which is operator-dependent and often unavailable in resource-limited settings, motivating methods that infer LVFP directly from B-mode echo. Existing deep learning approaches achieve high performance but remain largely black-box, limiting clinical interpretability. We propose HypOProto, a hyperbolic, ordinal prototype-based framework for interpretable LVFP classification using a frozen, explainable foundation model backbone. HypOProto arranges prototypes along the physiological $E/e'$ scale, placing borderline cases near the hyperboloid root where small angular differences separate similar cases, while normal and elevated cases occupy outward positions reflecting increasing diagnostic certainty. This hyperbolic geometry encodes clinically meaningful ordinal relationships and improves interpretability. We also introduce a novel Hyperbolic Prototype Angular Separation (HyperPAS) loss, enforcing inter-class prototype separation in hyperbolic space. HypOProto achieves SOTA performance while maintaining transparency, and highlights clinically relevant regions in visualizations. This work represents the first prototype-based framework for LVFP classification in echo. Our code can be found at https://github.com/DeepRCL/HypOProto.

AI.tldr: A hyperbolic, ordinal prototype-based classifier (HypOProto) for LVFP classification from B-mode echo that encodes E/e' ordinal relationships in hyperbolic space using a HyperPAS loss; achieves state-of-the-art accuracy with improved interpretability; code available at GitHub.
AI.motivation: LVFP classification conventionally relies on Doppler-derived E/e' and operator expertise; Doppler data are often unavailable in resource-limited settings. There is a need for interpretable, DL-based LVFP inference directly from B-mode echo, to improve accessibility and clinical trust. Prior DL methods are effective but opaque.
AI.method: HypOProto uses a frozen, explainable backbone and arranges class prototypes along the physiologic E/e' scale in hyperbolic space. Borderline cases lie near the hyperboloid root; normal and elevated cases populate outer regions to reflect increasing diagnostic certainty. A novel Hyperbolic Prototype Angular Separation (HyperPAS) loss enforces inter-class prototype separation in hyperbolic space. Outputs include region-based visual explanations highlighting clinically relevant areas.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance while preserving interpretability. Claims the first prototype-based framework for LVFP classification in echocardiography. Code available at https://github.com/DeepRCL/HypOProto.
AI.conclusion: Hyperbolic, ordinal prototype geometry aligns with clinical physiology, improving transparency and diagnostic reasoning for LVFP from B-mode echo. The approach supports interpretable deployment in settings lacking Doppler data and provides a foundation for future interpretable cardiac DL methods.

title: Exit-and-Join Dynamics for Decentralized Coalition Formation
summary: This paper studies coalition formation as a decentralized dynamical process driven by unilateral exit-and-join decisions. Agents evaluate local moves using the Aumann-Dreze value, so payoffs are computed within the agent's current coalition rather than through a globally negotiated coalition structure. The resulting model links cooperative payoff allocation with noncooperative best-response behavior: a terminal partition is precisely a coalition structure with no admissible, individually profitable exit-and-join deviation. We establish equilibrium characterizations, identify conditions under which the dynamics admit scalar Lyapunov or exact-potential representations, and analyze how switching and acceptance costs shape local stability. Numerical experiments test finite-time stabilization, cost sensitivity, and a special convex-game benchmark.

AI.tldr: Decentralized coalition formation via exit-join dynamics using Aumann-Dreze payoffs; terminal partitions are precisely stable structures; equilibrium characterizations and potential/Lyapunov representations; costs influence local stability; numerical validation on stabilization and a convex benchmark.
AI.motivation: Bridge cooperative payoff allocation with noncooperative dynamics by studying how locally computed Aumann-Dreze payoffs guide unilateral moves, and how stability and potential representations emerge in decentralized coalition formation.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis of a decentralized dynamical process with unilateral exit-and-join moves; payoffs computed within the current coalition via Aumann-Dreze value; derivation of equilibrium conditions; identification of Lyapunov scalar and exact-potential representations; analysis of switching and acceptance costs; numerical experiments on finite-time stabilization, cost sensitivity, and a convex-game benchmark.
AI.result: Terminal partitions are exactly those coalition structures with no profitable exit-and-join deviation; equilibrium characterizations established; conditions for Lyapunov or exact-potential representations derived; switching and acceptance costs shown to affect local stability; numerical experiments corroborate finite-time stabilization and sensitivity results, including a convex-game benchmark.
AI.conclusion: Decentralized exit-join dynamics with AD payoff allocation yield stable coalition structures that align with no-profitable-deviation criteria; analytical tools (Lyapunov potentials, exact potentials) capture stability; cost parameters shape dynamics; results validated numerically.

title: Spectral DPPs via NEPv: A Scalable Continuous Relaxation of Determinantal MAP for Diversity-Aware Data Selection
summary: Selecting a small, diverse, high-quality subset from a massive pool of candidates is a recurring primitive in modern machine learning -- data curation and coreset selection for training and fine-tuning large models, active-learning batch acquisition, prompt and exemplar selection for in-context learning, retrieval diversification, and experimental design. Determinantal Point Processes (\DPP s) give a principled, well-calibrated notion of diversity for this task, but their \emph{MAP} objective -- pick a size-$k$ subset $S$ maximizing $\logdet(L_S)$ -- is NP-hard, and the standard greedy and sampling algorithms scale superlinearly in the ground-set size $n$. This cost is prohibitive precisely in the data-centric regime where diversity matters most, where $n$ ranges over millions to billions of candidate examples, features, or embeddings. We recast \DPP-MAP as a continuous optimization problem over the Stiefel manifold, and show that its first-order optimality conditions form a \emph{Nonlinear Eigenvalue Problem with eigenvector dependency} (\NEPv) of a previously unstudied form. This \NEPv\ admits a self-consistent field (\SCF) iteration with a spectral-gap-based local contraction guarantee, giving a principled iterative solver where the diversity objective drives an eigenvector-dependent operator. The resulting algorithm, \OurMethod, requires only matrix-vector products with the kernel and runs in time $O\!\big((ndk+nk^2)\,t\big)$ for a small number of iterations $t$, scaling near-linearly in $n$ and integrating directly with low-rank and feature-map kernels common in ML. This paper focuses on the relaxation, solver, and scaling analysis; full real-data benchmarking is left to a planned empirical study.

AI.tldr: Reformulates DPP-MAP as a nonlinear eigenvalue problem on the Stiefel manifold and solves it with a self-consistent field (SCF) iteration. The proposed method (OurMethod) uses only kernel-matrix vector products, achieves near-linear scaling in dataset size n, and offers a spectral-gap-based local contraction guarantee. Empirical benchmarking is deferred to future work.
AI.motivation: Selecting small, diverse, high-quality subsets is a central challenge in data curation, coreset selection, active learning, in-context learning, retrieval diversification, and experimental design. DPP MAP is NP-hard and existing greedy/sampling methods scale superlinearly in n, making them impractical for datasets with millions to billions of candidates. A continuous optimization perspective on DPP-MAP promises scalable, principled diversity optimization.
AI.method: Recast DPP-MAP as a continuous optimization over the Stiefel manifold. Derive a nonlinear eigenvalue problem with eigenvector dependence (NEPv) whose first-order conditions define the problem. Solve via a self-consistent field (SCF) iteration that leverages a spectral-gap-based local contraction guarantee. The algorithm, OurMethod, relies on matrix-vector products with a kernel and runs in time O((ndk + nk^2) t) for a small number of iterations t, scaling near-linearly in n and integrating with low-rank and feature-map kernels.
AI.result: The paper provides relaxation, solver, and scaling analysis for the DPP-MAP reformulation. It establishes a principled SCF-based solver with contraction guarantees and demonstrates near-linear scaling in n via the stated complexity, using kernel-based operations. It does not include full real-data benchmarking within this work and frames empirical evaluation as future work.
AI.conclusion: A principled, scalable solver for DPP-MAP via NEPv and SCF is proposed, enabling efficient diversity optimization in large-scale ML settings. The approach offers a clear path to practical deployment when combined with common kernel representations, with empirical validation planned for future work.

title: ParaScale: Scale-Calibrated Camera-Motion Transfer via a Gauge-Invariant Parallax Number
summary: Transferring the camera motion of a reference video to a freshly generated one lets creators reuse cinematic moves. Yet reference and target often live at incompatible scales -- a sweep across a galaxy versus a nudge across a desk -- and naively reusing the recovered trajectory yields either imperceptible or violently exaggerated motion. We trace this to a geometric fact: translation-induced image motion scales as ||T||/Z, so a monocular trajectory is meaningful only up to a depth-scale gauge. We distill this into the Parallax Number Pi = ||Delta T|| / Zbar, a dimensionless, gauge-invariant descriptor of how strongly a camera move is felt, and prove that it -- not the raw trajectory -- is the quantity that scale-faithful transfer must preserve. ParaScale is a plug-and-play module that reads Pi off any reference video and re-realizes it against the target scene's own depth, per frame, leaving rotation untouched. Sitting between pose extraction and pose injection, it requires no retraining and drops into any pose-conditioned generator. We further introduce the Parallax Consistency Error (PCE), a scale-symmetric metric that -- unlike the similarity-aligned TransErr -- exposes scene-scale mismatch. Across scale regimes spanning four orders of magnitude and multiple backbones, ParaScale keeps the realized parallax on the identity line and cuts PCE by more than 3x over uncalibrated transfer with no loss of visual fidelity.

AI.tldr: Introduces Parallax Number Pi as a scale-invariant descriptor for monocular camera-motion transfer and presents ParaScale, a plug-and-play module that preserves the perceived parallax across scales by re-realizing reference parallax against the target depth per frame (without retraining). It also defines Parallax Consistency Error (PCE) to detect scale mismatch. Empirically, ParaScale maintains parallax accuracy across four scale regimes and various backbones, reducing PCE by over 3x with no loss in visual fidelity.
AI.motivation: Monocular camera-motion transfer suffers from scale ambiguity: image motion due to camera translation scales with ||T||/Z, so trajectories are meaningful only up to a depth-scale gauge. Naive transfers produce perceptually wrong motion when reference and target scenes differ in scale. A gauge-invariant, perceptually meaningful transfer metric and method are needed.
AI.method: Define Parallax Number Pi = ||Delta T|| / Zbar as a dimensionless, gauge-invariant descriptor of parallax strength. ParaScale reads Pi from a reference video and re-realizes it against the target scene’s depth per frame, leaving rotation unchanged and requiring no retraining (plug-and-play between pose extraction and injection). Also introduce Parallax Consistency Error (PCE), a scale-symmetric metric to reveal scene-scale mismatch. Evaluate across four scale regimes and multiple backbones.
AI.result: ParaScale keeps realized parallax on the identity line and reduces PCE by more than 3x compared to uncalibrated transfer, across four scale regimes and multiple backbones, with no loss of visual fidelity.
AI.conclusion: The Parallax Number Pi is the appropriate invariant to preserve for scale-faithful motion transfer, and ParaScale provides a practical, retraining-free solution. PCE offers a diagnostic for scale mismatch, and the approach demonstrates robust performance across wide scale disparities.

title: Beyond Static Leaderboards: Predictive Validity for the Evaluation of LLM Agents
summary: Agent benchmarks are growing fast, but no single benchmark touches more than four or five of the dimensions that deployment exposes. This paper aggregates the largest coordinated deep-dive of one MCP-based industrial-agent benchmark to date: fourteen parallel implementation studies covering new asset classes (including a multi-modal visual extension), alternative orchestrations, retrieval strategies, reasoning modes, infrastructure optimizations, and evaluation-methodology probes. Consolidating those studies with seven prior agent benchmarks, we argue that aggregate-score leaderboards systematically underspecify deployed-agent evaluation. Rankings derived from aggregate scores do not transfer to out-of-distribution settings; recent public-to-hidden competition retrospectives provide direct empirical evidence of this rank instability. We propose ranking configurations by predictive validity, the correlation between in-sample and out-of-sample rank, rather than in-sample mean, and report a twelve-tier measurement apparatus that exposes the deployment-relevant dimensions HELM and its agent-era successors collapse. The position is operationalized through three falsifiable out-of-distribution criteria with explicit thresholds; existing evidence partly supports it but is too thin to confirm. We close with a pre-registered pilot design and a field-level vision for what the next generation of agentic benchmarks should report.

AI.tldr: A comprehensive meta-analysis of MCP-based industrial-agent benchmarks shows aggregate-score leaderboards underspecify deployment realities and are unstable across out-of-distribution settings; proposing predictive-validity-based rankings and a 12-tier measurement apparatus to capture deployment-relevant dimensions, with three falsifiable OOD criteria, a pre-registered pilot, and a field-wide vision for next-gen benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Benchmarking for deployed agents remains underspecified by existing aggregate scores, leading to poor transfer to OOD settings. The study aggregates a large number of concurrent and prior benchmarks to diagnose deficiencies and proposes a more deployment-aligned evaluation framework.
AI.method: Synthesize 14 parallel implementation studies (including new asset classes, multi-modal extensions, and infrastructure/architecture variations) plus seven prior benchmarks; analyze ranking stability across settings; define a 12-tier measurement apparatus capturing deployment-relevant dimensions (HELM and successors); articulate three falsifiable out-of-distribution criteria; present a pre-registered pilot design and field-level vision for future benchmarks.
AI.result: Aggregate-score leaderboards undervalue deployment-relevant factors; rankings derived from aggregate scores do not reliably transfer to OOD settings, consistent with retrospective evidence of rank instability. A predictive-validity-based ranking approach and a twelve-tier measurement apparatus are proposed to expose HELM-related collapses; three explicit OOD criteria with thresholds are defined; evidence is preliminary but insufficient to confirm the claim. A pilot design is pre-registered and a field-level vision for next-gen benchmarks is offered.
AI.conclusion: To improve deployed-agent evaluation, benchmarks should report deployment-relevant dimensions and predictive validity of rankings, moving beyond in-sample means. The authors outline concrete criteria, a comprehensive measurement framework, and a roadmap for the next generation of agent benchmarks.

title: Spectral Retrieval-Augmented Time-Series Forecasting
summary: Time series forecasting leverages historical patterns to predict future values, but traditional methods face challenges when dealing with complex, non-stationary patterns that are difficult to memorize during training. Retrieval-augmented approaches have emerged as promising solutions by retrieving similar historical patterns to enhance predictions. However, existing retrieval methods suffer from two fundamental limitations: spectral blindness, which overlooks critical frequency-domain characteristics that capture underlying periodic structures, and temporal recency, which treats all historical data equally without emphasizing recent, more relevant patterns. In this paper, we propose SpecReTF, a novel retrieval method that addresses these issues by converting time series into windowed frequency representations, measuring similarity with a combined metric that captures both amplitude and phase information. To balance recency and historical context, we apply an exponential moving average weighting scheme that emphasizes recent windows. Extensive experiments on benchmark datasets demonstrate that SpecReTF outperforms time-domain retrieval methods, achieving superior forecasting accuracy across diverse, non-stationary time series.

AI.tldr: SpecReTF uses windowed frequency representations and amplitude-phase similarity with EMA weighting to retrieve relevant patterns for time-series forecasting, addressing spectral blindness and recency biases to improve accuracy on non-stationary data.
AI.motivation: Traditional time-series retrieval underutilizes frequency-domain information and treats all historical windows equally, limiting performance on non-stationary patterns. There is a need for methods that leverage spectral properties and emphasize recent, relevant patterns.
AI.method: Convert time-series windows into windowed frequency representations, measure similarity using a combined amplitude-and-phase metric, retrieve similar patterns, and apply exponential moving average weighting to emphasize recent windows during aggregation/phased retrieval integration.
AI.result: Empirical results on benchmark datasets show SpecReTF achieving higher forecasting accuracy than time-domain retrieval baselines across diverse non-stationary series.
AI.conclusion: SpecReTF mitigates spectral blindness and recency issues in retrieval-augmented forecasting and yields improved performance on non-stationary time series.

title: Training-Free Metrics for Synthetic Object Detection Data: A Proxy for Detector Performance
summary: With the recent advent of image generative models, synthetic data are increasingly being used to supplement limited real datasets for training computer vision models. However, not all synthetic datasets improve performance equally, and their effectiveness can only be assessed by training a downstream model, which is computationally expensive and time-consuming. This problem is pronounced in the task of object detection, where the required annotations are much more dense due to bounding boxes. In this paper, we propose a pre-computable metric family, dubbed Conditional-Composition Domain Match (CCDM), which serves as a proxy for the relative utility of candidate synthetic training sets for downstream detection. Experiments on the VisDrone-DET dataset show that the CCDM metric families achieve a Spearman correlation of 1.0 with the downstream performance of YOLOv8, clearly outperforming existing metrics for synthetic image evaluation.

AI.tldr: A pre-computable metric family, Conditional-Composition Domain Match (CCDM), predicts the downstream utility of synthetic training sets for object detection. On VisDrone-DET, CCDM correlates perfectly (Spearman 1.0) with YOLOv8 performance, outperforming prior synthetic-data evaluation metrics.
AI.motivation: Evaluating synthetic data quality for object detection is expensive due to the need to train downstream detectors; a fast proxy is needed to rank synthetic datasets before training.
AI.method: Introduce CCDM, a pre-computable metric family that measures domain match through conditional composition between synthetic and real data to estimate their utility for object detection. Validation uses downstream detector performance, here YOLOv8 on VisDrone-DET.
AI.result: CCDM achieves a Spearman correlation of 1.0 with YOLOv8 performance on VisDrone-DET, clearly outperforming existing metrics for synthetic image evaluation.
AI.conclusion: CCDM provides an effective, computation-efficient proxy for selecting synthetic training data for object detection, enabling better data curation without full model training, at least within the evaluated VisDrone-DET/YOLOv8 setting.

title: GLARE: A Natural Language Interface for Querying Global Explanations
summary: While global explanations are crucial for understanding vision models across datasets, classes, and decision contexts, their complex and monolithic nature often hinders practical exploration. Because users typically seek targeted answers to specific questions rather than static artifacts, we present an LLM-based interactive interface that provides natural language access to global explanations for black-box image classifiers. The system's core LLM acts as a mediator, translating natural language questions into structured SQL queries over local explanation data. This enables flexible aggregation without exposing users to low-level representations. For each query, the interface outputs statistics-augmented natural language responses, supporting local explanations, and intent-aligned visualizations. We evaluate the system on intent interpretation, query mapping accuracy, generalization to novel queries and datasets, and robustness to linguistic errors. Our results demonstrate that LLM-mediated querying substantially improves the accessibility and usability of global explanations for human-centered XAI.

AI.tldr: An LLM-mediated interface translates natural language questions into SQL over local explanations to enable flexible, user-facing access to global explanations for black-box image classifiers.
AI.motivation: Global explanations are often complex and monolithic, hindering targeted inquiries; providing natural-language access via an LLM mediator enhances usability for human-centered XAI.
AI.method: An LLM acts as a mediator that converts natural-language queries into structured SQL over local explanation data; outputs statistics-augmented, NL responses and intent-aligned visualizations; evaluates intent interpretation, query mapping, generalization, and linguistic robustness.
AI.result: The approach substantially improves accessibility and usability of global explanations for human-centered XAI, with strong performance in intent interpretation, query mapping accuracy, generalization to novel queries/datasets, and robustness to linguistic errors.
AI.conclusion: LLM-mediated querying is an effective paradigm to make global explanations more accessible, enabling targeted, human-centric exploration without exposing low-level representations.

title: Does Text Actually Help? Uncovering and Resolving Text Collapse in Multimodal Time Series Forecasting
summary: Multimodal time series forecasting, which pairs numerical sequences with domain-relevant textual reports, promises to inject world knowledge into forecasting pipelines. However, we uncover a critical failure mode in existing frameworks that we term text collapse: the text branch converges to a content-independent transformation, contributing negligible discriminative signal regardless of the input description. We argue that text collapse is a consequence of a fundamental asymmetry in time series forecasting: the numerical input is strongly autocorrelated with the output, making the numerical backbone inherently dominant, while the text branch, despite carrying complementary and often critical information, is insufficiently utilized, leading to its systematic underexploitation. To address this, we propose \textbf{REST-TS} (\textbf{R}esidual-\textbf{E}xclusive \textbf{S}upervision for \textbf{T}ext in \textbf{T}ime \textbf{S}eries), which turns the asymmetry into a design principle: the numerical backbone produces its own independent numerical forecast, and the text branch is exclusively supervised to predict the structured components of the residual, the prediction gap that numbers cannot explain. Because no numerical pathway can reduce these losses, the text branch must extract genuine content from the input description. Evaluated across diverse real-world domains and backbone architectures, REST-TS achieves state-of-the-art performance and consistently demonstrates greater text-branch utilization than existing frameworks, providing strong empirical evidence that supervising the text branch on the residual compels it to extract genuine content from the input.

AI.tldr: REST-TS introduces residual-exclusive supervision for text in time-series forecasting to overcome text collapse, forcing the text branch to extract meaningful information from textual reports by supervising it on the residuals left by the numerical backbone, achieving state-of-the-art results across domains and backbones.
AI.motivation: In multimodal time-series forecasting the numerical input is highly autocorrelated with the target, dominating the model; the text modality often carries valuable domain knowledge but is underutilized due to a text-collapse phenomenon where the text branch converges to a content-free transformation.
AI.method: Propose REST-TS: the numerical backbone predicts its own numeric forecast; the text branch is trained exclusively to predict the structured components of the residual (the gap between true target and numeric forecast). This residual-exclusive supervision forces the text branch to extract genuine content from the input text; the design prevents the numerical pathway from providing signals to the text loss.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation across diverse real-world domains and backbone architectures shows REST-TS achieves state-of-the-art performance and consistently higher text-branch usage than prior multimodal frameworks.
AI.conclusion: Residual-exclusive supervision effectively mitigates text collapse and promotes meaningful utilization of textual reports in multimodal time-series forecasting, establishing REST-TS as a robust principle for integrating text with numeric forecasts.

title: Comparative Study on Agility, Efficiency, and Impact Absorption of Bipedal Robots with Active Toes
summary: Human legs exhibit high efficiency, agility, and impact absorption, with toes playing a crucial role in these capabilities. While many attempts have been made to implement human-like toes in robots, they have not fully replicated human characteristics nor rigorously validated their benefits. We propose a 14-DOF biped robot emulating human toes' lightweight, high-torque, robust nature. To quantitatively analyze the effectiveness of the active toes in terms of agility, efficiency, and impact absorption, we developed a high-fidelity simulation training environment that reflects actual actuators with coupled transmissions and accurate power consumption. To ensure a fair comparison between configurations with and without active toes, we designed a minimal RL reward function and applied an identical training procedure to both. The simulation results indicate that, at 1.33 m/s walking, the toe-equipped robot reduced CoT by 17.5% and heel-strike GRF by 5.0% compared with the toe-ablation configuration. On the agility test, average and maximum path deviation decreased by 25.0% and 34.0%, respectively.

AI.tldr: 14-DOF biped robot with active toes shows improved efficiency, agility, and impact absorption in high-fidelity simulation; fair RL-based comparison indicates CoT reduced by 17.5%, heel-strike GRF reduced by 5%, and agility path deviations reduced by 25-34% with toes on at 1.33 m/s.
AI.motivation: Quantify the benefits of human-like toes in robotic locomotion using a rigorous, fair comparison in a high-fidelity simulator that captures actuator dynamics and power use.
AI.method: 14-DOF biped robot with active toes; high-fidelity simulation with coupled transmissions and accurate power modeling; minimal RL reward; identical training for configurations with and without active toes; evaluation at 1.33 m/s walking and achieved agility tests.
AI.result: Toe-equipped configuration reduces cost of transport by 17.5% and heel-strike ground reaction force by 5%; agility metrics show average and maximum path deviation reduced by 25% and 34%.
AI.conclusion: Active toes can measurably improve efficiency and agility, and reduce impact forces in simulated walking, validated through a fair comparative framework; further real-world validation is warranted.

title: CSWinUNETR: Segmentation of Thin Anatomical Structures in Medical Images
summary: Accurate segmentation of thin, tortuous anatomical structures, such as retinal vessels, cerebral vasculature, and facial wrinkles, remains challenging due to low contrast, frequent discontinuities, and severe class imbalance. Although recent convolutional and Transformer-based models have improved performance, they often yield fragmented predictions and fail to recover fine branches. We propose CSWinUNETR, a general-purpose backbone for 2D and 3D thin-structure segmentation. It employs cross-shaped stripe self-attention to model long-range principal-axis context and incorporates cyclic shifts to enhance information exchange across stripes. To better preserve fine-grained details, we further introduce a detail-enhanced multi-scale self-attention module that aggregates contextual features from multi-resolution representations. In addition, we propose sparse-control dynamic snake convolution, which reconstructs reliable dense curvilinear kernels from sparsely predicted control points to better follow tortuous geometry. Extensive experiments on four benchmarks across ophthalmology, neurovascular imaging, and dermatology demonstrate that CSWinUNETR consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods without task-specific post-processing or topology-aware losses. The code is available at https://github.com/labhai/CSWinUNETR.

AI.tldr: A general-purpose 2D/3D backbone CSWinUNETR for thin-structure segmentation, using cross-shaped stripe self-attention with cyclic shifts, a detail-enhanced multi-scale self-attention, and sparse-control dynamic snake convolution, achieving state-of-the-art results without post-processing across ophthalmology, neurovascular imaging, and dermatology; code released.
AI.motivation: Thin, tortuous anatomical structures (retinal vessels, cerebral vasculature, facial wrinkles) present low contrast, discontinuities, and severe class imbalance, leading to fragmented predictions. Existing CNN/Transformer-based methods struggle to preserve fine branches and long-range context.
AI.method: Proposes CSWinUNETR with (1) cross-shaped stripe self-attention to capture long-range principal-axis context, (2) cyclic shifts to improve inter-stripe information exchange, (3) detail-enhanced multi-scale self-attention to fuse multi-resolution contextual features, and (4) sparse-control dynamic snake convolution that reconstructs dense curvilinear kernels from sparse control points to follow tortuous geometry.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on four benchmarks spanning ophthalmology, neurovascular imaging, and dermatology show CSWinUNETR consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods without task-specific post-processing or topology-aware losses.
AI.conclusion: CSWinUNETR provides a versatile backbone for 2D/3D thin-structure segmentation with improved preservation of fine vessels and wrinkles, robust long-range context modeling, and accessible code for replication.

title: Interpreting Neural Combinatorial Optimization via Evolving Programmatic Bottlenecks
summary: Neural Combinatorial Optimization (NCO) achieves strong performance, yet its black-box nature remains a key roadblock to deployment and scientific diagnosis. Standard interpretability tools, such as Concept Bottleneck Models (CBMs), are ill-equipped for NCO, whose decisions are dynamic, state-dependent, and lack proper concept vocabulary definition. To close this gap, we introduce Evolving Programmatic Bottlenecks (EPB), to our knowledge, the first framework for interpreting NCO policies by distilling black-box NCO models into human-readable program portfolios. EPB employs an LLM to autonomously evolve a bank of programs, where each program's per-step action distribution serves as the bottleneck. EPB works through an iterative framework: Block I fixes program bank capacity and introduces a hybrid textual-numerical gradient descent scheme that couples numerical gradients for student router updates and textual gradients for LLM-based program revision; Block II dynamically adapts bank capacity via fault-targeted expansion and redundancy pruning. Extensive experiments demonstrate EPB's effectiveness and broad applicability, where the distilled program portfolios largely match original performance. EPB also reveals that NCO behavior shifts across optimization stages and can be approximated as a composition of classic heuristic variants. Our work advances interpretable NCO and establishes EPB as a promising tool for interpreting sequential decision-making models.

AI.tldr: EPB: a framework to interpret Neural Combinatorial Optimization (NCO) by distilling black-box policies into evolving, human-readable program portfolios via an LLM-guided bank of programs; uses a two-block iterative learning process to maintain interpretability with near-original performance.
AI.motivation: NCO models are powerful but opaque; standard interpretability tools (e.g., CBMs) struggle due to dynamic, state-dependent decisions and lack of stable concept vocabularies; a readable, adaptable interpretation framework is needed to aid deployment and scientific understanding.
AI.method: Two-block, iterative framework. Block I fixes the program bank capacity and uses a hybrid gradient descent: numerical gradients for updating the student router (NCO) and textual gradients for revising programs via an LLM. Block II adapts bank capacity through fault-targeted expansion and redundancy pruning. An LLM autonomously evolves a bank of programs; each program’s per-step action distribution serves as a bottleneck. Experiments assess interpretability and fidelity across tasks, and analyze whether NCO behavior can be approximated by composing classical heuristics.
AI.result: Distilled program portfolios largely match the original NCO performance, validating interpretability with minimal loss. EPB reveals that NCO behavior evolves across optimization stages and can be approximated as a composition of classic heuristic variants, highlighting the framework’s broad applicability to sequential decision-making models.
AI.conclusion: EPB advances interpretable neural combinatorial optimization by providing a scalable, LLM-guided method to distill black-box policies into interpretable program portfolios; it enables diagnosing sequential decision processes and suggests that NCO behavior aligns with known heuristic components.

title: MortarBench: Evaluating Mortgage Loan Origination Agents
summary: Loan origination is the process by which a lender creates a new loan, from application and underwriting through approval and funding. This process serves a critical role in evaluating the eligibility and level of risk posed by an applicant. Recently, firms have begun using mortgage loan agents to augment human loan officers, despite a lack of any public benchmark. To fill this gap, we present MortarBench, a loan origination agent benchmark. MortarBench uses a financial data synthesis and mutation pipeline to generate examples with broad edge case coverage that match real-world distributions and questions. We find that state-of-the-art large language models (LLMs) perform poorly, with closed-source models achieving at most 77.1\% exact match accuracy. We also discover systematic biases in LLM perception of foreignness related to non-English names. Noting these weaknesses, we introduce CRIT, a confidence calibration framework. Our method increases accuracy to 80.5\% while improving risk management steering and reducing bias.

AI.tldr: MortarBench provides a synthetic loan-origination benchmark to evaluate LLMs; findings show current LLMs underperform and exhibit biases, while CRIT improves calibration, accuracy, and fairness.
AI.motivation: Public, realistic benchmarks for loan origination agents were lacking; need broad edge-case coverage and distribution alignment to assess risk and fairness in automated lending.
AI.method: Construct MortarBench via financial data synthesis and mutation to generate diverse, realistic loan- Origination questions; evaluate SOTA LLMs (including closed-source) on exact-match accuracy; analyze non-English name biases; introduce CRIT, a confidence calibration framework; re-evaluate performance with CRIT.
AI.result: LLMs achieve at most 77.1% exact-match accuracy; CRIT raises accuracy to 80.5% and improves risk-management steering while reducing bias; biases related to perception of foreignness with non-English names were observed.
AI.conclusion: MortarBench fills a public benchmark gap for loan origination AI; CRIT provides a practical calibration approach to improve decision quality and mitigate bias in LLM-assisted lending.

title: A Differentiable Composite Approximation Framework for Autonomous Underwater Vehicle Maneuvering Modeling from Sea-Trial Data
summary: Field-based modeling from onboard measurements can produce autonomous underwater vehicle (AUV) maneuvering models that reflect real operating characteristics. From an approximation perspective, conventional maneuvering models use predefined constraint polynomial bases, whereas data-driven models use data-adaptive bases. Motivated by this basis-function view, this paper presents a differentiable composite-approximation formulation, in which the polynomial-basis component and the data-adaptive basis component are treated as differentiable parts of a single predictor and calibrated jointly. A gradient-based co-calibration method is developed for full-scale AUV maneuvering prediction, where a sensitivity-aware mechanism regulates bounded polynomial updates while the neural residual captures remaining nonlinear discrepancies under a shared prediction objective. To account for ocean-current effects in field data, a turning-motion-based current estimation and compensation procedure is incorporated to construct current-compensated learning targets for training and rollout. The framework is evaluated using sea-trial data collected from a 7-meter AUV under multiple maneuvering conditions. Results show that the proposed method improves recursive trajectory and velocity prediction compared with polynomial-only, neural-only, and frozen-prior hybrid baselines, demonstrating its applicability to field-data-based AUV maneuvering modeling.

AI.tldr: A differentiable composite-approximation model fuses polynomial bases with data-adaptive bases for AUV maneuvering, jointly calibrated via gradient-based updates, with current compensation; evaluated on sea-trial data showing improved trajectory and velocity predictions over polynomial-only, neural-only, and frozen-prior hybrids.
AI.motivation: Traditional AUV models rely on fixed polynomial bases or purely data-driven bases. A unified, differentiable framework that jointly tunes both components can better capture linear constraints, nonlinearities, and environmental effects (like ocean currents) in field data.
AI.method: Define a single predictor combining a polynomial-basis term and a data-adaptive (neural/residual) term. Use gradient-based co-calibration with a sensitivity-aware mechanism to bound polynomial updates; a neural residual handles remaining nonlinearity. Incorporate a current-estimation and compensation step based on turning motion to produce current-compensated training targets. Train and rollout on sea-trial data from a 7-meter AUV across multiple maneuvers.
AI.result: The proposed method improves recursive trajectory and velocity prediction compared with polynomial-only, neural-only, and frozen-prior hybrid baselines, demonstrating better fit to field data and applicability to real-world AUV maneuvering modeling.
AI.conclusion: A unified, differentiable composite-approximation framework that jointly calibrates linear (polynomial) and nonlinear (data-adaptive) components, with current compensation, provides robust, field-based AUV maneuvering models and superior predictive performance over traditional baselines.

title: 3D-PLOT-LLM: Part-Level Object Tokens for 3D Large Language Models
summary: 3D multimodal large language models (3D MLLMs) describe a 3D object as a whole but cannot address, name, or reason about its parts. Prior part-aware attempts add segmentation decoders, heavier 3D encoders, or bounding-box grammars at substantial parameter cost. We take a fundamentally different path: we reorganize the input token stream so that parts become directly addressable through the LLM's own vocabulary. Our model, 3D-PLOT-LLM, partitions the frozen point encoder's patches into K locally coherent regions and inserts, before each region's patch tokens, a learnable per-region marker and a reserved vocabulary token <part_k>; a Marker-Space Refinement (MSR) module then conditions each marker on its region's spatial statistics and adjacency neighbors. The model thus cites parts in its output and follows prompts that refer to parts by token, a capability absent from prior object-level 3D MLLMs. To probe this interface, we construct PartVerse-QA, a vocabulary-level part-QA benchmark adapted from PartVerse mesh annotations (77K training pairs and 588 held-out queries on disjoint object splits), on which 3D-PLOT-LLM reaches caption-to-slots Jaccard 0.459 and Exact-match 13.78%, with a slot-to-caption GPT-4o judge of 44.68. On the 3DCoMPaT-GrIn part-aware grounded description benchmark, 3D-PLOT-LLM outperforms PointLLM, Kestrel, PARIS3D, and SegPoint on every text-output metric, and ShapeLLM on 3 of 4, with up to +3.03 GPT-4o judge over PointLLM. On Objaverse whole-object captioning, adding PartVerse-QA at Stage 2 yields +0.65 SBERT and +1.85 GPT-4o over PointLLM, and tops PointLLM-PiSA on 4 of 5 traditional metrics (SBERT, SimCSE, BLEU-1, METEOR) despite targeting a different (part-grounded) objective. All with under 1M new trainable parameters on a frozen point encoder, an order of magnitude below prior part-aware 3D MLLMs, and no segmentation decoder or bounding-box head.

AI.tldr: 3D-PLOT-LLM enables part-level grounding in 3D MLLMs by inserting per-region markers and a reserved <part_k> token before region patch tokens, allowing the LLM vocabulary to address parts with minimal parameter growth.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of part-aware capabilities in 3D MLLMs without heavy decoders, segmentation heads, or bounding-box grammars; enable token-level part reference within a frozen 3D encoder framework.
AI.method: Freeze the point encoder, partition patches into K locally coherent regions, insert a learnable per-region marker and a <part_k> token before each region's patch tokens, and apply a Marker-Space Refinement (MSR) module to condition each marker on spatial statistics and regional adjacency.
AI.result: Achieves part-aware capabilities with under 1M new trainable parameters and no segmentation/bounding-box heads; outperforms baselines on PartVerse-QA, 3DCoMPaT-GrIn, and Objaverse captioning across multiple metrics, with GPT-4o judged gains up to +3.03 over PointLLM.
AI.conclusion: A lightweight, vocabulary-grounded approach to part-aware 3D MLLMs that delivers consistent improvements across diverse benchmarks while maintaining a small parameter footprint and avoiding heavy decoding heads.

title: A Comparative Study of Pretrained Transformer Models for Quranic ASR: Speech Representations, Label Formats, and Dataset Composition
comment: 30 pages, 9 figures, 5 tables, Submitted to International Journal of Speech Technology
summary: Quran Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) aims to convert Quranic recitation into text, enabling applications such as aided memorisation tools and Quranic search engines. However, existing ASR models often exhibit high Word Error Rates (WER) on user-recited verses and lack full coverage of the Quranic corpus. This paper presents a systematic empirical study of domain-specific fine-tuning of pretrained Transformer-based models for Quranic ASR, using advanced speech feature extraction methods: Wav2Vec2.0, HuBERT, and XLS-R. These models apply self-supervised learning by masking portions of input audio and using Transformer architectures to learn context-aware speech features. The pretrained models are fine-tuned on a filtered Quranic dataset exceeding 870 hours of professional and user recitations. Through comprehensive ablation studies across feature extractors, output label formats, training strategies, and clip durations, we identify the key factors that affect transcription accuracy in this domain. Our best-performing configuration achieves a WER of 0.08 on the EveryAyah subset and 0.11 on the combined EveryAyah+Tarteel setting, representing roughly a five-percentage-point gain over the Citrinet baseline (WER = 0.163) while reducing combined-model training time from 140 hours to 40 hours. Arabic text without diacritics yields the best fine-tuning results, and Wav2Vec2-XLSR-53 provides the strongest overall representation. Future work includes improving dataset quality and developing phoneme-aware models to extract deeper speech feature representations for Tajweed-sensitive applications.

AI.tldr: Fine-tuning pretrained transformer-based ASR models on a Quranic speech corpus yields strong WER gains, with Wav2Vec2-XLSR-53 performing best and non-diacritic Arabic text improving results; notable speedups over a Citrinet baseline.
AI.motivation: Improve accuracy and coverage of Quranic ASR for recitations, enabling applications like memorization aids and search, addressing high WER and limited corpus coverage in existing models.
AI.method: Fine-tune Wav2Vec2.0, HuBERT, and XLS-R on a filtered Quranic dataset (>870 hours of professional and user recitations). Conduct extensive ablations across feature extractors, output label formats, training strategies, and clip durations. Evaluate on EveryAyah and EveryAyah+Tarteel subsets; compare to Citrinet baseline; analyze effects of diacritics and representational choices.
AI.result: Achieves WER of 0.08 on EveryAyah and 0.11 on EveryAyah+Tarteel, about a 5-percentage-point improvement over Citrinet (WER=0.163); training time reduced from 140h to 40h; Arabic without diacritics yields best fine-tuning; Wav2Vec2-XLSR-53 offers strongest overall representation.
AI.conclusion: Domain-specific fine-tuning of self-supervised ASR models substantially improves Quranic transcription accuracy and efficiency. Future work should focus on dataset quality and developing phoneme-aware models to capture Tajweed-sensitive speech.

title: 3D-DLP: Self-Supervised 3D Object-Centric Scene Representation Learning
summary: We introduce 3D-DLP, a self-supervised object-centric representation learning model that decomposes scene-level RGB-D or voxel observations into a set of 3D latent particles. Building on the Deep Latent Particles (DLP) framework, each particle encodes disentangled attributes, including 3D keypoint position, bounding box dimensions, and appearance features, and represents a distinct entity in the scene. The model learns interpretable per-particle segmentation maps through an end-to-end self-supervised reconstruction objective. We demonstrate on both simulated and real-world datasets that the learned latent space is interpretable and controllable: by manipulating particle positions and decoding, we can generate novel scene configurations. Furthermore, we show that leveraging these compact 3D latent particles for downstream robotic manipulation improves performance over baselines that either lack explicit 3D information or rely on memory-intensive dense 3D inputs without object-centric structure. Code and videos are available at https://eubooks3003.github.io/3d-dlp.

AI.tldr: 3D-DLP is a self-supervised, object-centric 3D representation model that decomposes RGB-D/voxel scenes into a fixed set of 3D latent particles. Each particle encodes 3D position, size, and appearance, enabling interpretable per-particle segmentation and controllable scene generation. The approach improves downstream robotic manipulation by leveraging compact 3D latent factors, outperforming baselines that lack explicit 3D object structure or rely on dense 3D inputs.
AI.motivation: To obtain compact, interpretable, and controllable 3D scene representations suitable for robotics, addressing the limitations of dense 3D inputs and unstructured latent spaces in existing self-supervised 3D representation methods.
AI.method: Extend the Deep Latent Particles framework to 3D data by representing a scene with a fixed set of 3D latent particles. Each particle encodes disentangled attributes: 3D keypoint position, bounding box dimensions, and appearance features. The model learns interpretable per-particle segmentation maps via an end-to-end self-supervised reconstruction objective on RGB-D or voxel observations. The latent space supports manipulation (e.g., moving particle positions) to generate novel scene configurations. Trained and evaluated on simulated and real-world datasets; downstream tasks include robotic manipulation.
AI.result: Demonstrates that the learned latent space is interpretable and controllable, with per-particle segmentation aligning with scene entities. Manipulating particle positions and decoding yields novel scene configurations. In robotics, using compact 3D latent particles improves manipulation performance over baselines that lack explicit 3D information or rely on dense 3D inputs without object-centric structure. Code and videos are provided by the authors.
AI.conclusion: 3D-DLP offers a compact, interpretable, and controllable 3D object-centric representation for scenes, bridging 3D vision and self-supervised learning. It enables direct manipulation of scene components and yields tangible benefits for robotic manipulation, across simulated and real data.

title: Bidirectional Tutoring for Developmental Motor Learning in Robots: Co-Developed Interaction Dynamics Support Stable Learning
summary: Infants are well known to develop their motor skills through dense interaction with caregivers. Although such social interaction is crucial for human development, motor-skill learning in robots is often treated as a unidirectional process in which robots passively receive demonstrations from tutors. This overlooks a key property of social interaction: it is inherently bidirectional, with tutor and learner dynamically adapting to each other. In such interactions, the robot's past experiences may function as prior constraints that shape the dynamics of their co-developed trajectories. We hypothesize that bidirectional tutoring allows such constraints to guide the formation of consistent behavioral patterns that preserve behavioral coherence and support generalization, whereas unidirectional interaction lacks such constraints and leads to broader, less consistent behavioral patterns. To examine this hypothesis, we conducted two experiments with a physical humanoid robot performing an object manipulation task: one involving human-robot interaction and another employing an AI tutor interacting with the real robot through an adaptive intervention mechanism designed to examine whether similar effects would emerge under more controlled conditions. We implement the developmental learning framework using a free-energy-principle-based neural network extended with generative replay, which supports stable sequence-by-sequence learning from single tutored episodes. Across both settings, bidirectional tutoring fostered consistent behaviors and stage-wise generalization, while the robot gradually required less tutor guidance. These results suggest that bidirectional tutoring, as an embodied and socially grounded approach, provides an effective scaffold for developmental motor learning in robots.

AI.tldr: Bidirectional tutoring with embodied social interaction yields more coherent, generalizable motor learning in robots than unidirectional tutoring, implemented via a free-energy-principle neural network with generative replay across two experiments.
AI.motivation: Investigate whether bidirectional tutor–learner dynamics—where the robot’s prior experiences constrain learning—produce coherent, generalizable motor behaviors, addressing the common robotics assumption of unidirectional learning.
AI.method: Two experiments with a physical humanoid robot performing object manipulation. One used human–robot interaction; the other used an AI tutor with an adaptive intervention mechanism. The developmental framework is a free-energy-principle neural network extended with generative replay, enabling stable sequence-by-sequence learning from single tutored episodes. Measurements assessed behavioral consistency, stage-wise generalization, and tutor-dependence over time.
AI.result: Bidirectional tutoring fostered consistent behaviors and stage-wise generalization in both settings; the robot gradually required less tutor guidance.
AI.conclusion: Bidirectional tutoring provides an embodied, socially grounded scaffold for developmental motor learning in robots, promoting coherence and generalization beyond unidirectional tutoring.

title: Neural Events: Discrete Asynchronous Autoencoders for Event-Based Vision
summary: Event cameras capture dynamic scenes with exceptional temporal fidelity by representing them as a continuous stream of microsecond resolution \textit{events}. Each individual event, however, only carries minimal semantic value, merely signaling a localized brightness change. To derive meaningful signals, downstream algorithms need to quickly integrate cues from a potentially massive torrent of low-information events. Current architectures, however, are easily overwhelmed, struggling to balance capturing fine-grained temporal dynamics and maintaining a manageable data throughput. This paper proposes a framework to re-tokenize event streams into a small set of highly informative \textit{neural events}, each representing a local spatio-temporal context window with a discrete learnable code.
