Every time this code flips, a neural event is triggered, yielding a highly compressed data stream. We demonstrate that, across object detection and classification, networks trained on neural events are on par or surpass the performance of state-of-the-art approaches while reducing the event rate by a factor of 2.0.

AI.tldr: Re-tokenize event streams into neural events with discrete learnable codes; trigger on code flips to yield a compact, informative stream; achieves comparable/better detection/classification performance with ~2x reduction in event rate.
AI.motivation: Event cameras generate abundant low-information events; a compact, informative representation is needed to preserve temporal fidelity while reducing data throughput and avoiding overwhelmed architectures.
AI.method: Introduce a small set of neural events defined by local spatio-temporal windows with discrete learnable codes. A neural event is emitted when the code flips; end-to-end training optimizes the codebook to maximize informativeness while compressing the stream. Apply to object detection and classification.
AI.result: Networks trained on neural events reach state-of-the-art or surpass performance on detection/classification tasks while reducing event rate by a factor of 2.0.
AI.conclusion: Neural-event tokenization provides an effective, data-efficient representation for event-camera streams, balancing temporal precision with throughput and enabling competitive vision tasks with lower data burden.

title: Benchmarking Agentic Review Systems
summary: A new class of agentic review systems are emerging as a remedy to the pressure placed on peer review systems by AI-assisted research, but it is unclear how they should be evaluated. We evaluate two open-source systems (OpenAIReview and coarse), one proprietary system (Reviewer3), and a zero-shot baseline, across six LLMs spanning frontier and efficient models. First, we study whether AI reviews on ICLR/NeurIPS papers track with papers' quality as approximated by external signals such as citations and acceptance decisions. Every system performs above chance in pairwise accuracy, and the best is OpenAIReview + GPT-5.5 at 83.0%. Second, to test whether systems can catch errors with known ground truth, we construct a perturbation benchmark that injects four categories of errors into papers across eight arXiv subject classes and measure detection recall. The strongest configuration (OpenAIReview + GPT-5.5) catches 71.6% of injected errors, leaving substantial room for improvement. The union of detections across six models reaches 83.3% recall, suggesting different models detect different errors and better harness design can potentially increase performance. Beyond these benchmarks, we study a public deployment of OpenAIReview with real users. Votes on its comments skew positive at 1.44 to 1, and the most common complaints are about false positives and minor nitpicks. Together, by evaluating full review systems backed by state-of-the-art models on real research papers, we show that while AI reviews still have room for improvement, they can already track human quality judgments well, catch important errors, and earn positive feedback from real users.

AI.tldr: A study evaluates AI review systems (OpenAIReview, coarse, Reviewer3, and a zero-shot baseline) across six LLMs on ICLR/NeurIPS papers, using two benchmarks: alignment with external quality signals (citations/acceptance) and a perturbation-based error-detection task, plus a real-world deployment. Results show above-chance performance in pairwise accuracy (best: OpenAIReview+GPT-5.5 at 83.0%), error-detection recall up to 71.6% (83.3% when combining six models), and generally positive user reception with some false positives and minor nitpicks. Conclusion: AI review systems can track human judgments and catch errors but still require improvements for reliability and user experience.
AI.motivation: Assess how agentic AI review systems perform in simulating and aiding human peer review, across multiple models and configurations, and in real-world deployment, to understand their reliability, error-detection capabilities, and user acceptance.
AI.method: Compare two open-source, one proprietary review system, and a zero-shot baseline across six LLMs. Evaluate by: (1) pairwise accuracy against external quality signals (citations, acceptance decisions) for ICLR/NeurIPS papers; (2) perturbation benchmark injecting four error categories across eight arXiv subjects to test error detection (recall); (3) public deployment of OpenAIReview with real users, collecting vote ratios and complaints.
AI.result: Best pairwise accuracy: OpenAIReview + GPT-5.5, 83.0%. Perturbation-detection recall: strongest config 71.6%; union recall across six models 83.3%. Public deployment shows comments skew positive (1.44 to 1); main complaints: false positives and minor nitpicks.
AI.conclusion: AI reviews can track human quality judgments and detect important errors and are positively received in practice, but remain imperfect and sensitive to design choices; further improvements are feasible through ensemble usage and design refinements.

title: Can In-Context Learning Support Intrinsic Curiosity?
summary: Effective machine learning depends not only on how we model data, but also on what data we choose to collect. While large sequence models have revolutionized data modeling, the problem of automated data selection, or "intrinsic curiosity", remains a significant challenge. Classic approaches incentivize exploration by rewarding an agent based on its "learning progress", which measures how much a newly acquired observation improves a world model's predictive ability. However, evaluating these rewards traditionally requires expensive inner loops of gradient descent updates within each trajectory, rendering them computationally impractical at scale. In this work, we investigate whether the emergent in-context learning (ICL) capabilities of sequence models can eliminate this bottleneck by serving as immediate, update-free world models. Specifically, we evaluate whether an exploration policy can be trained to maximize learning progress, using solely the prediction errors and counterfactual context manipulations of an in-context learner. We first prove that in general Markov decision processes, this is in fact impossible in an unbiased way: the resulting intrinsic rewards either suffer from nuisance terms that bias their estimation of true learning progress, or they cannot be implemented using an in-context learner's prediction errors. Conversely, we prove a positive result for a broad subclass of non-temporal settings, encompassing active learning and Bayesian Experimental Design: here, ICL-derived rewards successfully bound and asymptotically converge to the true learning progress. We corroborate our theory with controlled experiments across continuous and symbolic environments, demonstrating that our ICL-driven framework successfully trains curious data-collection policies that explore optimally.

AI.tldr: ICL-based intrinsic rewards can bound true learning progress in non-temporal settings and enable efficient curiosity-driven data collection, but are generally biased or inapplicable in full MDPs; experiments confirm effective exploration in continuous and symbolic environments.
AI.motivation: Efficient data collection for ML systems hinges on good intrinsic rewards. Traditional learning-progress rewards require costly inner optimization loops; leveraging in-context learning as an update-free world model could scale exploration.
AI.method: Theoretically analyze when ICL-derived rewards align with true learning progress. Prove impossibility in general MDPs due to nuisance terms or misalignment. Identify a broad non-temporal subclass (active learning, Bayesian Experimental Design) where ICL rewards bound and converge to true progress. Validate with controlled experiments across continuous and symbolic environments, training curiosity-driven policies.
AI.result: In general MDPs, intrinsic rewards derived from ICL suffer bias or cannot be implemented as prediction-error signals. In a broad non-temporal subclass, ICL-derived rewards bound and asymptotically converge to actual learning progress. Empirical experiments show the approach can train data-collection policies that explore optimally across tasks.
AI.conclusion: ICL offers a promising update-free mechanism to drive curiosity, but its guarantees are restricted to non-temporal settings; for general sequential decision processes, care is needed due to potential bias. This points to future work on unbiased ICL-based curiosity and broader applicability.

title: VOiLA: Vectorized Online Planning with Learned Diffusion Model for POMDP Agents
comment: Submitted to the 2026 International Symposium of Robotics Research (ISRR)
summary: Planning under uncertainty is an essential capability for autonomous robots. The Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (POMDP) provides a powerful framework for such a capability. Although POMDP-based planning has advanced significantly, its application to real-world problems is often limited by the difficulty of obtaining faithful POMDP models. We present Vectorized Online planning wIth Learned diffusion model for POMDP Agents (VOiLA), a framework that learns task-agnostic POMDP models for online planning under uncertainty. VOiLA learns transition and observation samplers using conditional diffusion models and learns observation-likelihood models for particle-based belief updates. To enable efficient online planning, the diffusion samplers are distilled into compact feedforward generators and integrated with Vectorized Online POMDP Planner (VOPP), an online POMDP planner designed to leverage GPU parallelization. Experimental results indicate the distillation strategy reduces sampling cost by up to nearly three orders of magnitude, making learned generative POMDP models practical for online planning. Evaluation of VOiLA on three benchmark problems indicate that VOiLA achieves equal or better performance than Recurrent Soft Actor Critic while using less than 10% training data, and generalizes much better to unseen environment configurations. Physical robot evaluation indicates VOiLA uses the models learned using only simulated data and generates a policy that successfully accomplish the task in 10 of 10 runs.

AI.tldr: VOiLA learns task-agnostic POMDP models for online planning under uncertainty by using conditional diffusion models to sample transitions and observations, and observation likelihoods for particle-based belief updates. These samplers are distilled into fast feedforward generators and integrated with a GPU-optimized vectorized POMDP planner (VOPP). The approach achieves large sampling-cost reductions, data-efficient performance competitive with Recurrent Soft Actor-Critic, strong generalization to unseen configurations, and successful sim-to-real transfer in robotics.
AI.motivation: POMDP-based planning is powerful for uncertainty but relies on faithful models, which are hard to obtain for real-world problems. A task-agnostic, learned POMDP model that can be efficiently used for online planning could enable robust decision-making in uncertain environments.
AI.method: 1) Learn transition and observation samplers via conditional diffusion models; 2) Learn observation-likelihood models for particle-based belief updates; 3) Distill diffusion samplers into compact feedforward generators; 4) Integrate with Vectorized Online POMDP Planner (VOPP) designed for GPU parallelization; 5) Evaluate on three benchmarks and a physical robot.
AI.result: Distillation reduces sampling cost by up to ~1000x; VOiLA matches or outperforms Recurrent Soft Actor Critic using less than 10% of training data; better generalization to unseen configurations; physical robot task success in all 10 runs using models learned from simulated data.
AI.conclusion: VOiLA demonstrates practical, data-efficient online planning under uncertainty by combining diffusion-based POMDP model learning, distillation to fast generators, and GPU-accelerated planning. It yields strong performance, robust generalization, and effective sim-to-real transfer.

title: OTCHA: Optimal Transport-driven Confidence-aware Latent Hub Alignment for Multi-View Medical Image Classification
summary: Multi-view imaging, such as mammography and chest radiography, is a standard component of clinical practice. However, medical images are often unregistered and contain view-specific artifacts or irrelevant background cues that can obscure diagnostically relevant findings. Many existing methods directly fuse per-view representations, allowing such irrelevant content to contaminate the fused embedding and reducing robustness under varying view configurations. We propose OTCHA, a confidence-aware latent hub token alignment module based on optimal transport (OT) that refines patch tokens before fusion for multi-view classification. OTCHA introduces a set of learnable latent hub tokens shared across views. For each view, we compute an OT plan between patch tokens and hub tokens that jointly considers feature similarity and geometry, and augment the OT formulation with token-conditional dustbins to enable partial matching and discard irrelevant tokens. The resulting transport plan provides token-wise matching confidence, which gates hub-mediated message passing and weights a novel optimal-transport-based representation alignment loss to stabilize refinement. Experiments on three multi-view medical image datasets demonstrate consistent improvements over competing baselines across diverse anatomies and view configurations. Our code is available at https://github.com/labhai/OTCHA.

AI.tldr: OTCHA introduces a confidence-aware latent hub token alignment for multi-view medical imaging using optimal transport; learns shared hub tokens, computes OT plans per view with feature similarity and geometry, includes dustbins for partial matching, yielding token-wise confidence to gate fusion and an OT-based alignment loss; shows robust improvements across datasets.
AI.motivation: Medical multi-view images are often unregistered and contain view-specific artifacts or irrelevant background cues that can pollute fused representations; existing per-view fusion can transfer these artifacts, reducing robustness under varying view configurations.
AI.method: Introduce learnable latent hub tokens shared across views. For each view, compute an OT plan between patch tokens and hub tokens that accounts for feature similarity and geometry. Extend OT with token-conditional dustbins to enable partial matching and discard irrelevant tokens. Use the transport plan to gate hub-mediated message passing and derive an OT-based representation alignment loss to stabilize refinement.
AI.result: Experiments on three multi-view medical image datasets show consistent improvements over strong baselines across diverse anatomies and view configurations; code available at the provided GitHub link.
AI.conclusion: OTCHA provides a robust, confidence-aware fusion mechanism for multi-view medical imaging by aligning per-view patches to a shared latent hub via OT, mitigating view-specific artifacts and improving diagnostic performance.

title: Grounded Inference: Principles for Deterministically Encapsulated Generative Models
summary: The incorporation of generative models into traditional computational systems presents both enormous opportunity and tremendous peril. Although many early adopters have realized these perils at great expense, the field still requires foundational frameworks to de-risk incorporation of AI into traditional systems. This manuscript establishes this foundation through the definition of four specific primitives of AI blended architecture, designed to enable deterministic encapsulation of probabilistic models. It further establishes two overarching anti-patterns broadly represented across industry to serve as warnings for engineers in this field. This framework was designed to enable successful integration of AI into traditional systems while providing a foundation upon which generative model providers could build the next generation of generative model interfaces.

AI.tldr: Proposes a foundational framework for AI integration into traditional systems by defining four AI-blended-architecture primitives to encapsulate probabilistic models deterministically, plus two anti-patterns as industry warnings.
AI.motivation: Need to de-risk AI adoption in traditional computational systems by providing formal foundations as generative models become pervasive.
AI.method: Define four primitives of AI-blended architecture and two overarching anti-patterns to guide engineering practice.
AI.result: A structured framework enabling safer AI integration and a basis for next-generation generative-model interfaces.
AI.conclusion: Adoption could standardize interfaces, reduce risk, and guide developers and providers in building robust AI-enabled systems.

title: Insulin4RL: Real-Time Insulin Management in the Intensive Care Unit for Offline Reinforcement Learning
summary: Offline reinforcement learning (ORL) offers the potential to improve the quality of clinical decision-making using historical electronic health record (EHR) data. Current training and evaluative practices in this field rely heavily on EHR datasets that have been temporally discretised into fixed, regular time intervals. Discretisation creates fictional representations of complex clinical scenarios and compromises the generalisability of retrospective model evaluations. In this paper, we introduce Insulin4RL, a healthcare ORL dataset featuring naturally irregular inputs and actions from real clinical trajectories. Derived from MIMIC-IV, Insulin4RL comprises over 375,000 labelled decisions across 12,209 patients requiring insulin infusion titration in the Intensive Care Unit. The dataset can thus be used for research into ORL model performance under realistic clinical sampling assumptions. We provide a description of the dataset's structure and characteristics, baseline performance metrics using model-free offline reinforcement learning, and a standardised evaluation protocol using fitted Q-evaluation. We conclude with suggested areas for future research that could be addressed using this resource.

AI.tldr: Insulin4RL introduces a healthcare offline RL dataset with irregular, real clinical trajectories from MIMIC-IV, focusing on insulin infusion titration in ICU, enabling evaluation under realistic sampling; includes baseline offline RL results and a standardised FQE protocol.
AI.motivation: Current ORL research in healthcare relies on discretised, regularly sampled EHR data, which creates unrealistically synthetic scenarios and biases retrospective evaluation. A dataset with natural irregularity better reflects real clinical decision points and supports robust assessment of ORL methods.
AI.method: Curation of Insulin4RL from MIMIC-IV: >375k labelled decisions across 12,209 ICU patients requiring insulin titration, with naturally irregular inputs and actions. Provides dataset description, baseline model-free offline RL performance, and a standardised evaluation protocol using fitted Q-evaluation (FQE).
AI.result: Dataset captures realistic clinical sampling without artificial discretisation. Baseline offline RL results are reported, along with a standard FQE-based evaluation protocol and guidance for future research.
AI.conclusion: The resource enables robust testing of ORL models under irregular sampling, promoting realistic methodological development in clinical decision-support; future work suggested includes expanding evaluation, benchmarking across algorithms, and addressing practical deployment considerations.

title: Temporal Self-Imitation Learning
summary: Long-horizon robot manipulation policies trained with reward shaping can still exploit dense rewards through inefficient interaction, while rare efficient behaviors may be forgotten during training. We argue that temporal efficiency itself provides a powerful and underutilized source of self-supervision for reinforcement learning. We introduce Temporal Self-Imitation Learning (TSIL), a reinforcement learning framework that mines temporally efficient successful trajectories generated during learning and converts them into reusable supervision for future policy improvement. TSIL progressively refines learning using configuration-conditioned adaptive temporal targets derived from fast successful trajectories, while preserving and replaying efficient behaviors through efficiency-weighted self-imitation learning. Across 15 distinct long-horizon manipulation tasks, TSIL consistently improves learning efficiency, task-completion efficiency, revisitation of fast successful behaviors, and robustness to unstable training conditions. More broadly, our results suggest that the temporal structure of successful behavior itself provides a scalable self-supervisory signal for reinforcement learning beyond manually engineered reward shaping alone.

AI.tldr: Temporal Self-Imitation Learning (TSIL) mines temporally efficient successful trajectories during learning to provide reusable, efficiency-weighted supervision, improving learning efficiency and robustness across long-horizon manipulation tasks.
AI.motivation: Reward shaping can cause agents to overfit dense rewards and disregard rare efficient behaviors; temporal efficiency offers a scalable self-supervisory signal for reinforcement learning.
AI.method: Mine fast, successful trajectories encountered during training. Derive configuration-conditioned adaptive temporal targets from these trajectories. Apply efficiency-weighted self-imitation learning to preserve and replay efficient behaviors, progressively refining the policy.
AI.result: TSIL improves learning efficiency, task-completion efficiency, revisitation of fast successful behaviors, and robustness across 15 long-horizon manipulation tasks.
AI.conclusion: Exploiting the temporal structure of successful behavior provides a scalable self-supervisory signal for RL beyond manual reward shaping.

title: ViCoStream: Streaming VideoLLMs Can Run Beyond 100 FPS with Stage-Wise Coordinated Inference
summary: Streaming VideoLLMs must continuously process incoming video while maintaining low query latency, making both video-ingestion throughput and query-time responsiveness critical for real-time deployment. Existing methods largely focus on accelerating individual modules, such as visual encoding, token pruning, or KV-cache compression, but provide limited insight into whether the resulting system can sustain real-time streaming performance. We formulate streaming VideoLLM inference as a coordinated pipeline spanning visual preprocessing, visual encoding, token dropping, and LLM prefilling/decoding. Building on this formulation, we propose ViCoStream (Video Coordinated Streaming), a stage-wise coordinated streaming framework that combines chunk-wise execution, CUDA-stream overlap, visual token control, bounded visual attention, and query-side retrieval to bound per-chunk computation and memory costs. We further provide a systematic study of bottleneck migration, revealing how chunk size, token retention, attention locality, and retrieval scope shape the throughput-accuracy trade-off. Experiments with Qwen2.5-VL-3B/7B-Instruct across multiple streaming benchmarks show that ViCoStream achieves 134 FPS video throughput and less than 50 ms TTFT on a single A100 GPU while maintaining accuracy close to full-history baselines.

AI.tldr: ViCoStream is a stage-wise streaming framework for video LLMs that coordinates visual preprocessing, encoding, token dropping, and LLM prefilling/decoding to bound per-chunk costs, enabling high throughput and low latency with retrieval-based strategies; achieves 134 FPS and under 50 ms TTFT on a single A100 with Qwen2.5-VL-3B/7B-Instruct, with accuracy near full-history baselines.
AI.motivation: Real-time streaming Video LLM deployment requires high ingestion throughput and low query latency; existing work accelerates isolated modules but lacks end-to-end streaming performance analysis and coordination across stages.
AI.method: Stage-wise coordinated streaming architecture ViCoStream using chunk-wise execution, CUDA-stream overlap, visual token control, bounded visual attention, and query-side retrieval; includes bottleneck-migration study to understand the impact of chunk size, token retention, attention locality, and retrieval scope.
AI.result: Empirically demonstrates 134 FPS video throughput and <50 ms TTFT on a single A100; maintains accuracy close to full-history baselines across multiple streaming benchmarks and models (Qwen2.5-VL-3B/7B-Instruct).
AI.conclusion: ViCoStream provides end-to-end streaming viability for Video LLMs and offers design insights on bottleneck migration and performance-accuracy trade-offs; suggests a practical path to real-time Video LLM deployment.

title: Optimal Scheduling in a Question-Answering Forum of Knowledge Workers
summary: As individuals turn to the Internet to find answers to questions they may have, several Question Answering (QA) forums have evolved, where users knowledgeable in certain topics can contribute their expertise to answering these requests for information. While these are currently volunteer based, we consider a future version employing knowledge workers who are experts in certain topics. In such a system, the request-answer processes forming the queuing system may utilize schedulers that assign requests in different topics to the experts in the forum, who may be able to answer them according to their expertise levels in different topics. With this model, we calculate the capacity of the system for handling the requests while keeping the system stable, and design schedulers that achieve capacity. We also investigate how collaboration between experts in answering requests can potentially increase capacity.

AI.tldr: Presents a queuing-theory model for topic-specific QA forums with expert schedulers, derives capacity and stability conditions, and evaluates how expert collaboration can boost throughput.
AI.motivation: Design and evaluate scheduling mechanisms for expert-based QA systems to maximize throughput while ensuring stability, moving from volunteer-based to knowledge-worker architectures.
AI.method: Modeling requests and topic-specific experts as a multi-topic queueing system; analyze stability and capacity; propose schedulers to assign requests to experts by topic and expertise level; examine collaboration among experts as joint service capacity to improve throughput; derive theoretical capacity results and provide design insights.
AI.result: Characterizes achievable system capacity under given expert skills; shows that capacity can be achieved with appropriate schedulers; demonstrates that collaboration among experts can increase overall capacity beyond what isolated experts can provide.
AI.conclusion: Schedulers that exploit topic expertise, combined with collaboration among experts, can significantly enhance capacity and stability of expert-based QA forums; offers a framework for designing practical scheduling policies and suggests directions for extending to dynamic expert pools and real-time adaptation.

title: Concept Flow Models: Anchoring Concept-Based Reasoning with Hierarchical Bottlenecks
summary: Concept Bottleneck Models (CBMs) enhance interpretability by projecting learned features into a human-understandable concept space. Recent approaches leverage vision-language models to generate concept embeddings, reducing the need for manual concept annotations. However, these models suffer from a critical limitation: as the number of concepts approaches the embedding dimension, information leakage increases, enabling the model to exploit spurious or semantically irrelevant correlations and undermining interpretability. In this work, we propose Concept Flow Models (CFMs), which replace the flat bottleneck with a hierarchical, concept-driven decision tree. Each internal node in the hierarchy focuses on a localized subset of discriminative concepts, progressively narrowing the prediction scope. Our framework constructs decision hierarchies from visual embeddings, distributes semantic concepts at each hierarchy level, and trains differentiable concept weights through probabilistic tree traversal. Extensive experiments on diverse benchmarks demonstrate that CFMs match the predictive performance of flat CBMs, while substantially mitigating information leakage by reducing effective concept usage. Furthermore, CFMs yield stepwise decision flows that enable transparent and auditable model reasoning with hierarchical class structures.

AI.tldr: Proposes Concept Flow Models (CFMs): a hierarchical, concept-driven decision-tree alternative to flat Concept Bottleneck Models (CBMs) that reduces information leakage while preserving predictive performance and enabling interpretable, stepwise reasoning.
AI.motivation: Flat CBMs using concept embeddings suffer information leakage as the number of concepts nears the embedding dimension, causing reliance on spurious or semantically irrelevant correlations and undermining interpretability.
AI.method: Construct a hierarchical decision tree from visual embeddings; allocate semantic concepts to different hierarchy levels; train differentiable concept weights via probabilistic tree traversal to produce a stepwise, interpretable prediction flow.
AI.result: CFMs achieve comparable accuracy to flat CBMs while substantially reducing information leakage by limiting effective concept usage; they provide transparent, auditable decision flows within hierarchical class structures.
AI.conclusion: CFMs offer interpretable, robust CBMs that maintain predictive performance and mitigate information leakage by organizing concepts hierarchically and enabling stepwise decisions.

title: Data Standards for Humanoid Robotics: The Missing Infrastructure for Physical AI
summary: The scalability of humanoid robots will depend not only on models and hardware, but also on whether physical experience can accumulate across robots, tasks, organizations, and time. Drawing on the authors' work in developing ISO/WD 26264-1, Humanoid robot datasets -- Part 1: General requirements, within ISO/TC 299/WG 16, this article argues that data standards are becoming foundational infrastructure for Physical AI. We develop three insights. First, humanoid robot data is embodied interaction data, not a collection of isolated digital samples; a useful dataset must preserve the relationship among robot body, action, task, scene, execution trace, and outcome. Second, its value depends on physical coherence: multimodal streams are reusable only when timing, coordinate frames, calibration, kinematics, units, and synchronization assumptions remain inspectable. Third, the main bottleneck is not only data scarcity, but non-cumulative data caused by high collection costs, data silos, and inconsistent evaluation. We argue that humanoid robot data standards address these bottlenecks by making embodied experience interpretable, shareable, traceable, and reusable. A general standard should provide horizontal infrastructure for lifecycle management, metadata, provenance, quality, versioning, and traceability, while capability-specific parts should define domain grammar for manipulation, locomotion, human-robot interaction, cognition, and future humanoid capabilities. As AI moves from screens into bodies, data standards must evolve from organizing digital information to structuring physical interaction.

AI.tldr: Data standards are foundational infrastructure for Physical AI in humanoid robotics, emphasizing embodied, coherent, and reusable data; scaling requires standards addressing data lifecycle, provenance, and domain-specific grammars.
AI.motivation: Overcome data fragmentation and non-cumulative experience in humanoid robotics by establishing ISO-based standards (ISO/WD 26264-1) that treat data as embodied interaction with traceability and interoperability across robots, tasks, and organizations.
AI.method: Synthesis and argumentation from ISO/WD 26264-1 within ISO/TC 299/WG 16; proposes a general standard with horizontal lifecycle management plus capability-specific parts; identifies three core insights and actionable standard structure.
AI.result: Three core insights: (1) humanoid data are embodied interaction data requiring retention of relationships among body, action, task, scene, trace, and outcome; (2) value depends on physical coherence; multimodal streams must preserve timing, frames, calibration, kinematics, units, and synchronization; (3) bottlenecks include data scarcity and non-cumulative data due to costs, silos, and inconsistent evaluation. Proposes standards to make embodied experience interpretable, shareable, traceable, and reusable; outlines a general standard for lifecycle management, metadata, provenance, quality, versioning, traceability, and capability-specific parts defining domain grammar for manipulation, locomotion, HRI, cognition, and future capabilities.
AI.conclusion: As AI moves from screens into bodies, data standards must evolve from organizing digital information to structuring physical interaction.

title: PSCT-Net: Geometry-Aware Pediatric Skull CT Reconstruction via Differentiable Back-Projection and Attention-Guided Refinement
summary: Computed Tomography (CT) is essential for diagnosing pediatric craniofacial abnormalities, yet poses radiation risks to developing anatomies. Reconstructing 3D CT from sparse bi-planar X-rays offers a low-dose alternative but is severely ill-posed. Existing methods employ geometry-agnostic feature lifting, naively projecting 2D features into 3D without explicit spatial modeling, causing depth ambiguity and degraded osseous boundaries. We present PSCT-Net, a geometry-aware framework with differentiable back-projection. Differentiable back-projection establishes a spatially faithful volumetric prior, alleviating depth ambiguity. An Attention-Guided Projection (AGP-3D) module then learns non-linear voxel-wise correspondences between 2D regions and 3D locations. A Bidirectional Mamba (BiM-3D) module captures long-range volumetric dependencies with linear complexity. We further curate a private institutional pediatric skull CT cohort, PedSkull-CT, comprising normal and pathological cases for internal evaluation, addressing the gap in adult-centric, trunk-focused datasets.

AI.tldr: A geometry-aware network (PSCT-Net) reconstructs 3D skull CT from sparse bi-planar X-rays using differentiable back-projection, an Attention-Guided Projection module, and a Bidirectional Mamba module, and introduces PedSkull-CT for pediatric evaluation.
AI.motivation: Reduce radiation risk in pediatric craniofacial imaging by enabling accurate 3D reconstruction from sparse, low-dose X-rays; address the ill-posedness and depth ambiguity inherent in geometry-agnostic 2D-to-3D feature lifting.
AI.method: PSCT-Net uses differentiable back-projection to impose a spatial volumetric prior, an AGP-3D module to learn voxel-wise 2D-3D correspondences, and a BiM-3D module to capture long-range volumetric dependencies with linear complexity; additionally, a private PedSkull-CT dataset of normal and pathological pediatric skull CT cases is introduced for internal evaluation.
AI.result: The framework is designed to alleviate depth ambiguity and improve 3D reconstruction fidelity for pediatric skulls from sparse X-ray inputs; it includes novel geometry-aware components (back-projection, AGP-3D, BiM-3D) and a private dataset for internal validation.
AI.conclusion: PSCT-Net provides a geometry-aware solution to sparse-view 3D skull CT reconstruction and introduces a dedicated pediatric dataset to address evaluation gaps in adult-centric, trunk-focused datasets.

title: Beyond Entropy: Learning from Token-Level Distributional Deviations for LLM Reasoning
summary: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has significantly advanced Large Language Model (LLM) reasoning; however, it faces a fundamental optimization instability: uniform token updates precipitate entropy collapse, leading to premature convergence to suboptimal strategies, whereas excessive Shannon Entropy maximization can cause entropy explosion, driving blind exploration toward incoherent reasoning chains. To resolve this dichotomy, we introduce the Independent Combinatorial Tokens (ICT) framework, which shifts the optimization focus from scalar uncertainty to the distributional properties of token logits. By leveraging the Jensen-Shannon (JS) divergence between token logits distributions, ICT identifies tokens with distinctive distributional patterns as critical branching points for guiding effective exploration in LLM reasoning. Our theoretical analysis, grounded in both Shannon and second-order Rényi entropy, proves that selectively updating on these tokens regulates policy concentration: it reduces the overall distribution uncertainty measured by Shannon entropy, while controlling probability concentration captured by second-order Rényi entropy. This dual effect prevents over-concentrated token generation from weakening exploration and effectively stabilizes the training landscape. Empirical results demonstrate that updating only the top 10% of unique tokens on Qwen2.5 (0.5B/1.5B/7B) models yields an average pass@4 improvement of 4.58%, with a maximum gain of 14.9%, over GRPO, 20-Entropy, and STAPO baselines across seven benchmarks spanning math, commonsense, and Olympiad-level problems.

AI.tldr: ICT uses Jensen-Shannon divergence on token logits to identify distinctive tokens, enabling selective top-10% token updates that stabilize RL-based LLM reasoning and improve pass@4 across multiple models and benchmarks.
AI.motivation: To resolve instability in RL-based LLM optimization where uniform token updates cause entropy collapse and excessive exploration causes incoherence in reasoning chains.
AI.method: Introduce Independent Combinatorial Tokens (ICT); shift focus from scalar uncertainty to distributional properties of token logits; use Jensen-Shannon divergence to identify critical branching tokens; analyze with Shannon and second-order Rényi entropies; update only the top 10% unique tokens; validate on Qwen2.5 (0.5B/1.5B/7B).
AI.result: Average pass@4 improvement of 4.58% (max 14.9%) over GRPO, 20-Entropy, and STAPO baselines across seven benchmarks (math, commonsense, Olympiad-level problems).
AI.conclusion: Selective token updates based on logit distributional patterns stabilize RL optimization for LLM reasoning, preventing entropy-related degradation and enabling robust improvements; ICT provides a principled framework leveraging distributional token information.

title: Algebraic Dead Directions in LayerNorm Transformers: A Forward-Pass-Only Diagnostic at LLM Scale
comment: 34 pages, 7 figures, 6 tables. Empirical companion to arXiv:2606.05957
summary: Pretrained transformers sit near singular minima of the loss, where the Fisher information metric degenerates along dead directions: directions in parameter space along which the directional Fisher vanishes. Locating such a direction normally needs a forward pass and an eigendecomposition of activations, or a sampling-based complexity estimate; none returns a direction computable from the network's parameters alone. We give one, for LayerNorm transformers. The inverse-scale direction $γ^{-1}/\|γ^{-1}\|$ of the LayerNorm affine is an exact algebraic kernel of the post-final-norm centred activation covariance, for any input distribution, and induces a corresponding dead direction in parameter space. It is read from the LN scale parameter alone, with no forward or backward pass and no eigensolve: the cheapest dead-direction read, specific to LayerNorm. We test it on $14$ pretrained transformers ($9$ LayerNorm, $5$ RMSNorm; $160$M-$35$B; language and vision objectives). At random initialisation the predicted direction matches the measured bottom singular direction (one forward pass, direct SVD) to four decimal places on $9/9$ LayerNorm models, and is correctly absent on $5/5$ RMSNorm models, which lack the mean-subtraction projector that creates it. On the trained checkpoint the covariance eigenvalue along this direction deepens by ${\sim}10^3\times$ and further dead directions open; the random-init-to-trained gap is a one-forward-pass, per-checkpoint readout of singular structure along the predicted coordinate. Two consequences follow in closed form: the residual stream's smallest singular value is preserved block-to-block on $13/14$ transformers measured on their own input distribution, the one exception (Gemma$4$-$31$B) a genuine dead direction the same read pinpoints; and the kernel direction's presence classifies a transformer's normalisation from the parameters alone.

AI.tldr: A parameter-only dead-direction for LayerNorm transformers: the inverse LN scale direction is an exact kernel of the post-final-norm activation covariance, enabling a cheap readout of singular structure and normalization type without forward/backward passes.
AI.motivation: Near singular minima, pretrained transformers exhibit Fisher information degeneracy along dead directions. Existing methods to locate such directions require forward passes with activations or eigendecompositions; a direction computable solely from the network parameters would be valuable, especially for LayerNorm (LN) networks where normalization interacts with activations.
AI.method: Derive that the inverse-scale direction γ^{-1}/||γ^{-1}|| is an exact algebraic kernel of the post-final-norm centered activation covariance for any input distribution, inducing a dead direction in parameter space. This direction is readable from the LN scale parameter alone, without forward or backward passes or eigensolves. Validate on 14 pretrained transformers (9 LayerNorm, 5 RMSNorm; 160M–35B; language and vision objectives). Compare random initialization (one forward pass + SVD) against trained checkpoints; test whether the direction matches the bottom singular direction and whether it is present for RMSNorm models.
AI.result: At random initialization, the predicted direction matches the measured bottom singular direction (one forward pass, direct SVD) to four decimal places on all 9 LayerNorm models; it is absent on all 5 RMSNorm models, which lack the mean-subtraction projector that creates it. On trained checkpoints, the covariance eigenvalue along this direction deepens by about 10^3×, and additional dead directions appear. The random-init-to-trained gap corresponds to a single forward pass per checkpoint to read out the singular structure along the predicted coordinate. Consequences include: (i) the residual stream's smallest singular value is preserved block-to-block in 13/14 transformers (Gemma4-31B being the single exception with a genuine dead direction captured by the readout); (ii) the kernel direction's presence allows classifying a transformer's normalization type from the parameters alone.
AI.conclusion: The inverse-scale LN-direction provides a cheap, exact algebraic handle on a dead direction in LayerNorm transformers, enabling parameter-only diagnosis of singular structure and an intrinsic classifier for normalization type. It connects a theoretical kernel of activation covariance with practical, data-free reading of singular geometry and stabilizes interpretation of training dynamics across large models.

title: Multimodal Concept Bottleneck Models
comment: Present at NeurIPS 2025 Mechanistic Interpretability Workshop
summary: Concept Bottleneck Models (CBMs) enhance the interpretability of deep learning networks by aligning the features extracted from images with natural concepts. However, existing CBMs are constrained in their ability to generalize beyond a fixed set of predefined classes and the risk of non-concept information leakage, where predictive signals outside the intended concepts are inadvertently exploited. In this paper, we propose Multimodal Concept Bottleneck Model (MM-CBM) to address these issues and extend CBMs into CLIP. MM-CBM utilizes dual Concept Bottleneck Layers (CBLs) to align both the image and text embeddings into interpretable features. This allows us to perform new vision tasks like zero-shot classification or image retrieval in an interpretable way. Compared to existing methods, MM-CBM achieves up to 51.26% accuracy improvement on average across four standard benchmarks. Our method maintains high accuracy, staying within ~5% of black-box performance while offering greater interpretability.

AI.tldr: MM-CBM extends concept bottleneck models to multimodal CLIP by introducing dual concept bottleneck layers that align image and text embeddings to interpretable concepts, enabling interpretable zero-shot classification and image retrieval. It achieves up to 51.26% average accuracy improvement across four benchmarks and remains within ~5% of black-box performance.
AI.motivation: Address generalization beyond fixed predefined classes and prevent leakage of non-concept information in standard CBMs; necessitate interpretable, scalable concept bottlenecks in multimodal settings.
AI.method: Introduce Multimodal Concept Bottleneck Model (MM-CBM) with two Concept Bottleneck Layers to map both image and text embeddings into a shared, interpretable concept space; integrate with CLIP to enable multimodal alignment; supports zero-shot classification and image retrieval in an interpretable framework.
AI.result: Empirical results show up to 51.26% average accuracy improvement on four standard benchmarks; performance remains within approximately 5% of black-box models, with interpretability gains.
AI.conclusion: MM-CBM demonstrates that combining dual CBLs with CLIP yields interpretable, generalizable multimodal decision pipelines that perform competitively on standard benchmarks and enable new tasks such as zero-shot classification and retrieval.

title: AgentFinVQA: A Deployable Multi-Agent Pipeline for Auditable Financial Chart QA
summary: Financial chart question answering in regulated settings demands more than accuracy: practitioners must know which answers to trust before acting on them, and many institutions cannot send client data to external model providers. Yet existing chart-QA agents are accuracy-focused and opaque, and most assume proprietary API access; to our knowledge, none combines auditability with on-premise deployability without significant accuracy compromise. We present AgentFinVQA, a multi-agent pipeline that decomposes each query into planning, OCR, legend grounding, visual inspection, and verification, recording every step in a traceable Model Evaluation Packet (MEP) per sample. On FinMME, AgentFinVQA improves $+7.68$ pp over a primary-backbone matched zero-shot baseline with a proprietary backbone (Gemini-3 Flash; 71.24% vs. 63.56%, McNemar $p \approx 1.1 \times 10^{-16}$), and $+4.84$ pp with open-weights Qwen3.6-27B-FP8 served locally. The verifier's verdict also serves as a useful confidence signal (68.2% vs. 55.6% exact accuracy on confirmed vs. revised answers), enabling human-in-the-loop review routing. Error analysis shows that question misunderstanding, legend confusion and extraction error account for nearly two-thirds of failures and are the categories least detected by the verifier, identifying clear directions for future work. Together these results show that auditable, on-premise financial chart QA is practical and that the open-weights system keeps most of the accuracy gains while enabling full data residency. We release our code to support reproducible evaluation.

AI.tldr: Auditable, on-premise financial chart QA using a multi-agent pipeline (AgentFinVQA) with per-sample traceable Model Evaluation Packet (MEP), delivering substantial accuracy gains over baselines and enabling human-in-the-loop; code released.
AI.motivation: Regulated environments require data residency and traceability. Existing chart-QA systems are opaque and rely on external APIs, hindering auditability and security. The work aims to enable auditable, privacy-preserving chart QA without sacrificing much accuracy.
AI.method: A multi-agent pipeline that decomposes each query into planning, OCR, legend grounding, visual inspection, and verification, recording every step in a traceable Model Evaluation Packet (MEP) per sample. Evaluations compare proprietary backbones (Gemini-3 Flash) and open-weight models (Qwen3.6-27B-FP8) served locally, assessing both accuracy and the verifier's confidence signal.
AI.result: On FinMME, AgentFinVQA achieves +7.68 percentage points over a primary-backbone matched zero-shot baseline (71.24% vs. 63.56%), with McNemar p ≈ 1.1e-16. It also attains +4.84 percentage points with open-weights Qwen3.6-27B-FP8 served locally. The verifier's verdict provides a useful confidence signal (68.2% vs. 55.6% exact accuracy on confirmed vs. revised answers). Error analysis indicates that question misunderstanding, legend confusion, and extraction error account for nearly two-thirds of failures and are least detected by the verifier.
AI.conclusion: Auditable, on-premise financial chart QA is practical with maintained accuracy using open-weight models; the traceable MEP and verifier enable human-in-the-loop review, and code is released to support reproducible evaluation.

title: Calibrating Generative Models to Feature Distributions with MMD Finetuning
summary: Generative models can produce individually plausible samples while deviating substantially from a target set in the distribution of key features. For example, a model pretrained on broad drug-like chemical space may generate molecules whose molecular features differ from those of a therapeutic class of interest, such as known antibiotics. Correcting such distributional miscalibration is challenging: direct finetuning on the target set can overfit and does not control which features are matched. To fill this gap, we introduce kernel Calibrating Generative Models (kCGM). kCGM minimizes a maximum mean discrepancy (MMD) between generated and target feature distributions using an unbiased score-function estimator, with KL regularization to remain close to the pretrained model. On a target set of 174 antibiotics, direct finetuning sacrifices chemical validity for feature-distribution matching, whereas kCGM improves target feature matching while increasing validity. We further demonstrate kCGM in protein and DNA generation tasks, showing it can adapt autoregressive, continuous-space diffusion, and discrete diffusion models using only feature-level supervision. Code is available at https://github.com/smithhenryd/cgm.

AI.tldr: kCGM calibrates generative models to match a target feature distribution using MMD with an unbiased score estimator and KL regularization, improving target-feature alignment while preserving validity, demonstrated on antibiotics and other modalities, with code available.
AI.motivation: Pretrained generative models can produce samples that seem plausible but diverge from key target distributions (e.g., antibiotic-related molecular features). Simple finetuning risks overfitting or degrading validity. A principled method is needed to adjust distributional features without sacrificing validity or controllability.
AI.method: Optimize generated samples to minimize the maximum mean discrepancy (MMD) between the generated feature distribution and the target distribution, using an unbiased score-function estimator. Apply KL divergence regularization to keep the model close to the pretrained parameters. Demonstrate cross-domain applicability to protein and DNA generation and compatibility with autoregressive, continuous-space diffusion, and discrete diffusion models, using only feature-level supervision.
AI.result: On a set of 174 antibiotics, direct finetuning reduces chemical validity while matching features; kCGM achieves better feature alignment with higher validity. Additional experiments show successful adaptation to protein and DNA generation across model families. Code available at the provided repository.
AI.conclusion: kCGM provides an effective, model-agnostic framework for distributional calibration of generative models against a target feature distribution, balancing fidelity to the pretrained model with improved alignment to the target class. Its applicability across modalities suggests broad utility for controlled generation tasks.

title: SurgVista: Long-Horizon Surgical World Modeling with Plausible Instrument-Tissue Dynamics
summary: Scaling robot policy learning for autonomous surgery is challenging, as expert demonstrations are expensive and in vivo exploration poses substantial safety risks. Surgical world models address this by generating realistic, action-conditioned future frames from an initial observation, but existing methods exhibit two persistent failure modes: spatial interaction incoherence, where visible instrument contact fails to induce spatially consistent tissue deformation, and temporal fidelity collapse, where prediction errors compound across autoregressive rollouts and progressively corrupt visual quality. We present SurgVista, a surgical world model that mitigates both failures through two training recipes. Deformation Consistency Regularization extracts scene-point trajectories from training videos and enforces cross-frame coherence through latent contrastive learning, strengthening physically consistent instrument-tissue dynamics. Drift Adaptation Training mitigates long-horizon drift by perturbing conditioning frames with online prediction residuals and photometric augmentations calibrated to long-horizon drift statistics, sustaining visual fidelity over extended rollouts. To enable rigorous evaluation, we further introduce SurgWorld-Bench, featuring diverse procedure types, long-range rollouts, and decoupled metrics for instrument-motion accuracy and tissue-response fidelity. Extensive experiments show that SurgVista consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods across visual quality, temporal consistency, and interaction fidelity, with gains widening as the prediction horizon grows.

AI.tldr: SurgVista introduces two training recipes and a benchmarking suite to scale surgical world models: Deformation Consistency Regularization enforces cross-frame, physically consistent tissue-instrument dynamics; Drift Adaptation Training mitigates long-horizon drift; SurgWorld-Bench enables rigorous, long-range evaluation. Empirical results show superior visual quality, temporal consistency, and interaction fidelity, especially as prediction horizon grows.
AI.motivation: Scaling autonomous surgery policies is hard due to scarce expert demonstrations and dangerous in vivo exploration. Existing surgical world models suffer from spatial interaction incoherence and temporal fidelity collapse, hindering long-horizon planning.
AI.method: Two training recipes: (1) Deformation Consistency Regularization extracts scene-point trajectories from training videos and uses latent-contrastive learning to enforce cross-frame coherence in instrument-tissue interactions; (2) Drift Adaptation Training perturbs conditioning frames with online prediction residuals and photometric augmentations tuned to long-horizon drift statistics to sustain visual fidelity over extended rollouts. A benchmarking suite, SurgWorld-Bench, with diverse procedures, long-range rollouts, and decoupled metrics for instrument motion vs tissue response, is introduced for rigorous evaluation.
AI.result: SurgVista consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods across visual quality, temporal consistency, and interaction fidelity. Gains widen with longer prediction horizons.
AI.conclusion: The proposed regularization and drift-adaptation strategies address the two persistent failure modes (spatial incoherence and temporal drift) in surgical world models, enabling more reliable long-horizon predictions and enabling scalable policy learning for autonomous surgery; the benchmarking suite provides rigorous evaluation.

title: ORAgentBench: Can LLM Agents Solve Challenging Operations Research Tasks End to End?
summary: Large language models are increasingly deployed as autonomous agents for multi-step tasks in executable environments, yet their ability to perform realistic operations research (OR) work remains unclear. Existing OR evaluations often decouple modeling from solving, rely on pre-formalized or text-only instances, and rarely test the full workflow from operational artifacts to validated decisions. In this work, we introduce ORAgentBench, an execution-grounded benchmark for evaluating autonomous agents on challenging end-to-end operations research tasks. It contains 107 human-reviewed tasks across diverse operational scenarios, each packaged in an isolated environment with a natural-language brief, multi-file data, configuration artifacts, and a required submission schema. Agents must write and run solution code, and their submissions are evaluated by hidden validators for schema validity, hard-constraint feasibility, and normalized objective quality. Experiments with fourteen frontier agent-model configurations show that current agents remain far from reliable OR practice. The best agent passes only 35.51% of all tasks and 20.59% of hard tasks, and many feasible submissions still fall below the required quality threshold. Failure analysis further shows that errors are dominated by strategic weaknesses, including missed operational rules, brittle formulations, weak feasible-solution construction, and insufficient solution improvement. OR-specific procedural skills increase hard-task feasibility, but do not reliably improve solution quality or pass rate. These results suggest that progress in OR agents requires moving beyond plausible optimization code toward dependable, high-quality operational decision-making.

AI.tldr: End-to-end, execution-grounded OR benchmark (ORAgentBench) to evaluate autonomous agents on real-world OR tasks; reveals large performance gaps.
AI.motivation: Current OR evaluations decouple modeling from solving and rely on pre-formalized or text-only instances; a workflow-level assessment with operational artifacts is needed to gauge practical readiness of autonomous OR agents.
AI.method: 107 tasks across diverse operational scenarios, each in an isolated environment with a natural-language brief, multi-file data, configuration artifacts, and a required submission schema. Agents must write and run solution code; submissions are evaluated by hidden validators for schema validity, hard-constraint feasibility, and normalized objective quality. Experiments with fourteen frontier agent-model configurations.
AI.result: Best agent passes 35.51% of tasks and 20.59% of hard tasks; many feasible submissions still fall below the quality threshold. Failure analysis attributes errors to strategic weaknesses (missed rules, brittle formulations, weak feasible-solution construction, insufficient solution improvement). OR-specific procedural skills improve hard-task feasibility but not consistently solution quality or pass rate.
AI.conclusion: Progress in OR agents requires moving beyond plausible optimization code toward dependable, high-quality operational decision-making.

title: Interactive Pareto navigation for deep multi-task learning
summary: In multi-task learning, handling an increasing number of objectives can quickly become challenging, both in terms of the computational resources and the decision maker's capacity to choose appropriate trade-offs. A widely used approach is thus to aggregate the individual losses in a single loss function by a weighted sum. This often fails to capture either the decision maker's preferences as a result of the shape of the Pareto front, or requires multiple adjustments and computations which becomes prohibitively expensive in deep learning applications. To address these issues, we introduce a novel framework, Preference Pareto Exploration (PPE), which enforces the decision maker's preferences while accounting for the geometry of the Pareto set in an interactive exploration process. PPE is based on a predictor-corrector method that performs predictor steps tangential to the manifold of Pareto-optimal solutions, following the decision maker's preference. The subsequent corrector step results in a new trade-off reflecting this preference. To avoid explicit Hessian computations when characterizing the tangent space of the manifold, we employ a Krylov subspace method that relies solely on matrix-vector products. These products can be efficiently obtained via automatic differentiation, ensuring both efficiency and robustness throughout the optimization process. The method's functionality and performance are demonstrated using both toy problems and examples from deep learning.

AI.tldr: PPE is an interactive framework for exploring Pareto-optimal trade-offs in multi-task learning, integrating decision maker preferences via a predictor-corrector scheme on the Pareto manifold and using a Krylov subspace method to compute tangents without Hessians, enabling efficient, Hessian-free updates demonstrated on toy problems and deep learning tasks.
AI.motivation: To overcome the limitations of loss aggregation (weighted sums) in multi-objective deep learning, which often misses user preferences and is computationally expensive due to multiple adjustments and computations. PPE seeks to align optimization with the decision maker's preferences while respecting the geometry of the Pareto set.
AI.method: A predictor-corrector scheme that moves along the Pareto manifold in tangent directions aligned with user preferences (predictor step), followed by a corrective update to reflect the new trade-off. Tangent space characterization is performed via a Krylov subspace method that relies on matrix-vector products (avoiding explicit Hessians); these products are obtained efficiently through automatic differentiation. Demonstrations include toy problems and deep learning examples.
AI.result: The method is validated on toy problems and deep learning tasks, showcasing efficiency and robustness in navigating Pareto-optimal trade-offs under user preferences.
AI.conclusion: PPE enables preference-guided, geometry-aware exploration of Pareto-optimal solutions in multi-task learning with Hessian-free tangent computations, delivering efficient and robust interactive trade-off optimization in DL settings.

title: Linear Recurrent Unit with Semantic Modulation for Image Super-Resolution
summary: Linear recurrent unit (LRU), designed with a principled formulation for stable linear recurrence, has demonstrated promising accuracy and robustness on long-range dependency tasks. However, its static parameterization and single-scan method limits its applicability to 2D vision tasks. In this study, we propose a LRU-based restoration network with a semantic modulating unit (SMU) to achieve a harmonious balance between performance and efficiency in single-image super-resolution. The SMU plays three key roles: LRU modulation, spatial categorization, and feature enhancement through learned prototype. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method quantitatively and qualitatively surpasses recent state-of-the-art methods. Notably, our approach achieves superior performance with computational complexity on par with existing methods. The source code and models are available at https://github.com/MingyuChoi-run/LSM

AI.tldr: An LRU-based restoration network with a semantic modulating unit (SMU) for efficient single-image super-resolution, achieving state-of-the-art performance with complexity comparable to existing methods.
AI.motivation: LRU's static parameterization and single-scan operation hinder applicability to 2D vision tasks like SR; a flexible, efficient mechanism is needed to handle spatial variations and long-range dependencies in images.
AI.method: Introduce LRU-based restoration network augmented with SMU that provides LRU modulation, spatial categorization, and feature enhancement via learned prototypes. SMU enables dynamic adaptation and efficient processing for SR.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show quantitative and qualitative improvements over recent SOTA methods; computational complexity is on par with existing methods. Code and models released.
AI.conclusion: The proposed SMU-enabled LRU network achieves a favorable performance-efficiency trade-off for SR and demonstrates the viability of LRU-based approaches in 2D vision tasks.

title: CombEval: A Framework for Evaluating Combinatorial Counting in Large Language Models
summary: We present CombEval, a dynamic benchmark for evaluating combinatorial counting in large language models. CombEval represents each problem as a typed Cofola specification over entities, combinatorial objects, object dependencies, and constraints, enabling controlled generation of natural-language counting problems with exact solver-verified answers. Unlike static collections, CombEval supports systematic variation of object type, entity scale, constraint count, and reasoning depth. We evaluate 11 LLMs under direct and code-augmented settings and find that models remain brittle on ordered objects, indistinguishable elements, relatively positional constraints, and nested object dependencies. Error analysis further identifies failures in constraint interpretation and counting principles. CombEval provides a diagnostic testbed for studying when and why LLMs fail at combinatorial reasoning. The code and generated benchmark suites are publicly available at \url{https://github.com/YuxuZhou-CN/combination-problem-generation}.

AI.tldr: CombEval is a dynamic benchmark for evaluating combinatorial counting in LLMs using typed Cofola specifications, enabling controlled problem generation with solver-verified answers. It reveals brittleness in LLMs on ordered objects, indistinguishable elements, positional constraints, and nested dependencies, with errors tied to constraint interpretation and counting principles; code and benchmarks are publicly available.
AI.motivation: Address brittleness and gap in existing static benchmarks for combinatorial counting in LLMs by offering a scalable, parameterizable, solver-verified diagnostic platform.
AI.method: Represent problems as typed Cofola specifications over entities, combinatorial objects, dependencies, and constraints. Systematically vary object type, entity scale, constraint count, and reasoning depth. Evaluate 11 LLMs under direct and code-augmented settings; analyze errors focusing on constraint interpretation and counting principles.
AI.result: Demonstrates model brittleness in several structured counting scenarios; error analysis highlights core failure modes; provides a diagnostic testbed with publicly released code and benchmark suites.
AI.conclusion: CombEval offers a versatile, solver-grounded framework to study when and why LLMs fail at combinatorial reasoning and serves as a resource for ongoing research and improvement.

title: Techniques for Peak Memory Reduction for LoRA Fine-tuning of LLMs on Edge Devices
comment: Hassan Dbouk and Matthias Reisser contributed equally to this work
summary: Fine-tuning of Large Language Models (LLMs) using Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) on an end-user's data offers personalized experiences while keeping data private, but faces severe memory constraints on consumer hardware. Peak memory during fine-tuning often exceeds device limits, especially for models with billions of parameters and long-context training data. This paper introduces a suite of complementary techniques to reduce memory footprint without sacrificing model quality: (1) base model quantization with on-the-fly dequantization, (2) memory-efficient checkpointing combining selective activation caching and disk offloading, (3) softmax approximation using semantically relevant token subsets, and (4) logits masking. Experiments on Llama-3.2 3B and Qwen-2.5 3B demonstrate up to $26\times$ and $28\times$ reduction in peak memory, enabling fine-tuning on resource-constrained devices.

AI.tldr: A suite of complementary memory-saving techniques for LoRA fine-tuning of LLMs on end-user data, achieving up to 26–28x peak memory reduction for 3B models, enabling fine-tuning on consumer hardware.
AI.motivation: Enable personalized, private fine-tuning of large language models on end-user data without exceeding consumer hardware memory limits.
AI.method: (1) base model quantization with on-the-fly dequantization; (2) memory-efficient checkpointing with selective activation caching and disk offloading; (3) softmax approximation using semantically relevant token subsets; (4) logits masking.
AI.result: Experiments on Llama-3.2 3B and Qwen-2.5 3B show up to 26× and 28× peak memory reduction, enabling fine-tuning on resource-constrained devices.
AI.conclusion: A practical, multi-technique approach to reducing memory footprint during LoRA fine-tuning, preserving model quality while enabling private on-device customization.

title: World Engine: Towards the Era of Post-Training for Autonomous Driving
summary: Autonomous vehicles must operate safely in the real world, where errors can have severe consequences. Although modern end-to-end driving policies excel in routine scenarios, their reliability is limited by the scarcity of safety-critical ``long-tail'' events in real driving datasets. These rare interactions define the practical safety boundary of the learned policy, yet they are difficult to collect at scale in the real world. Here we show that this fundamental limitation can be addressed by post-training pre-trained driving models on synthesized high-stakes interactions. We introduce World Engine, a generative framework that reconstructs high-fidelity interactive environments from real-world logs and systematically extrapolates them into realistic safety-critical variations. This paradigm enables reinforcement-based post-training to align policies with safety constraints, circumventing the physical risks inherent in real-world exploration. On a public benchmark built on nuPlan, World Engine substantially reduces failures in rare safety-critical scenarios and yields significantly larger gains than scaling pre-training data alone. Furthermore, when deployed on a production-scale autonomous driving system, the resulting policy reduces simulated collisions and demonstrates measurable improvements in on-road testing, showing that post-training on synthesized, safety-critical interactions offers a scalable and effective pathway to safer autonomous driving. The full codebase suite, including training, is released to the public.

AI.tldr: A framework (World Engine) synthesizes high-stakes driving interactions from real logs to post-train policies with reinforcement learning, improving safety in rare events.
AI.motivation: Safety-critical, long-tail driving events are scarce in real-world data, limiting reliability; a scalable approach is needed to learn safety constraints without dangerous real-world exploration.
AI.method: Reconstruct high-fidelity interactive environments from real-world logs and extrapolate them into realistic safety-critical variations; apply reinforcement-based post-training to align policies with safety constraints using the synthesized scenarios.
AI.result: On the nuPlan-based benchmark, World Engine substantially reduces failures in rare safety-critical scenarios, yielding larger gains than simply scaling pre-training data; in production-scale deployment, it reduces simulated collisions and improves on-road testing.
AI.conclusion: Post-training on synthesized safety-critical interactions provides a scalable and effective path to safer autonomous driving, with the full codebase released.

title: Gaussian Process Prior Variational Autoencoder for Endoscopic Videos
summary:
Endoscopic video analysis is essential for gastrointestinal diagnosis and computer-assisted interventions, but video sequences are routinely degraded by specular reflections, motion artifacts, and missing frames. These transient corruptions can distract clinicians, reduce image interpretability, and disrupt downstream tasks such as 3D reconstruction and navigation. Effective restoration therefore requires methods that exploit temporal continuity rather than treating frames in isolation. We introduce a Gaussian Process Prior Variational Autoencoder (GPVAE) framework for endoscopic video restoration that replaces the standard factorized latent prior with a temporal Gaussian process prior, enabling interpolation of missing frames with uncertainty-aware reconstruction.
  The framework combines endoscopy-specific encoders, including a convolutional EndoVAE backbone and pretrained Vision Transformer encoders from GastroNet-5M, with two scalable GP approximations: Hierarchical Prior Approximation (HPA) and Sparse Precision Approximation (SPA). Specular reflections are handled using a DUCKNet-based masking pipeline that excludes corrupted pixels from the reconstruction objective. On the C3VDv2 colonoscopy dataset, the best GPVAE variants reduced image reconstruction RMSE by 21.9\% on average, and by up to 26.1\%, relative to matched VAE baselines. Downstream trajectory RMSE was reduced by 12.7\% on average across classical visual odometry and a pretrained PoseNet, at an average increase of 27.3\% in training time per epoch. Finally, the GP posterior provides per-frame uncertainty estimates that reflect temporal support and offer a confidence signal for restored frames.

AI.tldr: Temporal GP prior VAE framework for endoscopic video restoration (GPVAE) with HPA/SPA approximations and DUCKNet masking; achieves notable RMSE gains and improved VO with per-frame uncertainty estimates.
AI.motivation: Endoscopic videos suffer transient corruptions (specular highlights, motion artifacts, missing frames) that disrupt temporal continuity; restoring frames with uncertainty improves image quality and downstream tasks like 3D reconstruction and navigation.
AI.method: Replace factorized latent prior with a temporal Gaussian process prior in a VAE. Use endoscopy-tailored encoders (EndoVAE backbone, GastroNet-5M ViT); apply Hierarchical Prior Approximation (HPA) and Sparse Precision Approximation (SPA) for scalable GP inference; DUCKNet-based masking to exclude corrupted pixels during reconstruction; evaluate on C3VDv2 colonoscopy dataset; compare to matched VAE baselines; assess image reconstruction RMSE and downstream trajectory metrics; GP posterior yields per-frame uncertainty.
AI.result: GPVAE variants reduce image reconstruction RMSE by 21.9% on average (up to 26.1%); downstream trajectory RMSE reduced by 12.7% on average across visual odometry and PoseNet; training time per epoch increased by 27.3% on average; GP posterior provides per-frame uncertainty aligned with temporal support.
AI.conclusion: Temporal Gaussian process priors enable uncertainty-aware, temporally coherent restoration in endoscopy; scalable GP approximations (HPA, SPA) make it feasible; masking effectively handles specular reflections; benefits extend to downstream navigation tasks, with a trade-off in training time and computational cost.

title: Think Again or Think Longer? Selective Verification for Budget-Aware Reasoning
summary: Test-time reasoning is increasingly used as a serving-time control knob, but extra reasoning is not uniformly valuable: it can repair failed attempts, waste compute on already-correct answers, or introduce harmful answer changes. We study this as a deployment allocation problem rather than a new-verifier problem. We introduce \sevra, Selective Verification for Reasoning Allocation, a serving-layer controller that decides whether to preserve a frozen solver's initial answer or invoke active verification. Using a frozen Qwen3-4B solver, we log intervention outcomes and train recoverability-aware gates from serving-visible attempt state. On \mathfive, selective verification reaches 76.3\% accuracy, compared with 75.5\% for always verifying, while reducing post-generation tokens by 26.8\% and harmful flips from 2.2\% to 1.0\%. However, an 8,192-token initial solve reaches 76.0\% accuracy with 28\% fewer total model tokens, showing that selective recovery is useful but not the best tested cost frontier. In frozen transfer to \gsm, the selective policy verifies only 3.0\% of examples, improves accuracy from 93.4\% to 94.5\%, and reduces verification tokens by 91.2\% relative to always verifying; again, a longer initial solve matches its accuracy with fewer realized tokens. On CommonsenseQA, always-on verification hurts, while Self-Consistency@5 improves accuracy at about five times the realized token cost. The resulting deployment rule is: tune the initial budget first, then use selective recovery when explicit checks, bounded retries, auditability, or regression-risk control matter.

AI.tldr: Selective verification (Sevra) treats test-time reasoning as a deployable control knob, trading accuracy, compute, and risk. It uses a frozen model and recoverability-aware gates to decide when to verify, achieving modest accuracy gains with token savings across tasks, but not uniformly the best cost frontier.
AI.motivation: Test-time reasoning can repair failures or waste compute and may introduce errors. The goal is to optimize deployment cost and performance by selectively verifying rather than always verifying or never verifying.
AI.method: Introduce Sevra: a serving-layer controller that decides whether to preserve a frozen solver's initial answer or invoke active verification. Train recoverability-aware gates from serving-visible attempt state using a frozen Qwen3-4B. Log interventions and evaluate interventions across multiple tasks/datasets to learn when verification helps.
AI.result: MathFive: 76.3% accuracy vs 75.5% with always verify; tokens reduced by 26.8%; harmful flips down from 2.2% to 1.0%. An 8k-token initial solve yields 76.0% with 28% fewer tokens. GSM transfer: verify only 3.0% of examples; accuracy 93.4%→94.5%; verification tokens reduced by 91.2%. Longer initial solve matches accuracy with fewer tokens. CommonsenseQA: always-on verification hurts; Self-Consistency@5 improves accuracy at ~5x token cost. Overall, selective recovery is helpful but not always the best cost frontier.
AI.conclusion: Deployment guidance: first tune the initial budget, then apply selective recovery when checks, bounded retries, auditability, or regression-risk control matter. The approach yields cost-efficient, accuracy gains in several settings, but effectiveness is dataset-dependent and not universally optimal.

title: Tracking Representation Dynamics in Large Language Models with Persistent Homology
summary: Large language models are commonly aligned through supervised fine-tuning, yet little is known about how their internal representations evolve during this process. We study alignment dynamics using persistent homology by tracking the topology of activation spaces throughout fine-tuning. Across four transformer language models ranging from 1B to 7B parameters and three alignment objectives corresponding to helpful, harmless, and mixed training data, we find that the majority of topological reorganization occurs during the earliest stages of training. A dense checkpoint analysis reveals a transient peak in topological activity followed by rapid stabilization.
