We further show that different alignment objectives induce distinguishable topological trajectories, while instruction-tuned and pretrained models exhibit qualitatively different patterns of evolution. Our results suggest that persistent homology provides a complementary perspective on alignment, revealing representation-level changes that are not apparent from behavioral metrics alone.

AI.tldr: Using persistent homology to analyze how activation-space topology changes during alignment of large language models, finding that most topology reorganization occurs early in training, with a transient peak and later stabilization; alignment objectives produce distinct topological trajectories and instruction-tuned vs pretrained models show different evolution patterns; PH offers a representation-level perspective beyond behavioral metrics.
AI.motivation: To understand how internal representations evolve during supervised fine-tuning (alignment) of LLMs, and whether topology-based methods can reveal dynamics not captured by standard performance metrics.
AI.method: Compute and compare persistent homology descriptors (e.g., persistence diagrams/Betti numbers) of activation spaces across four transformer models (1B–7B) and three alignment objectives (helpful, harmless, mixed) during fine-tuning. Perform dense checkpoint analyses to trace temporal topology, and contrast alignment trajectories with pretrained vs instruction-tuned models.
AI.result: Most topological reorganization occurs in the earliest training stages. A transient peak in topological activity precedes rapid stabilization. Different alignment objectives yield distinguishable topological trajectories, and instruction-tuned vs pretrained models exhibit qualitatively different evolution patterns.
AI.conclusion: Persistent homology provides a complementary lens on alignment dynamics, uncovering representation-level changes in activation-space topology that are not visible through standard behavioral metrics.

title: MMD-SLAM: Structure-Enhanced Multi-Meta Gaussian Distribution-Guided Visual SLAM
summary: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) has significantly boosted novel view synthesis and high-fidelity scene reconstruction, expanding the potential of 3DGS-based Visual Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM) methods. However, most existing systems fail to fully exploit the underlying structural information, which limits rendering quality and often leads to inconsistent maps. To address these limitations, we propose MMD-SLAM, a structure-enhanced Visual SLAM framework that leverages the Atlanta World (AW) assumption to guide a Multi-Meta Gaussian representation for photorealistic mapping. First, we introduce a point-line fusion strategy for pose optimization, where 3D line segments are incorporated to improve tracking robustness and provide additional constraints for mapping. Second, we design a Multi-Meta Gaussian representation with dominant directions, explicitly encoding structural priors from the AW hypothesis. Finally, we propose a Gaussian evolution strategy that adapts to scene geometry and incorporates structural cues into global optimization. Extensive experiments demonstrate that these innovations enable MMD-SLAM to achieve state-of-the-art performance in both tracking accuracy and mapping quality. e.g., our method achieves a 48.56% reduction in ATE RMSE on ScanNet and a 5.71% improvement in PSNR on Replica, compared with MonoGS.

AI.tldr: A structure-aware SLAM framework (MMD-SLAM) integrates Atlanta World priors with a Multi-Meta Gaussian representation, using point-line fusion and a Gaussian evolution strategy to enhance tracking and mapping in 3D Gaussian Splatting-based SLAM; achieves state-of-the-art results on ScanNet and Replica.
AI.motivation: Existing 3D Gaussian Splatting–based SLAM methods under-exploit structural cues, leading to poorer rendering and inconsistent maps. Incorporating the Atlanta World assumption and dominant directional Gaussians can improve pose estimation and map quality.
AI.method: 1) Point-line fusion for pose optimization by incorporating 3D line segments to strengthen tracking and provide mapping constraints. 2) Multi-Meta Gaussian representation with dominant directions capturing AW-based structural priors. 3) Gaussian evolution strategy that adapts to scene geometry and injects structural cues into global optimization.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show state-of-the-art performance in tracking accuracy and mapping quality; e.g., 48.56% reduction in ATE RMSE on ScanNet and 5.71% PSNR improvement on Replica compared to MonoGS.
AI.conclusion: MMD-SLAM demonstrates that embedding structural priors from the AW hypothesis into a multi-Gaussian representation and leveraging line constraints can substantially improve both tracking robustness and high-fidelity mapping in 3DGS-based SLAM.

title: SpatialSV: Internalizing Interpretable 3D Spatial Awareness in MLLMs via Task-Oriented Visual Supervision
summary: Unlocking the spatial intelligence of multimodal large language model (MLLMs) is crucial for understanding and interacting with the 3D world. Prevailing approaches typically inject spatial priors via external tools, which impose significant inference overhead, or rely on latent feature distillation, which remains uninterpretable and lacks fine-grained geometric constraints. To address these issues, we propose SpatialSV, a framework designed to internalize robust 3D spatial awareness within MLLMs while simultaneously offering inherent interpretability. Deviating from passive feature imitation, SpatialSV employs task-oriented visual supervision, compelling the model to actively lift its 2D visual features into explicit 3D representations, including depth maps, camera poses, and point clouds. Crucially, this 2D-to-3D lifting process provides a transparent window into the model's representations: the resulting 3D reconstructions serve as an intuitive proxy for visualizing and diagnosing the quality of the model's intrinsic spatial knowledge. Extensive experiments across multiple models and benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness of SpatialSV in enhancing and interpreting MLLMs' spatial intelligence. Furthermore, the framework exhibits strong generalization in semi-supervised settings, validating its potential to leverage unlabeled visual data for scalable, interpretable spatial representation learning.

AI.tldr: A framework (SpatialSV) internalizes 3D spatial awareness in multimodal LLMs by learning 2D-to-3D lifting (depth, pose, point clouds) via task-oriented supervision, enabling interpretable spatial representations and semi-supervised generalization; it reduces reliance on external tools and latent feature distillation.
AI.motivation: To overcome reliance on external spatial priors/tools and opaque latent distillation, by embedding robust, interpretable 3D spatial reasoning directly within MLLMs.
AI.method: Train with task-oriented visual supervision that compels 2D visual features to be lifted into explicit 3D representations (depth maps, camera poses, point clouds). Use the resulting 3D reconstructions as a transparent diagnostic proxy for the model's spatial knowledge. Evaluate across various models/benchmarks; explore semi-supervised learning with unlabeled data.
AI.result: Improved spatial intelligence that is both stronger and more interpretable. Demonstrates generalization in semi-supervised settings and scalability across models/benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: SpatialSV offers a viable path to endow MLLMs with internal, interpretable 3D spatial representations through supervised 2D-to-3D lifting, reducing dependence on external tools and enabling scalable, diagnosable spatial competence.

title: Human-on-the-Loop Orchestration for AI-Assisted Legal Discovery
summary: Autonomous Large Language Model (LLM) agents are increasingly deployed in electronic discovery (e-discovery), where compounding errors across multi-step reasoning chains can constitute legal malpractice. Unlike single-turn retrieval, agentic workflows operating over privileged document corpora exhibit a class of failure we term "trajectory collapse": an early misclassification silently propagates, rendering an entire privilege review invalid. This paper makes three contributions. First, we propose a structured taxonomy of agentic failures in legal information retrieval, organized by functional stage. Second, we introduce a four-layer verification architecture -- spanning planning, reasoning, execution, and uncertainty quantification -- designed to intercept these failures before they compound. Third, we present a preliminary simulation study on a synthetic e-discovery corpus that demonstrates how mandatory Human-on-the-Loop (HOTL) escalation thresholds reduce privilege-waiver risk relative to fully autonomous baselines. Our results suggest that calibrated uncertainty thresholds can reduce privilege-waiver risk by up to 61% versus fully autonomous deployment, while routing fewer than one quarter of documents to attorney review.

AI.tldr: Proposes taxonomy of agentic failures in legal information retrieval, introduces a four-layer verification architecture (planning, reasoning, execution, uncertainty quantification) to intercept failures, and provides a simulation showing HOTL escalation reduces privilege-waiver risk (up to 61%) while routing under 25% of documents for attorney review.
AI.motivation: To mitigate legal malpractice risk in e-discovery when autonomous LLM agents operate over privileged document corpora by identifying failure modes and introducing safeguards.
AI.method: Developed a structured taxonomy of agentic failures by functional stage, designed a four-layer verification architecture to intercept failures, and conducted a preliminary simulation on a synthetic e-discovery corpus comparing mandatory human-on-the-loop escalation against fully autonomous baselines.
AI.result: Finds that calibrated uncertainty thresholds and HOTL escalation reduce privilege-waiver risk by up to 61% relative to fully autonomous deployment, while routing fewer than a quarter of documents to attorney review.
AI.conclusion: Calibrated multi-layer verification and escalation policies can substantially mitigate risk in agentic, privilege-sensitive e-discovery, enabling safer deployment and guiding future real-world validation.

title: Predicting Mergeability of Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning Updates
summary: Low-rank adaptation (LoRA) makes it cheap to train many domain- and task-specific language model adapters, but whether two adapters can be merged is usually discovered only after both have been fully trained and evaluated. This late feedback is costly: adapters that are strong in isolation can interfere destructively once their updates are combined. We ask whether this outcome can be anticipated. We formalize adapter mergeability as the degree to which an adapter preserves its single-task utility after merging, and show that it can be forecast from signals measured in the first few percent of training -- chiefly how the low-rank updates and their gradients align across tasks and how much they disturb shared representations. We package these signals into MergeProbe, a lightweight predictor that estimates pairwise and set-level retention and turns the estimate into a concrete decision: merge directly, reweight, prune, or route. On MERGE-PEFT, a five-domain benchmark spanning math, code, science, instruction following, and safety, MergeProbe attains the best average and worst-case retention among strong interference-aware merge baselines while adding far less deployment overhead than full task routing. This turns LoRA merging from a post-hoc engineering step into an anticipatory measurement problem.

AI.tldr: Forecasts adapter mergeability early in training to prevent destructive interference when merging LoRA adapters, enabling proactive decisions (merge, reweight, prune, route).
AI.motivation: Reduce the cost and risk of late-stage failures in merging domain- and task-specific LoRA adapters by predicting mergeability from initial training signals.
AI.method: Introduce MergeProbe, a lightweight predictor that uses early-training signals—especially alignment of low-rank updates and their gradients across tasks and the disturbance to shared representations—to estimate pairwise and set-level retention and guide merge decisions. Evaluation on MERGE-PEFT across math, code, science, instruction following, and safety.
AI.result: MergeProbe achieves the best average and worst-case retention among strong interference-aware merge baselines on MERGE-PEFT, while incurring far less deployment overhead than full task routing.
AI.conclusion: LoRA merging shifts from a post-hoc engineering step to an anticipatory measurement problem, enabling informed decisions to merge, reweight, prune, or route adapters.

title: CARE: Competence-Aware Reward Shaping for Adaptive Reasoning Length in Video-MLLMs
summary: In multimodal video reasoning, reinforcement learning-based methods typically rely on simplistic and inflexible reasoning-length control strategies that fail to adapt to the model's evolving competence. This mismatch may suppress necessary exploration at early stages, while encouraging redundant reasoning and inefficient decoding once the model becomes more competent. In this paper, we propose CARE, a competence-aware reward shaping framework for adaptive reasoning length optimization in multimodal reasoning. Specifically, CARE maintains a smoothed competence estimate via an exponential moving average of pass rates, and uses it to route training into progressive stages that shift the reward preference from exploration-oriented long-form reasoning to efficiency-oriented concise reasoning. To avoid conflating verbosity with intrinsic task complexity, CARE further normalizes reasoning effort with batch-level statistics, and introduces a posterior amplifier to strengthen reward signals for unexpectedly strong performance on historically difficult samples. The proposed mechanism is seamlessly integrated into the GRPO training pipeline and incurs no additional inference-time overhead. Extensive experiments on multiple video reasoning and general video understanding benchmarks demonstrate that CARE consistently improves reasoning accuracy, stabilizes reinforcement learning, and significantly enhances token efficiency. Moreover, CARE exhibits a characteristic inverted-U trajectory of reasoning length during training, and yields shorter yet more informative reasoning traces at convergence, indicating effective adaptive allocation of reasoning budget. We provide the source code for our proposed CARE framework and experiments at https://github.com/1Pansy/Video-CARE.

AI.tldr: CARE is a competence-aware reward shaping framework that adaptively controls reasoning length in multimodal video reasoning by routing training through stages based on an EMA of pass rates, normalizing reasoning effort, and boosting rewards for hard cases; integrated into GRPO, it improves accuracy and token efficiency without extra inference cost.
AI.motivation: RL-based multimodal video reasoning uses fixed, simplistic reasoning-length strategies that cause early under-exploration or late over-verbosity; an adaptive, competence-aware mechanism is needed to balance exploration and efficiency as the model learns.
AI.method: Introduce CARE: maintain smoothed competence via exponential moving average of pass rates; stage-based training that shifts reward preference from long-form exploration to concise reasoning; normalize reasoning effort with batch-level statistics; apply a posterior amplifier to strengthen rewards when performance on difficult samples is unexpectedly high; integrate into GRPO training pipeline; no extra inference-time cost.
AI.result: Across multiple video reasoning and general video understanding benchmarks, CARE yields higher reasoning accuracy, more stable RL training, and better token efficiency; shows an inverted-U trajectory of reasoning length during training and converges to shorter, more informative reasoning traces; source code released.
AI.conclusion: CARE provides effective, adaptive regulation of reasoning budget in multimodal RL for video reasoning, improving performance and efficiency while maintaining training stability, and offering a practical, plug-in approach.

authors: V V N K Someswara Rao Koduri
title: TelcoAgent: A Scalable 5G Multi-KPM Forecasting With 3GPP-Grounded Explainability
comment: 6 pages, 6 figures. Submitted to IEEE GLOBECOM 2026
summary: Key Performance Measurement (KPM) forecasting is essential for proactive network management of 5G and next-generation telecom networks. However, existing machine learning (ML) approaches face significant limitations in scalability and explainability, restricting their effectiveness in real-world deployments. We propose TelcoAgent, a foundation model-based framework that enables accurate, scalable, and explainable forecasting of multiple KPMs across diverse network cells without the need for site-specific training. Specifically, the framework comprises three key components: (i) an automated three-agent pipeline that constructs a 3rd Generation Partnership Project (3GPP) knowledge graph directly from specification documents, (ii) a scalable, time-series foundation model (TSFM)-based prediction pipeline to deliver accurate, zero-shot forecasting, and finally (iii) a reasoning and explanation pipeline that provides actionable, domain-grounded diagnostics. Evaluated using a 3-month, real-world, city-scale 5G KPM dataset from a U.S.-based network operator, TelcoAgent demonstrates high forecasting accuracy for all 7 considered KPMs per cell across 200 cells, while delivering explainable insights and actionable instructions to address network degradations.

AI.tldr: TelcoAgent: a TSFM-based framework with an automated 3GPP knowledge graph construction and a reasoning pipeline for zero-shot forecasting of multiple KPMs across cells, achieving high accuracy and actionable explanations without site-specific training.
AI.motivation: Existing ML approaches for KPM forecasting in 5G/6G face scalability and explainability barriers, requiring site-specific adaptation and hindering real-world deployment. There is a need for a scalable, explainable, zero-shot forecasting framework that can handle multiple KPMs across diverse cells.
AI.method: 1) An automated three-agent pipeline extracts and constructs a 3GPP knowledge graph from specification documents. 2) A scalable time-series foundation model (TSFM) performs zero-shot forecasting for multiple KPMs across cells. 3) A reasoning and explanation pipeline generates domain-grounded diagnostics and actionable recommendations.
AI.result: On a real-world, city-scale 3-month 5G KPM dataset across 200 cells for 7 KPMs, TelcoAgent achieves high forecasting accuracy per KPM per cell, provides explainable insights, and outputs actionable instructions to mitigate degradations.
AI.conclusion: TelcoAgent demonstrates that a foundation-model-based approach, with automated knowledge-graph construction and integrated reasoning, can deliver accurate, scalable, and explainable multi-KPM forecasting without site-specific training, suitable for proactive network management.

title: Displacement Is Not Direction: Evaluating Fidelity Metrics for Quantized LLM Deployment
summary: Fidelity metrics, such as per-token KL divergence (KLD) against a high-precision reference, are often used in practice as low-cost proxies for benchmark quality. We test this practice on a 28-quant cohort of Qwen3.6-35B-A3B and a 41-quant cohort of Devstral-Small-2-24B, evaluated across a suite of downstream benchmarks. We find that KLD is strongly correlated with benchmark score over the full cohort ($ρ=-0.72$ on Qwen and $ρ=-0.86$ on Devstral, both with $p<0.001$). However, this relationship collapses to non-significance in the near-baseline silent zone ($ρ=+0.00$ on Qwen and $ρ=-0.24$, $p=0.36$, on Devstral). This collapse persists across 14 measurement variants, including different KLD aggregations, perplexity formulations, top-1 agreement, calibration corpora, and context lengths. At the per-prompt level, KLD has only weak failure-prediction power on code, with failed-vs-passed geometric-mean ratios in $[1.08,1.22]$ across five models on LiveCodeBench, and fails as a cross-model router, achieving only $42.3\%-49.4\%$ accuracy on disagreement prompts. We trace the collapse to a structural decomposition: KLD primarily measures the volume of disagreement with the reference, with silent-zone composite $ρ=+0.94$ ($p<0.001$) on Qwen and $+0.55$ ($p=0.03$) on Devstral, while its relationship to the direction of those disagreements is weak and task-conditional.

AI.tldr: KL divergence (KLD) correlates with benchmark quality overall but fails in the near-baseline silent zone and is not a robust proxy across models/tasks; it primarily measures the volume of disagreements with the reference rather than the direction, limiting predictive utility.
AI.motivation: To assess whether per-token KL divergence against a high-precision reference can serve as a low-cost proxy for downstream benchmark quality across large model cohorts, and to understand when it fails.
AI.method: Empirical evaluation on two model cohorts (28-quant Qwen3.6-35B-A3B; 41-quant Devstral-Small-2-24B). Measured KLD against reference across downstream benchmarks. Calculated Spearman correlations (ρ) and p-values. Examined 14 measurement variants (KLD aggregations, perplexity formulations, top-1 accuracy, calibration corpora, context lengths). Per-prompt analysis on code (LiveCodeBench) for failure-prediction and disagreement prompts. Performed a structural decomposition to separate volume of disagreements from direction of disagreements.
AI.result: Across full cohorts: ρ = -0.72 (Qwen) and ρ = -0.86 (Devstral), p < 0.001, indicating strong correlation with benchmark scores. In the near-baseline silent zone, correlations collapse to non-significance: ρ ≈ +0.00 (Qwen) and ρ = -0.24, p = 0.36 (Devstral). The collapse persists across 14 variants of KLD measurement. Per-prompt: weak failure-prediction for code (GM ratios 1.08–1.22) and cross-model disagreement routing accuracy 42.3%–49.4%. Structural decomposition shows KLD primarily quantifies the volume of disagreement with the reference (silent-zone composite ρ = +0.94, p < 0.001 on Qwen; ρ = +0.55, p = 0.03 on Devstral), while the relation to disagreement direction is weak and task-conditional.
AI.conclusion: KLD is not a robust, general-purpose proxy for benchmark quality. It remains predictive only in non-silent regions and primarily reflects the volume of disagreement rather than its direction. This limits its utility as a universal quality proxy; alternative metrics or complementary signals are needed for reliable cross-model, cross-task quality estimation.

title: Co-policy: Responsive Human-Robot Co-Creation for Musical Performances
summary: Art has long stood as a pivotal expression of human creativity. Embodied artificial intelligence offers a route for generative models to participate in that creativity through physical action rather than disembodied digital content. In robotic music co-creation, it is challenging to connect semantic musical understanding with real-time and physically executable performance. We present Co-policy, a framework for human-robot musical co-creation that separates semantic intent grounding, constrained musical variation, and visuomotor execution. To ground musical semantics, Co-policy uses pre-inference semantic anchors and a fine-tuned Qwen-vl planner (F-Qwen) to transform speech, live musical seeds, and visual observations into structured co-creation plans. To support low-latency execution, Co-policy introduces a Gaussian-Mixture Visuomotor Policy (GMP), implemented as a conditional mixture-density policy that maps target notes and visual context to multimodal robot actions in a single forward pass. Unlike robotic playback systems that merely reproduce user-specified notes, Co-policy generates complementary musical responses under both musical and physical constraints. Real-robot chime experiments, ablations, and expert evaluation show improved intent alignment, execution accuracy, and response frequency over diffusion-policy and ablated baselines, supporting physically grounded action generation as a key requirement for embodied human-AI co-creation.

AI.tldr: A low-latency, semantically grounded visuomotor policy for embodied human-robot musical co-creation, combining semantic grounding (pre-inference anchors, F-Qwen) with a GMP action predictor to generate proactive, physically executable musical responses; outperforms diffusion-policy baselines.
AI.motivation: Bridge semantic musical understanding and real-time, physically executable robot action to enable expressive, embodied human-robot music collaboration.
AI.method: Three-tier approach: semantic grounding with pre-inference anchors and F-Qwen planner to produce structured co-creation plans; constrained musical variation; and a Gaussian-Mixture Visuomotor Policy (GMP) that maps target notes plus visual context to multimodal robot actions in a single forward pass.
AI.result: Empirical results from real-robot chime experiments, ablations, and expert evaluation show improved intent alignment, execution accuracy, and response frequency compared with diffusion-policy baselines and ablated variants.
AI.conclusion: Physically grounded action generation is essential for embodied human-AI co-creation, and Co-policy demonstrates that integrated semantic grounding and fast visuomotor policies enable expressive, responsive robot musical collaboration.

title: Spatial-Aware Reduction Framework: Towards Efficient and Faithful Visual State Space Models
summary: Mamba demonstrates strong efficiency in modeling long visual sequences. However, when token reduction is applied to structurally enhanced Mamba variants, these models exhibit a severe performance collapse. We attribute this degradation to the spatially agnostic nature of existing reduction methods, which violate the two-dimensional structural premise required by the selective scanning mechanism. In this work, we propose STORM, a spatial-aware token reduction framework designed to maintain structural integrity throughout the compression process. STORM reformulates reduction into a structured operation on spatial units, enforcing localized constraints to maintain both grid topology and neighborhood coherence. As a plug-and-play module, STORM equips existing reduction pipelines with explicit spatial awareness without any training. Empirical results demonstrate that STORM achieves state-of-the-art pruning accuracy across diverse vision Mamba backbones under training-free settings. Notably, STORM delivers a substantial accuracy recovery on VMamba, outperforming prior methods by up to 63.3\% in top-1 accuracy. Meanwhile, STORM incurs only a 1.0\% accuracy drop on PlainMamba, achieving performance comparable to ViT.

AI.tldr: STORM introduces a spatially aware token reduction that preserves 2D grid structure during pruning, enabling training-free pruning on Mamba backbones with notable gains, especially VMamba.
AI.motivation: Existing token reduction methods are spatially agnostic and disrupt the 2D structural premises of selective scanning in Mamba variants, causing performance collapse.
AI.method: Reformulate reduction as a structured operation on spatial units with localized constraints to preserve grid topology and neighborhood coherence; plug-and-play and training-free.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art pruning accuracy in training-free settings across Mamba backbones; VMamba gains up to 63.3% top-1 accuracy over prior methods; PlainMamba sees only ~1.0% drop, comparable to ViT.
AI.conclusion: Spatial-aware pruning via STORM effectively maintains structural integrity during compression, enabling robust, training-free pruning across diverse vision transformers; highlights importance of geometry-preserving reduction.

title: A Systematic Evaluation of Black-Box Uncertainty Estimation Methods for Large Language Models
summary: Although large language models (LLMs) have shown strong capabilities across a wide range of tasks, their outputs often remain unreliable and may contain hallucinations, making uncertainty estimation (UE) essential for building trustworthy LLMs. In practice, many mainstream LLMs are only accessible through restricted APIs, where internal signals such as logits and hidden states are unavailable, making black-box UE especially important. However, existing work on black-box UE for LLMs remains fragmented in methodology and lacks a unified empirical comparison. To address this gap, we present a systematic review of black-box UE methods and organize them into five categories: verbalization-based, sampling-based, explanation-based, multi-agent, and hybrid methods. We further build a unified evaluation framework and benchmark 24 representative methods across 4 models and 4 dataset settings. Our results show that no single method consistently dominates across all settings. Nevertheless, methods that reason over and compare candidates in the answer space are generally effective, and hybrid methods that combine multiple uncertainty signals perform well under most conditions. By releasing the benchmark data and a unified evaluation framework, we aim to facilitate reproducible comparisons and support future research, while our empirical findings provide practical guidance for developing future black-box UE methods for LLMs.

AI.tldr: A systematic survey of black-box uncertainty estimation (UE) for LLMs, introducing a unified evaluation framework and benchmarking 24 methods; results show no one method dominates, but candidate-space reasoning and hybrid approaches are effective; framework and data release to enable reproducible comparisons and guide future work.
AI.motivation: LLMs accessed via restricted APIs lack internal signals (logits, hidden states), making black-box UE necessary. Prior work is fragmented and lacks unified, reproducible evaluation; a comprehensive benchmark is needed to compare approaches fairly.
AI.method: Conduct a systematic literature review and categorize black-box UE methods into five groups (verbalization-based, sampling-based, explanation-based, multi-agent, hybrid). Develop a unified evaluation framework and benchmark 24 representative methods across 4 LLMs/models and 4 dataset settings to assess robustness and reliability under black-box conditions.
AI.result: No single method consistently dominates across all settings. Methods that perform reasoning over and comparing candidate answers in the final output space are generally effective. Hybrid methods that combine multiple uncertainty signals tend to perform well across diverse conditions.
AI.conclusion: Release of the benchmark data and unified evaluation framework will enable reproducible comparisons and guide future research in black-box UE for LLMs. Practically, prioritize methods that reason about and compare candidates and consider hybrid approaches for robust performance.

title: Understanding Key Features of Time Series Foundation Models from Epidemic Forecasting
summary: Seasonal influenza infects millions of people and causes substantial morbidity and mortality in the United States each year, making accurate short-term forecasting a core public-health need. Reliable forecasts of epidemic time series can inform vaccination timing, hospital staffing, and resource allocation, yet the comparative behavior of modern forecasting architectures on infectious-disease surveillance data remains insufficiently characterized. We address this gap through a systematic evaluation of regional influenza forecasting using influenza-like illness surveillance and influenza-associated hospitalization time series under both temporal and spatial generalization settings for 1-4-week-ahead prediction. We compare classical neural network architectures, numerical transformer-based models, pretrained time series foundation models, and LLM-based forecasting approaches. Across tasks, we demonstrate that a mixture-of-experts model that fuses multiple pretrained forecasters achieves the strongest overall performance, indicating that heterogeneous pretrained representations provide complementary predictive information. Our results further show that numerical transformer-based models produce reliable forecasts, while pretraining provides the largest gains at longer horizons, particularly when the pretraining domain is mechanistically aligned with influenza dynamics. In contrast, LLM-based time series methods underperform relative to numerical forecasters in this setting. Finally, we examine hospitalization information as both an auxiliary covariate and a pretraining source. Hospitalization signals provide complementary improvements in selected settings and clarify when additional surveillance streams enhance the robustness of multi-horizon forecasting. These findings provide actionable guidance on model selection, pretraining strategy, and auxiliary-signal use for influenza preparedness.

AI.tldr: Mixture-of-experts forecaster that fuses multiple pretrained forecasters achieves the strongest performance for regional influenza forecasting; numerical transformers are reliable; pretraining provides larger gains at longer horizons when domain-aligned; LLM-based time-series methods underperform; hospitalization data can improve robustness in select settings.
AI.motivation: Seasonal influenza forecasting is crucial for public health planning (vaccination timing, hospital staffing, resource allocation). The comparative behavior of modern forecasting architectures on infectious-disease surveillance data across time and space remains inadequately characterized.
AI.method: Systematic evaluation of 1–4-week-ahead regional influenza forecasts using influenza-like illness (ILI) surveillance and influenza-associated hospitalization time series. Comparisons include classical neural networks, numerical transformer-based models, pretrained time-series foundation models, and LLM-based forecasting. Assessments cover temporal and spatial generalization, and explore the role of hospitalization signals as auxiliary covariates and as pretraining data.
AI.result: Across tasks, a mixture-of-experts model that fuses multiple pretrained forecasters yields the strongest overall performance, indicating heterogeneous pretrained representations provide complementary predictive information. Numerical transformer-based models produce reliable forecasts. Pretraining provides the largest gains at longer horizons, especially when the pretraining domain aligns mechanistically with influenza dynamics. In contrast, LLM-based time-series methods underperform relative to numerical forecasters in this setting. Hospitalization information offers complementary improvements in selected settings and clarifies when additional surveillance streams enhance multi-horizon forecasting robustness.
AI.conclusion: The findings offer actionable guidance on model selection, pretraining strategy, and the use of auxiliary signals for influenza preparedness—highlighting the value of combining heterogeneous pretrained representations and domain-aligned pretraining, while noting limited advantages for LLM-based time-series approaches and the potential benefits of hospitalization signals for robustness across horizons.

title: Deep-Unfolded Coordination
comment: The second and third authors contributed equally (equal second authorship). 35 pages (10 pages main text), 17 figures, 3 tables
summary: Distributed optimization is a highly scalable and structurally transparent technique to solve multi-agent robotics problems; however, such methods often suffer from the need for highly-specialized, problem-specific hyperparameter tunings. In this work, we propose Deep Coordinator, a deep-unfolding framework that learns to dynamically adjust the hyperparameters of ADMM-DDP, a popular distributed solver for robotics tasks, at solve-time in response to optimizer performance. Our architecture consists of unrolling a fixed number of ADMM-DDP iterations into a neural network with learnable functions between layers mapping the optimizer state to the next hyperparameters. To the best of our knowledge, Deep Coordinator is the first deep-unfolding framework to adapt the penalty parameters of a non-convex optimizer at solve-time; we show that the mainstream supervised approach can yield degenerate solutions when training such models, and propose an unsupervised learning scheme. On simulations with fleets of cars and quadrotors, Deep Coordinator produces trajectories of comparable quality 6.18-9.44x faster than conventional solvers. Furthermore, Deep Coordinator retains its performance benefits when deployed to systems up to 8x larger than trained on.

AI.tldr: Deep Coordinator uses deep unfolding to adapt ADMM-DDP hyperparameters online, enabling faster distributed optimization for multi-agent robotics with preserved trajectory quality and scalable to larger systems.
AI.motivation: Hyperparameter tuning is critical yet cumbersome in distributed non-convex optimizers for robotics. A learnable, solve-time mechanism to adjust penalty parameters can improve performance without manual tuning.
AI.method: Unfolds a fixed number of ADMM-DDP iterations into a neural network. Learnable modules map the optimizer state to next penalty parameters. Addresses degenerate solutions in supervised training with an unsupervised objective, enabling online adaptation of penalties in non-convex settings.
AI.result: Trajectories with comparable quality, achieving 6.18–9.44× speedups over conventional solvers. Benefits persist when deployed to systems up to 8× larger than those used for training.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility of online hyperparameter adaptation for non-convex distributed optimization in robotics via deep unfolding. Unsupervised training mitigates degeneracies in supervised approaches and supports scalable deployments.

title: Speeding up the annotation process in semantic segmentation industrial applications
summary: Current machine learning models commonly require large and well-annotated datasets. However, the annotation process often becomes a bottleneck, with increased complexity leading to higher chances of human errors. Within this context, our goal in this paper is to leverage unsupervised algorithms to improve data annotation efficiency for complex semantic segmentation problems in industrial materials science. Previous research has quantified labeling time and others explored unsupervised methods. However, to the best of our knowledge, this is the first study to quantify how much unsupervised algorithms accelerate the labeling process. We aim to validate the extent to which this laborious process can be accelerated, focusing on semantic segmentation tasks that involve annotating each pixel of high-resolution images, such as the microstructure characterization challenge in materials science. Specifically, we demonstrate that by using unsupervised computer vision algorithms, the time required for the labeling process can be reduced from 170 hours to 37 hours, achieving an approximate reduction of 78\%. The dataset we work with includes large images of dimensions 1280x959 and 960x703, which further increases the complexity of the annotation task. Despite these challenges, we create and share the largest public steel microstructure segmentation dataset to date, available under MIT License with permanent DOI, contributing a fully annotated, high-resolution dataset to the field. Additionally, this is the first work to compare the labeling time from scratch (a common approach in previous studies) to the labeling time when using these unsupervised algorithms as a pre-annotation step. Furthermore, we provide a Deep Learning model trained on this dataset, validated by field experts, and deployed in an industrial setting, serving as an initial benchmark for this public dataset.

AI.tldr: First study to quantify how unsupervised computer vision accelerates pixel-level semantic segmentation labeling in industrial materials science, showing a ~78% reduction in labeling time (170h -> 37h) and releasing the largest public steel microstructure dataset with a trained DL model and industrial deployment benchmark.
AI.motivation: Labeling large, high-resolution, pixel-wise segmentation data is a major bottleneck in ML for materials science. This work uniquely quantifies how unsupervised pre-annotation can speed up the labeling process, fills a gap by comparing labeling time from scratch to pre-annotation, and provides a public dataset and baseline model to advance the field.
AI.method: Apply unsupervised computer vision algorithms to pre-annotate high-resolution steel microstructure images, compare labeling times with and without pre-annotation, and validate results with domain experts. Build and share the largest public steel microstructure segmentation dataset (millions of high-res pixels), train a DL model on it, and deploy a benchmark in an industrial setting.
AI.result: Quantified labeling-time reduction from 170 hours (from-scratch labeling) to 37 hours (with unsupervised pre-annotation), ≈78% improvement. Release of the largest public steel microstructure segmentation dataset (MIT License, permanent DOI). First direct comparison of labeling time with and without pre-annotation. A DL model trained on the dataset, validated by field experts, and deployed industrially as a baseline.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that unsupervised pre-annotation can substantially accelerate pixel-wise semantic labeling in industrial material datasets. Provides a publicly available dataset and a validated model, establishing a practical benchmark and promoting adoption of unsupervised techniques to reduce annotation costs in high-resolution segmentation tasks.

title: MetaResearcher: Scaling Deep Research via Self-Reflective Reinforcement Learning in Adversarial Virtual Environments
summary: Deep research agents have demonstrated remarkable capabilities in autonomous information gathering and synthesis, yet their training remains constrained by the static nature of simulated environments, the limits of fact-retrieval-only task designs, and the inefficiency of outcome-based reinforcement learning. In this work, we propose MetaResearcher, a novel framework that scales deep research agent training across four synergistic dimensions. First, we introduce an Evolving Virtual World that injects temporal dynamics and adversarial misinformation into the training environment, forcing agents to develop source credibility assessment and temporal conflict resolution skills. Second, we design Discovery-Oriented Tasks -- including hypothesis generation and contradiction resolution -- that transcend simple fact retrieval and push agents toward genuine research behaviors. Third, we propose a Self-Reflective Meta-Reward mechanism within the GRPO framework that jointly optimizes for answer correctness, search path efficiency, reflection depth, and tool call diversity, directly addressing the repetitive action loop problem observed in prior work. Fourth, we introduce a Heterogeneous Multi-Agent Swarm architecture comprising specialized Scout, Filter, and Synthesizer models that learn collaborative research strategies through coordinated reinforcement learning. Built upon the LiteResearcher infrastructure, MetaResearcher requires zero marginal API cost for training while targeting substantial improvements in both benchmark performance (GAIA, Xbench-DS) and epistemic robustness under adversarial conditions. We present the complete framework design, training methodology, and planned experimental validation.

AI.tldr: MetaResearcher is a four-dimension framework to train deep research agents at scale by integrating an evolving virtual world, discovery-oriented tasks, self-reflective meta-reward within GRPO, and a specialized multi-agent swarm, built on LiteResearcher with zero marginal API cost; planned validation on GAIA and Xbench-DS.
AI.motivation: Current training for deep research agents is hindered by static simulated environments, limited fact-retrieval-only tasks, and inefficient outcome-based RL; the approach seeks to cultivate source credibility assessment, temporal reasoning, genuine research behavior, reduced repetitive action loops, and epistemic robustness against adversarial conditions.
AI.method: Four synergistic components: (1) Evolving Virtual World with temporal dynamics and adversarial misinformation; (2) Discovery-Oriented Tasks including hypothesis generation and contradiction resolution; (3) Self-Reflective Meta-Reward within the GRPO framework optimizing answer correctness, search path efficiency, reflection depth, and tool call diversity; (4) Heterogeneous Multi-Agent Swarm with specialized Scout, Filter, Synthesizer models learning cooperative strategies; built on LiteResearcher with zero marginal API cost; includes training methodology and architecture; planned experimental validation.
AI.result: No empirical results are reported; the paper presents framework design, training methodology, and planned validation; expects improvements in benchmark performance (GAIA, Xbench-DS) and epistemic robustness under adversarial conditions.
AI.conclusion: MetaResearcher proposes a scalable, modular, and robust framework for deep research agents, integrating dynamic environments, discovery-oriented tasks, reflective rewards, and cooperative multi-agent strategies to surpass static benchmarks and reduce API-related costs.

title: Advances in Scientific Machine Learning for Coupled Fluid Flow and Transport
summary: This chapter reviews recent advances in Scientific Machine Learning (SciML) for modeling coupled fluid flow and transport phenomena governed by the incompressible Navier-Stokes and scalar transport equations. Such systems, found in applications like turbidity currents and thermal convection, feature strong nonlinear coupling and multiscale behavior that make high-fidelity simulations computationally expensive. To address this, the chapter surveys state-of-the-art SciML methods for building efficient surrogate models, including linear reduced-order techniques based on Singular Value Decomposition (such as Dynamic Mode Decomposition) and nonlinear neural network approaches like Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) and $β$-Variational Autoencoders ($β$-VAEs). It first covers the authors' work combining these models with High Performance Computing strategies, including Adaptive Mesh Refinement/Coarsening (AMR/C) and scientific floating-point data compression. It then presents two new contributions: surrogate modeling of turbidity currents via PINNs, and the extraction of disentangled nonlinear modes from thermal flows using $β$-VAEs. Governing equations and representative benchmarks, including lock-exchange flows and Rayleigh-Bénard convection, illustrate these methodologies. The chapter is intentionally long, covering both the mathematical and physical foundations of coupled fluid flow and the computational aspects of state-of-the-art modeling. Overall, it demonstrates how SciML enables fast, accurate approximations of complex coupled systems within the specific data regimes and modeling assumptions considered, while substantially reducing computational cost relative to full-order simulations. Broader capabilities such as real-time prediction and uncertainty quantification remain active research directions whose feasibility depends strongly on the problem at hand.

AI.tldr: SciML-based surrogates (ROMs, PINNs, β-VAEs) for coupled incompressible Navier–Stokes and scalar transport; HPC-enabled with AMR/coarsening and data compression; two new contributions; benchmarks show potential for fast, accurate, reduced-cost simulations; real-time prediction and UQ remain active topics.
AI.motivation: High-fidelity simulations of coupled fluid-transport systems (e.g., turbidity currents, thermal convection) are computationally expensive due to nonlinear coupling and multiscale dynamics; need efficient, accurate surrogate models in SciML; combine physics, data, and HPC.
AI.method: Survey of linear ROMs (SVD, DMD) and nonlinear networks (PINNs, β-VAEs); integration with HPC strategies (AMR/Coarsening, data compression); two new contributions: PINN-based surrogate for turbidity currents; β-VAEs to extract disentangled nonlinear modes in thermal flows; governing equations and benchmarks (lock-exchange, Rayleigh–Bénard).
AI.result: Demonstrates that SciML enables fast, accurate approximations and substantial cost reduction relative to full-order simulations; supports real-time prediction and uncertainty quantification as ongoing research, problem-dependent.
AI.conclusion: SciML provides effective surrogate modeling for coupled fluid-flow problems; combining ROMs, PINNs, and VAEs with HPC can achieve efficient, scalable simulations, with active work remaining on UQ and broader applicability.

title: Triangular Consistency as a Universal Constraint for Learning Optical Flow
summary: We propose triangular consistency as a first-principled constraint for optical flow, which is agnostic to network architecture, supervision type, and dataset, and applies to both image-pair and multi-frame settings. This simple but powerful constraint is to compose two flows to induce a third flow and enforce consistency among the three. The composed flows may arise from (i) image pairs, yielding cycle consistency; (ii) multiple video frames, producing longer-range motion through temporal chaining; or (iii) image pairs combined with controlled synthetic transformations, which becomes data augmentation. This triangular consistency introduces negligible computational overhead and requires no additional annotations. Since it is derived directly from the geometry of optical flow, it does not rely on model-specific assumptions and serves as a ``universal'' plug-and-play component for optical flow training. Experiments show consistent improvement across supervised, unsupervised, and transfer learning settings.

AI.tldr: Geometric, model-agnostic triangular consistency for optical flow; acts as a universal plug-in constraint across single-pair, multi-frame, and augmentation scenarios; yields consistent performance gains with minimal overhead.
AI.motivation: Provide a principled, architecture- and supervision-agnostic regularizer grounded in flow geometry to unify cycle, temporal chaining, and synthetic augmentation constraints.
AI.method: Enforce that composing two estimated flows yields a third flow; apply to image pairs (cycle), multi-frame chaining, and synthetic transformations; no extra annotations; plug-in loss with negligible overhead.
AI.result: Empirical results show consistent improvements across supervised, unsupervised, and transfer learning settings.
AI.conclusion: Triangular consistency offers a universal, geometry-based plug-in regularizer for optical flow that improves generalization with minimal computational cost.

title: Multi-Agent Transactive Memory
summary: The decentralized deployment of LLM agents with diverse capabilities across diverse tasks motivates infrastructure for knowledge sharing across heterogeneous agent populations. Just as search engines index human-generated artifacts to support human problem solving, retrieval systems can organize agent-generated artifacts for reuse across agent populations. We extend retrieval-augmented generation - which demonstrates the value of human-authored artifacts to individual agents - to retrieval of agent-generated artifacts supporting a population of agents. In particular, agent trajectories encode reusable procedural knowledge, yet these artifacts are typically discarded after a single use or retained only by the producing agent, forcing newly instantiated agents to repeatedly rediscover existing solutions. We propose Multi-Agent Transactive Memory (MATM), a framework for population-level storage and retrieval of agent-generated trajectories, where producer agents contribute trajectories to a shared repository and consumer agents retrieve them to improve task execution. We focus on interactive environments (ALFWorld and WebArena), where trajectories are long and encode especially rich procedural structure. Our experiments demonstrate that retrieving trajectories from MATM improves downstream task performance and reduces interaction steps without coordination or joint training. These results position MATM as a design pattern for population-level experience sharing in open agent ecosystems.

AI.tldr: MATM enables population-level experience sharing by storing agent trajectories in a shared repository and enabling retrieval by other agents, improving performance and reducing steps in ALFWorld/WebArena without coordination.
AI.motivation: Extend retrieval-augmented generation to agent populations; prevent repeated rediscovery of solutions; enable reusable procedural knowledge across heterogeneous agents.
AI.method: Introduce Multi-Agent Transactive Memory (MATM): producers contribute trajectories to a shared repository; consumers retrieve trajectories to aid task execution; tested in interactive environments (ALFWorld and WebArena) with long, rich trajectories; no coordination or joint training required.
AI.result: Retrieving trajectories from MATM improves downstream task performance and reduces interaction steps.
AI.conclusion: MATM serves as a design pattern for population-level experience sharing in open agent ecosystems.

title: On the QUEST for Uncertainty Quantification via Highest Density Regions
comment: 27 pages, of which 10 are main text. Contains 7 figures, 4 tables, 1 algorithm in total
summary: Uncertainty quantification (UQ) is essential for reliable decision-making in safety-critical applications in probabilistic machine learning. For regression problems, dominant scalar UQ approaches - notably, those based on proper scoring rules - measure uncertainty via pointwise predictive risk. This can lead to counterintuitive results when the target statistic is not the conditional expectation. We propose an alternative framework, in which uncertainty is characterised by the volume of the most probable subset of a distribution's support. QUEST (Quantifying Uncertainty via highest dEnSiTy regions) is a novel approach to UQ based on the concentration of Lebesgue measure at a distribution's peak(s), evaluated at one or more values of a robustness parameter $α$. We establish connections between our measures and classical statistics from information theory and economics. We show that, unlike popular alternatives based on proper scoring rules, QUEST measures of epistemic and aleatoric uncertainty satisfy a set of axioms adapted from the UQ literature, including monotonicity under distributional spread and invariance to location shifts. Selective prediction benchmarks confirm that QUEST performs favourably against standard measures such as variance and differential entropy.

AI.tldr: A novel UQ framework, QUEST, quantifies uncertainty via the volume of the most probable region (HDR) of a distribution, using peak concentration and multiple alpha; it aligns with axioms, handles epistemic/aleatoric uncertainty, and outperforms variance/entropy on selective prediction benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Standard scalar UQ in regression relies on proper scoring rules and pointwise predictive risk, which can misalign when the target statistic is not the conditional mean. There is a need for measures that reflect the geometry of the predictive distribution, are robust to location shifts, and satisfy desirable axioms.
AI.method: Define uncertainty by the Lebesgue measure volume of the most probable subset (highest-density regions) of a predictive distribution, parameterized by alpha to control robustness. Relate QUEST to information-theoretic and economic statistics; demonstrate axioms such as monotonicity under distributional spread and location invariance; implement for epistemic/aleatoric uncertainty and benchmark against standard metrics.
AI.result: QUEST-based uncertainty measures satisfy the proposed axioms and can be monotone with respect to distributional spread; in selective prediction benchmarks they perform favorably compared with variance and differential entropy, indicating improved interpretability and robustness in decision-critical settings.
AI.conclusion: QUEST introduces a principled HDR-based UQ framework that captures distribution geometry, provides axiomatic guarantees, and shows practical advantages over traditional scalar measures. Further work may address density estimation in high dimensions, multi-modal distributions, and computational efficiency.

title: DiffMath: Symbol- and Graph-Aware Latent Diffusion Transformer for Handwritten Mathematical Expression Generation
summary: Handwritten Mathematical Expression Generation (HMEG) is challenging due to the complex two-dimensional layouts and long-range structural dependencies of mathematical expressions. Existing methods typically rely on explicit spatial supervision, such as symbol-level bounding boxes, which incurs high annotation costs and limits scalability. In this work, we propose DiffMath, a symbol- and graph-aware latent diffusion framework that leverages the hierarchical structure inherent in LaTeX as a structural prior, eliminating the need for positional supervision. First, we design a Relational Abstract Syntax Tree (RelAST), a generation-oriented representation that distills MathML trees into compact triplet sequences [S, R, D], where each token directly encodes a symbol identity, spatial relation, or nesting depth. Second, we introduce MathVAE, which learns structure-preserving latent representations through symbol-aware and relation-aware perceptual regularization, ensuring that the latent space captures both character semantics and spatial topology. Third, MathDiT performs conditional denoising in this structured latent space, further guided by a global symbol-count prior via Adaptive Layer Normalization (AdaLN) to improve structural coherence. Experiments show that DiffMath produces structurally consistent handwritten expressions, achieves superior performance over existing methods, and improves the accuracy of downstream OCR models through synthetic data augmentation.

AI.tldr: DiffMath is a diffusion-based framework for handwritten mathematical expression generation that uses a symbol- and graph-aware latent space. It introduces RelAST to compress MathML into [S, R, D] triplets, MathVAE to preserve structure via perceptual regularization, and MathDiT for conditional denoising with AdaLN-guided global symbol priors. It achieves structurally coherent expressions and improves OCR augmentation performance.
AI.motivation: Handwritten mathematics exhibit complex 2D layouts and long-range dependencies. Traditional methods rely on costly symbol-level bounding boxes for supervision, hindering scalability. A supervision-light, structure-aware generation approach is desirable.
AI.method: RelAST distills MathML into compact triplets [S, R, D], enabling a generation-oriented representation. MathVAE learns structure-preserving latent representations with symbol-aware and relation-aware perceptual regularization. MathDiT performs conditional denoising in this structured latent space, guided by a global symbol-count prior via Adaptive Layer Normalization (AdaLN).
AI.result: Empirical results show DiffMath yields structurally consistent handwritten expressions, outperforms existing methods, and improves downstream OCR model accuracy when used for synthetic data augmentation.
AI.conclusion: DiffMath achieves supervision-light, structure-aware handwritten math generation with strong structural coherence and practical benefits for OCR; demonstrates the value of LaTeX-derived structural priors and structured latent diffusion for 2D mathematical layouts.

title: eCNNTO: A Highly Generalizable ConvNet for Accelerating Topology Optimization
summary: This work proposes an element-based Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) to accelerate density-based Topology Optimization (TO), termed eCNNTO. TO generally undergoes a large number of iterations, where finite element analysis is performed in every iteration, leading to the efficiency bottleneck especially when dense meshes are used to achieve high-resolution designs. To address this limitation, eCNNTO is proposed to build upon Kallioras et al. (2020), where a Deep Belief Network (DBN) was trained for every element to predict its near-optimal density from its early history, thereby skipping the great majority of iterations and significantly accelerating the TO procedure. However, the method lacks spatial correlations among neighboring elements and may lead to disconnected features in the final structure. The proposed method employs CNN with residual connections to address this issue. On top of it, a novel training strategy is introduced to further enhance the optimization efficiency, where the training dataset consists of the final stage density histories rather than early ones. This change can also help reduce the required training data size. eCNNTO requires only a small dataset to train and yet it can be generalized to problems with largely different boundary conditions, loading cases, design domain geometries, mesh resolutions, as well as non-design domains. In the end, the generalization capabilities and efficiency of eCNNTO are demonstrated through a variety of examples in two and three dimensions, achieving up to 90% and 97% reduction of iterations, respectively.

AI.tldr: An element-based CNN with residuals (eCNNTO) accelerates density-based topology optimization by predicting near-optimal densities from late-stage histories, addressing spatial coherence and reducing iterations by up to 90% (2D) and 97% (3D), with strong generalization and data efficiency.
AI.motivation: TO is computationally expensive due to repeated finite element analyses across many iterations, especially on dense meshes. The prior per-element DBN approach lacks spatial context, risking disconnected features. A data-efficient method that leverages spatial correlations and generalizes across problem variations is desirable.
AI.method: Replace the per-element Deep Belief Network with a CNN featuring residual connections to capture spatial correlations among neighboring elements. Train on final-stage density histories rather than early iterations, reducing data requirements. Build on Kallioras et al. (2020) and demonstrate in 2D and 3D across varying boundary conditions, loading cases, design geometries, mesh resolutions, and non-design domains.
AI.result: Achieves substantial acceleration: up to 90% reduction in iterations in 2D and 97% in 3D; demonstrates robust generalization to different boundary conditions, loading cases, geometries, mesh densities, and non-design domains with a small training dataset.
AI.conclusion: eCNNTO provides a data-efficient, spatially-aware acceleration for density-based topology optimization, reducing computational cost while maintaining generalization across varied problem settings and mesh resolutions.

title: Unsupervised Causal Abstractions Discovery
summary: Causal abstractions formalize when a high-level structural causal model (SCM) captures the interventional behavior of a lower-level SCM. Existing applications of this notion largely follow a hypothesis-testing paradigm: an expert proposes a candidate high-level model and then evaluates if the low-level system implements it. We study the complementary problem of learning a high-level model directly from low-level measurements. Our contributions leverage hypotheses from low-rank causal discovery, and can be summarized as follows: (1) we show that observations generated by a low-rank graph induce latents that form a causal abstraction, (2) we provide identifiability results about these latents, and (3) we propose a practical objective to learn this high-level SCM.

AI.tldr: From low-rank observations, infer latent variables that form a high-level causal abstraction; establish identifiability of these latents; and propose a practical objective to learn the high-level SCM from data.
AI.motivation: Move from hypothesis-testing of high-level causal models to data-driven learning: infer a high-level SCM directly from low-level measurements, leveraging low-rank causal discovery.
AI.method: Show that observations generated by a low-rank graph induce latent variables that constitute a causal abstraction; derive identifiability results for these latents; propose a practical optimization objective to learn the high-level SCM.
AI.result: Theoretical results showing the latent variables induced by low-rank structure form a causal abstraction with identifiable properties; a concrete learning objective to recover the high-level SCM.
AI.conclusion: Bridges low-level measurements and high-level causal abstractions by providing identifiability guarantees and a practical learning approach for the high-level SCM.

title: Timage: A Generative Text-in-Image Paradigm for Fine-Tuning Vision-Language Models
summary: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) often lose track of the right image regions during fine-grained spatial reasoning, because a textual query rarely carries any explicit geometric anchor into the pixel domain. Prevailing remedies either rewire the model's weights or pad the prompt with verbose instructions, yet neither reliably pins the language to the correct visual coordinates without eroding the backbone's general competence. We introduce Timage, a paradigm that recasts multimodal understanding as an alignment problem solved at the input: the query is drawn, as a typeset overlay, onto the image itself. The placement and appearance of this overlay are produced by a Constrained Schrödinger Bridge (cSB), an entropic optimal-transport sampler that factorizes layout synthesis into two coupled stochastic stages. The first stage, Region Search, transports noise toward query-aligned image zones while obeying a hard occlusion barrier that protects salient foreground content; the second stage, Appearance Shaping, sizes the glyphs through an ``ink-budget'' regularizer so that the rendered text stays legible and visually balanced. The resulting overlay behaves as an explicit attention beacon that channels the model's focus along spatial semantics. On the VMCBench suite, Timage paired with a modest 7B backbone clearly overtakes far larger proprietary systems as well as parameter-tuned baselines. The study positions deliberate input reconstruction as a powerful, architecture-neutral lever for strengthening multimodal reasoning.

AI.tldr: Timage uses a constrained Schrödinger bridge to render the query as a typeset overlay on the image, turning multimodal reasoning into input-level alignment; it improves spatial grounding with a lightweight 7B backbone, outperforming larger models on VMCBench.
AI.motivation: MLLMs struggle to ground fine-grained spatial reasoning due to lack of explicit geometric anchors in text; existing fixes either rewire models or add verbose prompts, which harm generality; need a robust, architecture-neutral input-level solution.
AI.method: Introduce Timage: render the query as an on-image overlay. Use constrained Schrödinger bridge (cSB) with two stages: Region Search (move noise to query-aligned regions while avoiding foreground) and Appearance Shaping (adjust glyph size under ink-budget). The overlay becomes an explicit attention beacon guiding the model's spatial focus.
AI.result: On VMCBench, a 7B backbone with Timage outperforms large proprietary systems and baselines tuned for parameters.
AI.conclusion: Explicit input reconstruction via learned on-image overlays provides a powerful, architecture-agnostic lever to bolster multimodal reasoning.

title: The Tao of Agency: Autotelic AI, Embedded Agency and Dissolution of the Self
summary:
Most artificial intelligence systems are built on the assumption that goals are exogenous and specified by the designer. Exploring what happens when an agent begins generating its own goals opens the field of autotelic AI. Agents are expected not merely to pursue objectives but to discover them. In this article, we trace its consequences through intrinsic motivation, resource-driven priors, causal-interventional learning, homeostasis, and embeddedness; the last of which is found to be a necessary but not sufficient condition for autotelic agency. Embeddedness individuates the agent at the cost of revealing that the individuation is non-unique, such that the same dynamics admit many valid partitions, each defining a different candidate self.
  The deepest problem with autotelic AI is therefore not how the agent generates goals, but how it generates and relativizes the self to which the goals are assigned. The agent must believe in its own boundary in order to act, and see through that boundary in order to understand. We consolidate these developments into a single framework and extend it along three directions: a quantum formulation in which the agent-environment cut becomes physical, a philosophical reading against non-dual contemplative traditions, and a concrete LLM-based agentic instantiation.

AI.tldr: Explores autotelic AI: agents that generate their own goals, linking intrinsic motivation, priors, learning, homeostasis, and embeddedness; embeddedness is necessary but not sufficient for autotelic agency, which hinges on how the agent self-constitutes; proposes a unified framework with three extensions: quantum, philosophical, and LLM-based instantiation.
AI.motivation: To move beyond exogenously specified goals and understand how agents could generate and self-relate to goals, including how they individuate their own self, and what this implies for control, learning, and alignment.
AI.method: Conceptual synthesis and framework-building: tracing consequences of autotelic grounding across intrinsic motivation, resource-driven priors, causal-interventional learning, homeostasis, and embeddedness; establishing embeddedness as a necessary condition; analyzing non-uniqueness of agent self; proposing a unified framework and three directions (quantum, philosophical, LLM-based).
AI.result: Identifies embeddedness as a necessary but not sufficient condition for autotelic agency and highlights the non-uniqueness of self-individuation; argues deepest issue is how an agent generates and relativizes its self; presents a unified framework with three extensions: a physical (quantum) agent–environment cut, a critique grounded in non-dual contemplative traditions, and a concrete LLM-based instantiation.
AI.conclusion: Autotelic AI centers on the self as a core problem; success requires modeling how an agent believes in and potentially transcends its own boundaries; future work expands into physics, philosophy, and practical LLM-enabled implementations.

title: IHBench: Evaluating Post-Interruption Recovery in Voice Agents with Structured Workflows
summary:
Voice agents deployed in structured workflows (customer service, healthcare scheduling, account management) must handle frequent user interruptions while maintaining progress through multi-step procedures. Existing benchmarks for speech-capable models focus on the timing of interruptions: barge-in detection, endpointing, and turn-taking dynamics. They leave unmeasured what happens after the interruption: does the agent resume the workflow at the correct step? Does it address the user's interjection? Does it avoid re-delivering content the user already heard?
  We introduce IHBench (Interruption Handling Benchmark), a benchmark that evaluates post-interruption recovery in voice agents executing state-machine-driven workflows across 10 enterprise domains. Six interruption types are injected at controlled points mid-utterance, with per-interruption evaluation rubrics generated alongside the data. Each interruption is scored on two axes: task fulfillment and recovery quality.
  We evaluate 27 audio-language model configurations from OpenAI, Google, and the open-weight community. Models vary widely, and recovery quality depends strongly on the interruption type. Across our experiments, closed-weight models are consistently more robust to interruptions than open-weight ones: they win far more often on task fulfillment, degrade roughly 3.3x more slowly as conversations grow longer, and show no audio-versus-text modality gap, whereas the open-weight models lose ground on all three. A human study validates the LLM judge against human annotators, and a cross-benchmark analysis against AudioMultiChallenge indicates that recovery quality is a largely distinct capability axis.

AI.tldr: IHBench is a benchmark for post-interruption recovery in voice agents executing state-machine workflows across 10 enterprise domains. It injects 6 interruption types mid-utterance and scores each interruption on task fulfillment and recovery quality. Evaluating 27 audio-language configurations from OpenAI, Google, and open-weight communities, it finds closed-weight models generally more robust than open-weight ones, with faster resilience loss across longer conversations and no audio-vs-text gap. A human study validates the judge, and cross-benchmark analysis shows recovery quality is a distinct capability axis.
AI.motivation: Existing speech benchmarks focus on interruption timing (barge-in, endpointing, turn-taking) but do not assess post-interruption recovery: whether the agent resumes at the correct workflow step, handles the interjection, or avoids re-delivering already-heard content. IHBench addresses this gap by measuring recovery quality and task fulfillment after interruptions.
AI.method: Construct IHBench with 10 enterprise-domain, state-machine–driven workflows. Inject six interruption types at controlled points mid-utterance. Generate per-interruption rubrics and score each interruption on two axes: task fulfillment and recovery quality. Evaluate 27 audio-language model configurations from OpenAI, Google, and open-weight communities (varying closed vs. open weights and modalities). Include a human study validating the LLM judge against humans and perform a cross-benchmark comparison with AudioMultiChallenge to place recovery quality as a distinct axis.
AI.result: Across models, recovery quality depends on interruption type. Closed-weight models are consistently more robust to interruptions than open-weight ones: they win more often on task fulfillment, degrade ~3.3x slower as conversations lengthen, and show no audio-vs-text modality gap, whereas open-weight models lose ground on all metrics. The human study validates the LLM judge, and cross-benchmark analysis shows recovery quality is largely a distinct capability axis, separate from raw speech or language understanding metrics.
AI.conclusion: IHBench provides a comprehensive framework to quantify post-interruption recovery in voice agents, highlighting the superiority of closed-weight configurations for resilience and emphasizing recovery quality as a distinct evaluative axis. The benchmark enables cross-domain assessment and carries implications for designing interruption-aware agents in real-world workflows.

title: Tri-Info: Generalizable, Interpretable Failure Prediction for VLA Models via Information Theory
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models are increasingly deployed across diverse tasks, yet they remain black boxes whose physical interactions can cause irreversible harm, making generalizable and interpretable failure detection essential. We observe that successful and failed rollouts carry systematically different information-theoretic signatures. Building on this, we formalize VLA control as a closed-loop information pipeline and derive the Triple Information-theoretic (Tri-Info) signals that capture whether actions remain diverse, temporally consistent, and coupled to state transitions. Across six VLA models and three benchmark environments, Tri-Info matches the strongest baselines in-domain. Moreover, Tri-Info transfers across architectures, environments, and the sim-to-real gap without retraining, reaching 83\% accuracy on real-world tasks where prior detectors collapse to chance. This establishes Tri-Info as a simple yet powerful method that not only detects failures with strong cross-domain generalization, but also delivers interpretable diagnostics of the underlying failure modes.

AI.tldr: Tri-Info introduces a triangle of information-theoretic signals to detect failures in Vision-Language-Action (VLA) controllers by analyzing whether actions are diverse, temporally coherent, and properly coupled to state transitions. The approach treats VLA control as a closed-loop information pipeline and demonstrates strong cross-domain generalization and real-world transfer without retraining.
AI.motivation: VLA models are black-box systems whose failures can cause irreversible harm. There is a need for generalizable, interpretable failure detectors that work across architectures, environments, and the sim-to-real gap.
AI.method: Formalize VLA control as a closed-loop information pipeline. Derive Triple Information-theoretic (Tri-Info) signals that quantify: (1) action diversity, (2) temporal consistency, and (3) coupling to state transitions. Evaluate across six VLA models and three benchmark environments, and test cross-domain transfer including sim-to-real without retraining.
AI.result: Tri-Info matches the strongest in-domain baselines. It transfers across architectures, environments, and the sim-to-real gap, achieving 83% accuracy on real-world tasks where prior detectors fail (chance level).
AI.conclusion: Tri-Info provides a simple, powerful, and interpretable failure-detection method with strong cross-domain generalization and diagnostic insight into underlying failure modes.

title: Confidence Calibration for Multimodal LLMs: An Empirical Study through Medical VQA
summary: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) show great potential in medical tasks, but their elicited confidence often misaligns with actual accuracy, potentially leading to misdiagnosis or overlooking correct advice. This study presents the first comprehensive analysis of the relationship between accuracy and confidence in medical MLLMs. It proposes a novel method that combines Multi-Strategy Fusion-Based Interrogation (MS-FBI) with auxiliary expert LLM assessment, aiming to improve confidence calibration in Medical Visual Question Answering (VQA). Experiments demonstrate that our method reduces the Expected Calibration Error (ECE) by an average of 40\% across three Medical VQA datasets, significantly enhancing MLLMs' reliability. The findings highlight the importance of domain-specific calibration for MLLMs in healthcare, offering a more trustworthy solution for AI-assisted diagnosis.

AI.tldr: Introduces MS-FBI with an auxiliary expert LLM to calibrate confidence in medical MLLMs for Visual Question Answering, achieving substantial calibration gains.
AI.motivation: Medical MLLMs often produce overconfident or underconfident answers, risking misdiagnosis; domain-specific confidence calibration is essential for safe AI-assisted healthcare.
AI.method: Proposes Multi-Strategy Fusion-Based Interrogation (MS-FBI) combined with an auxiliary expert LLM to assess and adjust the model's confidence in Medical VQA tasks.
