AI.result: Experimental evaluation on three Medical VQA datasets shows the approach reduces Expected Calibration Error (ECE) by an average of 40%, improving reliability of medical MLLMs.
AI.conclusion: Domain-specific calibration is crucial for trustworthy AI in healthcare; the MS-FBI + expert assessment approach offers a viable path to safer AI-assisted diagnosis.

title: PhysDrift: Bridging the Embodiment Gap in Humanoid Co-Speech Motion Generation
summary: Humanoid robots require co-speech motions that are not only expressive and speech-aligned, but also physically executable under embodiment constraints. Existing co-speech generation pipelines are predominantly human-centric: motions are first generated in human-body representations such as SMPL-X and subsequently retargeted to humanoid robots. In this work, we identify a fundamental embodiment gap in this paradigm, where the mismatch between human motion manifolds and humanoid embodiment constraints disrupts embodiment consistency during motion transfer and physical execution. Through extensive analysis, we show that although retargeting can preserve coarse motion semantics, it significantly compresses motion diversity and weakens prosody-motion synchronization, limiting expressive humanoid behaviors. To address this problem, we first propose IK-EER, a prosody-preserving humanoid motion curation framework that jointly optimizes kinematic feasibility and speech-motion temporal alignment during retargeting. Building upon the curated robot-native motion dataset, we further introduce PhysDrift, an embodiment-aware co-speech motion generation framework that directly predicts executable humanoid joint trajectories from speech without relying on intermediate human-body representations. Unlike conventional human-centric pipelines, PhysDrift maintains embodiment consistency throughout both training and inference while incorporating physical regularization to stabilize robot motion dynamics. Extensive experiments and real-world humanoid deployment demonstrate that embodiment-aware robot-native generation substantially improves speech-motion alignment, physical plausibility, motion smoothness, inference efficiency, and real-time interaction capability.

AI.tldr: We present IK-EER and PhysDrift to overcome embodiment gaps in co-speech motion generation for humanoids by shifting from human-centered retargeting to robot-native prediction, achieving better alignment, plausibility, and real-time capability.
AI.motivation: Existing pipelines rely on human representations (e.g., SMPL-X) and retargeting to humanoid robots. This creates an embodiment gap where human motion manifolds clash with robot constraints, reducing motion diversity and prosody-motion synchronization; a need exists for executable, embodiment-consistent co-speech motions.
AI.method: IK-EER: joint optimization of kinematic feasibility and speech-motion temporal alignment during retargeting; curated robot-native motion dataset. PhysDrift: end-to-end predictor of executable humanoid joint trajectories from speech, with physical regularization; maintains embodiment consistency during training and inference, avoiding intermediate human representations.
AI.result: Extensive experiments and real-world humanoid deployment show improved speech-motion alignment, physical plausibility, motion smoothness, inference efficiency, and real-time interaction capability.
AI.conclusion: Embodiment-aware, robot-native generation substantially outperforms traditional human-centric pipelines by preserving embodiment consistency and enabling robust, executable co-speech humanoid motions.

title: Comparing Linear Probes with Mahalanobis Cosine Similarity
summary: Linear probes are widely used in interpretability research and often compared by cosine similarity. The Mahalanobis cosine similarity (MCS) between two directions, which reweights the inner product by test data covariance, is a natural task-aware refinement. Ying et al. (2026) report that a probe's MCS to a reference probe trained on the out-of-distribution (OOD) data near-perfectly linearly predicts the probe's OOD AUROC (R^2 = 0.98). Here, we extend this empirical finding across models, layers, and concept domains, and prove this general phenomenon in closed form: For balanced classes whose projections are Gaussian, OOD AUROC and MCS to the reference probe are linear because both are sigmoid-shaped functions of the probe's signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) on the test data. The theory also predicts when this linearity fails, which we verify empirically. MCS offers a theoretically grounded and empirically effective alternative to Euclidean cosine similarity for comparing linear probes.

AI.tldr: MCS introduces a task-aware refinement for comparing linear probes by reweighting the inner product with test-data covariance; under Gaussian, balanced projections, both OOD AUROC and MCS are sigmoid functions of the probe's SNR, making their relationship linear and highly predictive (R^2≈0.98 in prior work). The paper provides a closed-form proof, identifies when linearity fails, and positions MCS as a principled alternative to Euclidean cosine similarity for probe evaluation.
AI.motivation: Address how to compare linear probes in interpretability research in a way that reflects task structure and data distribution. Extend observed empirical link between probe similarity and OOD performance to a general, theoretically grounded framework, across models, layers, and concept domains.
AI.method: Theoretical derivation under balanced Gaussian projections showing that OOD AUROC and MCS are sigmoid functions of the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR). Proves linearity of these metrics as a function of SNR and identifies the conditions under which linearity breaks. Empirical validation across models, layers, and concept domains to confirm the theory and compare MCS to standard cosine similarity.
AI.result: Demonstrates that MCS is linearly related to OOD AUROC across settings due to the shared sigmoid dependence on SNR, with high predictive power (R^2≈0.98 in prior report). Provides closed-form justification and practical guidance on when the linear relation holds or fails. Positions MCS as a theoretically grounded, empirically effective alternative to Euclidean cosine for comparing linear probes.
AI.conclusion: MCS offers a principled, robust metric for evaluating linear probes, capturing task-aware relationships via data covariance. Under specified assumptions (balanced classes, Gaussian projections), the relationship with OOD performance is linear and predictable, and the framework yields guidance on its applicability and limitations.

title: A Neuromorphic Reinforcement Learning Framework for Efficient Pathfinding in Robotic Mobile Fulfillment Systems
summary: Dynamic environmental changes, confined workspaces, and stringent real-time constraints make pathfinding in Robotic Mobile Fulfillment Systems (RMFS) a challenging problem for conventional search- and rule-based methods, which typically suffer from high computational complexity and long decision latency. While reinforcement learning (RL) has emerged as a powerful alternative, deploying learned policies with extreme energy efficiency on resource-constrained hardware remains an open challenge. We present SDQN-RMFS, an end-to-end framework that achieves high-fidelity deployment of an RL-trained policy from a full-precision artificial neural network (ANN) through to a neuromorphic chip. By computing only when triggered by sparse events, this framework unlocks ultra-low-power RMFS pathfinding. Our full-stack pipeline operates as follows: an ANN policy is first efficiently trained via a collision-allowing strategy to densify informative trajectories, and then converted into a spiking neural network (SNN) via a hard-label knowledge distillation approach. This effectively addresses the output distribution mismatch, preserving policy capability across the ANN-to-SNN pipeline while substantially reducing inference latency. Hardware experiments demonstrate up to 11,281$\times$ energy savings and a nearly two-fold reduction in latency compared to a high-performance GPU baseline, while maintaining decision quality on par with the original trained policy. These results establish physical neuromorphic inference as a practical and energy-sustainable pathway for large-scale RMFS operations.

AI.tldr: End-to-end neuromorphic deployment of an RL policy for RMFS pathfinding, achieving massive energy savings and latency reduction via ANN-to-SNN conversion and sparse-event inference.
AI.motivation: Dynamic RMFS pathfinding on resource-constrained hardware requires ultra-low-power, real-time decision-making; a neuromorphic approach promises substantial energy and latency improvements while preserving policy quality.
AI.method: Train an ANN policy using a collision-allowing strategy to densify informative trajectories, convert to a spiking neural network (SNN) via hard-label knowledge distillation to align output distributions, and deploy on neuromorphic hardware with event-driven, sparse computation.
AI.result: Hardware experiments report up to 11,281× energy savings and about a two-fold latency decrease compared to a high-performance GPU baseline, with decision quality on par with the original ANN policy.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that physical neuromorphic inference is a practical and energy-sustainable pathway for large-scale RMFS operations, enabling high-fidelity RL policies to run efficiently on constrained hardware.

title: SketchKeyAnime: Reference-anchored Sparse Key-Sketch Animation Synthesis
summary: Traditional animation production relies heavily on manual drawing and iterative refinement, particularly for key-pose design, in-betweening, and character coloring. While existing animation and video generation methods have made notable progress, they typically depend on RGB boundary frames, dense frame-wise conditions, or complete sketch sequences, limiting their applicability under low-cost input conditions. We present SketchKeyAnime, a video diffusion framework for generating structurally controllable, appearance-consistent, and temporally coherent animations from sparse key-sketch inputs. Given a single reference RGB image and a few temporally indexed key sketches, SketchKeyAnime introduces a dual-branch conditioning mechanism to encode local geometric constraints alongside semantic-temporal context. It leverages Sketch Cross Attention to fuse reference image and sketch conditions with learnable gating, and incorporates an Adaptive Weighted Loss to strengthen supervision on key-sketch frames and line-art regions. Experimental results on the Aesthetic subset of Sakuga-42M show that our approach consistently outperforms representative animation interpolation and sketch-guided generation baselines. Compared to the best-performing baseline, SketchKeyAnime reduces EDMD by 31.9\% and FVD by 9.5\%, demonstrating superior sketch fidelity and temporal coherence, while achieving the best overall performance across most quantitative metrics. These results validate the proposed framework and highlight its potential for low-cost, highly controllable animation creation.

AI.tldr: SketchKeyAnime is a diffusion-based framework that generates structurally controllable, appearance-consistent, and temporally coherent animations from sparse key sketches, using dual-branch conditioning, Sketch Cross Attention, and Adaptive Weighted Loss; it outperforms baselines on Sakuga-42M with significant gains in temporal coherence and sketch fidelity.
AI.motivation: Traditional animation is labor-intensive and often requires dense input (complete sketches or frames). There is a need for low-cost, controllable animation generation under sparse inputs that preserves structure, appearance, and temporal coherence.
AI.method: A dual-branch conditioning scheme encodes local geometric constraints and semantic-temporal context. Sketch Cross Attention fuses the reference image and sketch conditions with learnable gating. An Adaptive Weighted Loss emphasizes supervision on key-sketch frames and line-art regions. The approach is implemented in a video diffusion framework and evaluated on the Aesthetic subset of Sakuga-42M.
AI.result: Outperforms representative animation interpolation and sketch-guided generation baselines. EDMD is reduced by 31.9% and FVD by 9.5% relative to the best-performing baseline, with superior sketch fidelity and temporal coherence across most metrics.
AI.conclusion: The framework is effective for low-cost, highly controllable animation creation from sparse inputs and validates SketchKeyAnime as a strong option for sparse-input video generation.

title: Advancing DialNav through Automatic Embodied Dialog Augmentation
summary: For embodied agents capable of physical interaction, the capability to create and understand dialog is crucial to ensure both safety and effectiveness. While DialNav~\cite{han2025dialnav} provides a framework for holistic evaluation of the dialog--execution loop in photorealistic indoor navigation, its performance remains limited by a critical scarcity of training data (2K episodes). To address this, we propose an automatic generation pipeline, and construct the \textbf{RAINbow} dataset, a large-scale training dataset with 238K episodes for DialNav. Our pipeline converts existing VLN datasets into multi-turn dialog and creates cost-efficient and high-quality dataset. Then, we introduce two additional complementary advances to unlock the data's full potential: (1) Dual-Strategy Training, a navigation training scheme to align the navigation training with the dynamic dialog-navigation loop, and (2) a localization model that leverages VLN knowledge. By combining these complementary solutions, our model substantially outperforms the baseline in success rate on both \textbf{Val Seen} (58.24, \textbf{+89\%}) and \textbf{Val Unseen} (29.05, \textbf{+100\%}) splits, establishing a new state of the art.

AI.tldr: Large-scale, auto-generated dataset (RAINbow) for DialNav with 238K episodes; introduces Dual-Strategy Training and a localization model, achieving new SOTA in Val Seen and Val Unseen.
AI.motivation: DialNav's evaluation of dialog–execution in photorealistic indoor navigation is data-constrained (only ~2K episodes). A scalable data generation pipeline is needed to train robust dialog-enabled navigators and close the gap between VLN and multi-turn dialog interaction.
AI.method: An automatic generation pipeline converts existing VLN datasets into multi-turn dialog, yielding the RAINbow dataset (238K episodes). Two advances: (1) Dual-Strategy Training to align navigation training with the dynamic dialog-navigation loop; (2) a localization model that leverages VLN knowledge.
AI.result: Model substantially outperforms the baseline on Val Seen (58.24, +89%) and Val Unseen (29.05, +100%), establishing a new state of the art.
AI.conclusion: The approach demonstrates that large-scale, automatically generated dialog data combined with targeted training strategies can significantly boost dialog-driven navigation performance, with strong generalization to unseen environments. Further work could assess dialog quality and broader evaluation metrics.

title: Latent Confounded Causal Discovery via Lie Bracket Geometry
summary: Recent work on Kan-Do-Calculus (KDC) has established that the boundary between passive observation and active intervention in causal inference is a category-theoretic bi-adjunction, with interventions modeled by left Kan extensions and conditioning by right Kan extensions. This paper introduces two causal discovery algorithms under latent confounding, building on the information-geometric and categorical consequences of KDC. In smooth statistical settings, Radon-Nikodym derivatives between observational and interventional measures induce local causal vector fields; failures of these fields to close under Lie brackets become computable Frobenius residuals, which we interpret as witnesses of failed visible integrability and possible latent or unmodeled structure. Our first algorithm, BRIDGE (Bracket Residuals for Interventional Discovery and Geometric Estimation), combines an interventional density or Radon-Nikodym-ratio engine with a geometric screen that proposes a high-recall family of admissible arrows, identifies non-closing visible pairs as latent-obstruction candidates, and passes the reduced family to downstream score-based or differentiable discovery routines. The second algorithmic contribution, Spectral Kan-Do Flow Matching (SKFM), learns amortized intervention fields and factors latent curvature spectrally, exposing the direct Lie-space endpoint toward which BRIDGE points. A detailed set of experiments show that both algorithms are capable of discovering causal models with latent confounders while collapsing the super-exponential space of possible DAGs by many orders of magnitude. This paper introduces a new paradigm in causal discovery, where latent structure is inferred directly from the geometry of intervention-induced flows.

AI.tldr: Two new causal-discovery algorithms under latent confounding (BRIDGE and SKFM) grounded in Kan-Do-Calculus (KDC). They exploit left/right Kan extensions, Radon–Nikodym derivatives, and Lie-algebra geometry to identify latent structure and prune DAG search spaces; empirically capable of discovering causal models with latent confounders and reducing the combinatorial explosion of DAGs.
AI.motivation: To unify category-theoretic causal inference with information-geometric tools to infer latent structure directly from the geometry of intervention-induced flows, addressing latent confounding in causal discovery.
AI.method: Grounded in Kan-Do-Calculus: interventions via left Kan extensions; conditioning via right Kan extensions. In smooth settings, RN derivatives between observational and interventional measures induce local causal vector fields; non-closure under Lie brackets yields Frobenius residuals, signaling latent structure. BRIDGE builds an interventional density/RN-ratio engine plus a geometric screen to propose a high-recall set of admissible arrows, flags non-closing visible pairs as latent-obstruction candidates, and feeds a reduced set to downstream learners. SKFM learns amortized intervention fields and factors latent curvature spectrally, yielding an explicit Lie-space endpoint that BRIDGE points to.
AI.result: Demonstrates that BRIDGE and SKFM can discover causal models with latent confounders while drastically reducing the super-exponential DAG search space, indicating a viable new paradigm where latent structure is inferred from the geometry of intervention-induced flows.
AI.conclusion: The work introduces a principled, geometry-driven approach to causal discovery under latent confounding, leveraging Kan-Do-Calculus to connect category theory, information geometry, and practical causal structure learning. It offers scalable reduction of candidate models and a pathway to explicitly characterize latent obstructions via Frobenius residuals and Lie-algebra geometry.

title: Addressing Detail Bottlenecks in Latent Diffusion for RGB-to-SWIR Image Translation
summary: Latent diffusion models (LDMs) enable efficient image-to-image translation but discard fine spatial details during compression, degrading downstream perception tasks. We identify two bottlenecks: the autoencoder, which loses spatial information, and the conditioning pathway, which further degrades the source signal through naive downsampling. We propose two lightweight, backbone-agnostic fixes: a Source-Conditioned Autoencoder (SCAE) that injects high-resolution source features into the decoder via skip connections, and a Learnable Guidance Encoder (LGE) that replaces naive downsampling with a learned conditioning signal. Evaluated on RGB-to-SWIR translation for driving scenes with two denoiser backbones (U-Net and DiT), our approach improves detection mAP by up to 2x over the latent diffusion baseline, with up to 3.4x gains on small objects (COCO-small, <32^2 px^2), while achieving state-of-the-art FID. We further show that FID and detection performance are poorly correlated, motivating multi-axis evaluation. Results generalise zero-shot to the public RASMD benchmark. We will publicly release test data with annotations, all checkpoints, and training code.

AI.tldr: Proposes two lightweight, backbone-agnostic fixes for latent diffusion models to preserve spatial detail and improve conditioning: a Source-Conditioned Autoencoder (SCAE) with high-resolution skip connections, and a Learnable Guidance Encoder (LGE) for learned conditioning signals.
AI.motivation: LDMs compress images for efficiency but lose spatial detail. The autoencoder bottleneck and the naive downsampling in the conditioning pathway degrade downstream perception tasks such as object detection. A lightweight, backbone-agnostic remedy is needed to preserve spatial information and improve conditioning quality.
AI.method: Introduce SCAE by injecting high-resolution source features into the decoder via skip connections. Introduce LGE to replace naive downsampling with a learnable conditioning signal. Evaluate on RGB-to-SWIR translation for driving scenes using two denoiser backbones (U-Net and DiT).
AI.result: Detection mAP improves up to 2x over the latent diffusion baseline; up to 3.4x gains on small objects (COCO-small, <32^2 px^2). Achieves state-of-the-art FID. FID and detection are not well-correlated, motivating multi-axis evaluation. Zero-shot generalization to the public RASMD benchmark.
AI.conclusion: The proposed SCAE and LGE effectively preserve spatial information and improve conditioning without sacrificing backbone compatibility, delivering substantial gains in perception tasks and competitive generative fidelity. A public release of test data, checkpoints, and code is planned.

title: ENPIRE: Agentic Robot Policy Self-Improvement in the Real World
summary: Achieving dexterous robotic manipulation in the real world heavily relies on human supervision and algorithm engineering, which becomes a central bottleneck in the pursuit of general physical intelligence. Although emerging coding agents can generate code to automate algorithm search, their successes remain largely confined in digital environments. We conjecture that the missing abstraction to automate robotics research is a repeatable feedback loop for real-world policy improvement: reset the scene, execute a policy, verify the outcome, and refine the next iteration. To bridge this gap, we introduce ENPIRE, a harness framework for coding agents that instantiates this physical feedback routine with four core modules: an Environment module (EN) for automatic reset and verification, a Policy Improvement module (PI) that launches policy refinement, a Rollout module (R) to evaluate policies with one or multiple physical robots operating in parallel, and an Evolution module (E) in which coding agents analyze logs, consult literature, improve training infrastructure and algorithm code to address failure modes. This closed-loop system transforms real-world manipulation learning into a controllable optimization procedure, minimizing human effort while allowing fair ablations across training recipe and agent variants. Powered by ENPIRE, frontier coding agents can autonomously train a policy to achieve a 99% success rate on challenging, dexterous manipulation tasks, such as organizing a pin box, fastening a zip tie, and tool use, a process that further accelerates when we dispatch an agent team on a robot fleet. Our results suggest a practical and scalable path toward deploying coding agents to autonomously advancing robotics in the physical world.

AI.tldr: A closed-loop four-module harness (ENPIRE) enables coding agents to autonomously improve real-world dexterous manipulation policies, achieving high success with parallel robots.
AI.motivation: Overcome reliance on human supervision and engineering by establishing a repeatable physical feedback loop that bridges digital policy search with real-world robotics.
AI.method: Introduce ENPIRE with four modules: Environment (automatic reset/verification), Policy Improvement (policy refinement), Rollout (policy evaluation with one or more robots in parallel), and Evolution (log/literature analysis to address failure modes and improve infrastructure). The system forms a controllable optimization loop for real-world manipulation learning, including reproducible ablations across training recipes and agent variants.
AI.result: Claims 99% success on challenging dexterous tasks (e.g., organizing a pin box, fastening a zip tie, tool use) with a robot fleet, reduced human intervention, and scalable autonomous policy improvement.
AI.conclusion: ENPIRE presents a practical, scalable path for deploying coding agents to autonomously advance robotics in the physical world by closing the loop between code-driven agents and real-world manipulation.

title: SEAGAN: domain-Specific and Edge-Aware Graph Attention Network for Dynamic Plant Processes
summary: Graph neural networks (GNNs) provide a flexible framework for learning from scientific data linked through physical, biological, or functional relationships. One promising domain is plant physiology, where measured responses often arise from multiple interacting processes whose exact separation remains difficult even with manual intervention. In plant physiology, a key example is the A-Ci curve, which relates net CO2 assimilation rate (Anet) to leaf intercellular CO2 concentration (Ci) and is used to estimate photosynthetic parameters in leaf and crop-canopy models. However, reliable estimation requires identifying the active biochemical limitation state at each curve point, which remains a major source of uncertainty. Here, we formulate limitation-state identification along A-Ci curves as a graph-based node classification problem, with curve points as nodes. Domain-specific graph representations are created using distance-based k-nearest-neighbor (kNN) and auxiliary-signal-guided (ASG) connectivity, with edge attributes encoding pairwise relations. The framework was evaluated against conventional learning baselines, graph-based architectures, and an automated fitting-based benchmark. Results on a large synthetic dataset with known ground-truth limitation states show that graph-based models improve classification, particularly near biochemical transition regions. The best-performing configuration, SEAGAN (domain-Specific and Edge-Aware Graph Attention Network for Dynamic Plant Processes), integrates process-aware node features, edge attributes, kNN connectivity, and graph attention with weighted cross-entropy loss, achieving an F1-score of 0.857 and an accuracy of 0.882. The results show that representing A-Ci curves as graphs improves biochemical limitation-state analysis, with edge-aware attention over local kNN neighborhoods providing the most effective strategy.

AI.tldr: Graph-based SEAGAN framework for classifying biochemical limitation states along A-Ci curves using edge-aware graph attention, achieving high F1 and accuracy on synthetic data.
AI.motivation: Uncertainty in identifying active biochemical limitation states along A-Ci curves due to interacting processes; conventional methods struggle, especially near transition regions. A graph-based approach can model pointwise relationships and transitions to improve state classification.
AI.method: Represent A-Ci curve points as graph nodes with domain-informed features. Build edges via distance-based kNN and auxiliary-signal-guided (ASG) connectivity, with rich edge attributes encoding pairwise relations. Use SEAGAN (domain-Specific and Edge-Aware Graph Attention Network) incorporating process-aware node features, edge attributes, kNN connectivity, and graph attention with weighted cross-entropy loss.
AI.result: Evaluated on a large synthetic dataset with known ground-truth states. Graph-based models outperform baselines; best SEAGAN configuration achieves F1 = 0.857 and accuracy = 0.882, with edge-aware attention over local kNN neighborhoods providing the strongest performance, particularly near biochemical transition regions.
AI.conclusion: Graph-based representation of A-Ci curves, with edge-aware graph attention and domain-specific features, effectively improves biochemical limitation-state analysis and demonstrates the utility of SEAGAN for dynamic plant-process inference.

title: ROSE: Benchmarking the Perception-to-Action Gap in Multimodal Models
summary: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) are increasingly expected to act on visual information, yet the same scene may require different actions under different task contexts. How reliably can a model turn the same visual evidence into the action required by the current context? To answer this question, we introduce \textsc{ROSE} (\textbf{R}eference-conditioned \textbf{O}ddity and \textbf{S}ymbolic \textbf{E}xecution), a controlled benchmark that holds the visual scene fixed while varying region constraints and required symbolic outputs. Through coupled counting and coordinate-action tasks, \textsc{ROSE} tests whether models can infer an implicit majority reference and act on the resulting fine-grained visual evidence under changing contexts. Across nine recent MLLMs, performance drops by as much as 44.5 percentage points from counting-oriented tasks to region-conditioned action, despite 98.8\% human performance. The gap persists on paired scenes and regions for which the same model returns the correct count, while global-click and matched local controls show that coordinate grounding explains only part of the loss, revealing a distinct, model-dependent bottleneck in turning shared visual evidence into context-specific actions.

AI.tldr: ROSE introduces a controlled benchmark to probe how multimodal LLMs convert fixed visual evidence into task-context dependent actions; by varying region constraints and symbolic outputs, it reveals large performance gaps across 9 models, indicating a bottleneck beyond counting.
AI.motivation: To quantify reliability of MLLMs in mapping shared visual evidence to context-specific actions and to identify bottlenecks in action grounding under changing task constraints.
AI.method: ROSE holds the visual scene fixed, manipulates region-level constraints and required symbolic outputs; uses coupled counting and coordinate-action tasks; evaluates nine recent MLLMs; includes control conditions (global-click, matched local) to isolate grounding effects.
AI.result: Model performance drops up to 44.5 percentage points from counting tasks to region-conditioned action; human performance is 98.8%. The gap persists even when counts are correct in paired scenes/regions; coordinate grounding explains only part of the loss, suggesting a model-dependent bottleneck in turning evidence into context-specific actions.
AI.conclusion: Reliable action inference from shared evidence remains challenging for MLLMs; the bottleneck is not solely grounding but also higher-level symbolic execution and context adaptation; ROSE provides a diagnostic for future model improvements.

title: Reward as An Agent for Embodied World Models
summary: While RL has become a promising tool for refining world models, existing methods largely rely on conservative rollouts near the training distribution, limiting exploration, behavioral diversity, and richer dynamic discovery. In this work, we challenge this conservative paradigm. We argue that the core limitation is not exploration itself, but the lack of reliable verification strategies to support broader exploration. Without reliable verification, expanded exploration becomes highly susceptible to reward hacking, where policies exploit imperfect rewards without achieving genuine improvement. To evaluate this motivation, we instantiate our method in embodied world models, where physical plausibility, and task completion provide a rigorous testbed for scalable RL under complex dynamics. On the verification side, we introduce Reward as an Agent, an agentic reward framework that actively evaluates generated behaviors to provide robust reward signals and mitigate reward hacking under distribution shifts. On the exploration side, we introduce Dynamic-Aware Rollout Diversification through DynDiff-GRPO, which explicitly expands action-space exploration to diversify trajectories, broaden state-action coverage, and encourage richer embodied behaviors beyond conservative rollout regimes. By unifying Reward as an Agent with DynDiff-GRPO, we enable RL on a more reliable reward foundation with substantially diversified sampling, effectively mitigating reward hacking while yielding significant accuracy gains across multiple open-source world models, thereby demonstrating that broader exploration can scale successfully when grounded in robust verification.

AI.tldr: Broader exploration in embodied world models is feasible and beneficial when paired with a robust verification signal (Reward as an Agent) to mitigate reward hacking; introduces DynDiff-GRPO for diversified rollouts; shows accuracy gains on open-source world models.
AI.motivation: Conservative RL rollouts near the training distribution limit exploration, diversity, and dynamic discovery. The core issue is unreliable verification leading to reward hacking during broader exploration; embodied world models provide a rigorous testbed for scalable RL under complex dynamics.
AI.method: Introduce Reward as an Agent (agentic reward framework) to actively evaluate generated behaviors and provide robust rewards under distribution shifts; introduce Dynamic-Aware Rollout Diversification through DynDiff-GRPO (DynDiff-GRPO) to expand action-space exploration, diversify trajectories, and broaden state-action coverage; unify the two to enable RL with diversified sampling on a robust reward foundation.
AI.result: Significant accuracy gains across multiple open-source world models; mitigation of reward hacking under distribution shifts; broader exploration scales when grounded in robust verification.
AI.conclusion: Broader exploration can scale in embodied world models when supported by reliable verification; the combination of Reward as an Agent and DynDiff-GRPO yields improved performance and richer embodied behaviors beyond conservative rollouts.

title: MassSpecGym in the Wild: Uncovering and Correcting Evaluation Pitfalls in AI-Driven Molecule Discovery
summary: Reliable benchmarking is critical for developing machine learning models for tandem mass spectrometry (MS/MS) based molecule discovery. Subtle issues in experimental design and model evaluation procedures can degrade the trustworthiness of such benchmarks and lead to erroneous conclusions. We conduct a thorough review of model evaluation issues in the recent MS/MS machine learning literature, using the standard MassSpecGym benchmark suite as a case study to illustrate the impact of these issues. We find evaluation issues in at least 17 of 26 papers reporting MassSpecGym benchmark results in the first year of its adoption. We isolate three classes of failures: (i) data leakage, (ii) shortcut learning, and (iii) implementation bugs and metric divergence. Through extensive experimentation and code replication, we quantify the impact of these issues and show how they corrupt the evaluation standards MassSpecGym was designed to enforce. We distill our findings into recommendations generalizable to MS/MS challenges, benchmarks, and custom evaluation setups. We also release MassSpecGym v1.5, an implementation of our recommendations in the MassSpecGym benchmarking suite which addresses the failure modes identified in this audit. MassSpecGym v1.5 is publicly available at https://github.com/pluskal-lab/MassSpecGym.

AI.tldr: A meta-analysis of MS/MS ML benchmarks using MassSpecGym reveals pervasive evaluation flaws (data leakage, shortcut learning, and bugs) in early papers; quantifies impact, provides remediation, and releases MassSpecGym v1.5.
AI.motivation: To ensure trustworthy, reproducible benchmarking in MS/MS ML by auditing current practices, identifying common failure modes, and providing concrete fixes for a robust evaluation framework.
AI.method: Systematic review of papers using MassSpecGym, replication of experiments, identification of three failure classes (data leakage, shortcut learning, implementation bugs/metric divergence), quantitative assessment of impact, development of general recommendations, and implementation of MassSpecGym v1.5 incorporating these fixes.
AI.result: Found evaluation issues in at least 17 of 26 papers in the first year; demonstrated how issues bias or break benchmarking; proposed generalizable recommendations; released MassSpecGym v1.5 addressing the identified failure modes.
AI.conclusion: Robust MS/MS benchmarking requires rigorous data handling, validation protocols, and transparent reporting; the MassSpecGym v1.5 release provides a practical path to more trustworthy evaluations and serves as a blueprint for benchmarking in related domains.

title: Pose6DAug: Physically Plausible Multi-view Object Swapping for Robot Data Augmentation
summary: Vision-language-action (VLA) policies have shown strong potential for general-purpose manipulation, yet they often fail on novel, out-of-distribution objects whose appearance or geometry deviates from the training distribution. The standard remedy is to collect multi-view teleoperation data for every failure case, but this scales poorly in both cost and time. We introduce Pose6DAug, a failure-driven data augmentation framework that turns a policy's own successful episodes into targeted demonstrations for its failure modes, without any new data collection. Our key insight is that each successful episode already encodes a physically valid action trajectory together with calibrated multi-view observations. By swapping only the manipulated object while preserving this trajectory, we obtain new and physically grounded demonstrations. However, naive 2D video editing breaks multi-view consistency and physical plausibility, particularly under heavy occlusion and egocentric viewpoints. Our method instead operates directly in 3D, anchoring the target object with an explicit mesh driven by a temporally coherent 6D pose trajectory, ensuring geometrically consistent renderings across all camera views. Fine-tuning a VLA on data augmented by our method improves success rates by 16.5% relative to the state-of-the-art baseline on novel objects, while preserving in-distribution performance. These results show that multi-view and physically consistent augmentation is a practical path to scalable VLA generalization.

AI.tldr: Pose6DAug introduces a failure-driven, 3D data augmentation for vision-language-action policies by swapping the manipulated object in successful episodes while preserving the action trajectory via a temporally coherent 6D pose mesh. This yields improved generalization to novel objects (16.5% relative) and preserves in-distribution performance.
AI.motivation: VLA policies struggle with out-of-distribution objects whose appearance or geometry differs from training data; collecting multi-view teleoperation data for every failure is expensive and scales poorly. A scalable augmentation approach is needed to improve generalization without additional data collection.
AI.method: From successful episodes, generate new, physically grounded demonstrations by replacing the manipulated object but keeping the original 6D pose trajectory. Implement the augmentation in 3D by anchoring the target object with a mesh driven by a temporally coherent 6D pose, ensuring multi-view geometric consistency even under occlusion. Avoids 2D video-editing artifacts. Fine-tune the VLA policy on the augmented data.
AI.result: Fine-tuning on Pose6DAug-augmented data improves success rates on novel objects by 16.5% relative to a state-of-the-art baseline, while maintaining in-distribution performance.
AI.conclusion: Multi-view, physically consistent 3D augmentation is a practical and scalable path to improving VLA generalization to novel objects without extra data collection.

title: Semantic-Anchored Evidential Fusion for Domain-Robust Whole-Slide Survival Analysis
summary: Whole-slide images (WSIs) are widely used for computational cancer prognosis. However, most existing methods primarily focus on in-domain performance and fail to generalize across clinical centers. This limitation stems from their reliance on pixel-derived representations that are highly susceptible to domain-specific artifacts caused by staining protocols and scanner hardware. We hypothesize that high-level pathology semantics, such as tumor grade and micro-environmental architecture, provide a domain-invariant semantic representation that mirrors the robust diagnostic logic of human pathologists. Therefore, we propose a Semantic-Anchored Evidential Fusion Survival (SAEFS) framework, where SAEFS derives semantic anchors from WSIs via Visual Question Answering (VQA), employs a dual-stream WSI evidence extraction architecture, uses Dirichlet-based Subjective Logic to model uncertainty, and fuses semantic and visual evidence through a cautious conjunction rule to avoid overconfident fusion from correlated sources. Trained exclusively on one source domain and evaluated zero-shot across four unseen domains, SAEFS consistently outperforms state-of-the-art models both in prediction accuracy and reliability, improving the average C-index by 10.2%. Quantitative analyses further show that VQA-derived semantic features exhibit significantly lower cross-center divergence than pixel-derived features, highlighting their robustness for cross-center clinical applications.

AI.tldr: SAEFS proposes a cross-domain robust survival framework for WSIs that uses semantic anchors derived from VQA, dual-stream evidence extraction, and Dirichlet-based subjective logic with cautious fusion. Trained on one domain, it achieves zero-shot generalization to four unseen domains and improves C-index by 10.2% on average; semantic features show lower cross-center divergence than pixel features.
AI.motivation: Domain shifts in whole-slide image prognosis arise from staining and scanner artifacts, making pixel-based representations fragile. High-level pathology semantics (e.g., tumor grade, micro-environment) may provide domain-invariant signals akin to human diagnostic reasoning.
AI.method: SAEFS derives semantic anchors via Visual Question Answering (VQA); employs a dual-stream WSI evidence extraction architecture; models uncertainty with Dirichlet-based subjective logic; fuses semantic and visual evidence through a cautious conjunction rule to avoid overconfident fusion from correlated sources; trained on a single source domain and evaluated zero-shot on four unseen domains; assessed using C-index and cross-center divergence analyses.
AI.result: SAEFS outperforms state-of-the-art models in prediction accuracy and reliability, with an average C-index improvement of 10.2%. VQA-derived semantic features exhibit significantly lower cross-center divergence than pixel-derived features, indicating improved cross-center robustness.
AI.conclusion: Semantic anchors extracted via VQA provide a domain-invariant representation for cross-center WSI prognosis. The cautious fusion strategy mitigates overconfidence from correlated sources, enabling reliable zero-shot generalization to unseen centers.

title: Autonomous Event-Driven Multi-Agent Orchestration for Enterprise AI at Scale
summary: Enterprise AI aims to move toward continuous event monitoring, detection, and action across specialist agents, yet existing multi-agent systems largely assume discrete request-response workflows and remain underexplored at enterprise scale. We evaluate DAG Plan and Execute and ReAct across 208 production-derived enterprise scenarios spanning Persona (<10 agents), Department (20-80), and Enterprise (200) scales, and introduce a Task Manager for continuous operation via priority inference, related-event merging, and preemption. Results show that scale, not task complexity, dominates orchestration performance: both architectures perform well at small scale but degrade at enterprise scale as agent discovery noise becomes the primary bottleneck, with simple tasks degrading more sharply than complex ones. DAG Plan and Execute offers higher precision and structured parallelization at smaller scales, but its higher overhead worsens at enterprise scale; ReAct is more robust by handling failures incrementally. The Task Manager reduces high-priority queue latency by 14-75% and improves related-event correctness by over 20 percentage points at enterprise scale.

AI.tldr: Enterprise-scale orchestration is bottlenecked by agent discovery noise; DAG Plan and Execute and ReAct each exhibit scale-sensitive tradeoffs, with a Task Manager delivering meaningful latency reductions and correctness gains.
AI.motivation: Evaluate and compare two enterprise-oriented multi-agent architectures (DAG Plan and Execute, ReAct) across production-derived scales, and address enterprise-scale orchestration challenges with a Task Manager.
AI.method: Assessment over 208 production-derived enterprise scenarios spanning Persona (<10 agents), Department (20–80), and Enterprise (≈200) scales; direct comparison of DAG Plan and Execute and ReAct; introduction of a Task Manager implementing priority inference, related-event merging, and preemption for continuous operation.
AI.result: Scale, not task complexity, dominates orchestration performance: both architectures suffice at small scales but degrade at enterprise scale due to agent discovery noise; simple tasks degrade more than complex ones. DAG Plan and Execute offers higher precision and structured parallelization at small scales but incurs overhead that hurts at enterprise scale; ReAct is more robust by incremental failure handling. The Task Manager reduces high-priority queue latency by 14–75% and improves related-event correctness by >20 percentage points at enterprise scale.
AI.conclusion: For enterprise-scale multi-agent orchestration, consider architecture tradeoffs: DAG Plan and Execute excels in precision and parallelization at small scales but falters at scale due to overhead; ReAct provides robustness to failures; the Task Manager delivers substantial improvements in latency and correctness, particularly at enterprise scale, mitigating the main bottleneck (discovery noise).

title: Hard or Just Unreached? Diagnosing the Sampling Blind Spot in Math-Reasoning Difficulty Estimation
comment: 9 pages of main paper, 4 figures and 5 tables in the main paper, with more in the appendix
summary: Math and science reasoning benchmarks rely on pass@k, the fraction of sampled chains that reach gold, as the canonical per-example difficulty signal. The same signal drives RL with verifiable rewards, math data curation, synthetic curricula, and verifier training. We show this proxy has a persistent blind spot on its hardest stratum: on the eight free-form math cells we test (GSM8K and MATH across four open-weight models), 10.3-22.9% of the examples that no sampling seed solves in six tries are instead solved at matched compute by a six-chain deterministic regime. These are greedy decoding plus five cheap residual-stream perturbations applied via activation grafting, while greedy alone solves at most 6% on these math cells. Recovery scales with the additional budget, across perturbations whose mechanistic distinctness we verify across all twelve cells (cross-kind fix-set Jaccard <= 0.47 in every setup). Activation grafting is used as an intervention on internal representations, not a decoding method; we use it purely as a diagnostic and diversification tool, and our recovered items show that the pass@k= 0 % stratum is structurally identifiable in the residual stream rather than that the unmodified model reaches them under ordinary inference.

AI.tldr: The hardest examples in math benchmarks are not truly unsolvable; a six-chain deterministic regime with greedy decoding plus small residual-stream perturbations can solve a nontrivial fraction (10.3–22.9%) of examples previously deemed unsolvable with six seeds; this reveals a blind spot in pass@k metrics and shows internal representations can be leveraged diagnostically.
AI.motivation: To scrutinize pass@k as a proxy for difficulty, understand the limits of sampling-based evaluation, and investigate whether internal representations contain solvable signals masked by decoding choices; with implications for RL verifiable rewards, data curation, and curricula.
AI.method: Assess GSM8K and MATH across four open-weight models. Identify hardest stratum where six seeds fail. Apply a six-chain deterministic regime: greedy decoding plus five cheap residual-stream perturbations via activation grafting. Compare across twelve cells for mechanistic distinctness (Jaccard). Verify perturbations are intervention on representations, not decoding. Use activation grafting as diagnostic/diversification.
AI.result: In 8 math cells, 10.3–22.9% of previously unsolved examples become solvable under the six-chain deterministic regime for matched compute. Greedy alone solves ≤6%. Perturbations recover those items; performance improves with budget; perturbations are mechanistically distinct (Jaccard ≤0.47). Recovered items indicate pass@k=0% stratum is identifiable in residual stream rather than reachable by ordinary inference.
AI.conclusion: The pass@k=0% stratum is structurally identifiable in the residual stream; internal representations harbor solvable signals that are masked by decoding in standard inference. Activation grafting serves as a diagnostic/diversification tool rather than a decoding method, highlighting a fundamental blind spot in pass@k metrics and suggesting richer evaluation strategies that probe internal representations.

title: Dual-Agent Framework for Cross-Model Verified Translation of Natural-Language Protocols into Robotic Laboratory Platform
summary: Biological experiment protocols are written in natural language, whereas automation systems rely on predefined control commands, creating a semantic gap that limits autonomous execution. Microplate-based automatic experiments are particularly challenging due to the need to simultaneously control well mapping, sample-reagent combinations, replicate placement, and parallel dispensing. This study proposes an agent-based protocol translation framework that converts natural-language microplate-based protocols into executable control commands for a robotic laboratory platform. A Parser Agent formalizes the natural-language protocol into a structured representation, and a rule-based mapping engine deterministically incorporates the operational constraints of the robotic laboratory platform to generate device-level control commands. A heterogeneous LLM Validation Agent verifies completeness, parameter accuracy, and execution order, and triggers a self-correction loop with structured feedback when errors are detected. A sweep involving 7 Parsers and 3 Validators on randomly selected ELISA protocols evaluates how model scale and Validator type affect translation accuracy and pass rates under cross-model verification. The accuracy-latency trade-off is further verified by comparing the rule-based mapping of the proposed framework with LLM end-to-end direct mapping. Finally, Bradford assay-based protein quantification using a microplate was demonstrated on a robotic laboratory platform, validating end-to-end autonomous execution from natural-language protocols to real-world experiments. The proposed framework provides a flexible approach to narrowing the semantic gap between natural-language protocols and microplate-based self-driving laboratories.

AI.tldr: An agent-based system translates natural-language microplate protocols into executable robotic commands using a Parser Agent, a rule-based Mapping Engine, and an LLM Validation Agent with a self-correction loop; evaluated across multiple parsers/validators; demonstrated with Bradford assay for end-to-end automation.
AI.motivation: Bridge the semantic gap between NL laboratory protocols and automated microplate platforms, especially for complex coordination like well mapping, sample-reagent combos, replicates, and parallel dispensing.
AI.method: A Parser Agent converts NL protocols into a structured representation; a rule-based Mapping Engine deterministically encodes operational constraints to generate device-level commands; a heterogeneous LLM Validation Agent checks completeness, parameters, and execution order and triggers structured feedback-driven self-correction; evaluation with 7 Parsers x 3 Validators on ELISA protocols to study model scale and Validator type; compare with end-to-end LLM mapping; validation via Bradford assay on robotic platform.
AI.result: End-to-end autonomous execution demonstrated from NL protocols to real-world microplate experiments (Bradford assay); findings on accuracy and latency trade-offs between rule-based vs LLM mappings across varying model scales and validators.
AI.conclusion: The framework offers a flexible approach to bridge natural-language protocols and robotic microplate workflows, enabling more reliable, scalable autonomous experiments.

title: CrossFlow: One-Step Generation Across Latent and Pixel Spaces
summary: Most diffusion and flow-matching generators define the prior, probability path, and prediction target in the same representation space. Latent diffusion improves efficiency by moving this path into an autoencoder latent space, but the final sample is still produced by a separately trained decoder. This separation creates a mismatch: the generator is optimized for latent-space prediction, while final quality depends on how the decoder handles generated latents that may differ from clean encoder outputs. We introduce CrossFlow, a cross-space flow formulation that maps noisy latent inputs directly to pixel-space images. The key technical step is a velocity-free one-step objective: the latent trajectory defines the training path, but the supervised prediction is an image rather than a latent displacement. This lets one model act both as a one-step latent-to-pixel generator and as a decoder replacement for latent diffusion pipelines. On class-conditional ImageNet-1k at $256\times256$, CrossFlow-XL achieves 1.62 FID with one function evaluation. Ablations show that the latent encoder and pixel-space perceptual and adversarial losses are important for fidelity. These results indicate that cross-space flow objectives can combine the efficiency of latent representations with direct pixel-space supervision, without requiring a separate decoder at inference.

AI.tldr: CrossFlow introduces a cross-space flow objective that directly maps latent inputs to pixel-space images, removing the need for a separate decoder and enabling one-step latent-to-pixel generation; achieves 1.62 FID on ImageNet-1k 256×256 with a single function evaluation.
AI.motivation: Latent diffusion improves efficiency by operating in an autoencoder latent space, but final quality depends on a separately trained decoder. This mismatch motivates a cross-space approach that aligns training objectives with inference by predicting in pixel space from latent inputs.
AI.method: Proposes a velocity-free one-step objective where the latent trajectory defines the training path, but the supervised prediction is an image in pixel space. The model acts as both a latent-to-pixel generator and a decoder replacement by employing latent encoder features and pixel-space perceptual and adversarial losses. Ablations identify the roles of the latent encoder and pixel-space losses.
AI.result: On ImageNet-1k 256×256, CrossFlow-XL attains 1.62 FID with one function evaluation. Ablations indicate the latent encoder and pixel-space perceptual/adversarial losses are crucial for fidelity.
AI.conclusion: Cross-space flow objectives can fuse latent-efficiency with direct pixel-space supervision, removing the need for a separate decoder at inference while enabling high-quality generation with minimal function evaluations.

title: Process-Verified Reinforcement Learning for Theorem Proving via Lean
summary: While reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards (RLVR) typically has relied on a single binary verification signal, symbolic proof assistants in formal reasoning offer rich, fine-grained structured feedback. This gap between structured processes and unstructured rewards highlights the importance of feedback that is both dense and sound. In this work, we demonstrate that the Lean proof assistant itself can serve as a symbolic process oracle, supplying both outcome-level and fine-grained tactic-level verified feedback during training. Proof attempts are parsed into tactic sequences, and Lean's elaboration marks both locally sound steps and the earliest failing step, yielding dense, verifier-grounded credit signals rooted in type theory. We incorporate these structured rewards into a GRPO-style reinforcement learning objective with first-error propagation and first-token credit methods that balances outcome- and process-level advantages. Experiments with STP-Lean and DeepSeek-Prover-V1.5 show that tactic-level supervision outperforms outcome-only baselines in most settings, delivering improvements on benchmarks such as MiniF2F and ProofNet. Beyond empirical gains, our study highlights a broader perspective: symbolic proof assistants are not only verifiers at evaluation time, but can also act as process-level reward oracles during training. This opens a path toward reinforcement learning frameworks that combine the scalability of language models with the reliability of symbolic verification for formal reasoning.

AI.tldr: Leaning on the Lean proof assistant as a symbolic process oracle to provide dense, verifier-grounded rewards for RL in formal reasoning; integrating structured tactic-level feedback into a GRPO-style objective with first-error and first-token credit improves tactic-level supervision over outcome-only signals, with gains on MiniF2F and ProofNet benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Address the gap between unstructured RL reward signals and rich, structured feedback in formal reasoning by leveraging the Lean proof assistant to supply both outcome-level and fine-grained tactic-level feedback during training.
AI.method: Parse proof attempts into tactic sequences; use Lean elaboration to identify locally sound steps and the first failing step; adopt a GRPO-style reinforcement learning objective with first-error propagation and first-token credit; train with tactic-level supervision; evaluate on STP-Lean and DeepSeek-Prover-V1.5, reporting results on MiniF2F and ProofNet.
AI.result: Tactic-level supervision outperforms outcome-only baselines in most settings, yielding improvements on MiniF2F and ProofNet benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Symbolic proof assistants can function as process-level reward oracles during training, enabling RL frameworks that blend the scalability of language models with the reliability of symbolic verification for formal reasoning.

title: Convex training of Lipschitz-regularized shallow neural networks
summary: In this work, we introduce a training procedure for shallow neural networks that promotes robustness against adversarial attacks. We solve a non-convex Lipschitz-regularized training program by introducing a convex restriction that can be efficiently solved to global optimality. Our approach can be employed as a post-processing step by taking a pre-trained network as an initial solution to then solving the convex program whose optimal network is guaranteed to be no worse than the initial one. We illustrate the improvements of our training procedure with experiments using real world datasets for regression tasks under an adversarial setting. We show numerically that solving our proposed convex program yields networks with lower objective values on the Lipschitz-regularized program compared to existing methods. Additionally, we show that on certain datasets, networks obtained using our convex training program are both more accurate and robust with respect to adversarial attacks.

AI.tldr: We propose a convex restriction for Lipschitz-regularized training of shallow nets, enabling a globally optimal solution that can be used as post-processing on pre-trained models; experiments on real-regression tasks show improved objective and, on some datasets, improved accuracy and adversarial robustness.
AI.motivation: Non-convexity of Lipschitz-regularized training makes optimization hard and robustness hard; a convex relaxation can yield tractable global optima and better robustness.
AI.method: Introduce a convex restriction of the non-convex problem; solve to global optimum; can start from a pre-trained network; the convex solution dominates the initial in the Lipschitz-regularized objective; applied as a post-processing step; empirical evaluation on regression tasks under adversarial settings.
AI.result: The convex program achieves lower objective values than existing methods on the Lipschitz-regularized objective; on some datasets, networks obtained are both more accurate and more robust to adversarial attacks.
AI.conclusion: Convex training via restriction provides a practical, effective enhancement for robustness in shallow networks and can be used as a post-processing step to improve pre-trained models; results support improved robustness and accuracy on selected tasks.

title: Frequency-Aware Flow Matching for Continuous and Consistent Robotic Action Generation
summary: Flow matching has emerged as a standard paradigm for robotic manipulation owing to its strong expressive power for modelling complex, multimodal action distributions, alongside similar approaches like diffusion policy. However, existing methods rely on discretized action chunks, making them brittle to demonstrations collected at heterogeneous control frequencies and prone to temporally inconsistent actions that degrade control stability. In this paper, we propose Frequency-Aware Flow Matching (FAFM), which outputs continuous, temporally consistent actions. To handle heterogeneous frequency input, we transform discrete action sequences into the frequency domain with the discrete cosine transform (DCT), perform flow matching over the resulting coefficients, and reconstruct continuous actions via cosine basis expansion. To generate temporally consistent actions, we regularize the first-order temporal derivative to promote smooth actions. This corresponds to a Sobolev-type constraint that suppresses high-frequency errors and discourages abrupt action changes. Our FAFM is simple, introduces no additional network parameters and applies to standalone flow-matching policies and vision-language action models. Across synthetic toy benchmark, obstacle avoidance, LapGym, and LIBERO, FAFM improves success rates, multimodal expressivity, motion smoothness, convergence speed, robustness to mechanical bias and mixed-frequency input. These gains are consistent when deployed on a real-world Franka robot. Code available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/FAFM.

AI.tldr: FAFM extends flow matching to continuous, temporally smooth actions across heterogeneous input frequencies using DCT-based frequency-domain flow matching and Sobolev-type temporal regularization.
AI.motivation: Flow matching offers expressive multimodal action distributions but relies on discretized action chunks, making it brittle to heterogeneous control frequencies and causing temporally inconsistent actions. A method that yields continuous actions and robustness to mixed frequencies is desired.
AI.method: Convert discrete action sequences to the frequency domain via the discrete cosine transform (DCT). Perform flow matching on the DCT coefficients. Reconstruct continuous actions with cosine basis expansion. Impose a first-order temporal derivative regularization (Sobolev constraint) to promote smoothness and suppress high-frequency errors. No additional network parameters; applicable to standalone flow-matching policies and vision-language action models.
AI.result: FAFM achieves higher success rates and multimodal expressivity, smoother motions, faster convergence, and robustness to mechanical bias and mixed-frequency input across synthetic toy benchmarks, obstacle avoidance, LapGym, and LIBERO, with successful deployment on a real Franka robot.
AI.conclusion: FAFM provides a simple, parameter-free, frequency-aware extension to flow matching that yields continuous, temporally consistent actions while handling heterogeneous input frequencies, broadening applicability to real-world robotic manipulation.

title: Vision-Reasoning-Guided Occlusion Removal from Light Fields
summary: Occlusion-robust scene recovery remains a major challenge in computational imaging, particularly in natural environments where dense foreground vegetation severely limits visibility. We propose a vision-reasoning-guided light field occlusion removal framework that combines the visibility recovery capability of light field integration (LFI) with the semantic reasoning capacity of vision-language models (VLMs). Multi-view observations are first integrated via LFI to suppress foreground occlusions and produce an initial visibility-enhanced representation. A VLM is then incorporated as a conditional semantic prior to restore degraded structures and recover fine details, guided by the observed measurements. To improve recovery consistency and reduce hallucination artifacts, we introduce a multi-sample fusion strategy that aggregates multiple generated hypotheses into a unified estimate. Experimental results on synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate state-of-the-art performance, achieving the highest average SSIM across four synthetic light field benchmark scenes (4-Syn) and strong generalization across structured and unstructured acquisition settings. These results highlight the effectiveness of combining physical imaging constraints with vision-language reasoning for robust perception under severe occlusion, with applicability to search-and-rescue and exploratory robotic navigation.

AI.tldr: A vision-reasoning-guided light field occlusion removal framework that fuses light field integration (LFI) with a vision-language model (VLM) to recover visibility under heavy occlusion, aided by multi-sample fusion to reduce artifacts; achieves state-of-the-art SSIM on 4-Syn and generalizes across structured/unstructured acquisitions.
AI.motivation: Occlusion-heavy natural environments (dense vegetation) severely limit visibility; prior methods struggle to recover occluded regions robustly; need to combine physical imaging constraints with semantic perception to improve reliability and reduce hallucinations.
AI.method: 1) Use light field integration (LFI) to fuse multi-view data and suppress foreground occlusions, producing an initial visibility-enhanced representation; 2) incorporate a vision-language model as a conditional semantic prior to restore degraded structures and details guided by measurements; 3) apply a multi-sample fusion strategy to aggregate multiple generated hypotheses into a unified, consistent estimate.
AI.result: Experimental results on synthetic and real-world datasets show state-of-the-art performance, including the highest average SSIM on four synthetic light field scenes (4-Syn) and strong generalization across both structured and unstructured acquisition settings.
AI.conclusion: The integration of physical imaging constraints with vision-language reasoning enables robust perception under severe occlusion, with practical implications for search-and-rescue and exploratory robotic navigation; multi-sample fusion helps mitigate hallucinations and improve consistency.

title: Residual-Space Evolutionary Optimization via Flow-based Generative Models
comment: Accepted by ICML 2026 Workshop SPIGM, 5 pages, 3 figures
summary: Data editing with generative methods typically requires differentiable objectives and gradient-based search. However, these assumptions break down in flow-based settings, where edits are performed through forward and backward integration and often involve non-differentiable or black-box objectives. We introduce residual-space evolutionary optimization, a model-agnostic framework that addresses this gap by combining flow-based generative editing with evolutionary algorithms. Building on the observation that conditional flow matching (CFM) can disentangle condition-controlled factors from instance-specific residuals, our framework directly operates in residual space and separates two complementary search regimes: self-pollination performs local exploitation through feature-preserving residual refinement, and cross-pollination promotes broader exploration by recombining residuals across heterogeneous samples. As a proof of concept, we validate on MorphoMNIST, a benchmark dataset for counterfactual generation, and on crystal data, demonstrating that this exploration--exploitation decomposition provides a useful mechanism for balancing target alignment, instance preservation, and diversity, and extends beyond images to real-world scientific domains.

AI.tldr: A model-agnostic framework that uses residual-space evolutionary optimization for flow-based generative editing, leveraging conditional flow matching to separate condition-controlled changes from instance residuals, with self-pollination and cross-pollination search regimes; validated on MorphoMNIST and crystal data.
AI.motivation: Flow-based edits falter with non-differentiable or black-box objectives and rely on gradient-based search. There is a need for a model-agnostic approach that can operate in forward/backward-integrated settings and handle non-differentiable targets.
AI.method: Define residual-space evolutionary optimization that operates in the residuals of conditional flow matching (CFM). Introduce two search regimes: self-pollination for local, feature-preserving residual refinement (exploitation) and cross-pollination for broader exploration by recombining residuals across heterogeneous samples (exploration). Combine flow-based generative editing with evolutionary algorithms in a framework that is agnostic to the underlying model.
AI.result: The framework offers an exploration–exploitation decomposition that balances target alignment, instance preservation, and diversity. Proof-of-concept experiments on MorphoMNIST (counterfactuals) and crystal data demonstrate generality beyond images to real-world scientific domains.
AI.conclusion: Residual-space evolutionary optimization provides a practical, model-agnostic mechanism for flow-based editing that can accommodate non-differentiable objectives and extend to domains beyond images, enabling balanced editing with preserved identity and diverse outcomes.

title: LOKI: Memory-Free Null-Space Constrained Lifelong Knowledge Editing
summary: Lifelong knowledge editing aims to efficiently and sequentially update language models over time, as new knowledge becomes available or when the model makes mistakes, while preserving acceptable performance on past knowledge. One unresolved challenge is that existing methods modify a fixed set of layers for all new knowledge samples, reducing flexibility and increasing catastrophic forgetting. Another is requiring access to previous knowledge and extensive pre-processing to obtain data statistics. To address these challenges, we introduce LOKI, a novel approach that uses dynamic layer selection based on the Hilbert-Schmidt Independence Criterion and projects gradient updates onto the null-space of the model weights, bypassing the requirement for previous knowledge access. We show that LOKI achieves superior performance to existing approaches across a wide variety of experiments, achieving up to a 14\% improvement in average accuracy.

AI.tldr: LOKI introduces dynamic layer selection via HSIC and null-space gradient projection to enable flexible, data-free lifelong knowledge editing, surpassing fixed-layer methods with up to 14% average accuracy gains.
AI.motivation: To efficiently and sequentially update language models with new knowledge or fix mistakes while preserving performance on past knowledge; existing approaches are inflexible (use a fixed set of layers) and require access to prior data statistics.
AI.method: Dynamic layer selection guided by the Hilbert-Schmidt Independence Criterion (HSIC) to determine which layers to modify, and projection of gradient updates onto the null-space of the model weights to avoid altering previously learned information; the approach does not require access to previous knowledge statistics.
AI.result: Empirically outperforms existing lifelong editing methods across diverse experiments, achieving up to a 14% improvement in average accuracy.
AI.conclusion: LOKI provides a flexible, data-efficient lifelong editing framework by combining adaptive layer updates with gradient projections, reducing forgetting and removing the need for prior data access.

title: QG-MIL: A Gated Transformer Aggregator for Domain-Agnostic Multiple Instance Learning in Medical Imaging
summary: Attention-based Multiple Instance Learning aggregators in medical imaging are prone to attention concentration, producing overconfident and unstable predictions. We introduce QG-MIL, a gated transformer aggregator that addresses this through four synergistic architectural components: RMSNorm-based pre-normalization, per-head QK normalization, fine-grained attention output gating, and SwiGLU-style feed-forward modules. Together, these design choices stabilize training and distribute attention more uniformly across instances without auxiliary losses, masking, or multi-stage regularization. We evaluate QG-MIL across six benchmarks spanning whole-slide pathology and cell-level hematology, covering two fundamentally different MIL scales. The best-performing QG-MIL variants outperform leading baselines on all six benchmarks, with an average improvement of +6.1 mean macro F1 points. Attention overlays and attention mass analysis confirm more distributed instance weighting. Ablation studies show that while individual components can match the full model on specific datasets, the QG-MIL design provides the most consistent cross-domain performance and tightest variance when compared to selected baselines. We release a configurable implementation to support reproducibility at: https://github.com/unica-visual-intelligence-lab/QG-MIL

AI.tldr: QG-MIL introduces a gated transformer aggregator for MIL in medical imaging to reduce attention concentration and improve cross-domain generalization; it integrates RMSNorm pre-normalization, per-head QK normalization, output gating, and SwiGLU FFN, achieving +6.1 macro F1 on six benchmarks and more distributed attention without extra losses.
AI.motivation: Attention-based MIL often concentrates attention on a few instances, causing overconfident, unstable predictions and poor generalization; a more distributed, stable aggregation is needed across diverse MIL scales (whole-slide pathology to cell-level hematology).
AI.method: Design of QG-MIL with four components: RMSNorm-based pre-normalization; per-head QK normalization; fine-grained attention output gating; SwiGLU-style feed-forward networks. Evaluate on six benchmarks spanning two MIL scales; compare to baselines; perform attention overlays and ablations; provide open-source release.
AI.result: Best QG-MIL variants outperform baselines on all six benchmarks; average +6.1 macro F1; attention overlays show more distributed instance weighting; ablations show components contribute, with cross-domain consistency; code release.
AI.conclusion: QG-MIL offers a robust, cross-domain MIL aggregator that stabilizes training and distributes attention without auxiliary losses or multi-stage regularization; the architecture yields consistent gains across diverse MIL scales; potential for broad applicability in medical imaging MIL tasks.

title: Multi-Head Attention-Based Feature Extractor Integration with Soft Actor-Critic for Porosity Prediction and Process Parameter Optimization in Additive Manufacturing
summary: Additive manufacturing process optimization requires precise parameter control to minimize defects such as porosity. Traditional reinforcement learning (RL) approaches using discrete action spaces suffer from slow convergence and susceptibility to local optima, limiting their effectiveness for high-precision manufacturing tasks. This study addresses these limitations by employing a continuous action space combined with a novel architecture that integrates a multi-head attention mechanism with the Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) algorithm. The attention-based feature extractor enhances the agent's ability to capture subtle variations in low-dimensional input features, enabling more effective exploration-exploitation balance for navigating value spaces with local minima. We validate our approach on porosity prediction and process parameter optimization in laser powder bed fusion, demonstrating faster convergence and higher final reward values compared to standard RL methods including DQN, PPO, TD3, and vanilla SAC. The proposed methodology achieves a convergence value of 322.79 within 14 episodes, outperforming existing approaches while maintaining stability throughout training.

AI.tldr: Continuous-action SAC with a multi-head attention-based feature extractor improves reinforcement learning for laser powder bed fusion process optimization, yielding faster convergence and higher final rewards than DQN, PPO, TD3, and vanilla SAC; demonstrated convergence value 322.79 in 14 episodes.
AI.motivation: Discrete-action RL approaches struggle with slow convergence and getting trapped in local optima when optimizing high-precision manufacturing parameters, where porosity and defect minimization require precise, continuous control.
AI.method: Adopt a continuous action space and integrate a multi-head attention mechanism as a feature extractor within the Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) framework. The attention module purportedly better captures subtle variations in low-dimensional input features to balance exploration and exploitation in value spaces with local minima. Empirical validation on porosity prediction and parameter optimization in laser powder bed fusion, compared against DQN, PPO, TD3, and vanilla SAC.
AI.result: Sample-efficient learning with faster convergence and higher final reward values. Reported convergence value of 322.79 within 14 episodes, outperforming baselines and maintaining training stability.
AI.conclusion: The proposed attention-augmented continuous-action SAC framework improves optimization efficiency and stability for AM processes, with potential to reduce porosity-related defects. Further work could explore broader validation, ablation studies, and extension to multi-objective optimization and hardware experiments.

title: Multi-Granular Attention-Driven Reinforcement Learning Framework for Web Intelligent Enhancement Systems
comment: 2026 3rd International Conference on Integrated Intelligence and Communication Systems (ICIICS), 6 Pages
summary: From the past few years, web intelligent enhancement systems increasingly rely on heterogeneous and dynamic web data to deliver personalized, context-aware services. However, traditional machine learning, deep learning, and reinforcement learning models often struggle with semantic understanding, adaptability, and scalability in continuously evolving web environments.
