In this research, a Multi-Granular Attention-based Reinforcement Web Intelligent Enhancement System (MGAR-WIES) is proposed to address the challenges by integrating semantic graph modeling, attention mechanisms, and adaptive reinforcement learning. Initially, heterogeneous web data comprising structured, semi-structured and unstructured sources are collected and preprocessed for generating unified feature representations. These representations are transformed into a dynamic semantic graph, where entities and their relationships are modeled by using graph embeddings enhanced by attention mechanisms for capturing both local relevance and global contextual dependencies. Subsequently, an adaptive multi-agent reinforcement learning strategy leverages the attention-aware semantic states to optimize personalized web actions like content recommendation, navigation optimization, and service adaptation. Finally, the continuous online feedback is further integrated to update graph representations and learning policies in real time by ensuring sustained adaptability and performance. The proposed MGAR-WIES acheived better results in terms of accuracy (80%) when compared with existing approaches.

AI.tldr: MGAR-WIES integrates heterogeneous web data into a dynamic semantic graph using attention-augmented embeddings and adaptive multi-agent reinforcement learning to optimize personalized web actions, reporting 80% accuracy over baselines; however, task, data, and evaluation details are under-specified.
AI.motivation: Address semantic understanding, adaptability, and scalability challenges in continuously evolving web environments by combining semantic graph modeling, attention mechanisms, and reinforcement learning to enable personalized, context-aware web services.
AI.method: Collect heterogeneous structured, semi-structured, and unstructured data; generate unified feature representations; construct a dynamic semantic graph with entities and relations; apply graph embeddings enhanced by attention to capture local relevance and global context; deploy adaptive multi-agent reinforcement learning using attention-aware semantic states to optimize actions such as content recommendation, navigation optimization, and service adaptation; incorporate continuous online feedback to update graph representations and policies in real time.
AI.result: Reported accuracy of 80% in comparison with existing approaches; claims better performance, but lacks specifics on tasks, datasets, baselines, and statistical validation.
AI.conclusion: MGAR-WIES shows promise by integrating semantic graphs, attention, and multi-agent reinforcement learning for web personalization; however, detailed evaluation, reproducibility, and scalability analyses are needed, including dataset descriptions, baselines, task definitions, and privacy considerations.

title: ReA-OVCD: Reliability-Aware Open-Vocabulary Change Detection via Semantic and Spatial Refinement
summary: Unlike traditional remote sensing change detection that relies on predefined categories, Open-Vocabulary Change Detection (OVCD) identifies land cover changes flexibly using arbitrary text prompts. However, existing methods suffer from an inherent trade-off when modeling changes: instance-level comparison overlooks fine-grained semantic variations (e.g., partial building extensions), while direct pixel comparison proves unreliable, yielding unstable responses and boundary artifacts due to semantic ambiguity and spatial inconsistency. To this end, we propose an efficient training-free Reliability-Aware Open-Vocabulary Change Detection (ReA-OVCD) framework. It first derives candidate change regions from pixel-wise semantic discrepancies to ensure flexible and detailed localization. To ensure reliability, it subsequently introduces a collaborative refinement strategy to explicitly model change validity from both semantic and spatial perspectives. Specifically, we develop a Semantic Change Reasoning (SCR) module that reassesses changes by jointly analyzing distributional divergence and response variation, enabling the suppression of incidental inconsistencies while preserving reliable semantic shifts. In addition, a Boundary-aware Change Refinement (BCR) module is designed to mitigate artifacts stemming from boundary misalignment and uncertainty through validating whether candidate regions are supported by reliable interior pixels. Extensive experiments across multiple datasets (LEVIR-CD, WHU-CD, DSIFN, and SECOND) demonstrate that our method consistently outperforms state-of-the-art approaches, achieving $\mathrm{F}_{1}^{C}$ improvements of 2.13\% to 9.75\% with higher computational efficiency. The code is publicly available at \https://github.com/Funny0101/ReA-OVCD

AI.tldr: Proposes ReA-OVCD, a training-free, reliability-aware open-vocabulary change detection framework that derives candidate change regions from pixel-wise semantic discrepancies and refines them via semantic reasoning (SCR) and boundary refinement (BCR) to improve reliability and boundary quality, achieving state-of-the-art F1^C gains on multiple datasets with higher efficiency.
AI.motivation: Open-vocabulary change detection enables flexible prompts beyond predefined categories but suffers from reliability issues due to semantic ambiguity and spatial inconsistency. Traditional instance-level change methods miss fine-grained variations and pixel-wise comparisons produce unstable responses and boundary artifacts. A training-free, reliability-aware approach can jointly address semantic and spatial aspects to improve detection quality.
AI.method: 1) Derive candidate change regions from pixel-wise semantic discrepancies to enable fine-grained localization. 2) Semantic Change Reasoning (SCR) module jointly analyzes distributional divergence and response variation to suppress incidental inconsistencies while preserving genuine semantic shifts. 3) Boundary-aware Change Refinement (BCR) module validates candidate regions by checking support from reliable interior pixels, mitigating boundary misalignment artifacts. 4) Training-free framework designed for efficiency; evaluated on multiple datasets.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on LEVIR-CD, WHU-CD, DSIFN, and SECOND show consistent improvements in F1^C ranging from 2.13% to 9.75% over SOTA methods, with higher computational efficiency. Code available at the provided GitHub link.
AI.conclusion: ReA-OVCD delivers reliable open-vocabulary change detection by integrating semantic and spatial reasoning without training, achieving strong performance and efficiency gains across diverse datasets.

title: ScaffoldAgent: Utility-Guided Dynamic Outline Optimization for Open-Ended Deep Research
summary: Open-ended deep research (OEDR) requires systems to acquire knowledge through multi-round retrieval and generate coherent long-form reports. The outline plays a central role as a structural scaffold that coordinates retrieval, evidence organization, and generation. However, existing methods either fix the outline before writing or refine it with local heuristics, leading to scaffold drift under continuous information accumulation and delayed feedback for evaluating outline modifications. We propose ScaffoldAgent, a utility-guided dynamic outline optimization framework for OEDR. ScaffoldAgent models outline evolution as a structured decision process with three operations: Expansion, Contraction, and Revision, enabling controlled updates to the report scaffold. It further introduces a utility-guided feedback mechanism that estimates the downstream value of each outline operation from retrieval gain, structural coherence, and trial-generation quality. The resulting utility signal guides node selection, operation scheduling, and termination during inference. Experiments on DeepResearch Bench and DeepResearch Gym show that ScaffoldAgent consistently improves long-form report generation and factual grounding over existing deep research agents.

AI.tldr: ScaffoldAgent presents a dynamic, utility-guided outline optimization framework for open-ended deep research (OEDR) that evolves the report scaffold via Expansion, Contraction, and Revision, using a downstream utility signal (retrieval gain, coherence, trial-generation quality) to guide selection, scheduling, and termination, achieving improved long-form generation and factual grounding on DeepResearch Bench/Gym.
AI.motivation: Current OEDR pipelines fix or locally refine outlines, causing scaffold drift as information accumulates and delaying feedback on outline changes. A dynamic, utility-guided outline that adapts during generation could improve coherence and grounding.
AI.method: Define three outline operations (Expansion, Contraction, Revision). Model outline evolution as a structured decision process. Introduce a utility-guided feedback mechanism estimating downstream value from retrieval gain, structural coherence, and trial-generation quality. Use this utility to guide node selection, operation scheduling, and termination during inference.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on DeepResearch Bench and DeepResearch Gym shows ScaffoldAgent consistently improves long-form report generation quality and factual grounding compared to existing deep research agents.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic, utility-guided outline optimization effectively mitigates scaffold drift and enhances OEDR performance; the approach offers a principled framework for aligning retrieval, structure, and generation in long-form tasks.

title: Efficiently Representing Algorithms With Chain-of-Thought Transformers
summary: The increasing popularity of \emph{reasoning} models -- language models that output a series of reasoning or thought tokens before producing an answer -- is justified, in part, by theoretical results showing that chain-of-thought (CoT) transformers can simulate Turing machines, and thus perform arbitrary computation. However, the Turing machine, while suitable for complexity-theoretic analysis, is not convenient, intuitive, or efficient for discussing algorithms. Algorithms are typically designed and analyzed at a higher level of abstraction, captured by the \emph{Word RAM} model with random-access memory and unit-cost operations on $\bigO(\log n)$-bit words. As a result, Word RAM algorithms can be substantially more efficient than their Turing machine counterparts, raising the question: \emph{Can CoT transformers efficiently simulate Word RAM algorithms?} For instance, can they sort $n$ items in $\bigO(n \log n)$ steps or run Dijkstra's algorithm in $\bigO(E + V \log V)$ steps? We answer affirmatively, up to poly-logarithmic overhead. We first establish this for finite-precision transformers with poly-logarithmic width and rightmost unique hard attention, then strengthen the result to two more practical settings with finite width and log-precision: \emph{continuous} CoT, where reasoning takes the form of vectors rather than tokens, and a \emph{hybrid} architecture in which transformer layers sit atop a recurrent (linear RNN) layer. In all three cases, we find that CoT \emph{can} efficiently simulate any Word RAM algorithm with only a poly-logarithmic overhead in $n$. This overhead reduces to log-square when the Word RAM has a ``flat'' instruction set, and only logarithmic for multiplication-free flat instructions -- in stark contrast to known CoT simulations of Turing machines, which require quadratic overhead over Word RAM.

AI.tldr: CoT transformers can efficiently simulate Word RAM algorithms with poly-logarithmic overhead in n across multiple practical architectures, not just Turing-machine simulations. The overhead ranges from poly-log to log^2 or log for special instruction sets, under finite-width with rightmost unique hard attention, continuous CoT, and a hybrid RNN-backed setup.
AI.motivation: To evaluate the computational capabilities of chain-of-thought transformers in a practically relevant model of computation (Word RAM) rather than the traditional Turing machine, clarifying whether CoT can support efficient algorithmic reasoning.
AI.method: Construct simulations of Word RAM primitives inside CoT under three settings: (i) finite-precision transformers with poly-log width and rightmost unique hard attention; (ii) continuous CoT where reasoning is vector-based; (iii) a hybrid architecture with transformer layers atop a linear RNN. In each setting, analyze overhead to emulate Word RAM operations and algorithms.
AI.result: CoT can simulate any Word RAM algorithm with poly-logarithmic overhead in input size n. The overhead improves to O(log^2 n) for Word RAMs with a flat instruction set and becomes O(log n) for multiplication-free flat instructions.
AI.conclusion: CoT transformers possess practical, near-optimal algorithmic simulation power under realistic architectures, outperforming known Turing-machine-based CoT simulations which incur quadratic overhead; this supports the potential of CoT reasoning for implementing and analyzing efficient algorithms.

title: PU-UNet: Stable Multiplicative Interactions for Medical Image Segmentation
summary: Many dense prediction networks rely on additive feature transformations and model higher-order feature interactions only implicitly. Product units provide an explicit mechanism for multiplicative feature modeling, but their logarithmic--exponential formulation can cause numerical instability, which has limited their use in deep dense prediction networks. In this work, we propose Product-Unit U-Net (PU-UNet), a residual U-Net that integrates stable product-unit residual blocks into rich low-resolution stages for medical image segmentation. The proposed formulation combines smooth positivity mapping with log-domain clipping, enabling stable multiplicative feature learning with negligible computational overhead. On ISIC 2018, Kvasir-SEG, and BUSI, PU-UNet achieves Dice scores of 0.942, 0.959, and up to 0.925, respectively. Compared with a matched Residual U-Net baseline, PU-UNet consistently improves Dice and IoU while keeping parameters, FLOPs, and inference latency nearly unchanged, and reduces the image-level false-positive rate on normal BUSI cases from 0.077 to zero. Ablation studies suggest that the gains are associated with product-unit interactions, are strongest under low-resolution placement, and benefit from the proposed stabilization design. These results suggest that stable product-unit residual learning can be an effective way to enhance U-Net-style segmentation networks with explicit multiplicative interactions.

AI.tldr: PU-UNet introduces stable product-unit residual blocks into a U-Net, enabling explicit multiplicative feature interactions with log-domain stabilization. It achieves consistent Dice/IoU gains on medical segmentation datasets with negligible overhead, and reduces false positives in BUSI. Gains are strongest with low-resolution placement.
AI.motivation: Dense prediction networks largely rely on additive feature transforms. Product units offer explicit multiplicative interactions but suffer from numerical instability in the log-exp formulation, hindering deep networks. A stable, low-overhead multiplicative mechanism could improve segmentation performance.
AI.method: Integrate stable product-unit residual blocks into a residual U-Net (PU-UNet). The design uses smooth positivity mapping and log-domain clipping to enable stable multiplicative learning at low-resolution stages, preserving computational efficiency.
AI.result: PU-UNet achieves Dice scores of 0.942 (ISIC 2018), 0.959 (Kvasir-SEG), and up to 0.925 (BUSI). It outperforms a matched Residual U-Net in Dice and IoU with nearly identical parameters, FLOPs, and latency, and eliminates image-level false positives on normal BUSI cases (from 0.077 to 0). Ablation indicates benefits stem from product-unit interactions, especially at low-resolution placement and the stabilization design.
AI.conclusion: Stable product-unit residual learning is an effective mechanism to introduce explicit multiplicative interactions in U-Net-based segmentation without added computational burden.

title: Learning to Prompt: Improving Student Engagement with Adaptive LLM-based High-School Tutoring
summary: LLMs can personalize education, although current static-prompt tutoring systems struggle to adapt to diverse academic disciplines. We develop and test a system with subject-aware prompting, based on 14 pedagogical features (e.g., tutor scaffolding, student understanding) extracted from raw transcripts. We first train a prompt routing model in a simulation environment, and then deploy it for online adaptation with actual high-school students. The simulation benchmark shows the router outperforming two static baselines ($0.694$ vs. $0.647$ and $0.64$, $p<0.001$). A/B testing ($N=656$ conversations from 359 students) shows sim-to-real transfer where the model switches from analytical to scaffolding learning strategies. Our adaptive prompt selection mechanism improves instructional efficiency, maintains pedagogical quality and reduces interactions by around 3 turns ($p=0.007$). While a greedy router achieves a comparable exercise conversion rate with the baseline ($19.1\%$ vs. $19.6\%$), a stochastic router that samples strategies leads to a higher conversion rate ($28.1\%$).

AI.tldr: Subject-aware prompting with 14 pedagogical features, trained via simulation and deployed in real classrooms; adaptive routing improves efficiency and conversion, with stochastic routing giving the best performance.
AI.motivation: Overcome the rigidity of static-prompt tutoring that cannot adapt to diverse disciplines; enable LLMs to personalize instruction using pedagogy-informed strategies.
AI.method: Extract 14 pedagogical features from transcripts; train a prompt routing model in a simulation environment; deploy online with high-school students; compare greedy vs stochastic routing; evaluate instructional efficiency, pedagogical quality, and exercise conversion in real and simulated settings.
AI.result: In simulation, the router outperforms two static baselines (0.694 vs. 0.647 and 0.64; p<0.001). A/B test on 656 conversations from 359 students shows sim-to-real transfer with a shift from analytical to scaffolding strategies. Adaptive prompt selection reduces interactions by ~3 turns (p=0.007). Greedy router yields 19.1% vs baseline 19.6% conversion (comparable); stochastic router achieves 28.1% conversion (highest).
AI.conclusion: Subject-aware prompting with sim-to-real transfer enhances adaptive tutoring; stochastic strategy sampling can substantially boost engagement/conversion, suggesting scalable, pedagogy-informed LLM tutoring across disciplines.

title: Efficient Neural Network Model Selection for Few-Class Application Datasets
summary: While much effort has focused on developing and benchmarking high-performance neural networks, less attention has been given to how dataset properties, known to practitioners, can guide efficient model selection. Neural models are typically evaluated on datasets with thousands of classes, yet many real-world applications involve fewer than ten. To address this understudied but common setting, we develop a measure of classification difficulty based on data-side properties and show how it enables more efficient model selection for few-class datasets, where traditional approaches are less effective. We term this phenomenon "few-class distinctiveness". Our metric allows comparison of models and datasets 6 to 29$\times$ faster than repeated training and testing. Leveraging this insight, we extend scaled model families below the smallest published models, achieving greater efficiency at similar accuracy, for example models up to 42% smaller than YOLOv5-nano for a mobile robot task. Targeting resource-constrained applications, we demonstrate few-class model selection across mobile robot, drone, and IoT scenarios, highlighting practical gains in efficiency without sacrificing performance.

AI.tldr: Introduce a data-centric metric, few-class distinctiveness, to guide model selection for datasets with few classes, enabling faster evaluation and enabling smaller models with comparable accuracy in resource-constrained settings.
AI.motivation: Many real-world tasks involve few classes (often <10). Traditional model selection relies on large, multi-class benchmarks, making evaluation expensive and sometimes ineffective for scarce-class scenarios. A data-side difficulty measure could streamline model choice and deployment.
AI.method: Define and validate a data-centered classification difficulty metric (few-class distinctiveness) that correlates with model performance on few-class tasks; use it to rank models/datasets and guide selection. Extend scaled model families to sizes smaller than previously published models to improve efficiency; demonstrate via mobile robot, drone, and IoT tasks.
AI.result: The metric enables 6–29× faster comparison across models/datasets than repeated training/testing. Demonstrates that scaled-down models (e.g., up to 42% smaller than YOLOv5-nano) can achieve similar accuracy for few-class problems, with practical gains for resource-constrained deployments.
AI.conclusion: A data-property-based measure (few-class distinctiveness) facilitates efficient model selection and enables deployment of smaller, resource-efficient models without sacrificing performance in few-class applications.

title: FlowMaps: Modeling Long-Term Multimodal Object Dynamics with Flow Matching
summary: Joint spatial and temporal understanding of 3D scenes is a crucial requirement for robots deployed in everyday household environments. Such agents must not only comprehend and navigate spatial layouts, but also reason about how these spaces evolve over time. In particular, humans interact with objects daily, causing them to change position throughout the environment and making it difficult for robots to reliably associate current observations with previously seen objects. However, these interactions are not random: human habits and routines induce spatio-temporally consistent patterns in object locations, which robotic agents can potentially learn and then exploit for downstream tasks such as navigation. To this end, we introduce FlowMaps, a latent flow matching model for estimating multimodal distributions over the future locations of dynamic objects in a continuous 3D space. By learning the implicit dependencies among objects and their temporal evolution, FlowMaps predicts likely changes in object locations conditioned on past human interactions, while supporting generalization across previously unseen environments that share similar object routines. To demonstrate the utility of this method, we deploy FlowMaps in a downstream dynamic Object Navigation task in both simulated and real-world environments. Across more than 600 episodes, FlowMaps outperforms state-of-the-art approaches, showing that modeling object dynamics through continuous, multimodal spatio-temporal distributions improves robotic search and navigation in changing household environments. Code and additional material is available at https://fra-tsuna.github.io/flowmaps/.

AI.tldr: FlowMaps introduces a latent flow matching model to predict multimodal future object locations in continuous 3D space, conditioned on past human interactions, enabling generalization to unseen environments and improving dynamic Object Navigation; achieves superior performance over 600 episodes; code available.
AI.motivation: Robots in homes must interpret spatial layouts and temporal changes driven by human-object interactions. Humans create spatio-temporally consistent object routines; mastering these patterns can improve navigation under dynamics.
AI.method: Proposes FlowMaps, a latent flow matching model that learns multimodal distributions over future object locations in continuous 3D, capturing dependencies among objects and their evolution; predicts future changes conditioned on past interactions; designed to generalize to unseen environments with similar routines.
AI.result: Outperforms state-of-the-art on dynamic Object Navigation across 600 episodes in simulated and real-world tests; demonstrates improved search/navigation under dynamic object movement.
AI.conclusion: Modeling object dynamics as continuous multimodal spatio-temporal distributions enhances robustness of robotic navigation in changing households; code and materials available online.

title: FUSE: Frequency-domain Unification and Spectral Energy Alignment for Multi-modal Object Re-Identification
summary: Despite significant progress in multi-modal Re-Identification (ReID), existing methods tend to emphasize low-frequency cues. Consequently, they focus on attributes such as color, illumination, and coarse appearance, while overlooking mid and high-frequency structures that encode geometric, textural, and identity-discriminative details. This imbalance leads to incomplete spectral representations and unstable cross-modal alignment. To overcome these limitations, we introduce FUSE, a frequency-domain framework that reformulates multi-modal ReID as a two-stage process of spectral disentanglement and energy alignment. The proposed Spectral Decomposition Module (SDM) adaptively partitions features into low, mid, and high-frequency subspaces, enabling hierarchical spectral modeling. The Cross-Modal Alignment Module (CAM) further enforces energy alignment and subspace complementarity across modalities via frequency-consistency regularization. In addition, FUSE incorporates learnable frequency modulation to enhance robustness under varying illumination and heterogeneous sensor conditions. Extensive experiments on RGBNT201, RGBNT100, and MSVR310 show that FUSE achieves 9.1\% mAP and 9.5\% Rank-1 improvements, establishing an interpretable frequency-domain paradigm for multi-modal representation learning.

AI.tldr: Proposes FUSE, a frequency-domain framework for multi-modal ReID that disentangles spectral subspaces and aligns energy across modalities to improve robustness and performance, demonstrated with gains on RGBNT201/100 and MSVR310.
AI.motivation: Current multi-modal ReID methods emphasize low-frequency cues (color, illumination) and ignore mid/high-frequency information, resulting in incomplete representations and unstable cross-modal alignment.
AI.method: Two-stage approach: Spectral Decomposition Module (SDM) partitions features into low, mid, and high-frequency subspaces for hierarchical spectral modeling; Cross-Modal Alignment Module (CAM) enforces energy alignment and subspace complementarity via frequency-consistency regularization; includes learnable frequency modulation for robustness under varying illumination and heterogeneous sensors.
AI.result: Empirical improvements of 9.1% mAP and 9.5% Rank-1 on RGBNT201, RGBNT100, and MSVR310, indicating effective spectral-domain learning and cross-modal alignment.
AI.conclusion: Introduces an interpretable frequency-domain paradigm for multi-modal representation learning, showing improved performance and robustness through spectral disentanglement and energy alignment.

title: RACL: Reasoning-Agent Control Layers for Continuous Metaheuristic Learning
summary: This paper introduces RACL, a Reasoning-Agent Control Layer for metaheuristics. RACL places a reasoning agent above an existing optimizer. The agent does not replace the optimizer and does not modify business constraints. Instead, it controls the optimizer's internal search behavior by observing operational memory, reasoning over past behavior, formulating bounded hypotheses, testing interventions, evaluating outcomes, applying guardrails, consolidating useful policies and explaining its decisions. The experiment uses vehicle routing as a testbed, but the contribution is not a new routing solver, a particular ALNS configuration or a specific set of routing rules. The contribution is the RACL method: a way for a reasoning agent to discover, validate, consolidate and explain algorithmic control rules for a metaheuristic. In the current experimental setting, RACL improves or ties the Operational Memory Policy in 21 of 21 feasible cases and improves or ties a non-reasoning Stagnation-Triggered Policy in 18 of 21 feasible cases, with an average RACL vs STP cost delta of -0.641%. In the Sevilla-9/10 runtime sample, RACL improves average cost by -8.337% versus Fixed and -1.605% versus STP without showing material computational overhead. During the proof-of-concept, Codex was used as an in-the-loop reasoning agent observing executions, interpreting logs and proposing live bounded interventions. The policy proxy was later used only to make quantitative evaluation reproducible.

AI.tldr: RACL: a reasoning-agent control layer above metaheuristics that discovers and explains control rules to guide an optimizer without altering constraints, improving performance on vehicle-routing benchmarks with minimal overhead.
AI.motivation: To bridge the gap between fixed metaheuristic policies and adaptive, interpretable control by leveraging a reasoning agent to observe memory, hypothesize, intervene, and explain policy decisions, thereby enhancing search effectiveness while preserving problem constraints.
AI.method: Develop RACL architecture that sits above an optimizer; the agent observes operational memory, forms bounded hypotheses, tests interventions, evaluates outcomes, applies guardrails, consolidates policies, and explains decisions. Experimental PoC uses Codex as the reasoning agent; vehicle routing as testbed; compare against Operational Memory Policy (OMP) and a Stagnation-Triggered Policy (STP); quantify cost deltas and runtime overhead. Reproducibility via policy proxy.
AI.result: RACL matches or surpasses OMP in 21/21 feasible cases; matches or surpasses STP in 18/21 feasible cases; average cost delta vs STP = -0.641%. Sevilla-9/10 sample: -8.337% vs Fixed, -1.605% vs STP; no material computational overhead.
AI.conclusion: RACL provides a general methodology to discover, validate, consolidate, and explain algorithmic control rules for metaheuristics; not a new solver or fixed rule set; demonstrated effectiveness in vehicle routing and shown potential for reproducibility and interpretability via a reasoning agent approach.

title: OnDeFog: Online Decision Transformer under Frame Dropping
summary: In challenging real-world reinforcement learning applications, communication delays or sensor failures often cause frame dropping, in which the agent cannot receive the dropped states and associated rewards. To address the performance degradation caused by frame dropping, the Decision Transformer under Random Frame Dropping (DeFog) was developed by incorporating additional mechanisms into the decision transformer to tackle frame dropping. Although DeFog can mitigate performance degradation in frame-dropping environments, since DeFog is an offline learning method, it struggles to effectively generalize to novel states not adequately represented in the training dataset. In this study, we propose OnDeFog, which integrates the mechanisms in DeFog with the online decision transformer (ODT), an online reinforcement learning method that learns policies through direct environmental interaction. Comprehensive experimental evaluation demonstrates that our proposed OnDeFog achieves superior performance compared to ODT in environments characterized by high dropping frame rate and outperforms DeFog on datasets containing a large amount of low-reward data.

AI.tldr: OnDeFog extends DeFog by integrating its mechanisms with Online Decision Transformer (ODT) to enable online reinforcement learning under frame dropping, achieving better performance than ODT and DeFog in respective challenging regimes.
AI.motivation: Frame dropping due to delays or sensor failures degrades RL performance. Offline DeFog lacks generalization to unseen states; an online method is needed to adapt to new data, improving robustness under high frame-dropping and low-reward scenarios.
AI.method: Integrate DeFog's frame-dropping mitigation mechanisms into the Online Decision Transformer (ODT) to form OnDeFog, enabling online interaction with the environment while preserving DeFog's robustness to dropped frames.
AI.result: OnDeFog outperforms ODT in environments with a high frame-dropping rate and surpasses DeFog on datasets containing a large amount of low-reward data.
AI.conclusion: OnDeFog effectively combines DeFog's robustness to frame dropping with the online learning capability of ODT, yielding improved performance and generalization in challenging, frame-dropping RL scenarios.

title: Mobile Target Search with Imperfect Perception: A Partially Observable Stochastic Game Theoretical Approach
summary: This paper investigates mobile target search under imperfect perceptions caused by sensor limitations, malicious jamming, or communication noise. Searchers and targets operate in a grid-shaped area with bounded mobility, leading to a dynamic interplay between search and evasion. To capture this adversarial interaction under imperfect perceptions, we adopt the partially observable stochastic game (POSG) approach, which generalizes partially observable Markov decision processes (POMDPs) by incorporating target intelligence. To handle false alarms and missed detections caused by perceptual uncertainties, we propose a novel detectability concept to determine whether a search strategy guarantees eventual detection, and provide sufficient detectability criteria based on stochastic recurrence analysis. We further develop a server-assisted distributed algorithm that utilizes the aggregative potential game structure for searchers and a KL-divergence-based reduction for target prediction. Numerical simulations validate the effectiveness of the proposed algorithm and support the detectability analysis.

AI.tldr: A POSG-based framework for resilient mobile target search under imperfect perceptions, featuring a detectability concept, a server-assisted distributed algorithm leveraging aggregative potential games, and KL-divergence-based target prediction; validated by simulations.
AI.motivation: Addresses search under sensor limitations, jamming, and noise in adversarial settings, where partial observability and target intelligence complicate detection.
AI.method: Formulates searcher-target interaction as a POSG; introduces detectability criteria via stochastic recurrence; develops a distributed algorithm with aggregative potential game structure and KL-divergence-based reduction for prediction; tests via numerical simulations.
AI.result: Numerical simulations confirm effectiveness of the algorithm and support the detectability analysis.
AI.conclusion: The approach demonstrates a tractable, scalable framework for ensuring eventual detection under imperfect perceptions, combining game-theoretic and information-theoretic techniques in a POSG setting.

title: See-and-Reach: Precise Vision-Language Navigation for UAVs within the Field of View
summary: UAV Vision-Language Navigation (UAV-VLN) is typically formulated as a holistic search-and-reach problem, where long-range target discovery and final target approach are optimized and evaluated jointly. This formulation makes it difficult to assess a critical capability of aerial embodied agents, namely whether a UAV can accurately ground a visible target and translate vision-language evidence into precise 3D motion once the target enters its field of view. To address this limitation, we introduce UAV-VLN-FOV, a target-visible navigation task that isolates the see-and-reach stage and enables a more diagnostic evaluation of terminal reaching ability. We further propose 3DG-VLN, a vision-language waypoint prediction framework guided by dynamic 3D direction cues to enhance fine-grained visual grounding and spatial direction alignment for precise target reaching. Specifically, 3DG-VLN adaptively processes high-resolution front-view and downward-view observations to preserve fine-grained visual and geometric details for target grounding. It also updates the target-relative direction online during closed-loop navigation, allowing the agent to maintain spatial alignment with the target and reduce accumulated direction drift. To support this task, we construct a dedicated high-resolution benchmark which contains 2,717 trajectories with target-oriented high-level instructions, high-resolution front-view and downward-view egocentric observations, and continuous 3D waypoint annotations. Experiments show that 3DG-VLN outperforms competitive UAV-VLN baselines, achieving a 13.82\% improvement in success rate. Real-world trials further demonstrate the potential of 3DG-VLN for practical see-and-reach navigation. The source code and benchmark are available at https://github.com/xuefanfu/3DG-VLN.

AI.tldr: Introduces UAV-VLN-FOV to isolate see-and-reach, and 3DG-VLN to predict vision-language waypoints with dynamic 3D direction cues; builds a high-resolution 2,717-trajectory benchmark; achieves 13.82% SR improvement; real-world validation; code available.
AI.motivation: UAV Vision-Language Navigation (VLN) typically couples long-range search with final target approach, making it hard to diagnose the agent's terminal grounding and 3D motion accuracy once the target is visible. A diagnostic task focusing on see-and-reach is needed.
AI.method: 3DG-VLN uses high-resolution front-view and downward-view inputs, adaptively preserving fine-grained visual and geometric details for target grounding. It predicts vision-language waypoints guided by dynamic 3D direction cues and updates target-relative direction online during closed-loop navigation to maintain spatial alignment and reduce drift. A dedicated high-resolution benchmark with 2,717 trajectories, target-oriented instructions, and continuous 3D waypoint annotations is constructed.
AI.result: 3DG-VLN outperforms competitive UAV-VLN baselines, achieving a 13.82% improvement in success rate. Real-world trials demonstrate practical see-and-reach viability. Source code and benchmark are released.
AI.conclusion: The work enables diagnostic evaluation of the terminal reaching capability in UAV-VLN, showing that dynamic 3D direction cues and high-resolution grounding improve precision in see-and-reach tasks, and provides open resources to foster further research.

title: BIM-Edit: Benchmarking Large Language Models for IFC-Based Building Information Modeling
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly applied to computer-aided design (CAD) to generate design artifacts from textual instructions. In engineering practice, this requires more than creating new geometry, models must also understand existing scenes, edit them correctly, and preserve semantics and relations. However, many CAD benchmarks focus on creating new models rather than editing existing ones, and mostly evaluate geometric correctness. We introduce BIM-Edit, a benchmark for evaluating LLMs on natural-language editing of Building Information Models (BIM) represented in the Industry Foundation Classes (IFC) format. BIM provides a challenging testbed because building models encode geometry together with semantic and relational structure. BIM-Edit contains 324 editing tasks spanning 11 realistic building models and 36 synthetic scenes. Tasks are expressed using three instruction categories - direct, spatial, and topological - covering both explicit and scene-grounded edits. We evaluate outputs along three dimensions: geometric accuracy, semantic validity, and topological consistency. Across evaluated LLMs, the best-performing model achieves only 49.5% average score across the three metrics, and no model fully solves more than 3.4% of tasks. These results demonstrate a substantial gap between current LLM capabilities and the requirements of structured engineering design workflows.

AI.tldr: A BIM-Edit benchmark for natural-language editing of BIM/IFC models, with 324 tasks across 11 real models and 36 synthetic scenes, evaluating geometric accuracy, semantic validity, and topological consistency; current LLMs show limited capability (best ~49.5% average; none fully solves >3.4% of tasks).
AI.motivation: To evaluate LLMs on editing and reasoning over complex, semantically rich engineering models (BIM/IFC) where geometry, semantics, and topology must cohere, addressing a gap in existing benchmarks that focus mainly on generating new geometry.
AI.method: Construct BIM-Edit dataset comprising 324 editing tasks across 11 realistic building models and 36 synthetic scenes. Tasks are framed in three instruction categories (direct, spatial, topological) to cover explicit edits and scene-grounded edits. Outputs are evaluated along three axes: geometric accuracy, semantic validity, and topological consistency.
AI.result: Across evaluated LLMs, the best model achieves 49.5% average across the three metrics; no model fully solves more than 3.4% of tasks, signaling a substantial gap between current LLM capabilities and the demands of structured BIM editing workflows.
AI.conclusion: Current LLMs struggle with semantically and topologically constrained editing of BIM models. BIM-Edit provides a challenging benchmark to drive research toward integrating natural language understanding with rigorous semantic/topological reasoning in engineering workflows.

title: Federated Bilevel Performative Prediction
summary: Federated bilevel optimization is widely used for nested learning problems across distributed clients, such as federated hyperparameter tuning and meta-learning under privacy and communication constraints. Most existing formulations assume fixed client data distributions, which can be violated by performativity, where deployed decisions reshape client behavior and data collection, inducing client-specific, decision-dependent distribution shift. We study federated bilevel performative prediction, where both upper-level (UL) and lower-level (LL) objectives are evaluated under client-dependent, decision-dependent distributions. We formalize the federated bilevel performatively stable (FBPS) point under a decoupled-risk perspective and provide sufficient conditions for its existence and uniqueness. We then develop two federated methods to compute the FBPS solution: FBi-RRM, which converges linearly under a contraction condition, and FBi-SGD, a communication-efficient stochastic method based on federated hypergradient estimation with convergence guarantees under diminishing step sizes when sensitivities are sufficiently small. Experiments on strategic regression and meta strategic classification validate the predicted stability thresholds and demonstrate improved meta-generalization over non-performative baselines, and CNN-based classification further demonstrates the practical effectiveness of the proposed methods in nonconvex neural network settings.

AI.tldr: Extends federated bilevel optimization to performative settings with decision-dependent client distributions, defines FBPS (federated bilevel performatively stable) and provides two algorithms (FBi-RRM and FBi-SGD) with theoretical guarantees and empirical validation, including meta-generalization improvements and CNN nonconvex effectiveness.
AI.motivation: Address distribution shift caused by deployed decisions in federated bilevel learning. Standard models assume fixed client distributions, but performativity can alter data collection and client behavior, requiring stability analysis and new algorithms with guarantees.
AI.method: Introduce FBPS as a decoupled-risk stable point for federated bilevel problems under decision-dependent distributions. Prove existence and uniqueness under sufficient conditions. Propose two algorithms: (1) FBi-RRM with linear convergence under a contraction condition, (2) FBi-SGD, a communication-efficient stochastic method using federated hypergradient estimation, with convergence under diminishing step sizes when sensitivities are small.
AI.result: Sufficient conditions guaranteeing FBPS existence and uniqueness. FBi-RRM achieves linear convergence under contraction. FBi-SGD converges with diminishing steps under small sensitivities. Empirical validation on strategic regression and meta strategic classification shows stability thresholds and improved meta-generalization; CNN experiments demonstrate practicality in nonconvex neural networks.
AI.conclusion: The FBPS framework and two algorithms enable robust federated bilevel learning under performative distributions, offering theoretical guarantees and practical gains in meta-learning and nonconvex settings, with potential for broader application in privacy- and communication-constrained federated learning.

title: Finetuning Vision-Language-Action Models Requires Fewer Layers Than You Think
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models pre-trained on massive video-robot datasets have revolutionized robotic manipulation, yet their multi-billion parameter architectures impose prohibitive computational burdens during downstream fine-tuning and real-time inference. In this work, we reveal a highly non-trivial architectural characteristic of these continuous control foundation policies (e.g., pi_0, GR00T-N1.5): despite being trained on diverse physical trajectories, they exhibit severe layer-wise representational redundancy. To exploit this, we introduce a structural compression pipeline that is entirely training-free, bypassing the need of existing methods to load full-scale models to learn optimized token reductions or dynamic layer selectors. Instead, using only a single forward pass via Centered Kernel Alignment to identify redundant layer features, we remove twin layers to permanently compress the model depth by up to 50% across both the VLM backbone and the continuous control policy head. Downstream fine-tuning of this streamlined architecture yields a dual acceleration benefit: a 40-50% reduction in training time and up to 30% faster real-time inference, while matching or exceeding full-scale base model performance. We comprehensively validate our method across three simulation benchmarks (LIBERO, RoboCasa, SimplerEnv) and 10 diverse real-world manipulation tasks across 4 unique robotic embodiments. These results prove that advanced VLAs require significantly fewer layers than previously assumed, offering a highly compute-efficient paradigm for scalable robot learning.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models for robotic manipulation have strong layer-wise redundancy and introduces a training-free structural compression that prunes layers using a single forward pass with Centered Kernel Alignment, cutting depth by up to 50% and accelerating training and inference while maintaining performance.
AI.motivation: Massive VLA models are computationally costly for fine-tuning and real-time use. Despite training on diverse trajectories, their representations are redundantly encoded across layers. A training-free compression could yield substantial speedups without retraining.
AI.method: A training-free compression pipeline uses Centered Kernel Alignment (CKA) to identify redundant layer features from a single forward pass and permanently remove twin layers, halving model depth in both the VLM backbone and the continuous-control policy head. The approach does not require loading full-scale models for optimization and is followed by downstream fine-tuning on the compressed architecture.
AI.result: The method achieves 40-50% training-time reduction and up to 30% faster real-time inference across three simulation benchmarks (LIBERO, RoboCasa, SimplerEnv) and 10 real-world manipulation tasks over 4 robotic embodiments, while matching or exceeding the full-scale base model performance.
AI.conclusion: Vision-Language-Action models possess substantial unexploited layer redundancy. The proposed training-free structural compression yields scalable, compute-efficient policy learning and real-time control without sacrificing performance.

title: Variable-Length Tokenization via Learnable Global Merging for Diffusion Transformers
summary: Latent Diffusion Models (LDMs) have become dominant in visual synthesis, but their quality-compute trade-off is largely constrained by the tokenizer's fixed compression ratio. Variable-length tokenizers (VLTs) promise adaptive compression by varying token counts, allowing diffusion models to flexibly balance quality and compute. However, conventional VLTs modulate length by truncating ordered token sequences, which makes token semantics depend on token position and breaks representational alignment across lengths. This leads to a cross-length shift in the latent distribution that hinders a single variable-length diffusion model from operating effectively. To address this, we propose a novel variable-length tokenizer that modulates length by merging tokens. We show that encouraging similar tokens to merge enables direct cross-length representation alignment when the diffusion transformer operates according to the merging pattern. Since conventional merging methods are data-dependent, making the merging pattern inaccessible during generation, we introduce learnable global merging, which is data-independent, to ensure compatibility with diffusion transformers. On ImageNet 256$\times$256 generation, our merging-based variable-length tokenizer integrated with a diffusion transformer achieves a superior gFID-compute trade-off compared to prior VLT methods. Code is available at [this https URL](https://github.com/movinghoon/lgm)

AI.tldr: Merging-based variable-length tokenizer enables cross-length alignment for latent diffusion models, yielding a better gFID-compute trade-off on ImageNet 256x256 compared to truncation-based VLTs.
AI.motivation: Fixed compression by truncation-based VLTs makes token semantics depend on position and causes cross-length distribution shifts, hindering a single variable-length diffusion model.
AI.method: Replace length truncation with learnable token merging; design a data-independent global merging strategy so that the diffusion transformer can operate according to the established merging pattern; ensure cross-length representational alignment.
AI.result: On ImageNet 256×256 generation, the merging-based VLT integrated with a diffusion transformer achieves a superior gFID-compute trade-off relative to prior VLT methods.
AI.conclusion: Merging-based VLT enables cross-length representation alignment and single-model compatibility, improving efficiency and quality in LDMs; code available.

title: Modularity-Free Conflict-Averse Training for Generalized PINNs
summary: Physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) have become a powerful framework for solving PDEs by embedding physical laws into differentiable objectives. Despite their advances, training PINNs remains fragile: recent conflict-averse optimization schemes alleviate gradient interference between residual and boundary losses, but we show that their effectiveness deteriorates as model capacity increases. In this paper, we identify a capacity-induced failure mode, where overparameterized networks undergo functional modularity, self-partitioning into task-exclusive modules that suppress cross-objective interaction and hinder convergence toward Pareto-stationary points. To address this issue, we propose a novel framework, Modular-Sparsity Synchronization (ModSync), which integrates structural optimization into conflict-averse training by penalizing task-exclusive connections while preserving interaction-promoting pathways. Extensive experiments across diverse PDE benchmarks demonstrate that ModSync consistently prevents capacity-driven failures, sustains robust cross-objective coupling, and achieves state-of-the-art accuracy. Codes are available at \url{https://github.com/heejokong/ModSync}.

AI.tldr: Overparameterized PINNs exhibit capacity-induced functional modularity, where task-exclusive modules suppress cross-objective interaction and impede convergence to Pareto-stationary points. The paper introduces ModSync to counter this by structuring sparsity that penalizes exclusive connections while preserving interaction-promoting pathways, yielding improved training stability and accuracy.
AI.motivation: PINNs trained with conflict-averse objectives show fragility that worsens with model capacity. The root cause is capacity-induced modularization that isolates tasks and reduces cross-objective coupling, hindering Pareto-stationary convergence.
AI.method: Introduce Modular-Sparsity Synchronization (ModSync). Integrate structural optimization into conflict-averse training by penalizing task-exclusive connections and preserving cross-task interactions. Implement sparsity synchronization across tasks to maintain shared modules while pruning exclusive connections; evaluate on multi-objective PDE benchmarks.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across diverse PDE benchmarks show that ModSync consistently prevents capacity-driven failures, maintains cross-objective coupling, and achieves state-of-the-art accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Structural sparsity regularization via ModSync effectively mitigates capacity-induced modularity in PINNs, stabilizing training and boosting multi-objective performance; code is publicly available.

title: Manifold Bandits: Bayesian Curriculum Learning over the Latent Geometry of Large Language Models
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) is a central approach for improving reasoning capabilities in large language models (LLMs), where training efficiency depends critically on how problems are sampled during optimization. Existing adaptive curriculum learning methods typically prioritize prompts of intermediate difficulty, treating problem selection as a standard bandit problem with independent arms and overlooking the structured, heterogeneous nature of the task space. In this work, we frame problem sampling as a manifold-structured bandit problem with endogenous non-stationarity: problems are related through the model's latent representation space, and sampling decisions can steer how learning signals evolve across that space. To operationalize this perspective, we introduce Bayesian Manifold Curriculum (BMC), a structure-aware framework that organizes problems into a hierarchical task tree and applies Bayesian learning to guide sampling. Empirically, we find that different sampling strategies induce non-trivial tradeoffs between productivity (learning signal), diversity (coverage of the task manifold), and utility (evaluation relevance). These results show that prioritizing difficulty alone is insufficient for strong downstream performance, highlighting the importance of incorporating structure and type-awareness into problem sampling.

AI.tldr: A structure-aware problem-sampling framework for RL with LLMs that models the problem space as a manifold-structured bandit with endogenous non-stationarity. The Bayesian Manifold Curriculum (BMC) organizes tasks hierarchically and uses Bayesian learning to guide sampling, balancing learning signal, task diversity, and evaluation relevance over naive difficulty-based curricula.
AI.motivation: Improve training efficiency and downstream performance in RL for LLMs by leveraging the structured, heterogeneous nature of the problem space and the model's latent representations, rather than treating prompts as independent arms.
AI.method: Propose Bayesian Manifold Curriculum (BMC): a structure-aware framework that (i) organizes problems into a hierarchical task tree; (ii) treats problem sampling as a manifold-structured bandit with endogenous non-stationarity; (iii) applies Bayesian learning to guide sampling decisions, balancing productivity, diversity, and utility in the latent task space.
AI.result: Empirically observe non-trivial tradeoffs among productivity (learning signal), diversity (coverage of the task manifold), and utility (evaluation relevance) across sampling strategies; high-level finding that prioritizing difficulty alone is insufficient and that structure-aware, type-conscious sampling improves downstream performance.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating task structure and type awareness into problem sampling is essential for strong downstream performance; the BMC framework provides a principled approach to navigate structured task spaces and balance exploration-exploitation in RL for LLMs.

title: Efficiently Linking Real Scenes with Synthetic Data Generation for AI-based Cognitive Robotics and Computer Vision Applications
comment: Accepted and best paper award at MHI-Kolloquium 2024
summary: AI vision models are a driving factor for the potential use case scenarios of cognitive robotics within in the industry and household applications. A large array of methods from semantic environment analysis towards 6D and grasping pose estimation have been proposed based on the latest AI achievements. However, such advancements require further strong and efficient methods w.r.t. training data and AI-architectures, which are capable in synergy to tackle current challenges, precision limits, and scalability beyond domain gaps. In this paper, we discuss these current limits and trends in the related state-of-the-art which are challenging those. Further we discuss our current work in progress on bridging the domain gap between simulations and real world applications by linking those in the training data generation.

AI.tldr: A survey of AI vision in robotics highlighting current limits and trends, and presenting initial work to bridge sim-to-real via training data generation.
AI.motivation: To address data efficiency, architecture choices, domain gaps, and scalability hindering deployment of perception systems in industrial and household robotics.
AI.method: Literature survey of state-of-the-art, critical discussion of limitations, and presentation of ongoing work to link simulation-based and real-world training data generation.
AI.result: Conceptual insights and a proposed WIP approach; no empirical experiments reported in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: Calls for synergistic advances in data generation and architectures to close the domain gap and improve applicability of AI vision to robotics.

title: The Hidden Evolution of Disguised Visual Context inside the VLM
summary: Visual tokens enter Large Language Models (LLMs) as raw, foreign signals. How they are transformed into meaningful representations and interact with the language space depends entirely on the integration architecture. Whether by treating visual tokens as in-context prompts within the input sequence or injecting them directly into the LLM's intermediate layers. A controlled comparison and understanding of how these architectural choices affect visual information and its internal transformation to integrate with the LLM remains underexplored. We provide a fair comparison by evaluating in-context and layer-wise injection VLM integration paradigms under identical training conditions across single image, multi-image, and video benchmarks. In doing so, we uncover a hidden evolution where visual tokens enter the LLM as disguised visual context, raw representations lacking linguistic structure, but are progressively reshaped depending on the integration paradigm, each capturing fundamentally different frequency characteristics of the visual signal. We show that this evolution inside the LLM determines what visual features the VLM can utilize effectively, how visual representations align with the language space, and ultimately how each paradigm performs across different tasks. We further demonstrate that attention allocation alone is insufficient, and that performance is driven by the quality of visual representations at each layer.

AI.tldr: A controlled comparison of in-context prompts versus layer-wise visual integration for vision-language models (VLMs) under identical training, showing that visual tokens morph differently by integration type, capturing distinct frequency components, which shapes feature usability, alignment with language space, and task performance; attention alone is insufficient.
AI.motivation: Resolve the lack of a fair, controlled comparison between two prevalent VLM integration paradigms and understand how integration architecture shapes the evolution of visual representations inside LLMs.
AI.method: Train and evaluate VLMs under identical conditions across single-image, multi-image, and video benchmarks, comparing in-context prompt-based integration with layer-wise injection. Analyze internal representations, frequency characteristics, alignment with language space, and task performance.
AI.result: Visual tokens enter the LLM as disguised, raw visual context and are progressively reshaped by the integration paradigm, each shaping different frequency characteristics of the visual signal. This evolution within the LLM determines the visual features the VLM can effectively utilize, how representations align with the language space, and task performance. Simply allocating attention is not enough; the quality of visual representations at each layer is the primary performance driver.
AI.conclusion: The integration architecture fundamentally governs how visual information is transformed and used in LLMs. To advance VLMs, focus on cultivating robust, multi-layer visual representations that align with the language space, rather than relying solely on attention-based mechanisms.

title: Implicit Semantic-Aware Communication Based on Hypergraph Reasoning
comment: This work is accepted at IEEE Transactions on Communications
summary: Semantic-aware communication has emerged as a transformative paradigm for next-generation communication systems, shifting the fundamental goal from transmitting bit-level symbols to reliably recovering and understanding the semantic meaning of information. Previous studies have demonstrated that representing the semantic content of source messages as graph-based structures can significantly improve communication efficiency and the accuracy of semantic inference at the receiver. However, existing solutions typically employ graphs that capture only pairwise relationships, thereby neglecting higher-order implicit correlations commonly observed in real-world scenarios, such as group interactions, multi-entity associations, and complex relational contexts. This limitation reduces semantic expressiveness and makes semantic inference susceptible to ambiguity and performance degradation, particularly under noisy or corrupted channel conditions. To address these issues, this paper proposes a novel hypergraph-based implicit semantic reasoning framework, HISR, which leverages hypergraphs to represent complex multi-entity relationships among semantic knowledge entities. In HISR, entities and their associated higher-order relations are mapped into dedicated semantic subspaces tailored to distinct relational contexts. This design not only disentangles diverse semantic interactions to mitigate the over-smoothing effects commonly found in traditional graph embedding methods but also enables robust semantic inference even when partial information loss occurs during transmission. Numerical results show that the proposed HISR achieves up to a 36.6% improvement in implicit semantic interpretation accuracy over the state-of-the-art benchmarks.

AI.tldr: Hypergraph-based implicit semantic reasoning (HISR) for semantic-aware communication; models higher-order relations with dedicated semantic subspaces, improving implicit semantic interpretation accuracy by up to 36.6% over state-of-the-art.
AI.motivation: Limitations of pairwise graphs in semantic communication: unable to capture higher-order correlations, leading to reduced semantic expressiveness and robustness, especially under noisy or corrupted channels.
AI.method: Propose HISR: represent semantic entities and higher-order relations as hypergraphs; map entities and their relationships into relation-specific semantic subspaces to disentangle interactions, mitigate over-smoothing, and enable robust inference when partial information is lost during transmission.
AI.result: Numerical results show up to 36.6% improvement in implicit semantic interpretation accuracy over benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: A hypergraph-based implicit semantic reasoning framework extends semantic representations to higher-order interactions, improving expressiveness, robustness, and inference reliability in semantic communication and mitigating issues of traditional pairwise graphs.

title: Learning universal approximations for partial differential equations with Physics-Informed Broad Learning System
summary: Partial differential equations (PDEs) play a central role in modeling complex physical, biological, and engineering systems. While traditional numerical solvers are robust, they often incur prohibitive computational costs due to mesh dependencies, whereas recent Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) offer a mesh-free alternative but frequently suffer from slow convergence and optimization instability. To bridge this gap, this article proposes the Physics-Informed Broad Learning System (PIBLS), a novel backpropagation-free framework that reformulates PDE solving as a direct least-squares optimization. We improved an algorithm within this framework to handle nonlinear PDEs efficiently and provide a rigorous mathematical proof establishing the universal approximation property of PIBLS for these equations. Experiments on linear and nonlinear PDEs demonstrate that PIBLS is one to three orders of magnitude faster than conventional PINNs while achieving significantly higher solution accuracy. This framework provides a computationally efficient paradigm for scientific machine learning, offering a practical, high-speed alternative for real-time simulation and design optimization tasks.

AI.tldr: PIBLS is a backpropagation-free PDE solver that casts solving PDEs as a direct least-squares problem, extends to nonlinear PDEs, and provides a universal approximation proof; it is reported to be 1–3 orders of magnitude faster and more accurate than PINNs, enabling real-time simulation and optimization.
AI.motivation: To overcome the computational cost and instability of traditional mesh-based solvers and PINNs, by delivering a mesh-free, fast, and stable PDE-solving framework.
AI.method: PIBLS reformulates PDE solving as a direct least-squares optimization in a backpropagation-free setting; the authors improve an algorithm for nonlinear PDEs and prove a universal approximation property for PIBLS.
AI.result: Experimental evaluation on linear and nonlinear PDEs indicates PIBLS achieves 1–3 orders of magnitude faster performance than conventional PINNs and yields significantly higher solution accuracy.
AI.conclusion: PIBLS offers a computationally efficient, scalable paradigm for scientific machine learning, suitable for real-time simulation and design optimization.

title: Holo-World: Unified Camera, Object and Weather Control for Video World Model
summary: Video world models are moving toward preserving an observed world under controllable camera and object motion while allowing its environmental state to change. Yet these controls remain isolated, and weather generation typically relies on a source video or reconstructed scene that already specifies future structure. We study a first-frame-anchored source-to-state setting, where the model starts from a single image and follows explicit camera and object controls and an optional weather instruction, then generates a video that either preserves the source world or transfers it to a target weather state. To address these challenges, we first build HoloStateData, a state video dataset that turns diverse videos into unified control samples for camera, object, and weather supervision. Second, we introduce Holo-World, a unified controllable video world model that jointly controls scene from a single image. Its Unified Scene Adapter factorizes world preservation and weather transfer into distinct parameter subspaces, using rendered background, geometry buffers, and object controls to maintain controlled scene structure while modeling weather-dependent appearance and particle effects. Additionally, Scene-Weather Decomposed CFG guides scene and weather residuals separately, strengthening target weather effects without over-amplifying the full condition. Quantitative and qualitative experiments demonstrate that Holo-World maintains precise camera and object control with consistent scene structure while transferring scenes into diverse target weather state, outperforming video-to-video weather editing baselines on weather-state generation. Our project page is available at \url{https://xiangchenyin.github.io/Holo-World/}.

AI.tldr: Proposes Holo-World: a first-frame anchored, controllable video world model that preserves scene structure while enabling weather transfer from a single image. Builds HoloStateData for joint camera/object/weather supervision and uses a Unified Scene Adapter with weather-aware scene residuals; achieves precise controls and competitive weather transfer.
AI.motivation: Current video world models struggle to preserve an observed world under controllable motion while changing weather, often requiring source videos or reconstructed scenes. A first-frame anchored, controllable approach is needed to enable explicit camera/object controls and weather state manipulation from a single image.
AI.method: Introduce HoloStateData to convert diverse videos into control samples; develop Holo-World with Unified Scene Adapter that separates scene preservation and weather transfer via background/geometry/object controls and weather residuals; apply Scene-Weather Decomposed CFG to guide scene and weather residuals independently.
AI.result: The model maintains precise camera/object control and scene structure while transferring to diverse target weather states, outperforming video-to-video weather editing baselines on weather-state generation.
AI.conclusion: Holo-World demonstrates a unified framework for single-image–driven video generation with explicit controls and weather transfer, validated by quantitative/qualitative experiments and a project page for reproducibility.

title: Apparent Psychological Profiles of Large Language Models are Largely a Measurement Artifact
summary: Psychological instruments designed for humans are increasingly used to assign large language models (LLMs) stable psychological profiles that affect their usability, safety assessment, and use as proxies for human participants in research. Using a formal psychometric framework, we show that these profiles are largely a measurement artifact. Administering a battery of personality and risk-preference instruments spanning self-reports and behavioral tasks to 56 instruction-tuned LLMs alongside large human reference samples, we report four findings. First, differences between models are driven not by the traits an instrument targets but by a directional response bias, a tendency to respond toward one end of the scale, or one labeled option, regardless of item content; a variance decomposition attributes 81-90% of between-model variation to this bias, against 9-16% in humans. Second, the bias declines with model capability but is not eliminated by it. Third, because bias rather than trait drives responding, an instrument's apparent reliability is almost entirely predicted by its response orthogonality, a term we coin for the proportion of items for which trait and bias point in opposite directions. Fourth, the profile a model appears to have shifts with the items used and can be manufactured through item selection. These results demonstrate that the apparent psychological profiles of LLMs are artifacts of the instrument used to measure them, not properties of the models themselves. As instruments borrowed from human psychology are rarely fully orthogonal and may inherently lack validity for LLMs, we call for dedicated assessments centered on response orthogonality.

AI.tldr: LLMs' 'psychological profiles' are largely measurement artifacts driven by directional response bias; 81–90% of between-model variance is due to bias, not trait; bias declines with capability but persists; instrument reliability is tied to response orthogonality; profiles can be dictated by item choice; suggests orthogonality-focused assessments.
AI.motivation: To test whether LLMs' profiles derived from standard human psychometrics reflect genuine differences or are artifacts of measurement, given their use in usability, safety assessments, and as proxies for human participants.
AI.method: Administered a battery of self-report and behavioral instruments spanning personality and risk preference to 56 instruction-tuned LLMs and large human reference samples; applied formal psychometric framework, variance decomposition, and item-level analyses of bias and orthogonality.
AI.result: Four main findings: (1) model differences are driven by directional bias rather than trait; 81–90% of between-model variance attributed to bias (humans 9–16%); (2) bias declines with model capability but not eradicated; (3) apparent reliability is largely predicted by response orthogonality (proportion of items where trait and bias oppose); (4) model profiles shift with item content and can be engineered via item selection.
AI.conclusion: Human-like psychometric profiles for LLMs appear to be artifacts of measurement; traditional instruments lack orthogonality/validity for LLMs. Recommend dedicated assessments focused on response orthogonality to avoid spurious inferences about model traits.

title: An Information Theoretic Framework for Graph Novelty Generation via Latent Mixture Modeling
summary: We propose an information-theoretic framework for graph novelty generation, which aims to generate data that are distinct from existing patterns while preserving global structural consistency. Our approach embeds data into a latent space, models the latent distribution using finite mixture models, and generates novel samples by imposing explicit novelty and reliability conditions formulated in terms of description length. Specifically, novelty is enforced by requiring generated samples to be poorly explained by all existing mixture components, while reliability constrains their impact on the overall mixture structure under the Minimum Description Length (MDL) principle. We provide a theoretical analysis showing that, with appropriate threshold choices, the probabilities of misclassifying non-novel or unreliable samples converge to zero with explicit rates. Experiments on synthetic and benchmark graph datasets demonstrate that the proposed method enables principled novelty generation with quantifiable risk.

AI.tldr: An information-theoretic framework for graph novelty generation using latent representations and finite mixture models, enforcing novelty via description-length criteria and reliability via MDL-based impact constraints; with theoretical guarantees and empirical validation on synthetic and benchmark graphs.
AI.motivation: To generate graph data that are novel relative to existing patterns while preserving global structural consistency, and to provide principled risk control and theoretical guarantees under the MDL principle.
AI.method: Embed data in a latent space, model the latent distribution with finite mixture models, and generate novel samples by imposing explicit novelty and reliability conditions formulated in terms of description length. Novelty requires samples to be poorly explained by all existing mixture components; reliability constrains their impact on the overall mixture structure under MDL. The paper provides theoretical analysis showing misclassification probabilities converge to zero with explicit rates; experiments on synthetic and benchmark graph datasets.
AI.result: The framework yields principled novelty generation with quantifiable risk, supported by theoretical guarantees (zero misclassification likelihood under suitable thresholds) and empirical validation on synthetic and benchmark graph datasets.
AI.conclusion: The proposed MDL-based novelty generation approach offers controllable novelty for graphs with provable guarantees and demonstrated practical effectiveness on synthetic and real datasets.

title: EventVLA: Event-Driven Visual Evidence Memory for Long-Horizon Vision-Language-Action Policies
summary: Memory remains a critical bottleneck for long-horizon robotic manipulation, as standard Vision-Language-Action (VLA) policies often fail when task-relevant cues become occluded or unobservable over time. While existing memory-augmented methods utilize historical context, they either suffer from severe information bottlenecks, incur high latency via decoupled dual systems, or rely on unselective buffers that accumulate massive visual redundancies. To address these limitations, we introduce EventVLA, an end-to-end framework founded on the concept of sparse visual evidence memory that comprises two core components: foundational visual anchors to retain initial and short-term contexts, and a dynamic Keyframe Evidence Memory (KEM) module. Specifically, KEM directly predicts future keyframe probabilities from the VLA's latent embeddings to autonomously capture and store sparse, task-critical visual events. This foresight-driven mechanism empowers the policy to dynamically evaluate the future causal utility of current observations, preserving transient visual evidence before it becomes unobservable. Furthermore, we propose RoboTwin-MeM, a diagnostic benchmark specifically designed to evaluate non-Markovian manipulation tasks with interactive visual evidence. Extensive evaluations show that across 17 memory-requiring simulation tasks and 4 real-world bimanual tasks, EventVLA achieves an average success rate improvement of +40% over state-of-the-art memory-augmented VLAs.

AI.tldr: Proposes EventVLA, an end-to-end memory-augmented VLA policy using sparse visual evidence memory with visual anchors and a Keyframe Evidence Memory (KEM); introduces RoboTwin-MeM benchmark; shows ~40% improvement on 17 sim tasks and 4 real-world tasks.
AI.motivation: Memory remains a bottleneck in long-horizon robotic manipulation due to occlusions or unobservable task cues. Existing memory methods suffer from information bottlenecks, high latency from decoupled systems, or unselective buffers with visual redundancy.
AI.method: End-to-end framework with sparse visual evidence memory: foundational visual anchors to retain initial/short-term context and a dynamic Keyframe Evidence Memory (KEM). KEM predicts future keyframe probabilities from the VLA latent embeddings to autonomously capture/store sparse, task-critical visual events, enabling foresight into the future utility of current observations and preservation of transient evidence before it becomes unobservable.
AI.result: Extensive evaluations across 17 memory-requiring simulation tasks and 4 real-world bimanual tasks show EventVLA achieving an average success-rate improvement of +40% over state-of-the-art memory-augmented VLA policies.
AI.conclusion: EventVLA effectively mitigates memory bottlenecks in long-horizon manipulation by forecasting the future utility of observations and storing sparse keyframes. RoboTwin-MeM provides a diagnostic benchmark for non-Markovian manipulation with interactive visual evidence. The results indicate strong gains over prior memory-augmented policies; potential avenues include analyzing scalability, generalization to other modalities, and further ablations.

title: Beyond Accuracy: Measuring Logical Compliance of Predictive Models
