summary: Machine learning models are predominantly evaluated through predictive performance metrics such as ranking quality, prediction error, or classification accuracy. While these metrics effectively quantify how closely predictions match the ground truth, they do not assess whether model outputs respect predefined logical or domain-specific constraints. In high-stakes applications, including healthcare, finance, and autonomous systems, logical consistency can be as critical as predictive accuracy, yet no standard metric captures this dimension. We introduce the Rule Violation Score (RVS), a complementary evaluation metric that quantifies the extent to which a predictive model respects a given set of logical rules, independently of predictive accuracy. RVS treats hard rules (strict constraints) and soft rules (statistical regularities) differently, can be evaluated on any dataset and on any predictive model expressed over a relational vocabulary, and can be computed using SQL queries that are automatically generated for Horn rules. Beyond evaluating models, RVS can also evaluate the logical consistency of training datasets and help identify poorly defined rules. We evaluate RVS on three benchmarks covering knowledge graph link prediction and relational regression, including rule-based, embedding-based, and neuro-symbolic predictive models. Our results demonstrate that two models achieving comparable predictive accuracy can exhibit substantially different levels of logical compliance, revealing differences in model behavior that standard metrics fail to capture.

AI.tldr: Introduces Rule Violation Score (RVS), a complementary metric to predictive accuracy that quantifies how well models respect predefined logical rules, treating hard and soft rules differently. Computable for any relational dataset and model; generated via SQL for Horn rules; also evaluates dataset and rule quality. Demonstrates that models with similar accuracy can differ in logical compliance across benchmarks (knowledge graphs and relational regression).
AI.motivation: Predictive metrics alone miss logical and domain-specific constraints, which are crucial in high-stakes applications. There is no standard metric to assess a model's adherence to logical rules or rule consistency in relational settings. RVS fills this gap by measuring rule conformity independently of accuracy.
AI.method: Define Rule Violation Score (RVS) with separate treatment for hard (strict) and soft (statistical) rules. Applicable to any dataset and any predictive model over a relational vocabulary. Automatically generate SQL queries for Horn rules to compute violations. Also usable to assess training data quality and rule definitions. Evaluated by generating RVS on predictions and on datasets to identify inconsistencies.
AI.result: Evaluated on three benchmarks spanning knowledge graph link prediction and relational regression with rule-based, embedding-based, and neuro-symbolic models. Findings show that two models with comparable predictive accuracy can exhibit substantially different levels of logical compliance, highlighting aspects of model behavior not captured by standard metrics.
AI.conclusion: RVS provides a complementary evaluation dimension that captures logical consistency, enabling detection of poorly defined rules and data issues, and revealing behavioral differences among models that predictive metrics overlook.

title: Flow Map Denoisers: Traversing the Distortion-Perception Plane for Inverse Problems
summary: Image restoration faces a fundamental tradeoff: methods that minimize error produce blurry reconstructions, while those that maximize perceptual quality yield sharp but less faithful images. Existing approaches either commit to a single operating point on this distortion perception (DP) frontier or require paired-data supervision, auxiliary models, or hyperparameter tuning of the sampler to access different points. We show that flow map models, a recent extension of flow matching for few-step sampling that learns an average field, implicitly define a one-parameter family of denoisers that continuously spans the DP frontier. The lookahead parameter t acts as a control knob between the MMSE and perceptual regimes. For Gaussian targets, we prove that varying t exactly recovers the optimal DP frontier; for natural images, we observe similar behavior empirically. Within a Plug-and-Play solver, the same mechanism extends to general inverse problems, where it controls a tradeoff between perceptual alignment and data consistency. Despite the lack of exact optimality guarantees in this setting, a single trained flow map spans the DP tradeoff, matching or exceeding specialized baselines at both extremes. Extensive experiments on CelebA ($128\times 128$) and AFHQ ($256\times 256$) across several linear and nonlinear inverse tasks validate our findings.

AI.tldr: A flow map model yields a one-parameter denoiser family controllable by a lookahead t, spanning the distortion-perception (DP) frontier from MMSE to perceptual quality; a single model works for inverse problems, matching/exceeding baselines on CelebA and AFHQ.
AI.motivation: Overcome the image restoration DP tradeoff with methods that either fix an operating point, require paired supervision, auxiliary models, or sampler hyperparameter tuning; seek a single model to span the DP frontier.
AI.method: Introduce flow map models (extending flow matching for few-step sampling) that learn an average field. The lookahead parameter t acts as a knob between MMSE and perceptual regimes. Prove for Gaussian targets that varying t recovers the optimal DP frontier; empirically observe similar behavior on natural images. Extend to general inverse problems via Plug-and-Play solvers.
AI.result: For Gaussian targets, varying t recovers the optimal DP frontier; on natural images, similar behavior is observed. A single trained flow map spans the DP tradeoff, matching or exceeding specialized baselines at both extremes. Extensive experiments on CelebA (128×128) and AFHQ (256×256) across several linear and nonlinear inverse tasks.
AI.conclusion: Flow map models provide a simple, effective single-model approach to access the DP frontier, extendable to various inverse problems, and capable of outperforming specialized baselines on both ends of the tradeoff.

title: MakeupMirror: Improving Facial Attribute Preservation in Diffusion Models for Makeup Transfer
summary: Makeup transfer models enable fun augmented reality (AR) experiences as well as virtual try-on (VTO) for online makeup shopping. While recent state-of-the-art diffusion based solutions such as Stable-Makeup dramatically improve the accuracy and realism of makeup transfer, they still face limitations in identity and skin color preservation, making production-level VTO for makeup shopping unrealistic. In this work, we propose MakeupMirror, a diffusion-based approach to makeup transfer that makes significant progress towards preserving facial features and skin tone. We introduce several technical innovations over Stable-Makeup: (1) integration of facial geometry conditioning with ControlNets to maintain facial fidelity; (2) region-specific makeup transfer control to enable precise makeup application across facial regions such as skin, eyes and lips; (3) skin tone-based makeup transfer modulation that prevent skin tone alteration in cross-subject transfer scenarios; and (4) integration of a Levenberg-Marquardt Langevin sampler to speed up inference while maintaining generation quality. Our experiments on CPM-Real, Makeup Wild, and (herein newly collected, more diverse) MakeupSelfies datasets show that MakeupMirror improves relative facial recognition similarity by +60%, reduces relative skin tone difference by -50% over Stable-Makeup, with a latency of 0.7s, while achieving expert acceptance rate of 94% across core facial identity preservation criteria.

AI.tldr: A diffusion-based makeup transfer method (MakeupMirror) improves identity preservation and skin-tone consistency compared to Stable-Makeup by integrating geometry conditioning, region-specific transfer, skin-tone modulation, and a fast Levenberg-Marquardt Langevin sampler, achieving faster inference and high expert acceptance on diverse datasets.
AI.motivation: Overcome limitations in identity fidelity and skin color preservation in makeup transfer/diffusion methods to enable production-level virtual try-on for online makeup shopping.
AI.method: (1) Facial geometry conditioning with ControlNets to preserve facial structure; (2) Region-specific makeup transfer controlling skin, eyes, lips; (3) Skin tone–guided transfer modulation to prevent cross-subject skin-tone alteration; (4) Levenberg-Marquardt Langevin sampler to speed up inference without sacrificing quality.
AI.result: Measured on CPM-Real, Makeup Wild, MakeupSelfies: relative facial recognition similarity +60% vs Stable-Makeup; relative skin tone difference −50%; latency ~0.7s; expert acceptance rate 94% for core facial-identity preservation criteria.
AI.conclusion: MakeupMirror meaningfully advances diffusion-based makeup transfer toward production-ready VTO by combining geometry-aware conditioning, regional control, skin-tone safeguards, and fast sampling with strong empirical gains and practical inference speed.

title: Augmenting Game AI with Deep Reinforcement Learning
comment: Vision paper, published in Conference on Games 2026
summary: Immersion in video games depends not only on graphics, audio, and game mechanics, but also on the quality of in-game characters. Producing believable characters, or game AI, remains a significant challenge as behavioral complexity is hard to capture with hand-coded systems. Game AI is a source of immersion and engagement; however, the limitations stemming from the challenges of creating game AI often lead to frustration and the breaking of the illusion of realism within the game. The introduction of machine learning models opens the door to creating more believable, authentic, and relatable characters in games. The promise is that they either learn from interacting with the game, or from player data, to develop true human-like behavior. In this paper, we envision more applications of reinforcement learning for game AI in the future. For this to materialize, current research limitations are prohibitive to broad deployment across game genres. Therefore, we propose a framework for training reinforcement learning models with a set of requirements in mind that are suited towards game AI and game development. We present examples of games with reinforcement learning-augmented game AI and describe the practicalities of deploying player-facing machine learning agents in modern games. Furthermore, we identify bottlenecks and hard problems in these areas, which we believe offer promising research directions to accelerate the adoption of machine learning in game AI for the video game industry.

AI.tldr: Advocates a reinforcement learning framework for game AI to produce believable characters; outlines framework, examples, deployment considerations, bottlenecks, and future research directions to accelerate ML adoption in games.
AI.motivation: Bridge the realism gap in game AI by moving beyond hand-coded behavior, leveraging ML/RL to learn from interaction or player data, enhancing immersion across genres.
AI.method: Proposes a framework of requirements for RL-based game AI, discusses practical deployment of player-facing ML agents, provides game examples with RL augmentation, and catalogs bottlenecks and hard problems with suggested research directions.
AI.result: Conceptual framework with illustrative examples and deployment considerations; no empirical results reported in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: RL-driven game AI shows promise for realism but current limitations hinder broad deployment; a defined framework and identified research directions can accelerate adoption.

title: Uncertainty-Aware Reward Modeling for Stable RLHF
summary: Reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) aligns large language models by training reward models on preference data and optimizing policies to maximize predicted rewards. However, this pipeline faces two fundamental challenges: (1) reward models cannot signal when their predictions are unreliable, since they usually act as deterministic point estimators; and (2) modern group-based policy optimization can amplify unreliable reward signals, as exemplified by GRPO's uniform treatment of rewards during advantage computation. As policies explore increasingly diverse responses, these two limitations create a critical vulnerability: unreliable reward estimates may be granted disproportionate influence, triggering severe reward hacking. We propose Uncertainty-Aware Reward Modeling (UARM), which equips reward models with calibrated uncertainty via quantile-based conformal prediction and reweights GRPO advantages through heteroscedastic variance decomposition. Experiments across HelpSteer, UltraFeedback, and PKU-SafeRLHF demonstrate that UARM significantly improves reward model calibration, reduces reward hacking, and enhances downstream alignment quality compared to standard GRPO and uncertainty-agnostic baselines.

AI.tldr: Uncertainty-aware reward modeling (UARM) for RLHF that calibrates reward predictions with conformal uncertainty and reweights GRPO advantages using heteroscedastic variance, reducing reward hacking and improving alignment.
AI.motivation: Reward models in RLHF are often deterministic point estimators with no calibrated uncertainty, and group-based policy optimization (GRPO) can amplify unreliable signals. This leads to reward hacking as policies exploit inaccurate rewards. There is a need to signal unreliability and adjust training accordingly.
AI.method: Introduce quantile-based conformal prediction to provide calibrated uncertainty for reward models; decompose observed reward variance (heteroscedasticity) to reweight GRPO advantages, thereby down-weighting unreliable reward signals during policy optimization. Evaluate on HelpSteer, UltraFeedback, and PKU-SafeRLHF.
AI.result: UARM improves reward model calibration, reduces reward hacking, and enhances downstream alignment quality compared with standard GRPO and uncertainty-agnostic baselines across the three benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Calibrated uncertainty and variance-aware reweighting in RLHF reward modeling mitigate reward hacking and yield better alignment, offering a practical approach to robust RLHF.

title: Stitching and dimensionality effects on large artificially generated volume datasets
summary: Generating large images via deep learning requires patching input data to accommodate hardware memory limitations, then assembling output patches, a process that can introduce stitching artifacts when neighboring patches do not align at borders. While these artifacts are known to affect segmentation tasks, their impact on generative models for style-transfer remains poorly understood. We investigated three stitching approaches and two patch dimensionalities (2D vs 3D) using cycleGAN models trained on cryo-electron microscopy datasets. We evaluated both perceptual quality and performance on downstream mitochondria segmentation. Our key findings reveal that: (1) FID scores fail to detect subtle stitching artifacts that significantly impact downstream segmentation performance, (2) 3D models with artifact-free stitching marginally outperform 2D models on downstream tasks, though the improvement barely justifies the computational cost, and (3) 2D models train more stably due to larger batch sizes. Additionally, we demonstrate that ensembling predictions from three orthogonal directions can improve low-quality volumes but provides no benefit for high-quality outputs. These results demonstrate that maximizing generative model performance on large scientific datasets requires careful consideration and mitigation of stitching artifacts, and that perceptual metrics alone are insufficient for evaluating domain adaptation quality in biomedical imaging.

AI.tldr: Stitching artifacts in tiled generative models affect downstream biomedical segmentation; FID metrics miss these artifacts; 3D tiling offers marginal gains at high computational cost; 2D tiling is more stable; ensembling helps only for low-quality outputs; perceptual metrics alone are insufficient for domain-adaptation evaluation in biomedical imaging.
AI.motivation: Understand how patch-based tiling and 2D vs 3D model dimensionality influence CycleGAN-generated biomedical images, focusing on cryo-EM data and downstream mitochondria segmentation, and whether perceptual metrics capture task-relevant quality.
AI.method: Systematic comparison of three stitching approaches and two patch dimensionalities (2D vs 3D) using CycleGANs trained on cryo-EM datasets. Assess perceptual quality with FID and evaluate downstream segmentation performance. Explore ensembling predictions from three orthogonal directions for volume quality.
AI.result: FID fails to detect stitching artifacts that degrade segmentation. 3D models with artifact-free stitching provide marginal downstream gains over 2D, not justifying the computational cost. 2D models train more stably due to larger batch sizes. Ensembling improves low-quality volumes but offers no benefit for high-quality outputs.
AI.conclusion: Perceptual metrics are insufficient for evaluating domain adaptation in biomedical imaging. Artifact mitigation at stitching boundaries is essential for large-scale generative modeling; 2D vs 3D trade-offs must consider computational costs, and simple ensembling is not universally beneficial.

title: QMFOL: Benchmarking Large Language Model Reasoning via Quantifiable Monadic First-Order Logic Test Case Generation
summary:
Large Language Models (LLMs) have made significant progress in reasoning, particularly in deductive reasoning, which is crucial for high-stakes decision-making. As models improve, evaluation benchmarks should evolve to keep pace. However, existing benchmarks lack fine-grained control over logical complexity and struggle to balance semantic diversity with logical consistency.
  To address these issues, we propose QMFOL, an automated framework for generating monadic first-order logic reasoning tasks with quantifiable and controllable complexity. It constructs formal logical structures using conjunction and disjunction patterns, enabling precise control over reasoning depth, width, label types, and distractors. These structures are then translated into natural language via LLMs, with logical consistency ensured through round-trip verification using an external prover. Based on our framework, we build QMFOLBench, a benchmark comprising 2880 instances with 960 configurations across diverse logical and semantic dimensions. Evaluations on six large reasoning models (LRMs) and two LLMs show that performance degrades and computational overhead increases with rising logical complexity. Models perform better on True-labeled tasks than on False or Unknown ones, and exhibit sensitivity to semantic variation. Overall, QMFOL offers a scalable and reliable approach for constructing deductive reasoning benchmarks with controllable complexity, enabling more precise evaluation of reasoning capabilities in modern language models.

AI.tldr: QMFOL is a framework to generate monadic first-order logic reasoning tasks with tunable complexity, translated to natural language and verified with a prover, yielding QMFOLBench (2880 instances, 960 configurations). It reveals that model performance drops with higher logical complexity, with better accuracy on True-labeled tasks and sensitivity to semantic variation.
AI.motivation: Existing deductive reasoning benchmarks lack fine-grained control over logical complexity and struggle to balance semantic diversity with logical consistency; there is a need for scalable benchmarks that allow precise, quantitative manipulation of reasoning difficulty to properly evaluate contemporary models.
AI.method: Construct formal logical structures using conjunction/disjunction patterns to control reasoning depth, width, label types, and distractors; translate these structures into natural language via LLMs; enforce logical consistency with round-trip verification against an external prover; assemble QMFOLBench with 2880 instances across 960 configurations; evaluate using six LRMs and two LLMs.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows performance degrades as logical complexity increases and computational overhead rises; models perform better on True-labeled tasks than False/Unknown; semantic variation affects performance; QMFOLBench offers a scalable, reliable benchmark for deductive reasoning with controllable complexity.
AI.conclusion: QMFOL provides a scalable framework for generating controlled deductive reasoning benchmarks, enabling precise evaluation of reasoning capabilities in modern LLMs and LRMs; the approach balances semantic diversity with logical consistency through automated verification.

title: Enhancing Graph Neural Networks Using Proximity Graphs for Dust Source Emission Forecasting
summary:
Accurate prediction of dust source emissions is critical for mitigating the significant environmental and health hazards posed by dust storms. Traditional forecasting methods often struggle to capture the complex spatiotemporal dynamics of these phenomena.
  In this paper, we demonstrate that proximity graphs enable Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) to effectively model the intricate spatial and temporal relationships between data points. Specifically, we use proximity graphs--such as Delaunay triangulation, Gabriel graph, k-Nearest Neighbor graph, and Yao graph--as the input for GNNs (including GraphSAGE, Graph Convolutional Networks, and Graph Attention Networks) to perform message passing.
  Our approach highlights the effectiveness of integrating proximity graphs with GNNs for robust and accurate dust source forecasting. To emphasize the importance of proximity graph representations, we compare our method against GNNs using random graphs for message passing. The results show that GNNs with proximity graphs significantly outperform those with random graphs and are also far superior to Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) model in dust source emission forecasting.

AI.tldr: Proximity-graph–based GNNs improve dust source emission forecasting, outperforming random-graph GNNs and LSTM.
AI.motivation: Dust storm emissions require modeling complex spatiotemporal dynamics; traditional forecasting methods struggle to capture spatial dependencies. Proximity graphs offer principled spatial structure to feed GNNs, potentially enhancing predictive performance and robustness.
AI.method: Construct multiple proximity graphs (Delaunay triangulation, Gabriel graph, k-Nearest Neighbor, Yao graph) to encode spatial relationships among data points. Use various GNN architectures (GraphSAGE, Graph Convolutional Networks, Graph Attention Networks) to perform message passing on these graphs. Compare performance against GNNs using random graphs and against LSTM baselines.
AI.result: GNNs leveraging proximity graphs significantly outperform those using random graphs and also surpass LSTM baselines in dust source emission forecasting, demonstrating robust and accurate predictions.
AI.conclusion: Integrating proximity-graph representations with GNNs is effective for dust source forecasting, highlighting the importance of graph structure in spatiotemporal prediction tasks and suggesting a path for improved environmental forecasting models.

title: WeGenBench: A Multidimensional Diagnostic Benchmark towards Text-to-Image Model Optimization
summary: Recent text-to-image generation models have demonstrated remarkable capabilities in synthesizing highly realistic images from text inputs alone. Although existing benchmarks can evaluate the generation capabilities of various models to some extent, they struggle to comprehensively and accurately measure performance across multiple dimensions, often failing to reveal the inherent deficiencies of models in specific categories. To address these limitations, we propose WeGenBench, a novel benchmark designed for the comprehensive, multi-perspective evaluation of text-to-image generation capabilities. Our benchmark comprises a total of 4,000 test prompts across two primary categories, meticulously balanced between Chinese and English to evaluate bilingual and cross-cultural generation capabilities. Beyond macroscopic scene classification, we annotate each prompt with multi-dimensional tags tailored to the distinct content and challenges of each language, thereby refining the generation tasks into more specific sub-categories. Through a cross-dimensional evaluation mechanism leveraging both scene classifications and multi-dimensional tags, WeGenBench can precisely pinpoint model shortcomings in specific generation categories. Furthermore, to measure generation quality more accurately, we design and validate several novel evaluation metrics by integrating Vision-Language Models (VLMs), which assess model performance on domain-specific tasks from three core aspects. Crucially, our approach yields both the assessment outcomes and the detailed reasoning trajectories, facilitating a rigorous verification of the accuracy and soundness of the evaluation results. Finally, we conduct systematic benchmarking on current state-of-the-art methods and provide an in-depth analysis of the limitations present in existing models.

AI.tldr: WeGenBench introduces a bilingual, multi-dimensional benchmark for text-to-image generation with 4,000 prompts in Chinese and English, annotated with language-specific tags, plus cross-dimensional evaluation and Vision-Language Model–based metrics that yield reasoning trajectories to diagnose model weaknesses across categories.
AI.motivation: Current benchmarks fail to capture multi-faceted performance, especially across languages and content-specific challenges; there is a need for granular diagnostics and interpretable evaluation to identify model deficiencies.
AI.method: Construct 4,000 prompts, balanced across Chinese/English; annotate prompts with multi-dimensional language-specific tags; categorize prompts by scenes; employ cross-dimensional evaluation combining scene classifications and tags; develop novel VLM-based metrics across three aspects; provide reasoning trajectories for verification; benchmark SOTA models.
AI.result: Demonstrates ability to pinpoint model shortcomings across generation categories; validates the proposed metrics; benchmarks SOTA models and analyzes limitations of existing models.
AI.conclusion: WeGenBench offers comprehensive, interpretable, multi-perspective evaluation for text-to-image generation, enabling precise diagnosis of weaknesses and fostering robust progress; the approach yields both results and reasoning traces for verification.

title: Thermodynamic Measure of Intelligence
summary: Can intelligence be measured? We propose that intelligence can be defined as the lawful amplification of rare but valid futures: a system increases the probability of outcomes that would be unlikely under passive dynamics but remain admissible under the constraints of the domain. We start with the premise that an intelligent system must model the world and its own place within it. Because the system is part of the world it models, this leads naturally to recursive self-simulation: the system represents futures in which its own actions are part of the trajectory. Our central results give a necessity statement and a conditional near-sufficiency statement connecting this architecture to a precise thermodynamic measure of lawful amplification of rare-valid futures: high rare-valid lift is impossible unless the internal simulation identifies rare-valid futures with high fidelity; conversely, when rare-valid fidelity is high and the simulation contains an effective policy, the achievable lift approaches the actuation-limited optimum. Thus recursive self-simulation is not merely a plausible feature of intelligence but, under the stated assumptions, is necessary and nearly sufficient for high thermodynamic intelligence. The resulting framework makes intelligence measurable on a universal scale, from passive matter and feedback controllers, large language models, and humans as text generators to Maxwell-demon-like information engines.

AI.tldr: Defines intelligence as the thermodynamic, lawful amplification of rare but admissible futures, achieved via recursive self-simulation and world-modeling; establishes necessary and near-sufficient conditions, implying high lift requires high-fidelity internal simulations and effective policies; claims a universal, cross-domain measure from passive matter to humans and LLMs.
AI.motivation: Provide a universal, quantitative metric of intelligence grounded in physics and information theory, enabling cross-domain comparison and bridging systems from simple controllers to advanced AI and humans.
AI.method: Introduce the concept of rare-valid lift and internal recursive self-simulation; formalize a thermodynamic measure of lawful amplification of rare-valid futures; derive a necessity statement and a conditional near-sufficiency statement linking internal simulation fidelity and policy effectiveness to achievable lift; demonstrate universality across domains.
AI.result: Establishes that high rare-valid lift is impossible without high-fidelity internal simulation of rare-valid futures; with high fidelity and an effective policy, the lift approaches the actuation-limited optimum; thus recursive self-simulation is necessary and nearly sufficient for high thermodynamic intelligence; proposes a universal metric applicable to passive matter, feedback controllers, LLMs, humans as text generators, and information engines with Maxwell-demon-like behavior.
AI.conclusion: A universal, thermodynamic framework for measuring intelligence is proposed, enabling cross-domain assessment from physical systems to cognitive agents and AI, by quantifying the amplification of rare but admissible futures through recursive self-modeling.

title: When, Where, and How: Adaptive Binning for Tabular Self-Supervised Learning
summary: Medical tabular data are ubiquitous in clinical research, but deep learning for tables remains underexplored because reliable labels often require costly expert adjudication, even though structured clinical variables are routinely available in tabular form. Self-supervised learning can leverage these unlabeled tables, and recent binning-based pretexts offer a promising inductive bias, but existing objectives fix a single global quantile discretization and apply feature-agnostic supervision. We propose Adaptive Binning, a training-adaptive discretization pretext for tabular SSL that couples discretization to learning through a feature-wise coarse-to-fine curriculum. Motivated by the spectral bias of neural networks and the principles of curriculum learning, our method progressively refines discretization per feature upon plateau detection and selects representation-aware splits to jointly improve value-space concentration and representation-space coherence. A heterogeneity-aware objective unifies categorical reconstruction with ordinal supervision for numerical features, and experiments on public medical tabular datasets under unified evaluation protocols show consistent gains for linear probing and fine-tuning without dataset-specific discretization tuning. We further introduce a medical tabular SSL benchmark with standardized protocols to support reproducible progress in this underexplored domain. Our code is available at https://github.com/labhai/Adaptive-Binning.

AI.tldr: Adaptive Binning is a training-adaptive discretization pretext for self-supervised learning on medical tabular data. It uses a per-feature coarse-to-fine curriculum to update discretization during training, yielding consistent gains for linear probing and fine-tuning without dataset-specific discretization tuning, and it introduces a standardized medical tabular SSL benchmark with public code.
AI.motivation: Current tabular SSL methods rely on fixed, global discretization and feature-agnostic supervision, which underutilize unlabeled medical tables and ignore feature heterogeneity. Medical tabular data are costly to label, necessitating label-efficient learning. The approach leverages spectral bias and curriculum learning to adapt discretization to learning dynamics and feature types (categorical/ordinal/numeric).
AI.method: Adaptive Binning couples discretization with representation learning via a feature-wise coarse-to-fine curriculum. Discretization per feature is progressively refined upon plateau detection; representation-aware splits select splits that improve value-space concentration and representation-space coherence. The objective is heterogeneity-aware, unifying categorical reconstruction with ordinal supervision for numerical features.
AI.result: On public medical tabular datasets with unified evaluation protocols, Adaptive Binning achieves consistent gains for both linear probing and fine-tuning without dataset-specific discretization tuning.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive Binning advances SSL for medical tabular data, providing improved label-efficient learning, a reproducible benchmark, and open-source code for reproducibility.

title: Geometry-Preserving in 3D Gaussian Splatting for LiDAR-Camera Extrinsic Calibration
comment: Accepted to ECCV 2026. 15 pages (excluding references), 5 figures
summary: Accurate LiDAR-camera calibration is essential for robust multi-modal perception. Targetless approaches avoid manual setup but remain limited by the scarcity of discriminative cross-modal features. Recent methods address this by reconstructing the scene within a differentiable model, enabling extrinsic optimization through dense photometric supervision. Among these, 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) has been widely adopted as a geometric proxy that bridges LiDAR and camera within a single differentiable framework. However, since 3DGS was originally designed for novel view synthesis, existing methods tend to prioritize rendering quality, causing the proxy geometry to drift from the true LiDAR structure. We propose a framework that preserves the metric geometry of the Gaussian proxy by aggregating multi-view LiDAR observations for dense depth supervision and blocking photometric gradients from updating the Gaussian spatial parameters. We validate our method on public driving datasets, where it consistently outperforms existing targetless methods in calibration accuracy.

AI.tldr: Targetless LiDAR–camera calibration using 3D Gaussian Splatting with metric-geometry preservation via multi-view depth supervision and inhibited gradient updates, yielding improved calibration accuracy on driving datasets.
AI.motivation: 3DGS-based approaches optimized for rendering quality can drift from true LiDAR geometry; targetless calibration requires accurate cross-modal geometric alignment and metric fidelity.
AI.method: Aggregate multi-view LiDAR observations to provide dense depth supervision; block photometric gradients from updating Gaussian spatial parameters; integrate within a differentiable 3DGS framework to optimize extrinsics without distorting geometry.
AI.result: Outperforms existing targetless methods in LiDAR–camera calibration accuracy on public driving datasets.
AI.conclusion: Preserving the metric geometry of the Gaussian proxy during optimization improves calibration accuracy and mitigates geometry drift inherent in rendering-focused targets.

title: A Multi-Agent system for Multi-Objective constrained optimization
comment: Presented at the 17th Workshop on Optimization and Learning in Multiagent Systems (OptLearnMAS, https://optlearnmas.github.io), co-located with the 25th International Conference on Autonomous Agents and Multiagent Systems (AAMAS 2026)
summary: Many decision-making problems in computing and networking systems can be naturally formulated as cost-minimization problems under performance constraints. In dynamic environments, reinforcement learning (RL) is often used to solve such problems at runtime by embedding both costs and constraint violations into a single scalar reward through weighted penalty terms, following a Lagrangian-inspired formulation. However, in this context the behavior of the learned policy critically depends on the choice of these weights, which are typically selected manually. This makes it difficult to identify an appropriate trade-off between optimizing the primary objective and effectively avoiding constraint violations, particularly in non-stationary environments where their relative importance may change. This paper presents MAMO (Multi-Agent system for Multi-Objective constrained optimization), an approach to tackle this balancing problem through multi-agent RL. MAMO decouples task execution from objective design by formulating the selection of reward weights as a learning problem, providing a !rst step towards more autonomous and robust RL-based solutions for constrained optimization problems in dynamic environments.

AI.tldr: Proposes MAMO, a multi-agent RL framework to learn reward weights for constrained optimization, reducing manual tuning and improving robustness in dynamic environments.
AI.motivation: Manual tuning of penalty weights in Lagrangian-based RL is brittle in non-stationary settings; balance between objective and constraints is context-dependent.
AI.method: MAMO treats reward weight selection as a learning problem within a multi-agent RL setup, decoupling task execution from objective design.
AI.result: Introduces a conceptual framework and first-step approach; reports no empirical results in abstract.
AI.conclusion: MAMO is a promising direction toward autonomous, robust RL-based constrained optimization in changing environments.

title: Neural Additive and Basis Models with Feature Selection and Interactions
comment: Accepted at PAKDD 2024. Code is available at https://github.com/shiralab/NAM-FS
summary: Deep neural networks (DNNs) exhibit attractive performance in various fields but often suffer from low interpretability. The neural additive model (NAM) and its variant called the neural basis model (NBM) use neural networks (NNs) as nonlinear shape functions in generalized additive models (GAMs). Both models are highly interpretable and exhibit good performance and flexibility for NN training. NAM and NBM can provide and visualize the contribution of each feature to the prediction owing to GAM-based architectures. However, when using two-input NNs to consider feature interactions or when applying them to high-dimensional datasets, training NAM and NBM becomes intractable due to the increase in the computational resources required. This paper proposes incorporating the feature selection mechanism into NAM and NBM to resolve computational bottlenecks. We introduce the feature selection layer in both models and update the selection weights during training. Our method is simple and can reduce computational costs and model sizes compared to vanilla NAM and NBM. In addition, it enables us to use two-input NNs even in high-dimensional datasets and capture feature interactions. We demonstrate that the proposed models are computationally efficient compared to vanilla NAM and NBM, and they exhibit better or comparable performance with state-of-the-art GAMs.

AI.tldr: Proposed feature selection layer for NAM/NBM to enable two-input NNs and handle high-dimensional data with reduced computation; shows improved or comparable performance to state-of-the-art GAMs while lowering resource usage.
AI.motivation: Improve interpretability-friendly GAM-based models (NAM/NBM) by addressing computational bottlenecks when modeling feature interactions in high-dimensional data.
AI.method: Introduce a feature selection layer with trainable selection weights updated during training; integrate with NAM/NBM architectures to allow two-input neural nets and shrink model complexity.
AI.result: Computational efficiency is improved and model size reduced compared to vanilla NAM/NBM; ability to use two-input NNs in high-dimensional settings; performance is better or comparable to state-of-the-art GAMs.
AI.conclusion: The proposed feature selection NAM/NBM variants offer a pragmatic trade-off between interpretability and predictive performance, achieving efficiency gains while capturing feature interactions.

title: EFIQA: Explainable Fundus Image Quality Assessment via Anatomical Priors
summary: Image quality control is vital for a wide range of downstream applications. Deep learning-based image quality assessment methods typically train classifiers on dataset-specific quality labels, inheriting two limitations: (1) generalization is tied to the labeling criteria of the training set and (2) these methods cannot provide spatial feedback on where the quality is degraded, lacking explainability. In this work, we propose EFIQA, a framework that requires no quality-related supervision and produces spatial quality maps by design. Rather than learning ``what is degradation" from human-annotated labels, EFIQA learns ``what should be there" by leveraging anatomical priors. For fundus photography, we instantiate this as a two-stage approach, by first training an unsupervised anomaly detector via masked anatomical inpainting to identify regions of missing vasculature, and then distilling this prior knowledge into a shallow adapter mapping features of a frozen foundation model to precise quality maps. External-dataset evaluation demonstrates that this label-free approach with minimal adaptation achieves better performance and explainability compared with supervised methods across benchmarks with different quality criteria, highlighting its potential for real-world applications.

AI.tldr: EFIQA is a label-free, explainable image quality assessment framework for fundus photography that outputs spatial quality maps via a two-stage approach: unsupervised anomaly detection through masked anatomical inpainting and a shallow adapter on a frozen foundation model; external evaluations show superior performance and explainability versus supervised methods across varying quality criteria.
AI.motivation: Current deep learning IQA methods rely on dataset-specific quality labels, which limits generalization and provides little spatial explainability. There is a need for label-free IQA that can provide spatial feedback on degradation, leveraging domain priors to improve robustness.
AI.method: Stage 1 (unsupervised): train an anomaly detector using masked anatomical inpainting to identify regions with missing vasculature in fundus images. Stage 2: distill this anatomical prior into a shallow adapter that maps features from a frozen foundation model to precise, per-pixel quality maps, yielding spatial quality feedback.
AI.result: External-dataset evaluation demonstrates that the label-free EFIQA approach with minimal adaptation achieves better performance and explainability than supervised methods across benchmarks with different quality criteria.
AI.conclusion: EFIQA shows promise for practical, explainable image quality control without heavy labeling, and the approach could generalize to other domains by leveraging domain-specific anatomical priors to produce spatial quality maps.

title: Navigating Unreliable Parametric and Contextual Knowledge: Explicit Knowledge Conflict Resolution for LLM Inference
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have achieved strong performance across a wide range of language-based tasks by leveraging both extensive parametric knowledge and in-context learning ability, enabling them to incorporate external information provided in the input prompt. However, the integration of external knowledge can introduce conflicts, not only between the model's internal parametric knowledge and the external information, but also among multiple pieces of external contexts. Existing approaches typically assume that either the model or the provided context is reliable, overlooking the possibility that both sources may contain errors, and avoid conflicts by privileging one source over the other, rather than actively resolving inconsistencies. To address these limitations, we propose a novel framework MACR for LLM knowledge conflict resolution that moves beyond the conventional binary choice paradigm and incorporates an explicit conflict-resolution mechanism based on a multi-agent reasoning approach. Specifically, we first propose an adaptive knowledge assessment and retrieval approach that employs a modified semantic entropy measure to quantify an LLM's confidence in its answer to a given query. Based on this confidence estimation, MACR either externalizes the model's internal knowledge as textual representations or retrieves relevant external knowledge when internal knowledge is insufficient, generating basic contexts for subsequent reasoning. Then we introduce an inductive multi-agent reasoning framework with three specialized agents that, respectively, induce explicit rules, analyze potential conflicts, and resolve inconsistencies across all available contexts. Empirical results demonstrate that MACR significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines across benchmarks, while also providing interpretable resolutions of explicit conflicts.

AI.tldr: MACR introduces a multi-agent framework to resolve knowledge conflicts in LLMs by adaptively assessing confidence, optionally externalizing internal knowledge or retrieving external data, and using three specialized agents to induce rules, analyze conflicts, and resolve inconsistencies, achieving strong performance and interpretable explanations.
AI.motivation: LLMs often face conflicts between their internal parametric knowledge and external context, and among multiple external sources. Existing approaches assume one reliable source and avoid conflicts, rather than actively resolving inconsistencies.
AI.method: An adaptive knowledge assessment using a modified semantic entropy to estimate confidence in the answer; based on confidence, MA CR externalizes internal knowledge as textual representations or retrieves relevant external knowledge to form basic contexts. An inductive multi-agent framework with three agents: (1) induce explicit rules, (2) analyze potential conflicts, (3) resolve inconsistencies across all contexts.
AI.result: MACR significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on benchmarks and provides interpretable resolutions of explicit conflicts.
AI.conclusion: MACR advances knowledge integration in LLMs by moving beyond a binary choice paradigm to explicit, multi-agent conflict resolution, enabling robust and interpretable handling of conflicting information.

title: Physics-Informed Neural Network with Squeeze-Excitation-like Attention
summary: We introduce SEA-PINN, a novel architecture that incorporates a Squeeze-Excitation-like attention mechanism into physics-informed neural networks to dynamically recalibrate the importance of neurons across layers. A key feature of SEA-PINN is its highly stable initialization. On 17 out of 20 benchmark problems, SEA-PINN exhibit nearly negligible variance and significantly reduced initial loss, establishing a quasi-deterministic and favorable starting point for optimization. Notably, without employing Fourier feature embeddings or periodic activation functions, SEA-PINN attained competitive accuracy (83\% vs. 90\% improvement relative to FNN-PINN on the high-frequency case 7) as compared with TSA-PINN-a model specifically engineered for high-frequency problems via learnable frequencies in sinusoidal activations. Furthermore, integrating SEA-PINN into TSA-PINN boosted performance by 42.49\%. These results underscore SEA-PINN as a lightweight plug-in module that enhances nonlinear representation power, promotes more robust and efficient convergence, and strengthens the overall reliability of physics-informed learning.

AI.tldr: SEA-PINN adds a Squeeze-Excitation-like attention module to PINNs, providing stable initialization and reduced initial loss, with negligible variance on most benchmarks. It achieves competitive accuracy without Fourier features/periodic activations, and boosts TSA-PINN performance by 42.49% when integrated, indicating a lightweight, plug-in enhancement for nonlinear representation and robust convergence.
AI.motivation: PINNs often suffer from unstable optimization and high variance due to random initializations and challenging high-frequency dynamics. A lightweight attention-based mechanism can dynamically reweight neuron importance to improve convergence, reliability, and accuracy without requiring Fourier features or periodic activations.
AI.method: Incorporates a Squeeze-Excitation-like attention mechanism across layers of a PINN to recalibrate neuron importance. Emphasizes a highly stable initialization (low variance, low initial loss). It is presented as a plug-in module, not relying on Fourier feature embeddings or periodic activations. It can be integrated with TSA-PINN to enhance high-frequency problem performance.
AI.result: On 17 of 20 benchmarks, SEA-PINN shows negligible variance and substantially lower initial loss. It achieves competitive accuracy for high-frequency case 7 (about 83%), without Fourier features or periodic activations, and yields a 42.49% performance boost when integrated into TSA-PINN. This supports SEA-PINN as a lightweight, general enhancement for PINNs, improving nonlinear representation power and convergence reliability.
AI.conclusion: SEA-PINN functions as a lightweight plug-in module that strengthens nonlinear representation, stabilizes optimization, and enhances the reliability of physics-informed learning, with notable gains when combined with TSA-PINN for high-frequency problems.

title: FrozenDrive: Zero-Shot Text-Guided Driving Scene Generation and Data Augmentation with Parameter-Free Frozen Diffusion Model
summary: Synthetic data for autonomous driving is surging, powered by diffusion models that promise scalable scene generation. Yet key obstacles remain, as enforcing multi-view and temporal consistency often relies on backbone fine-tuning or added layers, which erodes pre-trained knowledge and weakens text alignment. Models also stay close to the training distribution, struggling under adverse weather and unseen configurations, and fidelity favors frequent over rare classes. We address these gaps with FrozenDrive, a controllable generative framework that preserves a pretrained diffusion models knowledge while achieving strong consistency. FrozenDrive conditions on rich driving-stack signals and text prompts, and introduces knowledge-preserving spatio-temporal attention to impose cross-view alignment and temporal coherence in a single pass within a parameter-free frozen diffusion backbone. An additional object-focused constraint improves per-object fidelity for rare categories. Without any weather- or scene-specific fine-tuning, our model synthesizes globally coherent multi-view driving scenes from text, particularly under adverse and rare conditions, and surpasses prior baselines. On nuScenes, FrozenDrive augmented data significantly improves AD models performance, especially at night and in rain, demonstrating stronger robustness when trained with our scenario-targeted data.

AI.tldr: FrozenDrive introduces a knowledge-preserving, diffusion-based framework for multi-view and temporal driving-scene synthesis without fine-tuning, achieving high consistency and rare-object fidelity conditioned on driving signals and text prompts, improving downstream autonomous driving models under adverse conditions.
AI.motivation: Current diffusion-based synthetic data struggles with cross-view/temporal consistency, generalization to adverse weather, and overfitting to frequent classes when backbone fine-tuning or extra layers are used, which degrades pretrained knowledge and text alignment.
AI.method: Use a parameter-free frozen diffusion backbone augmented with knowledge-preserving spatio-temporal attention to enforce cross-view alignment and temporal coherence in a single pass. Conditions on rich driving-stack signals and text prompts. Includes an object-focused constraint to boost fidelity for rare categories; requires no weather- or scene-specific fine-tuning.
AI.result: Generates globally coherent multi-view driving scenes; maintains high object fidelity for rare categories; augments training data that improves downstream autonomous driving models, especially in night and rain scenarios, demonstrating stronger robustness with scenario-targeted data.
AI.conclusion: FrozenDrive preserves pretrained diffusion knowledge while delivering strong cross-view and temporal consistency without fine-tuning, surpassing prior baselines and enabling robust synthetic data for AD under challenging conditions.

title: Confidence-Aware Automated Assessment of Student-Drawn Scientific Models
summary: Student-generated drawings are widely used in science education to assess learners' conceptual understanding in modeling-based tasks aligned with the Next Generation Science Standards (NGSS). However, scoring such drawings requires expert human judgment to interpret complex visual representations, making large-scale assessment costly to implement and sustain in classroom settings. In this work, we study automated scoring of student-generated scientific drawings using a vision-based model. We evaluate a Vision Transformer (ViT) with parameter-efficient adaptation and propose a confidence-aware scoring framework that derives response-level confidence from test-time predictive distributions. This confidence signal enables selective automation by scoring high-confidence responses automatically while deferring uncertain cases for human review. Experiments on six NGSS-aligned middle school assessment items show that the proposed approach improves scoring reliability while supporting a practical trade-off between automated coverage and scoring risk, highlighting the value of confidence-aware methods for trustworthy educational assessment.

AI.tldr: Vision-based automated scoring of student drawings using ViT with confidence-aware selective automation improves reliability and enables human-in-the-loop scoring for NGSS-aligned items.
AI.motivation: Reducing dependence on expert human raters for scoring student-generated drawings in modeling-based tasks; large-scale assessment is costly.
AI.method: Use Vision Transformer (ViT) with parameter-efficient adaptation; derive response-level confidence from test-time predictive distributions; selective automation to auto-score high-confidence responses and defer uncertain ones to humans; evaluation on six NGSS-aligned middle school items.
AI.result: Improved scoring reliability; practical trade-off between automated coverage and scoring risk; confidence-aware method demonstrated value for trustworthy educational assessment.
AI.conclusion: Confidence-aware, vision-based scoring is a viable approach to scalable, trustworthy assessment of student drawings, enabling efficient automation while preserving human oversight where uncertainty is high.

title: Global Convergence of Gradient Descent for Score Matching in Gaussian Mixtures via Reverse Fisher Divergence
summary: The score matching problem is a central training objective in modern generative modeling, diffusion models, fitting unnormalized statistical models, and inverse problems. A standard approach is to minimize the forward Fisher divergence, where the expectation is taken with respect to the teacher distribution. However, recent results show that even in simple Gaussian mixture model settings, this objective can lead to undesirable and initialization-dependent convergence behavior. In this paper, we study an alternative objective: the reverse Fisher divergence, where the expectation is taken with respect to the student distribution. We analyze gradient descent (GD) for fitting Gaussian mixture models and show that this change in the objective leads to significantly better optimization properties. First, when the teacher distribution is a single Gaussian and the student is a Gaussian mixture model with fixed weights and identity covariances, we prove the global convergence of GD from arbitrary initializations. Second, we extend the analysis to the case where the teacher is also a Gaussian mixture model and prove global convergence guarantees under a global random initialization scheme and a $\widetildeΩ(1)$-separation assumption on the target means. In particular, with high probability, each student component converges near its closest teacher component, and we provide conditions under which the student distribution converges in total variation distance. Our proofs rely on a new Lyapunov-based analysis of the gradient descent dynamics, showing that the reverse Fisher divergence has a much more favorable optimization landscape than the forward Fisher divergence.

AI.tldr: Replacing the forward Fisher divergence with the reverse Fisher divergence (taking expectations w.r.t. the student) yields a notably nicer optimization landscape for Gaussian mixtures, with provable global convergence of gradient descent under realistic assumptions. Two regimes are analyzed: (i) a single-Gaussian teacher and a fixed-weights, identity-covariance student GMM; GD converges globally from any initialization. (ii) a teacher that is itself a GMM; under a global random initialization and a separation assumption on the true means, each student component converges near the corresponding teacher component, with total-variation convergence under suitable conditions.
AI.motivation: Forward Fisher divergence can produce initialization-sensitive and suboptimal convergence even in simple Gaussian mixtures. The study investigates whether reversing the Fisher divergence (sampling the objective w.r.t. the student) yields a more favorable optimization landscape and improved convergence guarantees.
AI.method: Analyze gradient descent dynamics for fitting Gaussian mixtures under the reverse Fisher divergence. Case (i): teacher is a single Gaussian; student is a GMM with fixed weights and identity covariances; prove global convergence from arbitrary initialization. Case (ii): both teacher and student are GMMs; under a global random initialization and a ~Ω(1) separation of means, show that each student component converges to its nearest teacher component; derive conditions yielding convergence in total variation distance. The proofs employ a Lyapunov-based analysis of the gradient dynamics to establish improved landscape properties.
AI.result: Global convergence of gradient descent is established in Case (i) from any initialization. In Case (ii), with high probability under the stated initialization and separation, each student component tracks its nearest teacher component, leading to overall convergence of the student distribution in total variation under appropriate conditions.
AI.conclusion: The reverse Fisher divergence yields a substantially more favorable optimization landscape than the forward version for Gaussian mixtures, enabling stronger convergence guarantees for gradient-based fitting. These results suggest potential robustness and reliability improvements for score-matching-based training in diffusion models and related inverse problems, contingent on separation and initialization assumptions.

title: Pixel-Level Residual Diffusion Transformer: Scalable 3D CT Volume Generation
comment: Accepted at ICLR 2026. Code available at https://github.com/Fredy-Zhang/PRDiT
summary: Generating high-resolution 3D CT volumes with fine details remains challenging due to substantial computational demands and optimization difficulties inherent to existing generative models. In this paper, we propose the Pixel-Level Residual Diffusion Transformer (PRDiT), a scalable generative framework that synthesizes high-quality 3D medical volumes directly at voxel-level. PRDiT introduces a two-stage training architecture comprising 1) a local denoiser in the form of an MLP-based blind estimator operating on overlapping 3D patches to separate low-frequency structures efficiently, and 2) a global residual diffusion transformer employing memory-efficient attention to model and refine high-frequency residuals across entire volumes. This coarse-to-fine modeling strategy simplifies optimization, enhances training stability, and effectively preserves subtle structures without the limitations of an autoencoder bottleneck. Extensive experiments conducted on the LIDC-IDRI and RAD-ChestCT datasets demonstrate that PRDiT consistently outperforms state-of-the-art models, such as HA-GAN, 3D LDM and WDM-3D, achieving significantly lower 3D FID, MMD and Wasserstein distance scores.

AI.tldr: A two-stage, scalable diffusion-based framework (PRDiT) for voxel-level 3D CT synthesis that combines a local patch-based denoiser with a global residual diffusion transformer, achieving state-of-the-art results on LIDC-IDRI and RAD-ChestCT with lower FID, MMD, and Wasserstein distances while reducing optimization difficulties.
AI.motivation: To overcome the high computational demands, unstable optimization, and autoencoder bottlenecks in existing 3D GAN/diffusion models for high-resolution medical volumes, enabling voxel-level synthesis with better detail preservation.
AI.method: A coarse-to-fine two-stage approach: (1) a local denoiser implemented as an MLP-based blind estimator applied to overlapping 3D patches to extract low-frequency structure; (2) a global residual diffusion transformer with memory-efficient attention to model/refine high-frequency residuals across whole volumes.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on LIDC-IDRI and RAD-ChestCT shows PRDiT outperforms HA-GAN, 3D LDM, and WDM-3D, achieving substantially lower 3D FID, MMD, and Wasserstein distances.
AI.conclusion: PRDiT provides a scalable, stable training paradigm that preserves subtle anatomical details without an autoencoder bottleneck, enabling high-quality voxel-level 3D CT generation with improved performance over current state-of-the-art models.

title: Lagrange: An Open-Vocabulary, Energy-Based Sparse Framework for Generalized End-to-End Driving
summary: Scaling end-to-end autonomous driving to complex, open-world environments requires perceptual models that generalize to anomalous scenarios and planners that produce kinematically valid trajectories. Existing paradigms face a distinct dichotomy between representational efficiency and generalization capacity. Dense models (e.g., occupancy networks), while geometrically robust, incur critical computational bottlenecks and struggle with high-level semantic reasoning. Conversely, sparse, query-based planners are efficient but reliant on closed-set definitions, rendering them vulnerable to out-of-distribution (OOD) events. Although recent Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models offer open-vocabulary reasoning, their autoregressive, discrete token generation fundamentally conflicts with the continuous, high-frequency control requirements of vehicle dynamics. To address this, we propose Lagrange, an open-vocabulary, computationally sparse driving framework based on Masked Latent Fields (MLF). Rather than relying on dense volumetric reconstructions or closed-set query mechanisms, Lagrange exploits Vision-Language Models (VLMs) to encode class-agnostic object proposals into continuous semantic visual tokens. We introduce an intent-driven masked cross-attention module that temporally filters irrelevant entities, decoding the attended tokens into an implicit continuous energy field defined over spatial coordinates. By framing decision-making as a Lagrangian action minimization problem spanning this energy field, we enforce strict compliance with vehicle kinematics while executing collision avoidance. Extensive offline evaluations on both standard (nuScenes) and long-tail (CODA) benchmarks demonstrate that Lagrange establishes a promising framework for robust, interpretable, and kinematically feasible open-world autonomy.

AI.tldr: Lagrange is a novel open-vocabulary, sparse driving framework that uses Masked Latent Fields to produce a continuous semantic energy field from Vision-Language Models, and optimizes control via a Lagrangian action minimization under kinematic constraints for safe, collision-free autonomous driving; validated offline on nuScenes and CODA.
AI.motivation: To overcome the trade-off between dense, geometry-heavy representations and sparse, closed-set planners, enabling open-world perception and continuous, high-rate vehicle control while mitigating OOD risks.
AI.method: Encode class-agnostic object proposals as continuous semantic tokens via Vision-Language Models; employ an intent-driven masked cross-attention module to filter irrelevant entities; decode tokens into an implicit continuous energy field over spatial coordinates; formulate planning as a Lagrangian action minimization problem that enforces vehicle kinematics and collision avoidance.
AI.result: Offline evaluations on standard (nuScenes) and long-tail (CODA) benchmarks show that Lagrange achieves robust, interpretable, and kinematically feasible open-world autonomy, indicating promise for sparse, open-vocabulary driving.
AI.conclusion: The framework demonstrates a viable path to combine open-vocabulary perception with energy-based planning for safe, open-world autonomous driving, highlighting interpretability and practical feasibility while signaling areas for future work on real-time performance and broader validation.

title: On the Oracle Complexity of Interpolation-Based Gradient Descent
summary: Recent work on first-order optimizers for empirical risk minimization (ERM) has suggested that smoothness of ERM loss functions in the training data, rather than in the optimization parameters, can be leveraged to improve the oracle complexity of gradient descent (GD) methods. In this paper, we propose an inexact gradient method, piecewise polynomial interpolation-based gradient descent (PPI-GD), which approximates the full gradient in each iteration by querying the first-order oracle at equidistant points in the data domain to construct polynomial interpolants of the resulting gradient samples over appropriately sized patches of the data domain. We analyze the oracle complexity of PPI-GD for strongly convex and non-convex loss functions when the data space dimension is bounded by a polylogarithmic function of the number of training samples, and find it to outperform several GD variants in key regimes when the loss function is sufficiently smooth. Furthermore, our analysis extends several techniques from the error analysis of bicubic spline interpolants to the setting of $d$-variate tensor product polynomial interpolants which may be of independent interest in interpolation analysis.

AI.tldr: We propose PPI-GD, an inexact gradient method that uses piecewise polynomial interpolation of gradient samples in the data domain to approximate the full gradient. The method achieves improved oracle complexity for both strongly convex and non-convex ERM under data-domain smoothness, provided the data dimension is polylogarithmically bounded in the number of samples. The analysis extends error techniques from bicubic spline interpolation to d-variate tensor-product polynomial interpolants, which may be of independent interest.
AI.motivation: Leverage smoothness of ERM losses in the data domain to reduce gradient oracle evaluations and improve gradient-descent efficiency. Address both strongly convex and non-convex objectives in ERM under a manageable data-dimension regime and connect interpolation error analysis with optimization oracle complexity.
AI.method: Introduce PPI-GD: in each iteration, query the gradient at equidistant points in the data domain to form samples, construct polynomial interpolants over patches (piecewise, potentially tensor-product in d dimensions), and use the interpolant as an approximate full gradient for the GD update. Analyze the oracle complexity for strongly convex and non-convex losses under a data-space dimension bounded polylogarithmically in the sample size, and relate the analysis to extensions of bicubic spline error techniques to d-variate tensor-product polynomials.
AI.result: PPI-GD attains better oracle complexity than several standard GD variants in key regimes when the loss is sufficiently smooth, under the stated dimension bound and smoothness assumptions for both strongly convex and non-convex ERMs.
AI.conclusion: Exploiting data-domain smoothness via piecewise polynomial gradient interpolation can yield oracle-efficient GD methods. The work also advances interpolation theory by extending spline-based error analyses to d-variate tensor-product polynomial interpolants, with potential broader applicability beyond the presented optimization setting.

title: SAM3 Self-Distillation for Fine-Grained GOOSE 2D Semantic Segmentation
comment: 4th place in ICRA 2026 GOOSE 2D Semantic Segmentation Challenge
summary: We describe our 4th-place entry to the ICRA 2026 GOOSE 2D Fine-Grained Semantic Segmentation Challenge, which reached a composite mean Intersection-over-Union (mIoU) of 69.73% on the official 1,815-image test set. Our model adapts the image encoder of a recent visual foundation model, Segment Anything Model 3 (SAM3), with a lightweight decoder. Beyond this, we contribute two techniques and one empirical finding: (i) a self-distillation scheme that re-uses SAM3 itself, prompted with ground-truth boxes, as a teacher on the classes where it outperforms our own model; (ii) an image-level multi-scale test-time augmentation scheme that restores multi-scale inference for a fixed-input-size model by rescaling the image rather than the model input; and (iii) the finding that an aggressive photometric distortion from a winning 2025 GOOSE 2D entry, transplanted onto our pipeline, is its single largest source of improvement.

AI.tldr: A high-performing 4th-place ICRA 2026 GOOSE entry that adapts SAM3's image encoder with a lightweight decoder, plus self-distillation using SAM3 as teacher, image-level multi-scale test-time augmentation by image rescaling, and the use of aggressive photometric distortion from a previous winning entry; achieving 69.73% mIoU on 1,815 test images.
AI.motivation: Leverage a recent visual foundation model (SAM3) to boost fine-grained semantic segmentation while maintaining efficiency; investigate complementary strategies (self-distillation, test-time augmentation, and perturbation) to push performance on the challenging GOOSE dataset.
AI.method: Replace/augment the decoder of SAM3 with a lightweight decoder; perform self-distillation by prompting SAM3 (as teacher) on classes where it outperforms the model using ground-truth boxes; implement image-level multi-scale TT inference by rescaling the image rather than the model input; transplant aggressive photometric distortion from a 2025 winning entry into the pipeline.
AI.result: Achieves a composite mean IoU of 69.73% on the official 1,815-image test set, placing 4th in the competition.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that adapting a vision foundation model with lightweight decoding and targeted distillation, together with image-level TT augmentation and strategic perturbations, can achieve strong fine-grained segmentation performance; suggests future work on more robust teacher-student setups and perturbation strategies.

title: Leveraging systems' non-linearity to tackle the scarcity of data in the design of Intelligent Fault Diagnosis Systems
summary: Deep Transfer Learning (DTL) allows for the efficient building of Intelligent Fault Diagnosis Systems (IFDS). On the other hand, DTL methods still heavily rely on large amounts of labelled data. Obtaining such an amount of data can be challenging when dealing with machines or structures faults. This document proposes a novel approach to the design of vibration-based IFDS using DTL in condition of strong data scarcity. A periodic multi-excitation level procedure leveraging intrinsic non-linearities of real-world systems is used to produce images that can be conveniently analysed by pre-trained Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) to diagnose faults. A new data visualization method and its augmentation technique are proposed in this paper to tackle the typical lack of data encountered during the design of IFDS. Experimental validation on a railway pantograph structure provides effective support for the proposed method.

AI.tldr: DTL-based vibration Fault Diagnosis System under data scarcity using periodic multi-excitation to generate CNN-friendly images; introduces a new data visualization/augmentation method; validated on a railway pantograph.
AI.motivation: DTL typically requires large labeled datasets, which are hard to obtain for machinery faults. The work targets effective fault diagnosis under strong data scarcity using transfer learning and image-based representations.
AI.method: Generate fault-relevant images from vibration signals via a periodic multi-excitation procedure that exploits intrinsic non-linearities. Use pre-trained CNNs for diagnosis. Propose a novel data visualization and augmentation technique to address limited data.
AI.result: Experimental validation on a railway pantograph structure demonstrates the approach is effective for fault diagnosis under data scarcity.
AI.conclusion: DTL with image-based representations and targeted visualization/augmentation offers a viable solution for vibration-based IFDS in settings with limited labeled data; demonstrated on railway pantograph with promising results.

title: Matching Markets meet Cumulative Prospect Theory: Towards Optimal and Adversarially Robust Learning
summary: We study a multi-agent multi-armed bandit problem in the competitive setup with two-sided matching markets under a human centric decision making model. To capture human preferences, we use cumulative prospect theory (CPT) that weighs the actions of the agent in a nonlinear fashion using a ($α$-Hölder continuous) weight function. CPT has been widely used in behavioral economics and risk sensitive machine learning to emulate human preferences. We analyze the state-of-the-art learning algorithm with CPT weight distorted rewards and obtain a player optimal regret of $\mathcal{O}(K\log T \left(\frac{1}Δ\right)^{2/α})$, where $K$ denotes the number of arms, $T$ is the learning horizon, and $Δ$ represents (suitably defined) players' minimum preference gap. Noticing the dependence on $Δ$ to be sub-optimal, we further improve this regret by judiciously selecting the active set of arms during exploration, which removes the dependence on $K$ in the dominant term and achieves an improved (optimal) regret guarantees in the setting where the number of arms $K$ is significantly larger than the number of players $N$. In addition, we consider adversarial markets where the observed rewards of the agents may be corrupted. We propose and analyze algorithms for robust markets with CPT as risk sensitive measure in both settings where the total corruption budget is known and where it is unknown, and establish logarithmic player-optimal regret guarantees in both cases.

AI.tldr: CPT-weighted multi-agent bandits in competitive two-sided markets: derive player-optimal regret under CPT, improve by active-arm selection to remove K-dependence when K>>N, and extend to robust/adversarial markets with known/unknown corruption budgets, achieving logarithmic regrets.
AI.motivation: Model human-centric decision making in competitive matching markets by incorporating cumulative prospect theory (CPT) to capture nonlinear risk preferences, and address suboptimal dependence on the number of arms in regret bounds; extend to robust settings under potential reward corruption.
AI.method: Formulate a CPT-weighted bandit framework with α-Hölder weight functions; analyze existing CPT-based algorithms to obtain player-optimal regret O(K log T (1/Δ)^{2/α}); introduce an active-arm set exploration strategy to remove K from the dominant term when K >> N; extend to adversarial/robust market settings and develop algorithms for known and unknown corruption budgets; establish logarithmic regret guarantees in both robust cases.
AI.result: Initial CPT-based regret bounds scale as O(K log T (1/Δ)^{2/α}). Active-arm exploration eliminates K-dependence in the main term for large K, yielding improved (optimal) regret when K≫N. In adversarial/corrupted-reward settings, proposed robust algorithms achieve logarithmic player-optimal regret in both known and unknown budget scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that CPT-informed planning in multi-agent bandits can achieve strong, near-optimal regret zároveň under potential reward corruption; highlights the benefit of active-arm selection to handle large arm sets; opens avenues for empirical validation and extension to broader market dynamics and risk models.

title: GroundControl: Anticipating Navigation Failures in Vision-Language Agents via Trajectory-Consistent Uncertainty Estimates
summary: Vision-language navigation agents achieve competitive average success on benchmark tasks, yet failures often arise through predictable trajectory-level breakdowns such as oscillation, stagnation, or inefficient detours. Reliable deployment, therefore, requires uncertainty signals that anticipate emerging failure dynamics during execution rather than reflect only instantaneous action entropy. We introduce \emph{GroundControl}, a trajectory-consistent uncertainty estimator defined as statistical deviation from nominal goal-directed distance-to-goal dynamics aggregated over an episode. GroundControl models distance evolution using a constant-velocity Kalman filter and combines normalized innovation statistics with complementary trajectory features capturing progress, monotonicity, path efficiency, and oscillatory behavior. The resulting uncertainty score reflects geometric and temporal inconsistency in navigation behavior rather than local prediction dispersion. To evaluate uncertainty quality independently of task success, we formalize \emph{Selective Risk--Coverage Navigation (SRCN)}, a protocol that measures how effectively an uncertainty score ranks episodes by failure or inefficiency using risk--coverage curves and AURC / E-AURC summaries. Across five EB-Navigation splits ($N=300$ episodes), trajectory-consistent uncertainty achieves near-oracle ordering under success-based selective risk, with weighted-average $\mathrm{E\text{-}AURC}_{\mathrm{SR}}=0.0024$ for the GPT-4o model, substantially outperforming entropy-, conformal-, and heuristic baselines. Under SPL-based selective evaluation, GroundControl consistently achieves the lowest AURC and E-AURC across models and navigation splits. These results show that modeling deviation from goal-directed dynamics provides an interpretable and robust signal for anticipating navigation failures in vision-language agents.

AI.tldr: GroundControl provides a trajectory-consistent uncertainty score for vision-language navigation by modeling goal-directed distance-to-goal dynamics with a constant-velocity Kalman filter and combining trajectory features, enabling near-oracle risk ranking of episodes via SRCN.
AI.motivation: Current uncertainty signals for navigation often rely on instantaneous action dispersion (e.g., entropy) and fail to anticipate failures that manifest in trajectory dynamics (oscillations, stagnation, detours).
