A trajectory-consistent, interpretable uncertainty measure could improve reliable deployment by signaling emerging failure dynamics.
AI.method: Model distance evolution with a constant-velocity Kalman filter to capture nominal distance-to-goal dynamics; compute normalized innovation statistics; fuse with trajectory features (progress, monotonicity, path efficiency, oscillation) to produce GroundControl scores. Evaluate using Selective Risk–Coverage Navigation (SRCN) with risk–coverage curves and AURC/E-AURC metrics across five EB-Navigation splits (N=300).
AI.result: GroundControl achieves near-oracle episode ordering under success-based SRCN (weighted E-AURC_SR = 0.0024 for GPT-4o), outperforming entropy, conformal, and heuristic baselines. Under SPL-based evaluation, GroundControl attains the lowest AURC and E-AURC across models and splits.
AI.conclusion: Modeling deviation from goal-directed dynamics yields an interpretable, robust uncertainty signal for anticipating navigation failures in vision-language agents, enabling more reliable deployment.

title: TriFlow: Generating Artist-Like 3D Mesh Topology via Nearest-Vertex Vector Fields
summary: We present TriFlow, a new generative approach for producing compact 3D meshes with artist-like triangle topology directly from input geometry conditions such as signed distance fields. Our key insight is to represent mesh topology as a nearest-vertex vector field (NVF) defined over the surface, where each point encodes its association to the nearest triangle vertex in the local barycentric frame. We train a latent flow-matching model to synthesize this field, enabling topology generation conditioned on the input geometry. To extract a coherent mesh, we cluster surface regions using the generated NVF and guide a constrained quadric error metric (QEM) mesh simplification with topology-aware optimization. This yields output meshes that closely match the input geometry while exhibiting structured, artist-like connectivity. Experiments demonstrate that TriFlow achieves stronger generalization and significantly improved topology quality compared to state-of-the-art learning-based approaches, alongside 90% lower Chamfer Distance and an 8x speedup.

AI.tldr: TriFlow learns a geometry-conditioned NVF to generate artist-like mesh topology, then uses NVF-guided clustering and constrained QEM to produce coherent, topology-aware meshes with strong generalization and efficiency gains.
AI.motivation: Current learning-based topology generation for 3D meshes often yields poor topology quality, limited generalization, and heavy post-processing. A representation that encodes topology directly on the surface and can be conditioned on input geometry is needed to produce structured connectivity.
AI.method: Define a nearest-vertex vector field (NVF) on the surface encoding vertex association in a local barycentric frame. Train a latent flow-matching model to synthesize NVF conditioned on input geometry (e.g., signed distance fields). Cluster surface regions using the NVF to form topology regions, then perform topology-aware constrained quadric error metric (QEM) mesh simplification to extract a coherent mesh.
AI.result: TriFlow achieves stronger generalization and significantly improved topology quality compared to state-of-the-art learning methods, with about 90% reduction in Chamfer Distance and an 8x speedup.
AI.conclusion: NVF-based topology learning, coupled with flow-matching and topology-aware simplification, yields high-quality, artist-like meshes that align with input geometry, offering gains in topology quality, generalization, and efficiency.

title: SoftSkill: Behavioral Compression for Contextual Adaptation
summary: Agent skills are commonly deployed as natural-language Markdown files that encode answer policies, evidence-use habits, and task procedures. These files are readable and portable, but they are consumed indirectly: for each task instance, a frozen language model must translate a long textual artifact into generation-time behavior. This paper asks whether a natural-language skill can instead initialize a compact continuous context object, refined by a trainable soft delta while the base model remains frozen. We propose SoftSkill, a frozen-backbone method that tunes such soft skills with next-token prediction and deploys them as latent behavioral priors at inference time. In our main single-round setting, a length-32 SoftSkill prefix on Qwen3.5-4B improves over no-skill prompting by 8.3 points on SearchQA, 42.1 points on LiveMath, and 1.3 points on DocVQA. Relative to SkillOpt, SoftSkill improves accuracy by 5.2 points on SearchQA and 12.5 points on LiveMath, while replacing hundreds to thousands of Markdown skill tokens with a few virtual tokens. We further study agentic execution as a harder boundary case, where sparse trajectory imitation provides useful signal but does not yet robustly compress long-horizon procedural behavior. More broadly, the results suggest that some task skills are better treated not as additional Markdown to be reinterpreted at inference time, but as compact latent controls over how a frozen model enters the task.

AI.tldr: SoftSkill introduces a latent-prefix skill mechanism for frozen LMs, training a compact 32-token latent context that encodes task policies and evidence-use habits. It outperforms traditional Markdown-skill prompts and no-skill prompts on several benchmarks, reducing explicit tokenized skills and enabling better generalization.
AI.motivation: Reduce inference-time translation of natural-language skill artifacts by shifting to a compact, trainable latent context that guides a frozen base model, improving efficiency and potentially robustness for task execution.
AI.method: Train a length-32 SoftSkill prefix on a frozen LM (Qwen3.5-4B) via next-token prediction. Deploy as latent behavioral priors at inference. Compare to Markdown-based SkillOpt and explore sparse trajectory imitation for agentic execution.
AI.result: Single-round: +8.3 points on SearchQA, +42.1 points on LiveMath, +1.3 points on DocVQA vs no-skill prompting. Relative to SkillOpt: +5.2 points (SearchQA), +12.5 points (LiveMath). Markdown tokens reduced from hundreds/thousands to a few virtual tokens. Additional analysis suggests sparse trajectory imitation offers signal but does not robustly compress long-horizon behavior.
AI.conclusion: Some task skills are better realized as compact latent controls over a frozen model rather than extensive Markdown prompts, indicating a new direction for efficient, latent skill integration in LMs.

title: SL-S4Wave: Self-Supervised Learning of Physiological Waveforms with Structured State Space Models
summary: Modeling long-sequence medical time series data, such as electrocardiograms (ECG), poses significant challenges due to high sampling rates, multichannel signal complexity, inherent noise, and limited labeled data. While recent self-supervised learning (SSL) methods, based on various encoder architectures such as convolutional neural networks, have been proposed to learn representations from unlabeled data, they often fall short in capturing long-range dependencies and noise-invariant features. Structured state space models (S4) excel at long-sequence modeling, but existing S4 architectures fail to capture the unique characteristics of multichannel physiological waveforms. In this work, we propose SL-S4Wave, a self-supervised learning framework that combines contrastive learning with a tailored encoder built on structured state space models. The encoder incorporates multi-layer global convolution using multiscale subkernels, enabling the capture of both fine-grained local patterns and long-range temporal dependencies in noisy, high-resolution multichannel waveforms. Extensive experiments on real-world datasets demonstrate that SL-S4Wave (1) consistently outperforms state-of-the-art supervised and self-supervised baselines in a challenging arrhythmia detection task, (2) achieves high performance with significantly fewer labeled examples, showcasing strong label efficiency, and (3) maintains robust performance on long waveform segments, highlighting its capacity to model complex temporal dynamics in long sequences that most existing approaches fail to efficiently model, and (4) transfers effectively to unseen arrhythmia types, underscoring its robust cross-domain generalization. We additionally evaluate SL-S4Wave on multiple EEG tasks, achieving superior performance over strong baselines, demonstrating generalizability of our approach beyond cardiac waveforms.

AI.tldr: SL-S4Wave uses a structured state space model encoder with multiscale global convolution and contrastive self-supervised learning to model long, noisy multichannel biomedical waveforms; it achieves state-of-the-art results on ECG arrhythmia tasks with strong label efficiency and demonstrates generalization to unseen arrhythmias and EEG tasks.
AI.motivation: Long-sequence medical time series (e.g., ECG, EEG) are high-dimensional, noisy, and labeled data are scarce. Existing SSL and CNN-based encoders struggle to capture long-range dependencies and noise-invariant features. Structured state space models (S4) excel at long-sequence modeling but do not capture multichannel physiological waveform characteristics well.
AI.method: Propose SL-S4Wave, a self-supervised learning framework that pairs contrastive learning with a tailored encoder based on structured state space models. The encoder incorporates multi-layer global convolution using multiscale subkernels to capture both fine-grained local patterns and long-range temporal dependencies in noisy, high-resolution multichannel waveforms.
AI.result: Empirical results show SL-S4Wave consistently outperforms state-of-the-art supervised and SSL baselines on arrhythmia detection with limited labels, exhibits strong label efficiency, maintains performance on long waveform segments, transfers well to unseen arrhythmia types, and achieves superior EEG performance, indicating broad generalizability beyond cardiac data.
AI.conclusion: SL-S4Wave demonstrates that combining S4-based long-sequence modeling with multiscale convolutional augmentation in an SSL framework yields robust, label-efficient modeling of multichannel biomedical signals (ECG; EEG) and strong cross-domain generalization.

title: Fast Human Attention Prediction for Fixation-guided Active Perception in Autonomous Navigation
comment: Accepted to the IEEE/RSJ International Conference on Intelligent Robots and Systems (IROS 2026)
summary: Human visual attention relies on structured scanpaths to efficiently process scenes, yet instilling this behavior into robot autonomy is in its infancy and hindered by the high,computational costs of existing predictive models. To address this, we introduce GazeLNN, a computationally lightweight,scanpath prediction model that leverages Liquid Neural Networks as its recurrent engine and employs MobileNetV3 for feature extraction. Operating auto-regressively, the architecture predicts sequential fixation heatmaps conditioned on the current visual stimulus and fixation history. Despite requiring only 0.61 GFLOPs, GazeLNN achieves state-of-the-art performance on the MIT Low Resolution dataset achieving 0.47 ScanMatch score. It outperforms existing recurrent baselines across diverse evaluation metrics, while reducing computational costs by 99.40% and accelerating inference by up to six times. To investigate the role of human attention modeling in robot autonomy and demonstrate the practical utility of this highly efficient architecture, we integrate GazeLNN into an active camera-robot control policy trained via Reinforcement Learning. This integration enables human-fixation-guided perception during autonomous navigation, validated through successful real-world deployments on an aerial robot.

AI.tldr: GazeLNN is a lightweight, autoregressive gaze-prediction model for robots that uses Liquid Neural Networks and MobileNetV3, achieving state-of-the-art performance with minimal computation and enabling human-fixation-guided autonomous navigation, validated on aerial robotics.
AI.motivation: Reduce the computational burden of predictive gaze models while maintaining accuracy to enable real-time, gaze-guided perception in robotic systems.
AI.method: Liquid Neural Networks as RNN core; MobileNetV3 for feature extraction; autoregressive architecture predicting sequential fixation heatmaps conditioned on current stimulus and fixation history.
AI.result: Achieves 0.47 ScanMatch on MIT Low Resolution dataset with 0.61 GFLOPs; 99.40% reduction in compute; up to 6x faster inference; integrated into RL-based camera control with successful real-world deployments on an aerial robot.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that highly efficient, gaze-informed perception can be effectively integrated into autonomous navigation, offering practical benefits for real-time robot autonomy.

title: SA-VIS: Sparse frame Annotations for training Video Instance Segmentation
summary: Recent online video instance segmentation (VIS) methods have achieved impressive results, thus becoming the preferred approach to segment instances in videos. Despite the resurgence of impressive single image models, the online (or semi-online) VIS approaches outperform single-image models (e.g., based on SAM) by using long sequences of densely annotated frames during training. However,such a training setup of VIS is expensive in the sense of compute as well as dense annotations required. In order to solve these major flaws, we argue that the effective modeling of the instances and their evolution in videos do not require densely annotated frames. To that end, we propose a simple and effective module, called Past-frames Feature Propagation (PFP) which aggregates low-dimensional features from the image encoder of multiple frames. This simple low-compute module provides tremendous learning capability in using sparse video frame labels for end-to-end training. Combined with a light-weight frame-specific Instance Queries, our Sparse frame Annotation VIS (SA-VIS) significantly improves performance over its baseline. Most interestingly, our simple design that avoids complexities effectively bridges the gap in accuracy between training on sparsely and densely annotated video sequences. This translates to a mere 0.4% drop in performance of SA-VIS when using annotations for only 1/5 of the images in the dataset. Empirically, SA-VIS shows strong improvements over the baseline on YouTube-VIS 2019/2021/2022 and Occluded VIS (OVIS) and an over 1% improvement in AP on the state-of-the-art in a limited annotations scenario.

AI.tldr: A lightweight SA-VIS framework enabling effective video instance segmentation with sparse annotations by propagating past-frame features and using frame-specific Instance Queries, achieving near-dense-annotation performance with only a fraction of labeled frames.
AI.motivation: Reduce the compute and annotation burden of online VIS by leveraging temporal information without dense supervision; determine if instance evolution can be modeled with past-frame feature propagation.
AI.method: Introduce Past-frames Feature Propagation (PFP) that aggregates low-dimensional features from multiple frames' image encoders; combine with lightweight frame-specific Instance Queries for end-to-end training on sparsely annotated videos.
AI.result: SA-VIS significantly outperforms its baseline on YouTube-VIS 2019/2021/2022 and OVIS; with sparse annotations (1/5 of frames) the performance drops only 0.4%; achieves over 1% AP improvement in limited-annotation scenarios compared to state-of-the-art methods.
AI.conclusion: A simple, low-compute module (PFP) can bridge the gap between sparse and dense annotation VIS, enabling strong performance with substantially fewer labeled frames and robust results across multiple VIS datasets.

title: Automating SKILL.md Generation for Computer-Using Agents via Interaction Trajectory Mining
summary: Explicit skill libraries make computer-using agents easier to inspect, but it remains unclear whether such libraries can be mined from interaction data in a way that improves downstream policies. We study this question through a three-stage pipeline that segments GUI trajectories, clusters segments into candidate skills, and trains a skill-aware policy from the resulting annotations. The mined clusters are readable on the source benchmark: five of eight clusters have at least 0.95 purity against InteraSkill Workflows labels. However, readability does not imply transfer. GRPO improves IW skill-step accuracy only from 18.5\% to 20.5\%, leaves BrowseComp+ essentially unchanged, and underperforms trivial frequency priors on key source-domain metrics. We therefore present the method as a diagnostic study: trajectory mining can expose inspectable skill structure, but the current boundary detector, orderless segment representation, and offline reward model are insufficient for reliable cross-domain policy improvement.

AI.tldr: Three-stage pipeline to mine GUI skills from interaction data yields readable but non-transferable skill clusters; policy improvement across domains is marginal; current components insufficient for cross-domain transfer.
AI.motivation: Assess whether explicitly mined skill libraries from interaction data can improve downstream policies and whether readability of mined skills correlates with cross-domain transfer.
AI.method: Segment GUI trajectories; cluster segments into candidate skills; train a skill-aware policy from resulting annotations. Evaluate readability via purity against InteraSkill Workflows labels; assess cross-domain transfer on IW tasks (GRPO, BrowseComp+) with baseline and priors.
AI.result: Mined clusters show readability: five of eight clusters have at least 0.95 purity against IW labels. However, readability does not guarantee transfer: GRPO improves skill-step accuracy from 18.5% to 20.5%; BrowseComp+ largely unchanged; performance underperforms trivial frequency priors on key source-domain metrics.
AI.conclusion: Trajectory mining can reveal inspectable skill structure, but the current boundary detector, orderless segment representation, and offline reward model are insufficient for reliable cross-domain policy improvement; the work should be treated as diagnostic rather than demonstrating practical cross-domain transfer.

title: Adversarial Bandit Optimization with Globally Bounded Perturbations to Convex Losses
summary:
We study adversarial bandit optimization in which the loss functions may be non-convex and non-smooth. In each round, the learner selects an action and observes only the loss incurred at that action. The loss consists of an underlying convex and $β$-smooth component and an adversarial perturbation that may be chosen after observing the learner's action. The perturbations are subject to a global budget controlling their cumulative magnitude over time.
  This framework extends the globally budgeted, post-action perturbation model from underlying linear losses to general convex and $β$-smooth losses. For this broader class, we establish expected regret guarantees that explicitly characterize the effect of the perturbation budget.
  To establish these guarantees, we modify a standard bandit optimization algorithm and develop an analysis that controls the additional regret caused by the perturbations. In the absence of perturbations, our results reduce to regret guarantees for the standard bandit convex optimization setting with $β$-smooth losses.

AI.tldr: Extends globally budgeted, post-action perturbations from linear losses to general convex and β-smooth losses in adversarial bandit optimization; provides regret bounds that quantify perturbation budget impact and reduce to standard results when the budget is zero.
AI.motivation: Address robustness of bandit optimization to adversarial perturbations under a global budget, extending to non-linear, smooth convex losses beyond linear losses.
AI.method: Modify a standard bandit optimization algorithm and develop an analysis that bounds the additional regret due to perturbations, under a cumulative perturbation budget; treat loss as a convex β-smooth component plus adversarial perturbation; derive regret guarantees that explicitly depend on the budget.
AI.result: Expected regret bounds that explicitly quantify how the perturbation budget affects performance; when perturbations are absent, the guarantees reduce to those of standard bandit convex optimization with β-smooth losses.
AI.conclusion: Generalizes budgeted post-action perturbations to a broader loss class; perturbations incur extra, controllable regret; the framework recovers classical results in the zero-budget limit and sets the stage for further refinements or extensions.

title: HEad and neCK TumOR (HECKTOR) 2025: Benchmark of Segmentation, Diagnosis, and Prognosis in Multimodal PET/CT
comment: 17 pages, 4 figures, 4 tables. Overview paper for the HECKTOR 2025 challenge, held as a satellite event at MICCAI 2025. Challenge website: https://hecktor.grand-challenge.org/
summary: Head and neck cancers (HNC) represent a significant global health burden, with accurate tumor delineation being essential for effective radiotherapy planning. The complexity of the oropharyngeal anatomy, combined with the heterogeneous appearance of tumors on imaging, makes manual segmentation time-intensive and subject to inter-observer variability. Beyond segmentation, predicting long-term clinical outcomes, such as recurrence-free survival (RFS), and determining human papillomavirus (HPV) status from noninvasive imaging, remain challenging yet clinically valuable goals. The HECKTOR 2025 challenge addresses these needs by establishing a comprehensive benchmark for automated HNC analysis using multimodal PET/CT imaging and electronic health records. Building on previous editions (2020-2022), this challenge features an expanded multi-institutional dataset comprising over 1,100 patients from 10 centers worldwide. Participants were tasked with three complementary objectives: (1) segmenting primary gross tumor volumes (GTVp) and metastatic lymph nodes (GTVn), (2) predicting recurrence-free survival, and (3) classifying HPV status. The challenge attracted 35 registered teams, with 15 final submissions evaluated on a held-out test set. Top-performing algorithms achieved a mean Dice similarity coefficient of 0.75 for segmentation, a concordance index of 0.66 for survival prediction, and a balanced accuracy of 0.56 for HPV classification. This paper presents a comprehensive analysis of the submitted methodologies, evaluates their performance across different lesion characteristics, and discusses their implications for clinical translation in automated oncology workflows and decision support systems.

AI.tldr: HECKTOR 2025 establishes a multimodal PET/CT + EHR benchmark for automated head and neck cancer analysis across three tasks: (1) segmentation of primary GTVp and nodal GTVn, (2) recurrence-free survival prediction, and (3) HPV status classification. The expanded dataset includes >1,100 patients from 10 centers. 35 teams registered; 15 submitted for the held-out test set. Top results: Dice=0.75 for segmentation, C-index=0.66 for survival, balanced accuracy=0.56 for HPV classification.
AI.motivation: To reduce manual segmentation time and inter‑observer variability in head and neck cancer, enable noninvasive prognostication, and determine HPV status from imaging, by providing a standardized benchmark and dataset for automated analysis.
AI.method: A community challenge using multimodal PET/CT imaging and electronic health records, with three tasks (GTVp/GTVn segmentation, RFS prediction, HPV classification). Data from >1,100 patients across 10 institutions. 35 teams registered; 15 submissions evaluated on a held-out test set. Performance was analyzed across lesion characteristics and across methods.
AI.result: Top-performing algorithms achieved a mean Dice of 0.75 for segmentation, a concordance index of 0.66 for RFS prediction, and a balanced accuracy of 0.56 for HPV classification. The study provides a comprehensive methodology analysis and insights into cross-task performance and generalizability.
AI.conclusion: The HECKTOR 2025 benchmark demonstrates the feasibility and potential impact of automated HNC analysis pipelines for segmentation, survival prediction, and HPV status determination, while highlighting challenges in generalization and integration into clinical workflows; it lays groundwork for clinically translatable automated oncology decision support.

title: Rethinking Shrinkage Bias in LLM FP4 Pretraining: Geometric Origin, Systemic Impact, and UFP4 Recipe
summary: FP4 training promises substantial reductions in memory and computation cost for LLM pretraining, yet current FP4 hardware paths and recipes, including NVIDIA Blackwell/Rubin-class systems and AMD MI350-series GPUs, remain centered on E2M1 data elements. In this study, we identify a fundamental limitation of that choice: non-uniform formats such as E2M1 inherently suffer from Shrinkage Bias, a systematic negative rounding error caused by the geometric asymmetry of their representable bins. We show that this bias accumulates multiplicatively across layers and is amplified by the Random Hadamard Transform (RHT), providing a unified explanation for the training instability observed in existing E2M1-based FP4 recipes. In contrast, uniform grids (E1M2/INT4) bypass this grid-geometry error and better convert the improved bucket utilization from RHT into higher quantization quality. Based on this finding, we propose UFP4, a uniform 4-bit training recipe that applies RHT to all three training GEMMs while restricting stochastic rounding to dY alone. On Dense 1.5B, MoE 7.9B, and MoE 124B long-run pretraining, UFP4 consistently achieves lower BF16-relative loss degradation than strong E2M1-based baselines, supported by scaling-law analysis and ablation studies. Our results suggest that future accelerators should support E1M2/INT4-style uniform 4-bit grids as first-class training primitives alongside E2M1.

AI.tldr: Uniform 4-bit grids (E1M2/INT4) with Random Hadamard Transform (RHT) outperform non-uniform E2M1 in FP4 training by avoiding Shrinkage Bias, improving stability and quantization quality; propose UFP4 and advocate first-class support for uniform 4-bit training primitives in future accelerators.
AI.motivation: Address the high memory/computation costs of LLM pretraining via FP4 while identifying fundamental grid-geometry biases (Shrinkage Bias) in E2M1 that cause instability and degraded accuracy.
AI.method: Analyze grid-geometry bias in non-uniform E2M1 vs uniform E1M2/INT4, examine impact of RHT on bias, propose UFP4 applying RHT to all GEMMs with stochastic rounding restricted to dY, and validate on Dense 1.5B and MoE 7.9B/124B pretraining with scaling-law and ablation studies.
AI.result: UFP4 consistently yields lower BF16-relative loss degradation than strong E2M1 baselines across Dense and MoE models; gains are supported by scaling laws and ablation results, demonstrating the benefit of uniform 4-bit grids for FP4 training.
AI.conclusion: Future accelerators should support E1M2/INT4-style uniform 4-bit grids as first-class training primitives alongside E2M1 to realize more stable and accurate FP4 LLM pretraining.

title: Score Approximation for Diffusion Models on Arbitrary Low-Dimensional Structures
summary: The remarkable success of score-based diffusion models has spurred significant efforts to establish their theoretical foundations. However, existing complexity bounds for score approximation rely heavily on restrictive assumptions like Lipschitz continuous densities or smooth manifold supports, which are routinely violated by the singularities, sharp boundaries, and disjoint clusters inherent to real-world perceptual data. This work establishes a universal score approximation theorem that works for any distribution supported on any compact set of upper Minkowski dimension $d$. Using a novel discrete-mixture formulation, we prove that the score function can be approximated with a ReLU network whose complexity grows exponentially only with $d$, thus breaking the exponential curse of ambient dimensionality. Combined with existing theories on accurately solving the backward diffusion SDE for arbitrary compact distributions, our work shows that diffusion models readily adapt to irregular, non-smooth data structures, explaining their competence in real-world generative tasks.

AI.tldr: Universal score approximation for diffusion models on arbitrary compact supports with upper Minkowski dimension d; ReLU nets approximate score with complexity exponential in d, not ambient dimension.
AI.motivation: Existing complexity bounds for score approximation rely on restrictive assumptions (Lipschitz densities, smooth manifolds) that fail for real-world data with singularities, sharp boundaries, and disjoint clusters. A universal theory w.r.t. compact supports of finite Minkowski dimension aims to explain diffusion models' robustness.
AI.method: Introduce a discrete-mixture formulation to approximate the score function and prove that a ReLU network can implement this approximation with complexity growing exponentially only in the upper Minkowski dimension d of the support, rather than the ambient dimension. Combine with backward diffusion SDE theory for arbitrary compact distributions.
AI.result: Proves that the score function can be approximated by a ReLU network with complexity exp(O(d)). This breaks the exponential curse of ambient dimensionality and aligns with diffusion models’ ability to handle irregular, non-smooth data structures.
AI.conclusion: Provides a universal, dimension-aware theoretical guarantee for score approximation in diffusion models on arbitrary compact supports, supporting their empirical effectiveness on non-ideal data and guiding future work on finite-sample and training-error analyses.

title: NAMESAKES: Probing Identity Memorization in Text-to-Image Models
summary: Text-to-image (T2I) models generate realistic likenesses of some individuals when prompted with their names, raising privacy concerns. However, distinguishing whether a generated face is memorized or fabricated currently requires ground-truth photos, access to training data, or white-box access to model internals, limiting applicability. We introduce a fully black-box behavioral probe that distinguishes between these regimes while requiring no reference photos or prior knowledge of training data. To benchmark this task, we present the NAMESAKES dataset of over one thousand names and faces of public figures spanning a wide range of fame levels, along with perturbed, less famous names. Experiments on state-of-the-art T2I models show that our probe substantially predicts identity memorization and separates memorized from unrecognized names, with further insights into differences across model families.

AI.tldr: Introduces a fully black-box behavioral probe to distinguish memorized versus fabricated faces generated by text-to-image models, without reference photos or training-data knowledge, using the NAMESAKES dataset of 1000+ names/faces of public figures.
AI.motivation: Privacy and safety concerns around T2I models can generate recognizable likenesses of individuals. Existing methods require ground-truth photos, training data access, or model internals. A fully black-box method enables broad, practical memorization detection without those prerequisites.
AI.method: Develop a black-box behavioral probe that analyzes model outputs given prompts for names (including perturbed, less famous names) and distinguishes memorized (known from training) from unrecognized or fabricated faces. Construct the NAMESAKES dataset with 1000+ names/faces of public figures and perturbed variants to benchmark the task. Evaluate state-of-the-art T2I models to see if the probe predicts identity memorization and separates memorized from unrecognized names, with analysis across model families.
AI.result: The probe substantially predicts identity memorization and successfully separates memorized from unrecognized names across state-of-the-art T2I models, providing insights into differences among model families.
AI.conclusion: A practical, black-box approach can evaluate whether T2I models memorize sensitive identities without access to data or internals, and the NAMESAKES benchmark enables systematic evaluation across models.

title: Interpretable Sperm Morphology Classification via Attention-Guided Deep Learning
summary: Male infertility is a major cause of couple infertility, often linked to abnormal sperm morphology. While deep learning models offer automated analysis, most lack interpretability, limiting their clinical adoption. This study proposes an attention-guided deep learning framework for sperm morphology classification. We combine a pretrained EfficientNet-B0 with a Convolutional Block Attention Module (CBAM) to focus on key areas of the sperm head, improving both accuracy and interpretability. Evaluated on the SMIDS and HuSHem public datasets, our model achieves accuracies of 90.2% and 93.9% (macro F1 scores of 0.913 and 0.948), outperforming SimpleCNN and standard EfficientNet-B0. Furthermore, we use Grad-CAM++ visualizations to highlight features influencing the model's decisions. The results demonstrate that this accurate and transparent framework is a practical tool for automated sperm analysis in fertility clinics.

AI.tldr: Attention-guided EfficientNet-B0 with CBAM for sperm morphology classification using Grad-CAM++, achieving ~90–94% accuracy on SMIDS/HuSHem and improved interpretability.
AI.motivation: Clinical adoption of DL in sperm analysis is hindered by poor interpretability; an interpretable model can highlight relevant sperm-head features and enhance trust in automated analysis.
AI.method: Fine-tunes pretrained EfficientNet-B0 integrated with Convolutional Block Attention Module (CBAM) for focus on sperm head regions; uses Grad-CAM++ for visual explanations; evaluated on public SMIDS and HuSHem datasets; compares against SimpleCNN and standard EfficientNet-B0; macro F1 reported.
AI.result: Accuracies: 90.2% (SMIDS) and 93.9% (HuSHem). Macro F1: 0.913 and 0.948. Outperforms SimpleCNN and vanilla EfficientNet-B0.
AI.conclusion: The proposed attention-guided framework achieves high accuracy with greater interpretability, suggesting practicality for automated sperm analysis in fertility clinics.

title: ADaPT: Token-Level Decoupling for Efficient Large Reasoning Models
summary: Large reasoning models rely on long chain-of-thought to achieve strong performance, but applying such reasoning uniformly incurs high computational cost. Existing efficiency-oriented methods attempt to shorten or mix reasoning strategies, yet often degrade reasoning capability. We identify the root cause as sequence-level coupling between efficiency incentives and correctness optimization, which implicitly penalizes long but correct reasoning trajectories. To address this issue, we propose Adaptive Dual-Process Thinking (ADaPT), a token-level dual-process framework that explicitly decouples efficiency and correctness signals during training. ADaPT introduces a mode-selection token to control fast and slow reasoning, applying efficiency-related rewards exclusively to this token to avoid penalizing correct long reasoning while encouraging efficiency when appropriate. Moreover, ADaPT enables precise and continuous control over the efficiency-performance trade-off at inference time: by adjusting the generation probability of the mode-selection token, a single trained model can smoothly move along the efficiency-performance Pareto frontier. Extensive experiments demonstrate that ADaPT significantly reduces inference cost while maintaining strong reasoning performance across multiple benchmarks.

AI.tldr: ADaPT is a token-level dual-process framework that decouples efficiency incentives from correctness optimization using a mode-selection token. It enables inference-time control of the efficiency-performance trade-off, reducing cost while preserving reasoning ability across benchmarks.
AI.motivation: The root cause is a sequence-level coupling between efficiency rewards and correctness loss, which penalizes long but correct reasoning paths and hinders efficiency gains.
AI.method: Introduce a mode-selection token that governs fast vs slow reasoning. Apply efficiency-related rewards exclusively to this token, decoupling training signals. Train the model with token-level supervision to enable precise control. At inference, adjust the generation probability of the mode-selection token to traverse the efficiency-performance Pareto frontier.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show substantial reductions in inference cost while maintaining strong reasoning performance across multiple benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Token-level dual-process thinking enables precise control over efficiency-performance trade-offs and can generalize across tasks, offering a practical approach to deploy efficient yet capable large reasoning models.

title: ARTEMIS: Agent-guided Reliability-aware Temporal Mask Evolution for Imperfectly Supervised Video Polyp Segmentation
summary: Imperfectly supervised video polyp segmentation (VPS) aims to learn dense, temporally consistent masks from inexpensive supervision, including weak annotations (points, scribbles) and semi-supervision with few densely labeled frames. This setting is clinically valuable but challenging due to weak contrast, ambiguous boundaries, motion blur, and specular highlights, compounded by sparse pixel-level guidance. While SAM2 can generate dense masks from sparse inputs, direct pseudo-labeling often yields geometry-degraded masks with boundary leakage, underutilizes temporal consistency, and ignores reliability. To address these issues, we propose ARTEMIS, a unified framework for imperfectly supervised VPS driven by agent-guided reliability-aware temporal mask evolution. ARTEMIS initializes coarse masks from available supervision: SAM2 converts points/scribbles, while dense labels serve as reliable anchors. A debate-and-judge vision-language agent selects reliable temporal anchors under weak supervision, which are propagated bidirectionally with SAM2 to refine unreliable or unlabeled frames. Finally, ARTEMIS trains the segmenter using temporal reliability-aware robust learning, incorporating reliability-guided reference selection, a Reference Prototype Transport Module, and reliability-aware robust loss. These components assess mask reliability, evolve anchors over time, transport target identity across frames, and down-weight noisy supervision instead of discarding difficult samples. Experiments on SUN-SEG and CVC-ClinicDB-612 under scribble, point, and limited-label settings demonstrate that ARTEMIS achieves state-of-the-art performance. Code will be released at https://github.com/wangtong627/ARTEMIS.

AI.tldr: ARTEMIS is a unified framework for imperfectly supervised video polyp segmentation (VPS) that combines SAM2-based coarse mask generation, reliability-guided temporal anchor evolution, and robust learning to achieve state-of-the-art results under weak supervision.
AI.motivation: Tackling imperfect supervision in VPS where weak annotations (points, scribbles) and sparse dense frames lead to boundary leakage, geometry degradation, and underutilized temporal consistency; the goal is to leverage temporal reliability and agent-guided refinement to improve segmentation quality.
AI.method: 1) Initialize coarse masks from SAM2 using points/scribbles and dense labels as anchors. 2) A debate-and-judge vision-language agent selects reliable temporal anchors under weak supervision. 3) Propagate anchors bidirectionally with SAM2 to refine unreliable/unlabeled frames. 4) Train the segmenter using temporal reliability-aware robust learning: reliability-guided reference selection, Reference Prototype Transport Module, and reliability-aware robust loss to down-weight noisy supervision and propagate target identity across frames.
AI.result: Experiments on SUN-SEG and CVC-ClinicDB-612 across scribble, point, and limited-label settings show ARTEMIS achieves state-of-the-art performance for imperfectly supervised VPS.
AI.conclusion: ARTEMIS provides a cohesive, reliability-aware framework that evolves temporal anchors and leverages robust learning to address weak supervision in VPS, with released code to facilitate adoption and further improvements.

title: Context-Aware Hierarchical Bayesian Modeling of IVF Laboratory Environmental Conditions
summary: IVF pregnancy rates are routinely modeled using patient-level variables, while high-resolution laboratory environmental data remain underutilized. We show that this is a missed opportunity. Rather than relying on raw sensor averages, we engineer 55 context-aware temporal features, including rolling thermal stability, simultaneous temperature-humidity adherence, peak stress duration, and post-stress recovery speed, that capture the dynamics of incubator microenvironments. On 61 weeks of data from an Asian IVF clinic, these features reduce cross-validated prediction error to 1.27%, compared to 3-5% for raw averages. We then train a hierarchical Bayesian Beta regression model that shares environmental effects across an Asian and a Northern European clinic via partial pooling, while preserving site-specific baselines. On held-out data from the Northern European clinic, the model achieves R2 = 0.86 and a 64% error reduction for the 35-39 age group over a naive baseline, demonstrating that structured environmental monitoring contains clinically meaningful, transferable signal.

AI.tldr: Engineering context-aware temporal features from incubator environment improves IVF predictive modeling; cross-clinic transferability demonstrated with partial pooling in a hierarchical Bayesian Beta regression, achieving low error and high R2.
AI.motivation: Underutilization of high-resolution lab environmental data in IVF outcome prediction; opportunity to capture dynamics of incubator microenvironments.
AI.method: Engineered 55 features capturing temporal dynamics; 61 weeks of Asian clinic data; cross-validated prediction error reduced to 1.27% from 3-5%; hierarchical Bayesian Beta regression with partial pooling across Asian and Northern European clinics; preserves site baselines; hold-out European data.
AI.result: Prediction error 1.27% (vs 3-5% raw averages); R2=0.86 on hold-out European data; 64% error reduction for 35-39 age group; signals are transferable.
AI.conclusion: Structured environmental monitoring yields clinically meaningful signal, allowing transfer across sites via partial pooling; indicates potential for better IVF prediction models using environmental context.

title: Compositionality Emerges in a Narrow Depth-Connectivity Regime: Architecture Constraints and Solution Manifolds
summary: Compositionality is believed to be the foundation for generalization, enabling models to reuse meaningful primitives in novel combinations. Yet, models trained with standard gradient-based optimization rarely, and often only weakly, exhibit compositional internal structure, and it remains unclear how or why such compositionality forms. In this work, we show that compositionality emerges in a narrow connectivity-depth sweet spot. Along the connectivity axis, compositionality only appears in some specifically sparse networks, heavily depends on which connections remain rather than on weights' sparsity alone. Along the depth axis, compositionality emerges within a narrow, target-dependent regime, peaking at specific depths, while both shallower and deeper networks fail. When either the depth or connectivity condition is violated, gradient descent silently converges to fractured solutions rather than compositional ones. To discover and exploit this emergence, we introduce (i) similarity-based pruning (SP) to recover compositional connectivity and (ii) a heuristic depth predictor to estimate where compositionality is most likely to appear. Finally, we support these empirical findings with a theoretical framework based on compositional sparsity, volume-ratio arguments, and feature-interference bounds, explaining why compositional solutions are reachable only in a narrow depth-connectivity regime.

AI.tldr: Compositionality in neural networks emerges only in a narrow connectivity-depth regime, and can be recovered/exploited via similarity-based pruning and a depth predictor, supported by a theoretical sparsity/volume framework.
AI.motivation: Generalization is believed to rely on compositionality, but standard gradient-based training often fails to develop compositional internal structure. Understanding when and why compositionality arises helps explain generalization limits and guide methods to induce it.
AI.method: Empirical analysis across network depth and connectivity to identify emergence conditions; introduce similarity-based pruning (SP) to recover compositional connectivity; propose a heuristic depth predictor to estimate where compositionality is likely to appear; support findings with a theoretical framework based on compositional sparsity, volume-ratio arguments, and feature-interference bounds.
AI.result: Compositionality emerges only in a narrow connectivity-depth sweet spot; it is highly sensitive to which connections remain rather than mere sparsity of weights; within a certain depth range, compositionality peaks, with shallow or deep networks failing; when conditions are violated, gradient descent converges to non-compositional, fractured solutions; SP recovers compositional connectivity and the depth predictor helps locate where compositionality will arise.
AI.conclusion: Compositional solutions are reachable only within a narrow depth-connectivity regime; the paper provides practical tools (SP and depth predictor) and a theoretical justification (compositional sparsity, volume-ratio, feature-interference) for why this regime is special.

title: Evaluating and Enhancing Negation Comprehension in Remote Sensing MLLMs
summary: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have demonstrated remarkable success in various Remote Sensing (RS) tasks. However, their ability to comprehend negation remains underexplored, limiting deployment in real-world applications where models must explicitly identify what is false or absent, e.g., emergency responders need to locate non-flooded routes for evacuation. To comprehensively study this limitation, we introduce RS-Neg, the first benchmark to evaluate negation understanding across region-level to scene-level tasks. Specifically, we design an automated data generation pipeline for RS imagery, using LLMs to synthesize diverse negation queries, and introduce a dynamic visual focus module for verification. Our evaluation reveals that advanced RS MLLMs struggle with negation, exhibiting hallucinations and substantial performance degradation. To close this gap, we propose NeFo, a novel test-time learning method that explicitly incorporates the logical role of negation into the model optimization. Remarkably, using about 5\% unlabeled test samples, NeFo significantly improves the negation understanding of models and shows strong generalization to unseen tasks. Code and data will be released upon acceptance.

AI.tldr: Benchmarks negation understanding in Remote Sensing Multimodal LLMs; introduces RS-Neg dataset and NeFo method to improve negation reasoning using few-shot unlabeled data; demonstrates limitations of current MLLMs with negation and breakthrough via test-time learning.
AI.motivation: Negation remains underexplored in RS MLLMs; real-world tasks require identifying absent or false elements (e.g., non-flooded routes), necessitating robust negation comprehension.
AI.method: Automated data generation for RS imagery using LLMs to synthesize diverse negation queries; dynamic visual focus module for verification; NeFo method incorporates logical role of negation into optimization using ~5% unlabeled test samples.
AI.result: RS MLLMs show hallucinations and performance drop on negation; NeFo significantly improves negation understanding and generalizes to unseen tasks; code and data will be released.
AI.conclusion: RS-Neg is the first benchmark for negation in RS MLLMs; NeFo offers an effective test-time learning approach to mitigate negation failures and enhance deployment readiness.

title: What Do Safety-Aligned LLMs Learn From Mixed Compliance Demonstrations?
summary: Prior work has shown that in-context demonstrations can jailbreak language models, but it remains unclear how models interpret different types of compliance demonstrations. We study this by mixing benign compliance demonstrations (non-harmful request, helpful response) with harmful compliance demonstrations (harmful request, helpful response) and testing three hypotheses about how demonstration composition drives harmful compliance. Across four models, we find that benign and harmful demonstrations are not interchangeable: benign demonstrations can either reduce or increase harmful compliance depending on the model. We further show that preference optimization is the critical training stage that prevents benign demonstrations from increasing harmful compliance, that demonstration ordering exhibits strong recency bias, and that models differ in how refusal interacts with in-context learning: some adopt demonstrated formatting even when refusing, while others override all in-context signals upon refusal. Taken together, this work moves beyond showing that demonstration-based jailbreaking works to characterizing how it works: what models extract from compliance demonstrations depends on demonstration content, ordering, and training methodology.

AI.tldr: Demonstrations used in in-context learning are not interchangeable; the content, order, and training approach of compliance prompts determine the likelihood of harmful responses. Benign demonstrations can reduce or increase harm depending on the model, with preference optimization, recency bias in ordering, and model-specific refusal behavior shaping outcomes.
AI.motivation: To understand not just whether in-context jailbreaking occurs, but how different compliance demonstrations influence models’ harmful behavior, and how training and prompt structure modulate this effect.
AI.method: Experiments across four models mixing benign (non-harmful request, helpful response) and harmful (harmful request, helpful response) demonstrations. Tested three hypotheses about composition effects, analyzed the impact of demonstration content, ordering (recency bias), and training stage (preference optimization). Examined how refusing interacts with in-context learning, noting format adherence vs. override behaviors.
AI.result: Benign demonstrations are not interchangeable with harmful ones: they can either reduce or increase harmful compliance depending on the model. Preference optimization is crucial to prevent benign demos from increasing harm. Demonstration ordering shows strong recency bias. Models differ in how refusal interacts with in-context learning: some follow demonstrated formatting even when refusing; others override in-context signals when refusing.
AI.conclusion: Understanding jailbreaks requires considering demonstration content, ordering, and training methodology; these factors determine what models extract from compliance demonstrations and why some models are more susceptible to harmful compliance than others.

title: Towards Graph-Based Deep Learning for Map Generalization: Insights from Building Footprints Simplification and Aggregation
summary: Map generalization remains one of the fundamental tasks in cartography, especially for the simplification and aggregation of complex building footprints. This study presents the first exploratory application of graph-based deep learning to both tasks, reformulating simplification as node movement prediction and aggregation as link prediction within a unified graph learning framework. We evaluate representative graph neural network architectures (GCN, GAT, and GraphSAGE) on multi-scale building datasets, showing that GraphSAGE demonstrates relative strengths in link prediction accuracy, while also revealing persistent challenges in precise node movement prediction. Beyond quantitative performance, the results highlight that aggregation poses greater complexity and challenges than simplification, underscoring the difficulty of capturing higher-level spatial relationships in map generalization with current deep learning approaches. Although limitations such as data imbalance and the need for post-processing remain, the study provides valuable insights and methodological directions for advancing automated map generalization with deep learning approaches.

AI.tldr: Graph-based DL applied to map generalization: reframing simplification as node movement and aggregation as link prediction; GraphSAGE best for link prediction; movement prediction remains challenging; aggregation harder than simplification; data imbalance and post-processing limit performance; offers methodological directions for future work.
AI.motivation: Automate map generalization (simplification and aggregation) by leveraging graph neural networks to capture spatial relationships among building footprints in a unified framework.
AI.method: Reformulate two generalization tasks on a common graph: simplification as node movement prediction and aggregation as link prediction. Evaluate representative GNNs (GCN, GAT, GraphSAGE) on multi-scale building datasets and compare performance across tasks.
AI.result: GraphSAGE shows relative strengths in link prediction accuracy; precise node movement prediction remains challenging; aggregation poses greater complexity than simplification, indicating difficulties in capturing higher-level spatial relationships with current DL approaches.
AI.conclusion: Graph-based DL provides valuable insights and directions for automated map generalization, but limitations such as data imbalance and post-processing needs persist; future work should address higher-level spatial reasoning, data balance, and integration of post-processing into learning pipelines.

title: HilDA: Hierarchical Distillation with Diffusion for Advancing Self-Supervised LiDAR Pre-trainin
comment: Accepted to ECCV 2026. Maciej and Jesper contributed equally
summary: Leveraging Vision Foundation Models (VFMs) for camera-to-LiDAR knowledge distillation offers a promising solution to the scarcity of annotated data needed to represent the immense geometric and kinematic diversity of real-world autonomous driving (AD). However, current approaches typically treat VFMs as black-box teachers, relying exclusively on frame-wise feature similarity. Consequently, they do not fully exploit the teacher's layer-wise semantic structure and global context, as well as the rich spatiotemporal information inherent in LiDAR sequences. We propose HilDA, a self-supervised pretraining framework for LiDAR backbones that better captures the semantic what and geometric where needed for driving tasks. HilDA combines hierarchical distillation comprising multi-layer distillation for progressive semantic alignment and global context distillation for scene-level semantics, with a temporal occupancy diffusion objective promoting spatiotemporal consistency. Models pre-trained with HilDA achieve state-of-the-art results on cross-modal distillation benchmarks and outperform models trained via prior distillation approaches on 3D object detection, scene flow, and semantic occupancy prediction. Code available at: https://maxiuw.github.io/hilda.

AI.tldr: HilDA is a self-supervised pretraining framework for LiDAR backbones that uses hierarchical (multi-layer) and global context distillation along with a temporal occupancy diffusion objective to leverage Vision Foundation Models for driving tasks, improving cross-modal distillation and 3D perception tasks.
AI.motivation: Current VFM-based distillation treats VFMs as black-box teachers and relies on frame-wise feature similarity, underutilizing their layer-wise semantics, global scene context, and LiDAR's spatiotemporal information; data scarcity for diverse driving scenarios motivates a self-supervised approach that better aligns semantic (what) with geometric (where) aspects.
AI.method: HilDA combines: (1) multi-layer distillation for progressive semantic alignment across VFM layers, (2) global context distillation for scene-level semantics, and (3) a temporal occupancy diffusion objective to enforce spatiotemporal consistency in LiDAR sequences.
AI.result: Pre-trained models achieve state-of-the-art results on cross-modal distillation benchmarks and outperform prior distillation methods on 3D object detection, scene flow, and semantic occupancy prediction.
AI.conclusion: HilDA effectively captures semantic and geometric information needed for driving tasks, leveraging hierarchical and temporal cues from VFMs to improve LiDAR backbone pretraining.

title: Multi-LCB: Extending LiveCodeBench to Multiple Programming Languages
summary:
LiveCodeBench (LCB) has recently become a widely adopted benchmark for evaluating large language models (LLMs) on code-generation tasks. By curating competitive programming problems, constantly adding fresh problems to the set, and filtering them by release dates, LCB provides contamination-aware evaluation and offers a holistic view of coding capability. However, LCB remains restricted to Python, leaving open the question of whether LLMs can generalize across the diverse programming languages required in real-world software engineering.
  We introduce Multi-LCB, a benchmark for evaluating LLMs across twelve programming languages, including Python. Multi-LCB transforms Python tasks from the LCB dataset into equivalent tasks in other languages while preserving LCB's contamination controls and evaluation protocol. Because it is fully compatible with the original LCB format, Multi-LCB will automatically track future LCB updates, enabling systematic assessment of cross-language code generation competence and requiring models to sustain performance well beyond Python.
  We evaluated 24 LLMs for instruction and reasoning on Multi-LCB, uncovering evidence of Python overfitting, language-specific contamination, and substantial disparities in multilingual performance. Our results establish Multi-LCB as a rigorous new benchmark for multi-programming-language code evaluation, directly addressing LCB's primary limitation and exposing critical gaps in current LLM capabilities.

AI.tldr: Multi-LCB extends LiveCodeBench to 12 programming languages by translating Python tasks into equivalents in other languages while preserving contamination-aware evaluation, enabling cross-language LLM assessment. It reveals Python overfitting, language-specific contamination, and large multilingual performance disparities across 24 LLMs, establishing a rigorous cross-language code-evaluation benchmark.
AI.motivation: LCB is Python-only and lacks cross-language generalization assessment. A multilingual benchmark is needed to evaluate whether LLMs can transfer coding competence across languages and to uncover contamination and overfitting issues that mask true multilingual capability.
AI.method: Translate Python tasks from LCB into 11 additional languages (total 12 languages) while preserving LCB's contamination controls and evaluation protocol. Ensure full compatibility with the original LCB format so updates propagate automatically. Evaluate 24 LLMs on instruction and reasoning tasks across Multi-LCB.
AI.result: Empirical results show Python overfitting, language-specific contamination, and substantial disparities in multilingual performance among models. Multi-LCB proves to be a rigorous benchmark for cross-language code evaluation and highlights critical gaps in current LLM capabilities.
AI.conclusion: Multi-LCB successfully addresses LCB’s primary limitation by enabling systematic cross-language evaluation. It provides a robust platform to track multilingual progress alongside future LCB updates and to diagnose where LLMs struggle in multilingual code generation.

title: Low-Energy Reduced RISC-V Instruction Subset Processor for Tsetlin Machine Inference at the Edge
comment: 6 pages, 6 Figures, Accepted in IEEE ISVLSI Conference 2026
summary: Tsetlin Machine (TM) is a logic-based machine learning approach that relies on simple bitwise operations and finite-state automata, which makes it attractive for edge AI deployments. Recent work has focused on co-processor and accelerator designs based on Tsetlin Machines (TMs). Although these designs achieve high performance, they typically depend on tightly coupled interfaces, microcode-style programming, and external host processors, limiting flexibility and ease of programming. In this work, we present a domain-specific RISC-V microprocessor architecture and design flow tailored for TM inference. Leveraging the modular structure of RISC-V, we design a reduced instruction subset processor that retains programmability while targeting improved performance and lower energy consumption for TM workloads. Instruction profiling is employed to guide instruction reduction, followed by datapath and control path simplifications tailored to TM inference. Both the baseline RV32IM core and the proposed reduced core are evaluated across multiple datasets and compared with Binarized Neural Networks (BNNs), which serve as a hardware-efficient baseline due to their reliance on bitwise operations during inference. Results show that TM achieves comparable or higher accuracy (e.g., up to 88.18% on CIFAR-2 compared to 60.0% for BNN) while reducing execution time by up to 98% across multiple datasets. Furthermore, the proposed design achieves an average $29.7\times$ reduction in energy consumption, demonstrating its effectiveness for programmable and efficient edge AI systems.

AI.tldr: A domain-specific, reduced-RV32IM microprocessor for Tsetlin Machine inference significantly improves energy and latency while preserving accuracy, outperforming BNN baselines on several datasets.
AI.motivation: To overcome the rigidity and energy inefficiency of existing co-processor approaches for TM inference by providing a programmable, TM-tailored RISC-V core.
AI.method: Develop a reduced-instruction-set RV32IM core optimized for TM workloads via instruction profiling-guided reduction and datapath/control simplifications; evaluate against baseline RV32IM and BNNs on multiple datasets.
AI.result: TM achieves comparable or higher accuracy (up to 88.18% on CIFAR-2 vs 60.0% for BNN); execution time reduced up to 98%; energy consumption reduced by an average of 29.7x across datasets.
AI.conclusion: A TM-focused, domain-specific RISC-V core enables programmable, energy-efficient edge TM inference with superior speed and substantially lower energy usage compared with conventional BNN baselines.

title: Distill Once, Adapt Life-Long: Exploring Dataset Distillation for Continual Test-Time Adaptation
summary: Continual Test-Time Adaptation (CTTA) aims to maintain model performance under evolving target domains by adapting online without labeled data. However, practical deployments often cannot retain the source dataset due to privacy or licensing constraints, and purely source-free CTTA methods tend to become unstable under long-term distribution shift, suffering from compounding self-training errors and catastrophic forgetting. We introduce DO-ALL (Distill Once, Adapt Life-Long), a plug-and-play framework that revisits source information in a compact and privacy-conscious form via Dataset Distillation (DD). Before deployment, DO-ALL performs DD to produce a small set of synthetic distilled anchors that summarize the source distribution. During adaptation, each target sample is matched with its most semantically aligned anchor, which provides a stable reference for various CTTA via source replay, representation alignment, and manifold-smoothing regularization. DO-ALL can be seamlessly integrated into existing CTTA algorithms, consistently improving long-term robustness across CIFAR100-C, ImageNet-C, and the CCC benchmark. This demonstrates the potential of leveraging DD to enable stable and continuous adaptation without retaining raw source data. The code is available at https://github.com/blue-531/DOALL.

AI.tldr: DO-ALL proposes dataset distillation to create a compact, privacy-preserving source-summary (distilled anchors) for continual test-time adaptation, improving long-term robustness without retaining raw source data by matching target samples to anchors and using them in source replay, representation alignment, and manifold-smoothing regularization.
AI.motivation: Purely source-free CTTA struggles under long-term distribution shifts due to accumulated self-training errors and forgetting, and privacy/licensing constraints prohibit keeping the source dataset. A compact source proxy that preserves distributional information could stabilize adaptation.
AI.method: Before deployment, perform dataset distillation to obtain a small set of synthetic anchors that summarize the source distribution. During adaptation, match each target sample to its most semantically aligned anchor and use that anchor as a stable reference for CTTA via (i) source replay, (ii) representation alignment, and (iii) manifold-smoothing regularization. The approach is plug-and-play and compatible with existing CTTA algorithms.
AI.result: DO-ALL consistently improves long-term robustness on CIFAR100-C, ImageNet-C, and CCC benchmark across various CTTA setups, demonstrating that distilled source information can enable stable, continuous adaptation without raw source data.
AI.conclusion: Dataset distillation can enable privacy-preserving, stable continual test-time adaptation by providing a compact, semantically aligned source proxy that integrates with existing CTTA methods and enhances long-term performance.

title: FlowEdit: Associative Memory for Lifelong Pronunciation Adaptation in Flow-Matching TTS
summary: Flow-matching text-to-speech systems achieve remarkable zero-shot quality but remain static after deployment: pronunciation errors on out-of-vocabulary proper nouns persist unless the model is retrained. We introduce FlowEdit, a life-long adaptation framework for frozen flow-matching TTS that learns pronunciation corrections as latent conditioning edits rather than weight updates. When corrective feedback is provided, FlowEdit optimizes a token-level perturbation in the text embedding space, then stores the correction in a Modern Hopfield Network serving as content-addressable episodic memory. At inference, corrections are retrieved via soft attention with a similarity gate, enabling fuzzy morphological matching. On our curated benchmark of 312 multilingual proper nouns across 18 language families, FlowEdit reduces target-word Phoneme Error Rate by 92.7% relative to the zero-shot baseline while maintaining identical general-speech quality. Corrections complete in approximately 15 seconds on a single GPU.

AI.tldr: FlowEdit enables life-long pronunciation corrections for frozen flow-matching TTS by editing text embeddings and storing corrections in a Modern Hopfield Network; achieves large PER reduction on multilingual proper nouns with fast inference.
AI.motivation: Address static deployment where proper nouns are mispronounced and retraining is impractical; enable lifelong adaptation for TTS without updating model weights.
AI.method: Optimize token-level perturbations in the text embedding space upon corrective feedback; store corrections in a Modern Hopfield Network as episodic memory; retrieve via soft attention with a similarity gate for fuzzy morphological matching during inference.
AI.result: Evaluated on 312 multilingual proper nouns across 18 language families; 92.7% relative reduction in target-word Phoneme Error Rate versus zero-shot baseline; general speech quality unchanged; corrections complete in ~15 seconds on a single GPU.
AI.conclusion: FlowEdit demonstrates effective lifelong, memory-augmented pronunciation correction for flow-matching TTS with fast adaptation and preserved speech quality; scalability to multilingual proper nouns.

title: Kolmogorov-Arnold Reservoir Computing
summary: Reservoir computing offers a lightweight framework for forecasting dynamical systems but may struggle to capture long-range dependencies due to limited representational capacity. Conventional reservoir computing recurrently uses trainable reservoirs with hyperparameter sensitivity, while the next-generation reservoir computing removes recurrence at the cost of rapidly growing feature dimensions. Here, we develop Kolmogorov-Arnold Reservoir Computing (KARC), which replaces reservoirs with explicit basis-function expansions inspired by the Kolmogorov-Arnold representation theorem. We rigorously show that KARC is a lightweight design of Kolmogorov-Arnold networks (KANs), preserving the potential expressive capacity of KANs while admitting efficient closed-form training of reservoir computing. At comparable cost, KARC outperforms existing reservoir computing methods on challenging benchmarks including partial differential equations. It can also be integrated with generative diffusion models for text-to-image generation. This work thus establishes a principled bridge between reservoir computing and KANs, enabling efficient and high-fidelity dynamical system forecasting.

AI.tldr: Introduces Kolmogorov-Arnold Reservoir Computing (KARC), a basis-function expansion-based alternative to traditional reservoirs that preserves the expressive capacity of Kolmogorov-Arnold networks (KANs) while enabling closed-form, efficient training; demonstrates improved performance on PDE benchmarks and potential integration with diffusion models.
AI.motivation: RC struggles with long-range dependencies and hyperparameter sensitivity; existing approaches either recurrently trained reservoirs or non-recurrent expansions with rapidly growing feature dimensions; aim to combine efficiency with high expressiveness.
AI.method: Replace recurrent reservoirs with explicit basis-function expansions inspired by the Kolmogorov-Arnold representation theorem, formulating KARC as a lightweight design of KANs; provide rigorous justification and closed-form training procedures.
AI.result: At comparable cost, KARC outperforms conventional RC methods on challenging benchmarks including PDEs; demonstrates compatibility with generative diffusion models for text-to-image generation.
AI.conclusion: Establishes a principled link between reservoir computing and Kolmogorov-Arnold networks, enabling efficient, high-fidelity dynamical forecasting and expanding the design space for data-driven forecasting.

title: Evaluation of Image Matching for Art Skills Assessment
summary: While some individuals possess a natural talent for drawing, mastering this skill requires dedicated training and practice. Determining one's skill in the art of drawing requires proper comprehensive assessment. In this paper, we propose a method to measure drawing skill by by matching the hand-drawn image with the original template. Existing techniques often involve complex processes. However, advancements in computer vision allow us to train computers to perform these comparisons at a human-like level, thereby resolving the tedious and overwhelming traditional process. Using computer vision applications, determining image similarity involves identifying the level of similarities in an image with a reference image. We have implemented and analyzed the SIFT feature and Siamese network to measure image similarity. Our results indicate that it is feasible to assess art skill levels. Through feature analysis, we found that SIFT-based key point matching provides a more effective means of detecting drawing skills.

AI.tldr: Proposes an image-similarity approach to assess drawing skill by matching a hand-drawn image to a reference template, comparing SIFT-based keypoint matching and a Siamese network; finds SIFT more effective and demonstrates feasibility of computer-vision-based skill assessment.
AI.motivation: Provide an objective, scalable method to evaluate drawing skill beyond subjective human assessment and reduce tedious traditional evaluation by leveraging image similarity to a template.
AI.method: Implement and compare two approaches: (1) SIFT feature-based keypoint matching to quantify similarity between hand-drawn and reference templates; (2) a Siamese network to learn image similarity. Analyze results to determine which better detects drawing proficiency.
AI.result: Feasibility demonstrated: computer-vision-based assessment of drawing skill is possible. Feature analysis shows SIFT-based keypoint matching is more effective than the Siamese approach for detecting drawing skills.
AI.conclusion: A practical, computer-vision-based framework for skill assessment in drawing; SIFT-based keypoint matching provides a more reliable signal for drawing proficiency than a Siamese network in this study.

title: DeepSWIP: Quotient-WMC Counterfactuals for Neural Probabilistic Logic Programs
summary: Neurosymbolic systems such as DeepProbLog combine neural perception with probabilistic logic, but standard inference is associational. Counterfactual reasoning additionally requires a causal semantics for interventions and evidence. We introduce DeepSWIP, a single-world counterfactual semantics for DeepProbLog programs. Using neural materialization, we reduce fixed-context neural predicates to ordinary ProbLog choices, apply Single World Intervention Programs (SWIPs), and compute counterfactuals by weighted model counting (WMC) over a single transformed program. Under finite grounding and unique-supported-model assumptions, DeepSWIP is exact relative to the learned materialized FCM. The standard quotient-WMC form of ProbLog conditionals identifies active neural probabilities and explains intervention cleaning, calibration sensitivity, and rare-evidence instability. Experiments on MPI3D confirm the transformation against a DeepTwin construction against 12,000 queries, as predicted and a 2.14$\times$ inference speedup from avoiding the Twin's endogenous duplication. A SUMO HOV experiment shows that neural calibration degradation biases plug-in estimates, while a correctly scoped randomized-policy AIPW estimator removes most first-order bias for population mean and ATE estimands. Code is at https://github.com/saibib/deep_SWIP.

AI.tldr: DeepSWIP delivers a single-world counterfactual semantics for DeepProbLog by transforming neural predicates into ProbLog choices and using SWIP-based weighted model counting, enabling exact counterfactuals under finite grounding with practical speedups.
AI.motivation: Neurosymbolic systems fuse neural perception with probabilistic logic, but standard inference is largely associational. Counterfactual reasoning requires a causal semantics for interventions and evidence, motivating a framework that supports such semantics in DeepProbLog.
AI.method: 1) Neural materialization converts fixed-context neural predicates into ordinary ProbLog choices. 2) Apply Single World Intervention Programs (SWIPs) to encode interventions. 3) Compute counterfactuals via weighted model counting (WMC) on a single transformed program. 4) Under finite grounding and unique-supported-model assumptions, results are exact relative to the learned materialized FCM.
AI.result: Empirical validation on MPI3D shows the transformation matches a DeepTwin construction across 12,000 queries, achieving a 2.14× inference speedup by avoiding the Twin’s endogenous duplication. A SUMO HOV experiment reveals neural calibration degradation biases plug-in estimates, while a correctly scoped randomized-policy AIPW estimator removes most first-order bias for population mean and ATE estimands.
AI.conclusion: DeepSWIP provides an exact, scalable single-world counterfactual semantics for DeepProbLog under finite grounding and unique-supported-model assumptions, clarifying intervention cleaning, calibration sensitivity, and rare-evidence instability. Code is available at the referenced GitHub repository.

title: Activation- and Influence-Aware Ranks (AIR): Function-Preserving SVD Compression for LLMs
comment: Accepted at the ICML 2026 Workshop on Resource-Adaptive Foundation Model Inference (AdaptFM), Seoul, South Korea (non-archival)
summary: We present Activation- and Influence-Aware Ranks (AIR), an SVD-based LLM compression framework that guides each weight matrix's low-rank approximation with a backward-signal influence metric. Starting from the activation-aware optimum of SVD-LLM(W), AIR runs a single closed-form alternating least squares (ALS) sweep that integrates influence element-wise under a monotone-descent guarantee. AIR is layer-local and composes orthogonally with end-to-end methods: alone it exceeds ACIP, and AIR+LoRA outperforms it further. AIR improves perplexity over SVD-LLM(W) by >18% at <=60% parameter retention, matches its quality with ~90% less calibration data, and turns parameter savings into FLOP, peak-memory, and per-token latency gains.

AI.tldr: AIR is a layer-local, SVD-based LLM compression method that guides low-rank weight matrices using a backward-signal influence metric via an ALS sweep, achieving strong accuracy with reduced parameters and data, and it complements end-to-end methods like LoRA.
AI.motivation: To improve SVD-based LLM compression by incorporating influence signals from backward propagation, enabling effective low-rank approximations with fewer calibration data and reduced compute.
AI.method: AIR starts from the activation-aware optimum of SVD-LLM(W). It then runs a single closed-form alternating least squares (ALS) sweep that incorporates a backward-signal influence metric element-wise, with a monotone-descent guarantee. It is layer-local and orthogonal to end-to-end methods, and can be combined with methods like LoRA.
AI.result: AIR yields >18% perplexity improvement over SVD-LLM(W) at <=60% parameter retention. It matches the original quality with ~90% less calibration data. It also yields FLOP, peak-memory, and per-token latency gains due to parameter savings. AIR alone outperforms ACIP, and AIR+LoRA outperforms AIR alone.
AI.conclusion: AIR offers a practical, effective, layer-local compression framework that enhances SVD-based LLM compression with a provable monotone-descent ALS sweep and a backward-signal influence metric, and it synergizes with end-to-end methods like LoRA to reduce calibration data and compute while maintaining or improving quality.

title: The Token Is a Group Element: On Lie-Algebra Attention over Matrix Lie Groups
summary: We place the attention token on the group: a token is an element $g_i$ of a matrix Lie group $G$ -- a bare transformation, with no feature payload and no external action $ρ(g)$ carrying it. To our knowledge this is the first attention construction whose tokens are bare matrix Lie group elements: their score is the closed-form algebra norm of the relative pose rather than a learned kernel, and it reaches the affine full-frame groups that every irrep- or surjective-exp-based method must exclude. We call it Lie-Algebra Attention. Once tokens are group elements, the rest follows with none of the usual representation-theoretic machinery.
