The relative geometry of a pair is canonical, $g_i^{-1} g_j$, so the pairwise invariant $w_{ij} = \log(g_i^{-1} g_j)$ is intrinsic rather than designed; equivariance under the diagonal $G$-action is tautological, and the cocycle condition holds automatically. The attention score is the negative squared algebra norm, $s_{ij} = -\|\log(g_i^{-1} g_j)\|_λ^2/τ$: the canonical proximity kernel under a block-weighted Frobenius inner product, with no irreducible representations, spherical harmonics, Clebsch-Gordan products, or learned kernel. The construction applies to any matrix Lie group on a chosen logarithm chart containing the relative poses, including the non-compact non-abelian affine groups with scale and shear that no vector-token attention method reaches: neither the irrep tradition nor surjective-exp methods. Three sequence-completion experiments, on SE(2), SO(3), and Aff(2), bear this out: the closed-form score matches a learned MLP kernel on the same invariant and outperforms it on SE(2), using 50 to 80x fewer score parameters, while a vector-token baseline breaks invariance by five to twelve orders of magnitude.

AI.tldr: A novel attention mechanism where tokens are bare elements of a matrix Lie group; scores are closed-form log-map based distances; works for SE(2), SO(3), Aff(2); achieves invariance and parameter efficiency; outperforms learned kernels and vector-token baselines.
AI.motivation: To avoid reliance on representation-theoretic machinery and learned kernels, and to enable attention over group elements with canonical relative geometry, including affine transformations with scale and shear.
AI.method: Represent tokens as g_i in a matrix Lie group G; compute attention scores s_ij = -||log(g_i^{-1} g_j)||_λ^2 / τ; use log map within a chart; no payloads; equivariance follows; cocycle condition; no irreps, spherical harmonics, Clebsch-Gordan; apply to SE(2), SO(3), Aff(2).
AI.result: In three sequence-completion tasks, Lie-Algebra Attention matches a learned MLP kernel on the same invariant and outperforms it on SE(2); requires 50-80x fewer score parameters; vector-token baselines break invariance by 5-12 orders of magnitude.
AI.conclusion: Provides a principled, closed-form, invariant attention mechanism for matrix Lie groups, including non-compact affine groups, enabling efficient, representation-free attention without learned kernels.

title: DeepForestVisionV2: Ecology-Driven Taxonomy Expansion for Camera-Trap Monitoring in African Tropical Forests
comment: Accepted at ICPR 2026 - Computer Vision for Biodiversity Monitoring and Conservation Workshop
summary: Camera-trap monitoring in African tropical forests increasingly extends beyond closed-canopy interiors to riverbanks, clearings, and park edges. Among available open tools for African forest camera-trap classification, DeepForestVision is the only one providing a matched offline workflow for both photographs and videos, and previous work showed that it outperformed other available baselines on a comparable benchmark. However, it was designed for closed-canopy, ground-level forest interiors and uses a 35-class prediction space that becomes too coarse when deployments encounter arboreal primates, birds, semi-aquatic taxa, or human-associated confounders such as livestock. We present DeepForestVisionV2, an ecology-driven expansion from 35 to 64 prediction classes (61 animal classes plus human, vehicle, and blank) designed to address three recurrent deployment gradients: vertical stratification, scene openness, and anthropogenic interfaces. DeepForestVisionV2 retains the same offline workflow and is trained on 1,535,010 photographs and 243,354 videos from multi-country African tropical-forest projects. Evaluation combines a cross-country cropped-photo validation set, used to assess robustness across sites and camera-trap settings, with three held-out Uganda video benchmarks spanning the targeted gradients. On the validation set, DeepForestVisionV2 reaches 0.86 accuracy, 0.82 macro-F1, and 0.81 balanced accuracy. On the deployment benchmarks, it preserves or improves baseline accuracy despite its harder classification task, while increasing the number of identified taxa from 22 to 29 in forest-interior videos and from 4 to 9 at riverbanks. In the park-edge use case, it raises accuracy from 0.62 to 0.86 and reduces false alarms from 11 to 0. These results show that DeepForestVisionV2 materially improves field utility while preserving robustness across sites, habitats, and camera-trap settings.

AI.tldr: DeepForestVisionV2 expands an open-source camera-trap classifier from 35 to 64 classes (61 animals plus human, vehicle, blank) to address vertical stratification, scene openness, and anthropogenic interfaces in African tropical forests, retaining an offline workflow and achieving robust accuracy across sites while increasing taxa identification.
AI.motivation: To overcome the limitations of the prior DeepForestVision (designed for closed-canopy interiors with a 35-class space) when deployed in open, arboreal, semi-aquatic, or human-impacted habitats across Africa, enabling finer-grained, ecologically relevant detection.
AI.method: Extend the model from 35 to 64 classes; preserve offline workflow; train on 1,535,010 photos and 243,354 videos from multi-country African tropical-forest projects; evaluate on a cross-country cropped-photo validation set and three held-out Uganda video benchmarks spanning vertical, openness, and anthropogenic gradients.
AI.result: Validation: 0.86 accuracy, 0.82 macro-F1, 0.81 balanced accuracy. Deployment benchmarks: maintains/improves accuracy with broader taxa—forest-interior videos from 22 to 29 taxa; riverbanks from 4 to 9; park-edge accuracy 0.62 to 0.86, false alarms reduced from 11 to 0.
AI.conclusion: DeepForestVisionV2 materially improves field utility and robustness across sites, habitats, and camera-trap settings by enabling finer taxonomic resolution and better performance at ecological edges and anthropogenic interfaces.

title: LedgerAgent: Structured State for Policy-Adherent Tool-Calling Agents
summary: Policy-adherent tool-calling agents in customer-service domains must maintain task states across turns while calling tools and obeying domain policies. Task states consist of relevant facts, identifiers, constraints, and conditions observed through user interaction and tool calls. In standard agents, task states are not represented separately. Observations, tool returns, and policy instructions are placed in the prompt, leaving agents to reconstruct the relevant states from the prompt each time they decide what to do next. This design makes state management implicit, creating two common failure modes. An agent may retrieve the right facts but later ground its decision in stale, missing, or incorrect information; and a syntactically valid tool call may still violate a domain policy that depends on the current task state. We introduce \textsc{LedgerAgent}, an inference-time method for tool-calling agents that maintains observed task states in a separate ledger and renders the states into the prompt. The ledger is also used to check state-dependent policy constraints before environment-changing tool calls are executed, blocking policy violations. Across four customer-service domains and a mixed panel of open- and closed-weight models, \textsc{LedgerAgent} improves average pass\textasciicircum{}k over a standard prompt-based tool-calling approach, with the largest gains under stricter multi-trial consistency metrics.

AI.tldr: LedgerAgent uses an explicit, inference-time ledger of observed task states to guide tool-calling and enforce policies, improving consistency and policy adherence across domains and models.
AI.motivation: Implicit state management in standard, prompt-based tool callers can ground decisions on stale or incomplete information and violate domain policies depending on current task state.
AI.method: Maintain a separate ledger of observed facts, identifiers, constraints, and conditions; render ledger states into the prompt; check state-dependent policy constraints before environment-changing tool calls and block violations.
AI.result: Across four customer-service domains and mixed-model panels, LedgerAgent achieves higher average pass@k than standard prompt-based tool calling, with the largest gains under stricter multi-trial consistency metrics.
AI.conclusion: Explicit, inference-time state management via a ledger improves reliability and policy compliance in tool-calling agents, suggesting robust gains in multi-turn, policy-constrained environments.

title: Connect the Dots: Training LLMs for Long-Lifecycle Agents with Cross-Domain Generalization Via Reinforcement Learning
comment: Work in progress; we will continuously update the codebase and arXiv version
summary: This work presents a general framework for training large language models (LLMs) to "Connect the Dots" (CoD), a meta-capability required by long-lifecycle agents: as an LLM-based AI agent gets deployed in an environment, it solves a long sequence of tasks while continuously exploring the environment, learning from its own experiences, and iteratively self-updating its context about the environment, thereby achieving progressively better performance on future tasks conditioned on the updated context. Major components of the CoD framework include: (1) algorithm design and infrastructure for end-to-end reinforcement learning (RL) with long rollout sequences interleaving solve-task and update-context episodes; (2) tasks and environments for incentivizing and eliciting the targeted meta-capability in LLMs during training, as well as for faithfully measuring progress during evaluation. We present proof-of-concept implementations of the CoD framework, including a GRPO-style RL algorithm with fine-grained credit assignment, as well as tasks and environments tailored to the targeted meta-capability (rather than domain-specific LLM capabilities or standard task-by-task RL). Empirical results validate the efficacy of end-to-end RL training in the CoD setting, and demonstrate the potential for out-of-distribution generalization -- within the training domains, across different domains, and from CoD to Ralph-loop settings -- of the elicited meta-capability. Our investigation of CoD connects several lines of prior works, and opens up new opportunities for advancing LLMs and AI agents. To facilitate further research and applications, we release our implementations at \url{https://github.com/agentscope-ai/Trinity-RFT/tree/research/cod/examples/research_cod}.

AI.tldr: A general RL-based framework (CoD) to train LLMs as long-horizon agents capable of connecting dots by updating context from experiences, with long rollouts that interleave task solving and context updating; evidence via proof-of-concept implementations and potential cross-domain generalization; code released.
AI.motivation: To enable LLM-based agents that operate over long lifecycles, continuously learn from experiences, and iteratively refine their environment context, a capability not well-supported by existing short-horizon RL or static evaluation setups.
AI.method: End-to-end reinforcement learning with long rollout sequences interleaving solve-task episodes and update-context episodes; a GRPO-style RL algorithm with fine-grained credit assignment; task and environment design to elicit the targeted meta-capability; evaluation protocols; proof-of-concept implementations; release of code (GitHub).
AI.result: Empirical results validate end-to-end RL training in the CoD setting and suggest potential for out-of-distribution generalization within training domains, across domains, and from CoD to Ralph-loop settings.
AI.conclusion: CoD connects prior work and opens new opportunities for advancing LLMs and AI agents; the authors provide implementations to facilitate further research and applications.

title: Cinematic Compositing Using Character-Environment-Harmonized Video Generation Models
summary: Cinematic compositing aims to integrate green-screen characters into novel environments while maintaining physical and photometric realism. Previous methods often fail to capture the complex bidirectional interactions between characters and their surroundings, which we characterize as Character-to-Environment (C2E) physical interaction and Environment-to-Character (E2C) lighting harmonization. To address this, we propose an end-to-end video diffusion framework that jointly models C2E and E2C interactions, specifically handling the challenges of interactive props. Our approach introduces a tri-mask-guided architecture with RGB-D joint denoising to ensure physically consistent interactions among the character, props, and environment. We further develop an efficient prior-driven data curation pipeline to construct high-quality relighting pairs without expensive rendering. Finally, a reference-conditioned mechanism enables controllable environment synthesis and precise prop replacement. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our framework significantly outperforms existing methods in cinematic-quality dynamic video compositing.

AI.tldr: A diffusion-based video framework for cinematic compositing that jointly models Character-to-Environment (C2E) and Environment-to-Character (E2C) interactions, using a tri-mask-guided RGB-D denoising architecture, a data-curation pipeline for relighting pairs, and a reference-conditioned mechanism for controllable environment synthesis and prop replacement; claims superior cinematic-quality dynamic video compositing.
AI.motivation: To address the bidirectional physical and photometric interactions between green-screen characters, props, and surroundings (C2E and E2C) that previous methods struggle to capture, especially with interactive props.
AI.method: End-to-end video diffusion framework that jointly models C2E and E2C. Key components include a tri-mask-guided architecture with RGB-D joint denoising, a prior-driven data curation pipeline to construct relighting pairs without expensive rendering, and a reference-conditioned mechanism for controllable environment synthesis and precise prop replacement.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show that the proposed framework significantly outperforms existing methods in cinematic-quality dynamic video compositing.
AI.conclusion: The approach effectively captures bidirectional character-environment interactions and interactive props, enabling physically consistent relighting and controllable environment synthesis, leading to superior cinematic-quality compositing.

title: How Do Instructions Shape Speech? Cross-Attention Attribution for Style-Captioned Text-to-Speech
summary: Style-captioned text-to-speech systems use natural language to control voice characteristics, but how individual words influence acoustic output remains unclear. Understanding this is critical for diagnosing failure modes and improving controllability in expressive TTS. We propose cross-attention attribution for speech diffusion models, adapting the DAAM framework to the speech domain for the first time, and apply it to CapSpeech-TTS. Our method extracts per-token heatmaps across 25 layers and 24 ODE steps. We analyze 3,600 (style caption, text transcript) combinations comprising 120 style captions conditioning the generation of 30 text transcripts each, revealing how caption tokens shape waveforms. Results show: (1) style tokens have lower temporal variance than content/function tokens, confirming global conditioning; (2) style attention correlates with F0 and energy; (3) style conditioning peaks in early steps and deep layers; (4) attention entropy reaches its minimum at layer 17, co-occurring with the style importance peak, indicating maximal network selectivity at the most style-critical stage. This is the first study of how natural language influences cross-attention in speech diffusion models

AI.tldr: Cross-attention attribution is adapted to speech diffusion models (CapSpeech-TTS) to map how style-caption tokens influence acoustics, yielding per-token heatmaps across 25 layers and 24 ODE steps and revealing when and where style information acts.
AI.motivation: Understand word-level influence on acoustic output in style-captioned TTS to diagnose failures and improve controllability; extend DAAM attribution to speech diffusion models.
AI.method: Adapt the DAAM cross-attention attribution framework to speech diffusion models. Compute per-token heatmaps across 25 layers and 24 ODE steps for 3,600 style-caption/text pairs (120 captions × 30 transcripts). Analyze correlations with acoustic features (F0, energy), temporal variance, layer/depth localization, and attention entropy.
AI.result: (1) Style tokens show lower temporal variance than content tokens, indicating global conditioning. (2) Style attention correlates with F0 and energy. (3) Style conditioning peaks in early steps and deep layers. (4) Attention entropy minimizes at layer 17, coinciding with peak style importance, suggesting maximal network selectivity at the most style-critical stage.
AI.conclusion: This is the first study to analyze how natural language controls cross-attention in speech diffusion models, revealing when and where style information is encoded and how attention dynamics relate to perceptual style phenomena.

title: StreamKL: Fast and Memory-Efficient KL Divergence for Boosting Attention Distillation
summary: Attention distillation, which trains one attention distribution to match another by minimizing their Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence, is widely used in knowledge distillation, model compression, continual learning, and sparse-attention LLM training. However, existing approaches materialize both attention distributions before computing the KL reduction, incurring $O(N_QN_K)$ memory and IO costs that become prohibitive at long context lengths. We present StreamKL, the first fused GPU primitive for attention KL divergence that eliminates this quadratic materialization. StreamKL derives a novel online formulation for the coupled two-distribution KL reduction, enabling a single one-pass forward kernel that streams query-key tiles through on-chip SRAM. For the backward pass, StreamKL recomputes attention probabilities tile-by-tile, avoiding storage of quadratic intermediates. We further design and implement efficient GPU kernels with dedicated optimizations. Experiments show StreamKL delivers up to $43\times$ and $14\times$ speedups over baseline methods in the forward and backward passes, respectively. Most importantly, StreamKL reduces the extra HBM footprint of attention distillation from $O(N_QN_K)$ to $O(1)$, enabling long-context distillation on a single GPU.

AI.tldr: A one-pass streaming KL-divergence computation for attention distributions (StreamKL) reduces memory from quadratic to O(1) by online, tile-based processing, enabling long-context attention distillation on a single GPU.
AI.motivation: Attention KL distillation requires materializing both attention distributions and their KL divergence, leading to O(N_Q N_K) memory/IO costs that scale poorly with context length.
AI.method: Introduce an online formulation for coupled KL reduction and a single forward kernel that streams query-key tiles through on-chip SRAM; backward pass recomputes attention probabilities tile-by-tile to avoid storing quadratic intermediates; implementGPU kernels with optimizations to realize a fused attention-KL primitive.
AI.result: Achieves up to 43x speedup in the forward pass and 14x speedup in the backward pass compared to baselines; reduces extra memory footprint from O(N_Q N_K) to O(1), enabling long-context distillation on a single GPU.
AI.conclusion: StreamKL provides a memory-efficient, high-performance fused primitive for attention KL divergence, eliminating quadratic materialization and enabling practical long-context attention distillation on standard GPUs.

title: BAFIS: Dataset + Framework to assess occupational Bias and Human Preference in modern Text-to-image Models
comment: Accepted at the IEEE Winter Conference on Applications of Computer Vision, WACV 2026
summary: Generative artificial intelligence has the potential to improve productivity and transform the production of creative content. However, existing research indicates that image generation models are significantly influenced by biases. This work investigates the inherent biases and language-induced biases present in text-to-image models within the context of occupation-related image generation, complementing established metrics with human preference feedback. We present a comprehensive evaluation of five current text-to-image models: Midjourney v6.1, Stable Diffusion 3 Medium, DALL-E 3, Playground v2.5, and FLUX.1-dev , focusing on gender and ethnicity bias, image quality, and prompt alignment. To facilitate this evaluation, we developed the "Battle-Arena for Fair Image Synthesis" (BAFIS), a platform designed to collect human feedback on bias in generated images. Furthermore, we created a dataset comprising 21,140 synthetic images generated using multilingual prompts, which serves as a basis for our analysis. We further place our results within a broader social context by comparing them to official statistics from the German Federal Employment Agency. Our findings reveal systematic biases in text-to-image models, with established evaluation metrics in partial correlation with subjective user ratings. Thus, our research emphasizes the need for including human preferences to develop fairer and more inclusive text-to-image models.

AI.tldr: Evaluates gender/ethnicity biases in five text-to-image models using human feedback and a multilingual synthetic image dataset; finds systematic biases and partial alignment between traditional metrics and subjective ratings; advocates incorporating human preferences for fairer models.
AI.motivation: Address known biases in image generation models and extend evaluation with human preferences and multilingual prompts; situate findings in real-world demographics by comparing with official employment statistics.
AI.method: Five models are evaluated (Midjourney v6.1, Stable Diffusion 3 Medium, DALL-E 3, Playground v2.5, FLUX.1-dev). Developed BAFIS (Battle-Arena for Fair Image Synthesis) to collect human bias feedback. Created a dataset of 21,140 synthetic images generated from multilingual prompts. Analyzed gender/ethnicity bias, image quality, and prompt alignment; compared results to German Federal Employment Agency statistics.
AI.result: Reveals systematic biases across text-to-image models. Traditional evaluation metrics show partial correlation with subjective human ratings. The BA FIS platform and large multilingual synthetic dataset provide a basis for bias analysis and highlight the importance of human preferences in assessment.
AI.conclusion: Human preferences should be incorporated to develop fairer and more inclusive text-to-image models; multilingual prompts and real-world demographic benchmarks can enhance fairness evaluation.

title: Toward Calibrated Mixture-of-Experts Under Distribution Shift
summary: Calibration aligns a model's predictive uncertainty with the frequencies of its empirical outcomes and is important for understanding and trusting reported probabilities. Recent work shows that enforcing calibration at the level of individual predictors can improve ensemble accuracy and calibration, with mixture-of-experts (MoE) models showing strong empirical improvements in particular; however, the conditions under which calibration helps MoE are not well understood. In this work, we study how MoE models behave under distribution shift, focusing on how routing mechanisms interact with expert-level calibration. We show that expert calibration is sufficient to ensure calibration of the overall model under a broad class of distribution shifts in hard-routed models, but is insufficient for calibrating soft-routed models. To address this, we propose an adversarial reweighting that penalizes calibration errors of the routed aggregate under distribution shift, and we demonstrate that it improves the accuracy-calibration tradeoff both on average and on difficult subsets of the data, across model classes, prediction tasks, and distribution shifts.

AI.tldr: MoE calibration under distribution shift: expert-level calibration suffices for hard-routing MoEs but not for soft-routing; adversarial reweighting improves accuracy-calibration tradeoffs across models, tasks, and shifts.
AI.motivation: Understand how MoE routing interacts with predictor calibration under distribution shift and how to enhance reliability of aggregated predictions.
AI.method: Theoretically analyze hard vs. soft routing; show expert calibration suffices for overall calibration in hard-routed MoEs under broad shifts; show insufficiency for soft routing; propose adversarial reweighting that penalizes calibration errors of the routed aggregate under shift; evaluate across model classes, tasks, and distribution shifts.
AI.result: Hard routing: expert calibration suffices to calibrate the overall model under distribution shifts; soft routing: expert calibration alone is insufficient; adversarial reweighting improves the accuracy-calibration tradeoff on average and on difficult subsets, across architectures and tasks.
AI.conclusion: Calibration transfer depends on routing: calibrate experts for hard routing to obtain calibrated aggregates; for soft routing, incorporate calibration-aware training via adversarial reweighting to align the routed ensemble under shift; method shows robustness across tasks and distribution shifts.

title: VIMPO: Value-Implicit Policy Optimization for LLMs
summary: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards has become a central tool for improving the reasoning ability of large language models, but current methods face a trade-off between simplicity and credit assignment. Group-relative methods such as GRPO avoid training a critic, but typically assign a trajectory-level advantage to every token. Actor-critic methods provide denser learning signals, but require a learned value function with its own training instability. We introduce VIMPO, a critic-free policy optimization method that derives a policy-implied value function from the optimality conditions of KL-regularized reinforcement learning. For autoregressive generation, the resulting value recurrence can be written in terms of policy-reference log-ratios and anchored by the terminal condition that no future reward remains at the end of a trajectory. This gives a simple value loss that incorporates outcome-level verifiable rewards without training a critic. The same derivation also yields a critic-free actor advantage, allowing VIMPO to separate reward incorporation through the value loss from policy improvement through a PPO-style actor update. On mathematical RLVR benchmarks, VIMPO improves over GRPO across MATH-500, AIME 2024, AIME 2025, and OlympiadBench, with especially larger gains on competition-style evaluations. Under noisy rewards, VIMPO retains a consistent advantage over GRPO, suggesting that policy-implied value optimization can provide finer credit assignment while preserving the practical simplicity of critic-free training.

AI.tldr: VIMPO is a critic-free policy optimization framework for verifiable rewards in autoregressive generation. It derives a policy-implied value function from KL-regularized RL optimality, providing a simple value loss and a critic-free actor advantage for PPO-like updates. It yields improved credit assignment and outperforms GRPO on math-reasoning benchmarks, with robustness to noisy rewards.
AI.motivation: Current methods trade off simplicity (critic-free approaches) against credit assignment (actor-critic). Group-relative methods avoid training a critic but give coarse trajectory-level advantages; actor-critic methods offer dense signals but require training a value function with instability. There is a need for a method that provides dense credit assignment without training a critic.
AI.method: For autoregressive generation, derive a value recurrence from the optimality conditions of KL-regularized RL, resulting in a policy-reference log-ratio based value function anchored by the terminal condition that no future reward remains at the end of a trajectory. This yields a simple value loss that incorporates outcome-level verifiable rewards without a critic. The same derivation yields a critic-free actor advantage, enabling separation of reward incorporation (value loss) from policy improvement (PPO-style actor update).
AI.result: On mathematical RLVR benchmarks, VIMPO improves over GRPO across MATH-500, AIME 2024, AIME 2025, and OlympiadBench, with larger gains on competition-style evaluations. Under noisy rewards, VIMPO maintains an advantage over GRPO, suggesting policy-implied value optimization provides finer credit assignment while preserving critic-free training simplicity.
AI.conclusion: Policy-implied value optimization can deliver denser credit assignment without training a critic. VIMPO demonstrates that a critic-free approach can match or exceed the performance of critic-based or group-relative methods on math-reasoning benchmarks, particularly under noisy rewards.

title: SPOT-E: Test-Time Entropy Shaping with Visual Spotlights for Frozen VLMs
summary: Vision-language models (VLMs) often underperform on evidence intensive tasks because decisive visual evidence are small, localized, and easy to overlook, leading to failures in evidence readout even when high-level reasoning is intact. Prior inference-time visual interventions can improve grounding without retraining, but they are largely open-loop and lack a mechanism to verify whether highlighted evidence is actually used. We study answer-span prediction entropy as a model-internal feedback signal and show that naive entropy minimization is ambiguous, since low entropy may arise from evidence-grounded confidence or shortcut collapse. To resolve this ambiguity, we introduce low-entropy anchors and an entropy-shaping objective that reduces answer uncertainty while preserving baseline high-confidence tokens. We instantiate this principle in SPOT-E, a plug-and-play test-time method that produces question-conditioned spotlights, optimized per instance via light-weight tuning based on Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO). Across all benchmarks and different VLM families, SPOT-E yields consistent gains and improved robustness under visual corruptions. Code is publicly available at: \url{https://github.com/YinBo0927/SPOT-E}

AI.tldr: SPOT-E is a plug-in test-time method that generates question-conditioned visual spotlights using entropy-shaping to improve evidence reading in vision-language models, addressing ambiguity in entropy-based feedback and yielding consistent gains and robustness across VLMs.
AI.motivation: Evidence-intensive VQA tasks suffer when decisive visual cues are small and easy to overlook. Existing inference-time interventions are open-loop and lack a mechanism to verify actual evidence usage, motivating a reliable internal readout signal and targeted guidance.
AI.method: Use answer-span prediction entropy as an internal feedback; show naive entropy minimization is ambiguous; introduce low-entropy anchors and an entropy-shaping objective to reduce answer uncertainty while preserving high-confidence tokens; instantiate SPOT-E as a plug-in, question-conditioned spotlight generator optimized per instance via lightweight GRPO-based tuning (Group Relative Policy Optimization).
AI.result: SPOT-E yields consistent performance gains across multiple benchmarks and VLM families and improves robustness to visual corruptions.
AI.conclusion: A practical, retraining-free approach to improve evidence readout in VLMs by shaping answer uncertainty and producing targeted, test-time spotlights, with open-source code available.

title: Human Universal Grasping
summary: Humans can grasp objects effortlessly, whereas multi-fingered robots are far from this level of generality. We argue that the most natural source of robot grasping data is from humans, who pick up thousands of objects every day. We present HUG, a flow-matching model that generates diverse human grasps for any user-specified object in a single RGB-D image captured from a stereo camera. Using smart glasses, we first collect 1M-HUGs, an egocentric dataset of human grasps spanning 1M frames (27.8 hrs) and 6,707 object instances across 41 buildings. Next, to model the distribution of natural human grasps, our novel flow-matching model fuses RGB and depth observations to output a grasp parameterized by wrist translation, wrist rotation, and MANO hand pose. Predicted grasps can be retargeted to various robot hands, enabling zero-shot grasping in everyday scenes. To standardize evaluation, we build a new simulated benchmark, HUG-Bench, of 90 unseen objects from five geometric categories and various sizes, with metric-scale 3D meshes. We evaluate HUG in the real world on the 30-object test set of HUG-Bench across multiple stereo cameras, robot embodiments, and household environments. HUG outperforms the state-of-the-art grasping baselines by +23% and +34% on our challenging object set. Code, data, benchmark, checkpoints, and an interactive demo are released on our website: https://grasping.io/

AI.tldr: HUG is a flow-matching model trained on 1M egocentric human grasps to generate diverse, retargetable robot grasps from a single RGB-D image, with HUG-Bench for standardized evaluation and strong real-world performance.
AI.motivation: To close the gap between human and robot grasping by leveraging abundant human grasp data; robots currently struggle with generality and dexterity across diverse objects.
AI.method: Collect 1M-HUGs using egocentric smart-glasses; train a flow-matching model that fuses RGB and depth to predict a grasp defined by wrist translation, wrist rotation, and MANO hand pose; retarget predicted grasps to various robot hands for zero-shot grasping; introduce HUG-Bench, a 90-object unseen benchmark with metric-scale 3D meshes; evaluate across stereo cameras and robot embodiments; release code, data, and demo.
AI.result: HUG outperforms state-of-the-art grasping baselines by 23% and 34% on the challenging HUG-Bench object set; demonstrates robust zero-shot grasping in real-world scenes; provides a scalable dataset, benchmark, and tooling.
AI.conclusion: Using human grasp priors distilled via flow matching enables transferable, zero-shot robot grasping across diverse objects and hands, with a standardized benchmark fostering future progress.

title: Self-Adaptive Scale Handling for Forecasting Time Series with Scale Heterogeneity
comment: This is the full version of the paper accepted by the IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP). The code and dataset are available at https://github.com/Meteor-Stars/ASTSF
summary: Current time series forecasting (TSF) research predominantly focuses on scale-homogeneous data, where different time series share similar numerical magnitude ranges. However, in real-world industrial scenarios such as financial product sales, different time series often differ by orders of magnitude (scale heterogeneity). Since these series share similar temporal patterns, joint modeling is desirable for better data utilization, yet existing scaling methods either compress low-scale signals (global normalization) or destroy semantic discriminability and amplify inverse-scaling errors (window-based scaling). This paper proposes a self-Adaptive Scale-handling (AS) module that learns adaptive scale factors tailored to each input, preserving semantic discriminability while reducing inverse-scaling errors. AS consists of Scale Calibrating (SC), which calibrates prior mean scaling factors through neural networks, and Scaling Selection (SS), which decides whether to apply calibration or retain the original factor, avoiding over-calibration. Experiments on real-world fund sales datasets from Ant Fortune and Alipay show that AS seamlessly integrates into popular TSF models and consistently improves their performance. The code and dataset are available at the link https://github.com/Meteor-Stars/ASTSF.

AI.tldr: A self-adaptive scale-handling module for time series forecasting that addresses scale heterogeneity by per-input adaptive calibration and a selective gating mechanism, yielding consistent improvements across models on real-world datasets.
AI.motivation: In scale-heterogeneous TSF, different series span orders of magnitude. Global normalization and window-based scaling either compress signals or harm semantic discriminability, limiting joint modeling benefits. A per-input adaptive, discriminative scaling mechanism can improve data utilization without over-calibration.
AI.method: Introduce AS module with two components: Scale Calibrating (SC) that learns and calibrates prior mean scale factors via neural networks, and Scaling Selection (SS) that decides to apply calibration or keep the original factor, mitigating over-calibration. AS is plug-and-play and integrates into popular TSF models. Evaluated on real fund-sales data from Ant Fortune and Alipay; code/dataset released.
AI.result: AS consistently improves performance of baseline TSF models across experiments and architectures. Ablation studies show both SC and SS contribute to gains, and the method preserves semantic discriminability while reducing inverse-scaling errors. Real-world datasets demonstrate practical effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: AS provides a lightweight, plug-and-play solution to scale heterogeneity in time series forecasting, enabling better joint modeling and data utilization without sacrificing semantic information. The approach is validated on real industrial data and accompanied by public code and dataset releases.

title: Single-Stage Hierarchical Rectification for Weakly Supervised Histopathology Segmentation
comment: Accepted to MICCAI 2026. This is the pre-review submitted version, not the camera-ready version. The final authenticated version will be available in the MICCAI 2026 proceedings
summary: Existing weakly supervised semantic segmentation (WSSS) methods in computational pathology rely on a multi-stage paradigm: class activation map (CAM) generation, offline pseudo-mask refinement, and fully supervised retraining. While established, this decoupled approach presents fundamental limitations. The multi-stage process not only incurs high computational training costs but also suffers from error propagation: local texture biases in shallow CNN layers generate false-positive artifacts that subsequent refinement steps often fail to correct. To address these persistent challenges through a simple yet highly effective approach, we propose the Single-Stage Hierarchical Rectification (SSHR) framework. Rather than passively refining CAMs post-hoc, our method proactively purifies intermediate feature representations during the forward pass. We introduce a Hierarchical Feature Rectification Module (HFRM) that utilizes deep global semantic context to filter out local anomalies in shallow layers. This mechanism generates high-fidelity activation maps directly within a single training loop. Experiments on the LUAD-HistoSeg and BCSS datasets demonstrate that SSHR outperforms state-of-the-art multi-stage methods. Furthermore, SSHR reduces training duration by 2 to 5 times. This efficiency minimizes computational overhead and accelerates clinical translation for large-scale histopathology workflows. The code is available at: https://github.com/trongduc-nguyen/SSHR

AI.tldr: Single-stage hierarchical rectification (SSHR) improves weakly supervised semantic segmentation in histopathology by rectifying features during forward pass, outperforming multi-stage approaches and reducing training time by 2–5x.
AI.motivation: Addresses limitations of decoupled WSSS that suffer from high compute and error propagation due to local texture biases in shallow CNN layers.
AI.method: Introduces Hierarchical Feature Rectification Module (HFRM) that leverages deep global semantic context to purify intermediate features during forward pass, enabling high-fidelity CAMs in a single training loop.
AI.result: On LUAD-HistoSeg and BCSS datasets, SSHR surpasses state-of-the-art multi-stage methods and significantly reduces training time.
AI.conclusion: A simple yet effective single-stage paradigm offering improved performance and efficiency, with potential for scalable clinical deployment.

title: Hierarchical Control in Multi-Agent Games: LLM-based Planning and RL Execution
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) has achieved strong performance in sequential decision-making, yet scaling to complex multi-agent environments remains challenging due to sparse rewards, large state-action spaces, and the difficulty of learning coordinated strategies. We propose a hierarchical architecture where a pretrained large language model (LLM) acts as a centralized strategic controller that selects among specialized RL skill policies for a team of agents, while RL policies handle reactive low-level execution. We evaluate this hybrid system in a competitive 2v2 King of the Hill environment against behavior tree (BT) and \emph{``Flat''} RL (end-to-end training without skill decomposition) baselines. The LLM+RL system achieves task performance statistically equivalent to hand-crafted BT (46.4\% vs 51.5\% win rate, $p=0.103$) while both significantly outperform Flat RL trained without skill decomposition. A user study ($n=15$) reveals that 60\% of participants perceive LLM+RL agents as the most human-like ($p=0.027$), citing behavioral adaptability and tactical variability. These results demonstrate that pretrained LLM reasoning can effectively orchestrate pretrained RL skills, achieving competitive multi-agent coordination and superior perceived believability without manual rule engineering.

AI.tldr: A hierarchical RL framework uses a pretrained language model as a centralized strategist to select among pretrained RL skill policies for a multi-agent team, with RL policies handling low-level control. It achieves competitive performance against hand-crafted behavior trees and outperforms end-to-end Flat RL, and yields higher perceived human-likeness in a user study.
AI.motivation: Address the scalability and coordination challenges of multi-agent RL in complex environments with sparse rewards and large state-action spaces. Leverage the planning and generalization capabilities of pretrained LLMs to orchestrate a set of reusable RL skills, reducing manual rule engineering.
AI.method: Propose a hierarchical architecture in which a pretrained LLM serves as a centralized strategic controller to choose among specialized RL skill policies for a team of agents; RL policies manage reactive low-level execution. Evaluation in a 2v2 King of the Hill environment against two baselines: Behavior Tree (BT) and Flat RL (end-to-end training without skill decomposition). Metrics include win rates and statistical significance; a user study (n=15) assessed perceived human-likeness.
AI.result: LLM+RL achieved task performance statistically equivalent to hand-crafted BT (46.4% vs 51.5% win rate, p=0.103); both BT and LLM+RL significantly outperform Flat RL without skill decomposition. User study (n=15) showed 60% perceived LLM+RL agents as most human-like (p=0.027).
AI.conclusion: Pretrained LLM reasoning can effectively orchestrate pretrained RL skills to achieve competitive multi-agent coordination and enhanced perceived believability without manual rule engineering.

title: Adaptive Distance-Aware Trunk Deep Operator Learning for Long-Span Roadway Bridges
summary: Long-span roadway bridges exhibit highly localized structural responses under vehicular loading, making repeated FE analysis computationally expensive for applications such as influence surface generation and structural digital twins. Existing SciML approaches struggle to accurately capture these localized responses. To address this challenge, this study proposes an adaptive-trunk DeepONet for localized structural response prediction in large-scale bridge systems. The framework dynamically constructs a load-dependent learning domain using a KNN strategy, allowing the network to focus on structural influence zones. The trunk network is further enhanced using distance-aware features that encode the geometric relationship between the load and structural nodes. A physics-based full-field reconstruction is incorporated through a stiffness-informed Schur complement formulation, enabling predictions at adaptive nodes to be extended to the entire structural domain. To enable scalable training, response data are generated using a reduced-order equivalent shell model that preserves the dominant global behavior while significantly reducing computational cost. The proposed framework is validated on both a benchmark bridge model and the real-world Mussafah Bridge. Results show that the method achieves FEM-level accuracy with relative errors below 5%, while reducing the total response evaluation time (including full-field reconstruction) by approximately 60x; excluding the post-processing reconstruction step, the AD-DeepONet inference is up to four orders of magnitude faster than FEM. In addition, the framework enables rapid generation of full-field responses, influence lines, and influence surfaces under arbitrary vehicular loading configurations, demonstrating strong potential for large-scale bridge analysis and digital twin applications.

AI.tldr: Adaptive-trunk DeepONet with load-aware domain and physics-based reconstruction enables fast, accurate localized response predictions for large-scale bridges, achieving FEM-level accuracy with ~60x speedup and up to 4 orders-of-magnitude faster inference when reconstruction is excluded.
AI.motivation: Long-span bridges exhibit highly localized responses under vehicular loads, making repeated FE analyses expensive for influence surfaces and digital twins. Existing SciML models struggle with localization. A method that efficiently predicts local responses and extends them to full-field predictions is needed.
AI.method: Dynamically constructs a load-dependent learning domain via a KNN strategy; incorporates distance-aware features encoding load-node geometry; uses a stiffness-informed Schur-complement formulation for full-field reconstruction; trains on data from a reduced-order equivalent shell model to preserve global behavior while reducing cost; validates on a benchmark bridge and Mussafah Bridge.
AI.result: Achieves FEM-level accuracy with relative errors <5%. Reduces total response evaluation time (including reconstruction) by ~60x. Inference speed (excluding post-processing reconstruction) is up to 10^4x faster than FEM. Enables rapid generation of full-field responses, influence lines, and influence surfaces under arbitrary vehicular loading.
AI.conclusion: The framework shows strong potential for scalable, real-time analysis and digital-twin applications in large-scale bridge systems, addressing the localization challenge and enabling efficient, accurate predictions across the structural domain.

title: CMDS-AD: Cross-Modal Dual-Stream Decoupling for Few-Shot Anomaly Detection
summary: Few-shot anomaly detection remains challenging due to limited training data. Multi-modal anomaly detection (MAD) offers a viable solution, leveraging 3D geometric cues to enrich 2D RGB representations and compensate for this scarcity. However, existing MAD methods apply spatially uniform feature processing, conflating stable macroscopic structures with high-frequency localized defect signals, exacerbating cross-modal misalignment and inflating false-positive rates. To overcome this, we present CMDS-AD, a Cross-Modal Dual-Stream Anomaly Detection framework. A LoRA-guided diffusion model generates diverse RGB samples to mitigate extreme data scarcity. For 3D normal augmentation, we employ a pre-trained diffusion model as a normal estimator. Crucially, this estimator inherently acts as a non-linear low-pass filter, directly extracting low-frequency normal representations from RGB inputs. This establishes an auxiliary estimated stream of purely low-frequency information, anchoring robust structural templates and assisting the uncompressed real stream, containing coupled high- and low-frequency components, to precisely isolate micro-defects. A Coordinate-Aware Hierarchical Feature Mapper adaptively aligns cross-modal semantics, while a multiplicative scoring mechanism filters modality-specific noise. Under the extreme 1-shot setting, CMDS-AD achieves absolute performance gains of 5.7% (I-AUROC) and 2.0% (AUPRO) on MVTec 3D-AD, alongside 7.7% and 5.6% improvements on EyeCandies, establishing a new state-of-the-art.

AI.tldr: CMDS-AD: a cross-modal dual-stream anomaly detector for ultra-sparse data that uses LoRA-guided diffusion to augment RGB and a diffusion-based normal estimator as a low-frequency anchor, with adaptive feature alignment and multiplicative fusion; achieves SOTA in 1-shot MAD on 3D-AD and EyeCandies.
AI.motivation: Tackle few-shot multi-modal anomaly detection by avoiding uniform spatial processing that blurs stable structure with defect signals; leverage 3D cues and diffusion models to enrich representations and reduce false positives.
AI.method: A Cross-Modal Dual-Stream architecture (CMDS-AD) with: (1) LoRA-guided diffusion to generate diverse RGB samples; (2) a pre-trained diffusion-based normal estimator for 3D normal augmentation, acting as a nonlinear low-pass filter to produce a low-frequency structural stream; (3) a Coordinate-Aware Hierarchical Feature Mapper for adaptive cross-modal alignment; (4) a multiplicative scoring mechanism to suppress modality-specific noise.
AI.result: Under 1-shot, CMDS-AD yields absolute gains of 5.7% in I-AUROC and 2.0% in AUPRO on MVTec 3D-AD, and 7.7% and 5.6% improvements on EyeCandies, establishing a new state-of-the-art.
AI.conclusion: The approach demonstrates robust cross-modal fusion under extreme data scarcity, leveraging a low-frequency normal anchor and adaptive alignment to reduce misalignment and false positives; the method shows strong empirical gains on standard MAD benchmarks and suggests broader applicability to cross-modal few-shot anomaly tasks.

title: Exploring the potential of AlphaEarth and TESSERA embeddings for Fine-scale Local Climate Zone Mapping: A case study across five cities in Switzerland
summary: Understanding urban spatial morphology is critical for climate modeling, risk assessment, and sustainable urban design, and Local Climate Zone (LCZ) mapping provides the basic framework for this. However, many cities still use coarse ~100-m resolution LCZ records, which are unsuitable for fine-scale urban research. In this study, precomputed embeddings from TESSERA (Feng et al., 2025) and AlphaEarth (Brown et al., 2025) are compared to traditional Sentinel-1/2 (S1S2) composites in five Swiss cities to see if they can upscale coarse LCZ maps to 10-m resolution using an attention-based U-Net. Three experiments assess multi-city transferability, the impact of higher-resolution reference data, and temporal robustness to year-to-year phenology changes. We find that all datasets achieve strong performance with test data Intersection-over-Union (IoU) ranging from 0.59-0.69 and 0.77-0.82 in the first two experiments. TESSERA consistently outperforms both S1S2 and AlphaEarth across both settings As expected, we find that the transfer of embedding-based models from one year to another remains an open challenge. Overall, however, our results demonstrate the promising potential of embeddings derived from EO foundation models to reduce time consuming preprocessing, respectively, manual feature engineering tasks and to guide a universal deep learning-based LCZ mapping workflow. When combined with a simple location-aware attention U-Net architecture, the embeddings enhance regional transferability and scalability, supporting the development of comprehensive and reproducible fine-scale LCZ maps for global urban climate applications Improving reference data quality remains the strongest lever for further accuracy gains.

AI.tldr: Embedding-based LCZ upscaling using an attention U-Net with EO foundation-model embeddings (TESSERA, AlphaEarth) shows strong IoU (0.59–0.69 in exp.1; 0.77–0.82 in exp.2), with TESSERA outperforming S1S2/AlphaEarth; year-to-year transferability remains challenging; data quality is the key lever for accuracy and scalability.
AI.motivation: To achieve fine-scale (10 m) LCZ maps from coarse references by leveraging precomputed embeddings from EO foundation models, reducing manual feature engineering and enabling scalable, transferable urban climate datasets.
AI.method: Compare embeddings from TESSERA, AlphaEarth, and Sentinel-1/2 (S1S2) in five Swiss cities. Train an attention-based U-Net to upscale LCZ maps to 10 m. Conduct three experiments: (1) multi-city transferability, (2) impact of higher-resolution reference data, (3) temporal robustness across years. Evaluate using IoU.
AI.result: All datasets yielded strong IoU; exp.1: 0.59–0.69; exp.2: 0.77–0.82. TESSERA consistently outperformed S1S2 and AlphaEarth across settings. Transfer of embedding models across years remains an open challenge. Embeddings improve regional transferability and scalability; higher-quality reference data provides the strongest gains.
AI.conclusion: EO foundation-model embeddings are a promising avenue for universal DL-based LCZ mapping workflows, particularly when paired with location-aware attention architectures. They can reduce preprocessing and enable reproducible, fine-scale LCZ maps for urban climate applications. The biggest gain likely comes from improving reference data quality; longitudinal transferability requires further research.

title: CUPID: Reconstructing UV Texture Maps for Interpretable Person-of-Interest Deepfake Detection
summary: Deepfakes targeting a high-profile individual, known as Person-of-Interest (POI), are a threat to modern democracies and societies. Current POI deepfake detection methods still struggle to combine robustness to post-processing, efficiency and interpretability, focal aspects of modern deepfake detectors. In this paper we propose CUPID, a POI video deepfake detector that combines UV texture maps, a facial appearance representation derived from 3D face reconstructions, with the representation learning capabilities of the Masked Autoencoder (MAE). Our method does not require any deepfake videos in its training phase. Moreover, it does not even require to include a specific POI in the training set: the combination of UV texture maps extracted from real video frames and the MAE context-guided reconstruction yields a latent space that captures rich and discriminative facial features also for identities unseen during training. In the testing phase, the embeddings extracted from a query video depicting the POI can be matched against pristine reference videos to assess the video authenticity. Furthermore, operating in the UV space naturally provides an additional layer of interpretability. Specifically, we can extract decoded residual maps that highlight which facial regions of a test video deviate most from the identity representation of the corresponding POI. Experiments on four deepfake datasets show that CUPID outperforms current state of the art on most datasets and achieves the best overall robustness against strong downscaling and compression, providing also substantially faster inference. Our experimental code will be released at https://github.com/polimi-ispl/CUPID.

AI.tldr: Zero-shot POI deepfake detection using UV texture maps and MAE-based appearance learning; achieves strong generalization to unseen identities, interpretability via UV residual maps, and robustness to post-processing with faster inference.
AI.motivation: POI deepfakes threaten public discourse; existing detectors struggle to generalize across identities and withstand post-processing while remaining interpretable and efficient. A detector that works without FP/dl fake data in training and generalizes to unseen POIs is highly desirable.
AI.method: Integrates UV texture maps from real video frames with a 3D face reconstruction-derived appearance representation, coupled with Masked Autoencoder (MAE) context-guided reconstruction. No deepfake videos are required in training; POI need not appear in training. Inference compares embeddings from a query POI video to pristine reference videos; interpretability via decoded residual maps in UV space.
AI.result: Outperforms current state-of-the-art on most datasets and shows superior robustness to strong downscaling and compression; faster inference. Four deepfake datasets used for evaluation; code to be released at the provided URL.
AI.conclusion: CUPID offers robust, fast, and interpretable POI deepfake detection without requiring deepfake data during training, achieving strong generalization to unseen identities and resilience to common post-processing artifacts.

title: Alzheimer's Disease Diagnosis using a Multimodal Approach with 3D MRI and PET
comment: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Bioinformatics and Biomedicine (BIBM)
summary: Alzheimer's disease (AD) is an irreversible neurodegenerative disorder and a leading cause of death worldwide. Early diagnosis plays an important part especially at the Mild Cognitive Impairment stage, where timely intervention can help slow its progression before it advances to AD. Neuroimaging data, like Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) and Positron Emission Tomography (PET) scans, can help detect brain changes early by providing structural and functional brain changes related to the disease. Yet, many multimodal models still fuse MRI and PET with static concatenation and apply identical computation to all subjects, which limits robustness to patient/site heterogeneity and can waste computation. To address these limitations, we present the first study of combining 3D convolutional feature extractors with three fusion strategies - concatenation, Gated Multimodal Unit (GMU), and gated self-attention - and a sparsely gated Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) classifier that performs input-adaptive routing, activating only the most informative experts per case. Finally, we utilize Grad-CAM to visualize disease-related regions, ensuring model interpretability. Experiments are performed across three binary classification tasks (NC vs. MCI, MCI vs. AD, and NC vs. AD). Results show that GMU achieves accuracies of 80.46 % (NC vs. MCI) and 95.47 % (NC vs. AD), while gated self-attention attains 82.08 % on MCI vs. AD. Ablations show that removing the MoE consistently degrades accuracy across all tasks. These findings underscore the value of input-adaptive, multimodal modeling for AD diagnosis by leveraging the complementary nature of MRI and PET.

AI.tldr: A multimodal MRI-PET AD diagnosis framework that uses 3D CNN feature extractors and input-adaptive fusion (concatenation, GMU, gated self-attention) with a sparsely gated MoE classifier; achieves strong binary-class accuracies and interpretable Grad-CAM visualizations.
AI.motivation: Overcome robustness issues due to subject/site heterogeneity and inefficiency of static fusion in multimodal AD diagnosis by introducing input-adaptive fusion and a conditional, modular classifier.
AI.method: Three-dimensional CNN feature extractors process MRI and PET data. Three fusion strategies are evaluated: simple concatenation, Gate Multimodal Unit (GMU), and gated self-attention. A sparsely gated Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) classifier performs input-adaptive routing, activating only informative experts for each case. Grad-CAM is used to visualize disease-related regions. Evaluated on three binary tasks: NC vs MCI, MCI vs AD, NC vs AD.
AI.result: GMU reaches 80.46% (NC vs MCI) and 95.47% (NC vs AD). Gated self-attention achieves 82.08% on MCI vs AD. Ablation by removing MoE degrades accuracy across all tasks.
AI.conclusion: Input-adaptive, multimodal modeling that leverages MRI and PET complementarity improves AD diagnosis, with MoE-based routing boosting performance and Grad-CAM providing interpretability.

title: GEN-Guard: Correcting Generalization Failures for Deployable Federated Surgical AI
summary: Federated Learning (FL) in surgical video AI enables collaborative model training without sharing sensitive data. However, standard evaluation practices - selecting the "best" global model based only on validation data from participating hospitals - can lead to suboptimal deployment choices. We identify this critical failure mode as performance leakage, where the selected model overfits internal federation data and fails to generalize to unseen institutions. We propose GEN-Guard, a practical post-hoc framework to detect and correct generalization failures in federated surgical AI. It integrates Generalization Detection via Client-Blocked Evaluation (CBE), which validates performance on isolated client distributions to prevent performance leakage, and Generalization Correction through Disagreement-Aware Distillation (DAD), which learns adaptive feature-level corrections for cross-institutional robustness. Both components operate after standard FL convergence while providing robust support for zero-shot adaptation to unseen environments. We first quantify the severity of performance leakage, observing Model Selection Failures (MSFs) exceeding 80% under standard evaluation. GEN-Guard is evaluated on two multi-center clinical challenges: surgical phase recognition in laparoscopic cholecystectomy and polyp segmentation in colonoscopy. Across both datasets, GEN-Guard consistently corrects these failures, improving in-federation F1 scores by up to 2 points, unseen-institution performance by up to 3 points, and worst-case institutional performance by 3-9 points. Performance leakage represents a systematic and previously under-recognized risk in federated surgical AI. GEN-Guard provides a practical solution for detecting and correcting such failures. By improving cross-institutional robustness and zero-shot generalization, it strengthens the reliability of FL for real-world surgical deployment.

AI.tldr: GEN-Guard is a post-hoc framework that detects and corrects generalization failures in federated surgical AI by (1) validating performance on isolated client distributions to prevent leakage, and (2) applying adaptive feature-level corrections for cross-institution robustness, improving in-federation and unseen-institution performance.
AI.motivation: Standard FL model selection overfits to federation-wide validation data, causing performance leakage where models fail to generalize to unseen institutions. This poses reliability risks for clinical deployment.
AI.method: GEN-Guard comprises two post-hoc components: Generalization Detection via Client-Blocked Evaluation (CBE) to assess models on isolated client distributions, and Generalization Correction through Disagreement-Aware Distillation (DAD) to learn adaptive feature-level corrections. Both operate after FL convergence to enable zero-shot adaptation to unseen environments.
AI.result: Across two clinical tasks (surgical phase recognition in laparoscopic cholecystectomy and polyp segmentation in colonoscopy), GEN-Guard reduces Model Selection Failures (MSFs) by addressing leakage, improves in-federation F1 by up to 2 points, unseen-institution performance by up to 3 points, and worst-case institutional performance by 3–9 points.
AI.conclusion: Performance leakage is a systemic risk in federated surgical AI. GEN-Guard provides a practical detection-and-correction approach that enhances cross-institutional robustness and zero-shot generalization, strengthening deployment reliability.

title: Comparative Study of Neural Surrogate Architectures for Autoregressive Prediction of Internal Battery States
summary: The Doyle-Fuller-Newman (DFN) model resolves internal electrochemical states in lithium-ion batteries with high fidelity. However, the numerical solution of its governing equations is computationally prohibitive for real-time deployment, limiting scalability from individual cells to pack and fleet-scale applications. While machine learning surrogates can substantially reduce inference latency through GPU acceleration, most existing approaches learn solution approximations tied to specific operating conditions rather than learning generalizable state-evolution dynamics. This work presents a systematic comparison of four neural network architectures (MLP, ResNet, U-Net, FNO) formulated as autoregressive state-transition operators that predict full DFN internal states across a wide range of operating conditions. To ensure a controlled architectural comparison, all models are trained under a unified framework using multi-step unrolling and current-conditioning, isolating the impact of spatial inductive bias. Results demonstrate that the U-Net's multi-scale feature hierarchy achieves a mean final-step nRMSE of 3% averaged across all internal state variables after 300-step autoregressive rollouts, while providing a 5.38x speed-up over the numerical solver. These findings highlight spatial inductive bias as a critical determinant of surrogate performance, advancing the development of surrogates for internal state observability for next-generation battery management systems and digital twins.

AI.tldr: A comparative study evaluates four neural architectures (MLP, ResNet, U-Net, FNO) as autoregressive state-transition surrogates for the Doyle-Fuller-Newman DFN battery model. U-Net with multi-scale features outperforms others, achieving ~3% final-step nRMSE after 300-step rollouts and ~5.38× speed-up over the solver, highlighting spatial inductive bias as crucial for accurate internal-state prediction and real-time BMS/digital twin deployment.
AI.motivation: DFN provides high-fidelity internal electrochemical states but is too computationally expensive for real-time use. There is a need for generalizable, fast surrogates that can evolve states across diverse operating conditions rather than condition-specific approximations.
AI.method: Four architectures (MLP, ResNet, U-Net, FNO) are reformulated as autoregressive state-transition operators. The models are trained under a unified multi-step unrolling with current-conditioning framework to isolate the effect of spatial inductive bias. Performance is evaluated across a wide operating condition range, focusing on internal state evolution accuracy (nRMSE) and inference speed, compared to a full DFN solver.
AI.result: U-Net achieves the best performance with a mean final-step nRMSE of ~3% across all internal state variables after 300 autoregressive steps, and provides a 5.38× speed-up relative to the DFN solver. The study finds spatial inductive bias is a critical determinant of surrogate performance.
AI.conclusion: Spatial inductive bias, particularly the multi-scale feature hierarchy of U-Net, enables more accurate and faster surrogates for internal state observability in DFN-based battery models. This supports deploying surrogates in next-generation BMS and digital twins and underscores the importance of architecture choice when designing physics-informed surrogates.

title: Through the PRISM: Preference Representation in Intermediate States of Video Diffusion Models
summary: Evaluating video generation with clean, pixel-based reward models disconnects evaluation from the noisy diffusion process and incurs massive VAE decoding costs. In this paper, we challenge this paradigm by asking a fundamental question: Can a powerful video generator inherently discriminate preferences directly from noisy latents? To answer this, we introduce \textbf{PRISM} (\textbf{P}reference \textbf{R}epresentation in \textbf{I}ntermediate \textbf{S}tates of Diffusion \textbf{M}odels). PRISM employs a lightweight Query-based Aggregation head with a frozen video diffusion backbone to decode preference signals from noisy latents. Surprisingly, PRISM not only achieves SOTA preference accuracy but also unlocks strong noise-robustness, which enables early-stage Best-of-$N$ sampling. This allows for filtering suboptimal candidates at the very beginning of denoising, drastically reducing computation while boosting video quality. We also reveal a strong positive correlation between a backbone's generative performance and its inherent evaluative power, enabling self-improving video backbones.

AI.tldr: PRISM enables preference evaluation directly from noisy latent states in diffusion video models, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy, robustness to noise, and enabling early Best-of-N sampling to reduce computation and boost video quality.
AI.motivation: Traditional evaluation uses clean, pixel-based reward models tied to the diffusion process, incurring pixel decoding costs and noise from VAE; a latent-space, noise-robust evaluator could be more efficient and potentially more aligned with generation quality.
AI.method: Introduce PRISM with a lightweight, query-based aggregation head on a frozen video diffusion backbone. Decode preference signals from noisy latents without decoding to pixels. Use Best-of-N sampling at early denoising stages to filter suboptimal candidates. Demonstrate correlation between backbone generative performance and evaluative power to enable self-improvement of backbones.
AI.result: PRISM achieves state-of-the-art preference accuracy, shows strong noise robustness, enables early-stage Best-of-N sampling reducing computation, and yields better video quality. Also reveals a positive correlation between generative performance and evaluative power, enabling self-improvement of video backbones.
AI.conclusion: Evaluating preferences in intermediate diffusion states is feasible and advantageous for video generation. PRISM offers a robust, efficient alternative to pixel-based rewards, and suggests backbone quality is linked to evaluative capability, enabling self-improvement and more efficient model deployment.

title: PaAno+: Multiscale Encoding and Cross-Variable Attention for Time Series Anomaly Detection
summary: Time-series anomaly detection has significant practical value for industrial and medical monitoring, as well as other critical domains. Current Transformer- and large-model-based detection approaches incur excessive computational overhead, while existing lightweight alternatives are constrained by insufficient feature extraction and inadequate modeling of dependencies across multivariate variables. To mitigate the above drawbacks, this study develops a lightweight, efficient anomaly detection model, dubbed PaAno, within the patch-oriented representation learning paradigm. In the encoder module, a multiscale feature-extraction backbone is constructed using convolutional kernels with differentiated receptive fields to capture hierarchical temporal characteristics; subsequent cross-scale adaptive attention aggregation, combined with residual connection optimization, further stabilizes feature representation learning. A cross-variable fusion attention module is embedded to explicitly characterize inter-variable correlations, empowering the model to identify anomalous patterns amid intricate operational conditions. Moreover, a novel pretext task based on temporal patch-window sorting is customized to uncover intrinsic structural properties of time series, and triplet loss is leveraged to optimize the patch embedding space for enhanced feature discrimination. Extensive experiments on the TSB-AD benchmark demonstrate that the proposed PaAno achieves state-of-the-art detection accuracy on both univariate and multivariate tasks, yielding significant performance gains across evaluation metrics, including VUS-PR, relative to the original PaAno. Leveraging a compact network design, the presented model achieves favorable computational efficiency, enabling deployment on resource-limited terminals for real-time anomaly inference.

AI.tldr: A lightweight, patch-based time-series anomaly detector PaAno with multiscale feature extraction, cross-variable fusion attention, and a temporal patch-window sorting pretext task, achieving state-of-the-art performance with efficient inference on the TSB-AD benchmark.
AI.motivation: Current Transformer- and large-model-based anomaly detection approaches incur high computational costs, while existing lightweight methods lack sufficient feature extraction and effective modeling of dependencies across multivariate variables.
AI.method: Encoder with a multiscale convolutional backbone to capture hierarchical temporal features, cross-scale adaptive attention with residual connections, cross-variable fusion attention to model inter-variable correlations, a pretext task based on temporal patch-window sorting, and triplet loss to optimize the patch embedding space.
AI.result: On the TSB-AD benchmark, PaAno achieves state-of-the-art detection accuracy for both univariate and multivariate tasks, with significant gains in metrics such as VUS-PR relative to the original PaAno, and maintains computational efficiency suitable for real-time inference on resource-constrained devices.
AI.conclusion: PaAno advances lightweight time-series anomaly detection by integrating patch-based representation learning, cross-variable fusion, and a specialized pretext task, delivering high accuracy and efficiency for industrial/medical monitoring scenarios.

title: Reliability-Aware Prototype Calibration for Frozen Pose-Flow Video Anomaly Detection
comment: 15 pages, 5 figures, 7 tables. Code available at https://github.com/iNing10/RPC
summary: Pose-flow video anomaly detectors are attractive for one-class surveillance because they provide likelihood-based rankings for tracked skeleton windows. However, a single likelihood score may hide multimodal normal behavior and be sensitive to pose-observation noise. We study a frozen-detector setting in which the pose-flow backbone, cached skeleton tracks, and evaluation pipeline are fixed. Reliability-Aware Prototype Calibration (RPC) is a post-hoc score calibration method for this setting. It adds a standardized nearest-prototype deviation in the frozen latent space to the standardized flow score, and uses keypoint confidence only to gate this added geometric evidence. Thus, RPC preserves the original density signal while correcting the ranking with empirical normal-mode structure under pose reliability. Across two frozen pose-flow backbones and four datasets, RPC improves frame-level AUROC in all eight backbone-dataset pairs, with gains ranging from 0.34 to 4.49 percentage points and averaging 2.03 points. Ablation and reliability analyses show that prototype deviation is the main corrective signal, while reliability gating is most useful when pose observations are less trustworthy. These results suggest that lightweight post-hoc calibration can strengthen cached pose-flow systems when retraining or reproducing the full pose pipeline is impractical.

AI.tldr: Post-hoc Reliability-Aware Prototype Calibration (RPC) improves anomaly detection in frozen pose-flow systems by augmenting the standardized flow score with a standardized nearest-prototype deviation in latent space, gated by keypoint confidence.
AI.motivation: In one-class surveillance with pose-flow, a single likelihood can be multimodal and sensitive to pose-noise, and retraining the full pipeline may be impractical. A lightweight calibration in a frozen setting aims to reveal normal-mode structure and improve rankings.
AI.method: RPC adds a standardized nearest-prototype deviation in the frozen latent space to the standardized flow score, and gates this geometric evidence with keypoint confidence to reflect pose reliability. It is a post-hoc calibration applied without retraining the detector.
AI.result: Across two frozen pose-flow backbones and four datasets (eight backbone-dataset pairs), RPC improves frame-level AUROC in all cases, with gains from 0.34 to 4.49 percentage points (mean ≈2.03pp). Ablation shows prototype deviation is the main signal; reliability gating helps when pose observations are less trustworthy.
AI.conclusion: Lightweight post-hoc calibration via RPC strengthens cached pose-flow systems in settings where retraining the full pipeline is impractical, effectively leveraging normal-mode structure while respecting pose reliability.

title: What Makes Effective Supervision in Latent Chain-of-Thought: An Information-Theoretic Analysis
summary: Latent Chain-of-Thought (CoT) internalizes reasoning within continuous hidden states, offering a promising alternative to verbose discrete reasoning traces. However, robust latent reasoning remains difficult because outcome supervision provides weak learning signals and leaves latent trajectories prone to semantic drift. In this work, we analyze Latent CoT from an information-theoretic perspective and identify this failure as a dual collapse: gradient attenuation along the optimization path and representational drift in the latent space. We further decompose process supervision into two complementary dimensions: Trajectory Supervision, which injects dense stepwise reasoning signals, and Space Supervision, which preserves the semantic structure of the latent manifold. Our analysis shows that rigid geometric compression can collapse the reasoning space, whereas generative reconstruction provides a more flexible semantic anchor that better preserves information capacity. To measure these effects, we introduce the Unified Latent Probe (ULP), which quantifies the mutual information between latent trajectories and explicit reasoning steps. Experiments reveal a clear Information-Performance Binding: reasoning accuracy depends on the information fidelity preserved in the latent chain. These findings provide a principled framework for latent reasoning supervision and suggest shifting from geometric imitation toward mutual information maximization. Our code is available at \href{https://github.com/EIT-NLP/Supervision-in-Latent-CoT}{this repository}.

AI.tldr: Latent CoT faces dual information-theoretic collapse (gradient attenuation and representational drift); proposes two supervision axes (Trajectory and Space) and a unified MI-based probe (ULP); findings support mutual information maximization over geometric imitation to preserve latent reasoning.
AI.motivation: Diagnose why latent Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning struggles under weak outcome supervision and semantic drift, and develop a principled supervision framework that preserves information in latent reasoning trajectories.
AI.method: Information-theoretic analysis of Latent CoT; decompose supervision into Trajectory Supervision (dense stepwise signals) and Space Supervision (semantic structure of latent manifold); compare rigid geometric compression with generative reconstruction; introduce Unified Latent Probe (ULP) to measure mutual information between latent trajectories and explicit reasoning steps; empirical validation across tasks; release code.
AI.result: Identifies dual collapse (gradient attenuation along optimization path and representational drift in latent space); Trajectory and Space supervision are complementary; rigid geometric compression collapses reasoning space; generative reconstruction yields a more flexible semantic anchor and preserves information capacity; ULP quantifies latent-step mutual information; stronger information fidelity in latent chains correlates with higher reasoning accuracy (Information-Performance Binding).
