AI.conclusion: Shift supervision from geometric imitation toward maximizing mutual information between latent trajectories and explicit reasoning steps; adopt a principled framework for latent reasoning supervision and extend to MI-based objectives; code released for reproducibility.

title: Geometry-Aware Superpixel Graph Transformer with Metadata for Skin Lesion Classification
summary: Automated skin cancer classification from dermoscopic images remains challenging due to heterogeneous lesion structure, strong intra-class variability, and subtle visual differences between benign and malignant cases. Existing CNN/ViT pipelines typically rely on global or patch-level features and often combine patient metadata via late fusion, which limits spatially grounded multimodal reasoning. We present a novel region-based graph learning framework that explicitly models lesions as graphs of spatially coherent superpixel regions represented as frozen CNN features. To capture fine-grained lesion arrangements, we encode inter-regional geometry as edge attributes and introduce a dedicated metadata context node connected to all regions, providing structured integration of demographic/clinical variables within the same relational space. Node representations are updated using our edge-aware graph transformer followed by attention-driven propagation, and a final graph-level embedding for benign-malignant classification. Experiments on four public benchmarks demonstrate that explicit region-level relational modeling and graph-native multimodal fusion yield consistent gains over the state-of-the-art. Consequently, we establish a new graph-centric perspective in which CNN features are modeled as relational nodes and improved through contextual integration, yielding more expressive and robust classifications.

AI.tldr: A region-based graph learning framework for dermoscopic lesion classification that models lesions as graphs of spatially coherent superpixel regions with edge attributes and a metadata context node, enabling graph-native multimodal fusion and improved accuracy.
AI.motivation: Skin cancer classification via dermoscopy is challenged by heterogeneous lesion structure, high intra-class variability, and subtle benign/malignant differences. Standard CNN/ViT pipelines with global/patch features and late fusion poorly capture spatial relationships and multimodal context.
AI.method: Construct graphs where each node is a spatially coherent superpixel region encoded by frozen CNN features. Encode inter-region geometry as edge attributes. Introduce a metadata context node connected to all regions to fuse demographic/clinical variables within the relational space. Update node representations with an edge-aware graph transformer and attention-based propagation, yielding a graph-level embedding for classification.
AI.result: Experiments on four public benchmarks show consistent gains over state-of-the-art by leveraging explicit region-level relational modeling and graph-native multimodal fusion.
AI.conclusion: A graph-centric view treats CNN features as relational nodes that are enhanced via contextual integration, yielding more expressive and robust skin cancer classifications.

title: Sensorimotor World Models: Perception for Action via Inverse Dynamics
summary: Perception for action suggests that representations of the world should be shaped not by visual fidelity alone, but by their relevance for actions. At the same time, latent JEPA-style world models advocate learning compact predictive states from high-dimensional observations to facilitate the prediction of future states, but end-to-end training of these models is nontrivial because representations may collapse if our only goal is to construct a latent state that is easy to predict. We introduce a sensorimotor world model (SMWM): a latent world model trained end-to-end with inverse dynamics regularization. This single regularizer addresses both issues: it prevents representation collapse and induces action-aligned representations. By forcing latent states to preserve information about the action underlying a transition, it biases the model toward the controllable degrees of freedom of the environment while discarding uncontrollable distractors. This yields stable latent world models trained from offline, reward-free trajectories, without frozen encoders, exponential moving averages, or complex latent regularizers. Empirically, SMWM learns compact, interpretable latent spaces and enables competitive planning performance across simple 2D and 3D control tasks.

AI.tldr: Train a sensorimotor world model with inverse dynamics regularization to prevent representation collapse and learn action-aligned latent states from offline data, enabling stable planning in simple 2D/3D tasks.
AI.motivation: Need for representations that reflect controllable, task-relevant factors rather than mere visual fidelity; existing JEPA-style world models risk collapse when trained end-to-end; offline, reward-free data complicates learning.
AI.method: Propose SMWM: a latent world model trained end-to-end using inverse dynamics regularization; this single regularizer preserves action information in latent states, biases representations toward controllable degrees of freedom, and discards distractors; works with offline trajectories without frozen encoders, EMAs, or complex regularizers.
AI.result: SMWM learns compact, interpretable latent spaces and achieves competitive planning performance on basic 2D and 3D control tasks.
AI.conclusion: Inverse dynamics regularization is an effective, simple regularizer that yields stable, action-aligned latent world models suitable for offline learning and planning.

title: FlowBender: Feedback-Aware Training for Self-Correcting Conditional Flows
summary: Conditional diffusion and flow models routinely fail to satisfy the very constraints that define their task. For instance, a depth-conditioned model often produces images whose re-extracted depth disagrees with the input, even though the forward operator--the depth predictor defining the constraint--is available during both training and inference. Existing approaches generally fall into two categories: supervised models that treat the conditioning signal as a static cue and ignore alignment information at inference, and guidance-based methods that consult it through hand-tuned linear updates, typically trading fidelity to the condition against the plausibility of the generated sample. We argue that the fundamental gap in both paradigms is that the model is never trained to utilize its own alignment error. We introduce FlowBender, a closed-loop framework that treats this error as a first-class input, training the network to learn a correction policy conditioned on inference-time feedback. At each step, an unguided look-ahead pass estimates the clean signal, a task-specific deviation is computed via the forward operator, and a refinement pass consumes this signal to produce a corrected velocity. We propose several variants of FlowBender, including a gradient-based formulation for differentiable operators and a zero-order variant for non-differentiable settings such as JPEG compression. For efficient sampling, we introduce a prior-step shortcut that enables closed-loop correction at a minimal additional computational cost. Across image-to-image translation, restoration, and 3D mesh texturing, FlowBender consistently outperforms standard supervised baselines, alignment-loss-augmented training, and state-of-the-art inference-time guidance, improving fidelity and plausibility simultaneously rather than trading them against each other. Project page: https://flow-bender.github.io/

AI.tldr: FlowBender is a closed-loop, alignment-aware correction framework for conditional diffusion/flow models that uses inference-time feedback to correct predictions, with gradient-based and zero-order variants and a prior-step shortcut for efficiency, improving fidelity and plausibility across tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing conditional generative models fail to satisfy task-specific constraints because they are not trained to utilize their own alignment error; supervised conditioning and inference-time guidance lack an effective feedback mechanism.
AI.method: FlowBender adds an inference-time loop: an unguided look-ahead pass estimates a clean signal, a forward operator computes the deviation from the constraint, and a refinement pass generates a corrected velocity; supports differentiable (gradient-based) and non-differentiable (zero-order) operators; includes a prior-step shortcut to enable low-cost closed-loop correction.
AI.result: Empirically, FlowBender outperforms standard supervised baselines, alignment-loss augmented training, and state-of-the-art guidance across image-to-image translation, restoration, and 3D mesh texturing, improving both fidelity to constraints and plausibility of samples.
AI.conclusion: Training models to condition on and correct their own alignment error yields better constraint satisfaction without sacrificing realism, enabling robust closed-loop correction in diverse tasks.

title: Quantile of Means: A Bonus-Free Ensemble Method for Minimax Optimal Reinforcement Learning
summary: Optimal Reinforcement Learning (RL) algorithms typically rely on carefully constructed count-based uncertainty estimates to drive exploration. Although theoretically sound, such estimates are hard to compute in practical settings and therefore offer limited insight for designing exploration heuristics. Meanwhile, ensembling has emerged as a practical approach, but remains without theoretical justification. Building on a recent ensemble-based method for Multi-Armed Bandits, we propose a quantile-based ensemble method for finite-horizon Markov Decision Processes (MDPs). Our simple count-free approach achieves optimal variance-dependent regret bounds, providing theoretical grounding for ensemble-based exploration in RL.

AI.tldr: A count-free, quantile-based ensemble method for finite-horizon MDPs achieves variance-dependent regret bounds, giving theoretical grounding to ensemble-driven exploration in RL.
AI.motivation: To overcome the practical difficulty of constructing count-based uncertainty in RL and to justify ensemble methods for exploration with theoretical guarantees.
AI.method: Extend a recent ensemble-based approach from multi-armed bandits to finite-horizon MDPs by introducing a quantile-based, count-free uncertainty estimate that guides exploration; derive variance-dependent regret bounds.
AI.result: The proposed method achieves optimal variance-dependent regret bounds, providing a solid theoretical justification for ensemble-based exploration in RL.
AI.conclusion: The count-free, quantile-based ensemble framework marries practical ensemble exploration with rigorous regret guarantees in finite-horizon RL, suggesting robust exploration strategies and a path toward broader empirical validation.

title: Spectral Query-Key Product Weight Steering for Training-Free VLM Hallucination Mitigation
summary: Vision-language models (VLMs) often generate fluent but visually unsupported descriptions, especially by mentioning objects absent from the image. We propose QK Product Steering, a data-free, training-free, and zero-inference-cost weight edit for reducing object hallucination. The method directly edits the per-head query-key product, the operator that produces pre-softmax attention logits, by suppressing a small number of dominant singular modes in selected middle layers. The edited product is then mapped back to the query weights through a closed-form query-only update while keeping shared key weights fixed, making the edit compatible with grouped-query attention. We further decompose the QK product into symmetric and antisymmetric components to distinguish mutual content-similarity patterns from directional attention patterns. Across three GQA-based VLMs, QK Product Steering achieves an average relative CHAIR$_s$ reduction of $4.0\%$, while matched random-mode controls show negligible change. Interpretability ablations show that the hallucination signal is specific to dominant QK modes and is primarily localized to the symmetric mutual-attention channel. Overall, QK Product Steering offers a simple alternative to decoding-time mitigation, requiring no additional data, fine-tuning, or inference-time overhead while largely preserving general multimodal capability.

AI.tldr: A data-free, training-free method (QK Product Steering) to reduce object hallucination in vision–language models by editing the per-head QK product to suppress dominant singular modes, preserving multimodal capabilities with no extra data or inference cost.
AI.motivation: Address object hallucination in VLMs without requiring fine-tuning, extra data, or decoding-time interventions; leverage internal attention mechanism (QK product) to suppress spuriously dominant modes.
AI.method: Edit the per-head QK product by suppressing a small number of dominant singular modes in selected middle layers; map the edit back to query weights via a closed-form query-only update while keeping key weights fixed; ensure compatibility with grouped-query attention; decompose QK product into symmetric and antisymmetric components to separate content-similarity from directional patterns.
AI.result: Across three GQA-based VLMs, achieved an average relative CHAIR_s reduction of 4.0%, with matched random-mode controls showing negligible change; hallucination signal localized primarily to the symmetric mutual-attention channel; no data, fine-tuning, or inference-time overhead; general multimodal capability largely preserved.
AI.conclusion: QK Product Steering offers a simple, data-free alternative to decoding-time mitigation for hallucinations, balancing mitigation with preservation of multimodal performance.

title: When Calibration Fails the Vulnerable Hospital: Federated Conformal Risk Control via Risk-Curve Shrinkage
comment: 9 pages, 3 figures, 2 tables. Submitted to the DeCaF Workshop at MICCAI 2026
summary: Conformal risk control (CRC) provides distribution-free guarantees on segmentation quality by calibrating a prediction-set threshold on held-out data. In federated deployments, the standard approach pools calibration scores across sites into a single threshold. We provide the first quantification, on real multi-institutional brain tumor data (FeTS-2022, 1,251 subjects, 20 institutions), showing that this naive pooled CRC protects the average hospital but violates coverage at 40% of individual institutions, with the worst site exceeding the target false-negative rate by 7.8 percentage points. The naive alternative, per-site local CRC, largely restores coverage but inflates prediction sets by 83x, rendering them clinically useless. We propose a shrinkage-based federated CRC protocol: each site transmits only its empirical risk curve (G scalars) to a server, which computes a shrinkage-regularized threshold per site. A single hyperparameter n0 smoothly trades worst-case coverage for prediction-set efficiency; leave-one-site-out sensitivity analysis identifies n0=19, achieving 2.7/20 violations at 2.0x stretch. We further show that direct Lagrangian optimization of coverage budgets fails, concentrating risk on vulnerable hospitals, and that the finite-sample correction term is essential: removing it triples violations. The marginal CRC guarantee is preserved by construction under the stated site-mixture assumption; per-site coverage is validated across four targets with three seeds. No patient-level images, masks, or per-volume scores leave any site.

AI.tldr: Federated conformal risk control (CRC) for medical image segmentation: pooling calibration across sites yields coverage violations at many hospitals; per-site CRC preserves site coverage but greatly inflates prediction sets. A shrinkage-based federated CRC uses site empirical risk curves to compute site-specific, shrinkage-regularized thresholds with a tunable hyperparameter n0. This approach preserves marginal CRC while improving per-site coverage, and outperforms naive pooling and local CRC in FeTS-2022 data (1,251 subjects, 20 sites). Lagrangian optimization fails and finite-sample corrections are essential.
AI.motivation: To achieve distribution-free, per-site coverage guarantees in federated medical imaging without sharing sensitive data, addressing heterogeneity across institutions and the shortcomings of naive pooling or fully local calibration.
AI.method: Sites compute empirical risk curves G (scalars) and share them with a central server. The server applies shrinkage-regularized thresholding per site to calibrate CRC thresholds. A single hyperparameter n0 trades worst-case coverage against prediction-set efficiency. Leave-one-site-out analysis identifies a robust n0. Analysis includes comparison against pooled CRC and per-site local CRC, as well as a critique of Lagrangian coverage-budget optimization and the role of finite-sample correction.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on FeTS-2022 (1,251 subjects, 20 institutions) shows pooled CRC protects the average hospital but violates at 40% of institutions; worst site exceeds target FNR by 7.8 percentage points. Per-site CRC restores coverage but inflates prediction sets by 83x. Shrinkage-based federated CRC with n0≈19 achieves 2.7/20 violations at 2.0x stretch. Lagrangian optimization concentrates risk on vulnerable hospitals; removing finite-sample correction triples violations. Marginal CRC guarantees hold under a site-mixture assumption; per-site coverage validated across four targets with three seeds. No patient-level data leaves any site.
AI.conclusion: Shrinkage-based federated CRC provides robust, data-preserving per-site coverage guarantees with efficient prediction sets, addressing cross-site heterogeneity better than pooling or fully local calibration. Finite-sample corrections are essential; Lagrangian approaches are inadequate for preserving site fairness.

title: InfantFace: Detecting infant faces in neonatal clinical environments
comment: 32 pages, 7 figures, 4 tables; supplementary information included
summary: Reliable localisation of the neonatal face is the first step for several video-camera based non-contact assessments such as pain and distress related facial expression analysis, pain scoring, cardiorespiratory signal extraction and cessation of breathing alerts. However, major challenges persist in neonatal clinical environments. Cluttered backgrounds, illumination changes and poor lighting conditions can reduce the accuracy of face detection models. Clinical interventions, monitoring equipment and, in some cases, medical devices can obstruct the face, making visual assessment difficult. We propose a one-stage YOLOv11m-based model tailored for face detection of infants in neonatal clinical environments. We combined multiple publicly available datasets (VGGFace2, CelebA, FDDB, WIDER FACE) to train and evaluate our proposed model. We then fine-tuned our model on a neonatal research dataset involving 228 videos from 114 recording sessions of 113 independent infants. Before fine-tuning, our model achieved an AP50 of 0.87, surpassing the performance of three state-of-the-art general face detectors. Performance improved further to an AP50 of 0.96 after clinical-domain adaptation. Evaluating face detection performance across different datasets remains a challenge due to the lack of publicly available neonatal datasets. Prioritising the creation of such datasets, while upholding appropriate privacy safeguards and ethical standards in their creation and use, would greatly support further progress in this field.

AI.tldr: One-stage YOLOv11m-based neonatal face detector trained on mixed public datasets and fine-tuned on a neonatal video dataset; achieves AP50 improvements from 0.87 to 0.96, highlighting the value of clinical-domain adaptation and the need for neonatal-specific datasets.
AI.motivation: Reliable neonatal face localisation is critical for non-contact assessments (pain, distress, physiological signal extraction) in NICUs, but is challenged by clutter, occlusion, and varied lighting; lack of neonatal datasets hinders progress.
AI.method: A one-stage YOLOv11m detector trained on public face datasets (VGGFace2, CelebA, FDDB, WIDER FACE) and then fine-tuned on a neonatal dataset (228 videos, 114 sessions, 113 infants). Evaluation compared to general face detectors; emphasizes clinical-domain adaptation.
AI.result: AP50 improved from 0.87 (pre-fine-tuning, outperforming three state-of-the-art general detectors) to 0.96 after neonatal-domain fine-tuning; acknowledges challenges in cross-dataset comparisons and the need for neonatal datasets with privacy protections.
AI.conclusion: Clinical-domain adaptation yields substantial gains for neonatal face detection. The field would benefit from standardized neonatal face datasets, created with ethical safeguards, to enable reliable evaluation and progress.

title: Multi-Modal Contrastive Learning for Implicit Earth Embeddings via Location Tying
summary: Spatial prediction tasks are often limited by a lack of high-quality labelled ground-truth observations. To overcome this challenge, self-supervised pre-training is a possible solution, with contrastive learning dominant for location encoders. Those approaches usually align geographic coordinates with just one additional modality. We propose two multimodal contrastive learning architectures: Multimodal Embedding via Location Tying (MELT) and Sequential Alternating Location Training (SALT). These architectures expand this framework beyond two modalities by utilising unpaired geospatial data. Both methods are technically viable and match the performance of the strongest two-modality baseline (SATCLIP) across four downstream tasks. However, increasing the number of modalities does not consistently improve performance, suggesting that the chosen location encoder is the main limitation - the contrastive objective reaches its peak early, regardless of modality diversity or pre-training volume. MELT provides more stable training than SALT and presents a stronger foundation for future scaling.

AI.tldr: Multimodal contrastive pretraining MELT and SALT extend location representations to unpaired geospatial data, achieving parity with SATCLIP; more modalities do not reliably improve performance; MELT offers stable training and scaling potential.
AI.motivation: Address the scarcity of high-quality labeled ground-truth data for spatial prediction by leveraging self-supervised, multimodal data; evaluate whether adding more modalities improves contrastive learning and downstream performance.
AI.method: Two architectures, MELT and SALT, implement multimodal contrastive learning beyond two modalities using unpaired geospatial data; assess performance relative to SATCLIP across four downstream tasks.
AI.result: Both architectures are viable and match the strongest two-modality baseline (SATCLIP) across four tasks; increasing the number of modalities yields inconsistent gains; the location encoder is the primary bottleneck; the contrastive objective saturates early regardless of modality count or pre-training volume; MELT exhibits more stable training than SALT and is a better base for scaling.
AI.conclusion: MELT is the more stable and scalable option; adding more modalities alone does not guarantee improvements—focus should shift to the quality of the location encoder and the design of the contrastive objective.

title: PCFootprint: A Large-Scale Dataset and Benchmark for Vectorized Building Footprint Extraction from Aerial LiDAR Point Clouds
summary: Building footprint extraction is a fundamental task in photogrammetry, remote sensing, and computer vision. Recent image-based methods have achieved remarkable progress in extracting vectorized footprints from high-resolution optical imagery. However, optical imagery inherently susceptible to occlusions, perspective distortions, and residual relief displacement, yielding incomplete or misaligned footprint extraction. Furthermore, the lack of explicit elevation information limits its direct applicability to Level of Detail building modeling. In this paper, we present PCFootprint, the first large-scale public dataset for footprint extraction from airborne laser scanning point clouds. PCFootprint comprises \num{33000} tiles derived from the Estonian Land and Spatial Development Board, covering diverse urban and rural landscapes. Each tile spans \qtyproduct{128 x 128}{\m} with systematically aligned vectorized footprints aligned to point clouds. The dataset includes a \num{3000} tiles cross-domain test set for evaluating generalization across geographic regions. We establish comprehensive benchmarks by evaluating mainstream methods. Experimental results reveal significant challenges including high intra-class variance, data imbalance, and noise across complex geospatial environments. We believe PCFootprint will advance future research in building modeling, urban scene understanding, and geospatial analysis. The PCFootprint dataset is publicly available at \url{https://huggingface.co/datasets/Haoyuan-Shen/PCFootprint}.

AI.tldr: First large-scale public dataset for building footprints in airborne LiDAR point clouds (PCFootprint): 33k tiles (128x128 m), aligned footprints, cross-domain test set; baseline benchmarks show data complexity and generalization challenges.
AI.motivation: Fill the resource gap for footprint extraction in 3D LiDAR data, enabling integration with elevation information for Level of Detail (LOD) building modeling and addressing limitations of optical imagery (occlusions, relief displacement).
AI.method: Curated from Estonian Land and Spatial Development Board; produced 33,000 tiles, each with vectorized footprints aligned to ALS point clouds; added 3,000 tiles cross-domain test set; evaluated mainstream methods and established benchmarks.
AI.result: Dataset reveals high intra-class variance, data imbalance, and noise; current methods face significant challenges; dataset is publicly accessible.
AI.conclusion: PCFootprint is expected to advance research in building modeling, urban scene understanding, and geospatial analysis; resources are publicly available.

title: Predicting gestational age at birth in the context of preterm birth from multi-modal fetal MRI
comment: Accepted for publication at the Journal of Machine Learning for Biomedical Imaging (MELBA) https://melba-journal.org/2026:013
summary: Preterm birth is associated with significant mortality and a risk for lifelong morbidity. The complex multifactorial aetiology hampers accurate prediction and thus optimal care. A pipeline consisting of bespoke machine learning methods for data imputation, feature selection, and regression models to predict gestational age (GA) at birth was developed and evaluated from comprehensive multi-modal morphological and functional fetal MRI data from 333 control cases and 93 preterm birth cases. The GA at birth predictions were classified into term and preterm categories and their accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity were reported. An ablation study was performed to further validate the design of the pipeline. Performance was evaluated using stratified 10-fold cross-validation. The pipeline achieves an R2 score of 0.13 and a mean absolute error of 2.74 weeks. It also achieves a 0.77 accuracy, 0.59 sensitivity, and 0.82 specificity across folds. The predominant features selected by the pipeline include cervical length and statistics derived from placental T2* values. The confluence of fast, motion-robust and multi-modal fetal MRI techniques and machine learning prediction allowed the prediction of the gestation at birth. This information is essential for any pregnancy. To the best of our knowledge, preterm birth had only been addressed as a classification problem in the literature. Therefore, this work provides a proof of concept. Future work will increase the cohort size to allow for finer stratification within the preterm birth cohort. Our code is available at https://github.com/dfajardorojas/ml-for-preterm-birth-.

AI.tldr: ML pipeline on multi-modal fetal MRI to predict gestational age at birth and classify term vs preterm. Reported R2=0.13 and MAE=2.74 weeks for regression; accuracy=0.77 with sensitivity=0.59 and specificity=0.82 for classification across stratified 10-fold CV. Ablation study supports design. Cohort: 333 controls, 93 preterm. Code available at GitHub.
AI.motivation: Preterm birth is a leading cause of neonatal morbidity and mortality, with multifactorial etiology that complicates prediction. The study aims to move beyond binary classification to predict the actual gestational age at birth using multi-modal fetal MRI and ML, potentially enabling earlier and more nuanced risk assessment.
AI.method: Developed a bespoke ML pipeline including data imputation, feature selection, and regression models applied to comprehensive multi-modal morphological and functional fetal MRI data. Sample comprises 333 control cases and 93 preterm birth cases. Employed stratified 10-fold cross-validation and conducted an ablation study. Key predictive features include cervical length and statistics derived from placental T2*. The authors provide code at a GitHub repository.
AI.result: Regression: R2 = 0.13; MAE = 2.74 weeks. Classification (term vs preterm): accuracy = 0.77; sensitivity = 0.59; specificity = 0.82. Ablation study corroborates the pipeline design. Feature analysis highlights cervical length and placental T2* statistics as predominant predictors.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates feasibility of predicting gestational age at birth from fast, motion-robust, multi-modal fetal MRI using ML, offering a proof of concept beyond prior literature that largely treats preterm birth as a classification problem. Future work should expand the cohort to enable finer stratification within preterm birth; code is publicly available.

title: Scalable Training of Spatially Grounded 2D Vision-Language Models for Radiology
comment: Accepted for MICCAI 2026. First two authors: equal contribution. Last two authors: equal supervision
summary: We study how to train visually grounded vision-language models (VLMs) for radiology without manual spatial annotations. We introduce RefRad2D, a large-scale bilingual (German/English) dataset of 1.2M CT and MR image-text pairs derived from clinical practice, with task-specific VQA and spatial grounding subsets generated automatically via LLM-based curation and automated segmentation. Trained on this data, our model RadGrounder jointly performs report generation, visual question answering, and spatial grounding via bounding-box detection or segmentation. On external VQA benchmarks (Slake, VQA-RAD), RadGrounder achieves competitive results with specialized medical VLMs. Adding our clinical data to the training mixture improves open-ended VQA over fine-tuning on the downstream datasets alone, showing the transferability of our dataset. Crucially, adding grounding supervision does not degrade language quality, enabling spatially verifiable outputs at no cost to VQA performance.

AI.tldr: RadGrounder: a bilingual radiology vision-language model trained on RefRad2D to jointly generate radiology reports, answer visual questions, and ground findings, without manual spatial annotations; achieves competitive VQA results and maintains language quality while adding spatial grounding at no cost to VQA performance.
AI.motivation: Overcome the scarcity and costs of spatial annotations in medical VLMs by deriving task-specific grounding data automatically via LLM-guided curation and automated segmentation, leveraging a large-scale bilingual clinical dataset to improve radiology language and grounding capabilities.
AI.method: Assemble RefRad2D: 1.2M CT/MR image–text pairs (German/English). Automatically generate VQA and spatial grounding subsets via LLM-based curation and automated segmentation. Train RadGrounder to jointly perform report generation, visual question answering, and spatial grounding (bounding boxes or segmentation).
AI.result: RadGrounder attains competitive results on external VQA benchmarks (SLAKE, VQA-RAD) compared to specialized medical VLMs. Adding clinical data to the training mix improves open-ended VQA beyond downstream fine-tuning alone, indicating good transferability. Grounding supervision does not degrade language quality, enabling spatially verifiable outputs without sacrificing VQA performance.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates the feasibility and transferability of grounding-enabled radiology VLMs trained on large-scale automatic annotations, with multilingual data, providing spatially verifiable outputs at no additional cost to VQA accuracy.

title: Computational Methods and Challenges in Cell-Free DNA Analysis for Multi-Cancer Early Detection
summary: Cell-free DNA (cfDNA) is a promising avenue for non-invasive multicancer early detection (MCED), in that, it can enable multiple cancer detection simultaneously from a single blood draw, with particular sensitivity to cancers that currently lack established screening programs. Here we review the computational methods developed between 2022 and 2025 for cfDNA-based MCED. We focus on how fragmentomics and epigenetic features are extracted and analyzed to detect cancer at early stages. We first briefly outline the biological basis of cfDNA signals, then review classical statistical and machine learning approaches alongside deep learning frameworks including autoencoder-based models. For each method we discuss biological interpretability, validation strategy, and readiness for clinical integration. Furthermore, we categorize the current challenges into technical, computational, and methodological while outlining open problems in the field. This review shows that multimodal ensemble approaches have the strongest promise for clinical integration and the highest readiness. However, for better assessment of future work and side-by-side comparison, standardization of evaluation protocols and reporting results will be crucial.

AI.tldr: A comprehensive review of cfDNA-based multicancer early detection (MCED) methods published 2022–2025, emphasizing fragmentomics and epigenetics; compares classical statistics, ML, and deep learning (including autoencoders); discusses interpretability, validation, and clinical readiness; highlights multimodal ensembles as most promising while stressing need for standardized evaluation and reporting.
AI.motivation: To synthesize recent computational methods for cfDNA MCED, identify which features and modeling approaches are most effective for early cancer detection, and assess readiness for clinical translation.
AI.method: Systematic review of 2022–2025 literature on cfDNA MCED, categorizing methods by features (fragmentomics, epigenetic signals), modeling approaches (classical statistics, ML, deep learning such as autoencoders), and evaluating interpretability, validation strategies, and clinical readiness; discussion of challenges (technical, computational, methodological) and open problems; evaluation of multimodal ensemble approaches.
AI.result: Findings indicate that multimodal ensemble methods offer the strongest potential for clinical integration and higher readiness, but standardization of evaluation protocols and reporting is required for robust cross-study comparison and clinical deployment.
AI.conclusion: The cfDNA MCED field is promising with substantial methodological diversity; to accelerate translation, standardized benchmarking, transparent reporting, and rigorous prospective validation are essential, especially for multimodal and interpretability-enhanced models.

title: How Fragile Are Training-Free AI-Generated Image Detectors? A Controlled Audit of Score Direction, Preprocessing, and Compression
summary: Training-free detectors of AI-generated images promise generator-agnostic deployment without classifier training, yet their reported numbers are rarely compared under a single controlled protocol. We audit two representative training-free scores -- an autoencoder-reconstruction score (AEROBLADE-style) and a noise-perturbation feature-similarity score (RIGID-style) -- plus a naive feature-kNN control, on a common 1,500-image GenImage-derived benchmark spanning seven generators and JPEG compression at quality 70 and 50. The audit yields three cautionary findings. (i) Implementation details masquerade as method differences: replacing the LPIPS backbone (AlexNet -> VGG-16) changes overall AUROC by +0.085, and switching between resize-to-512 and native-resolution preprocessing flips per-generator conclusions by up to 0.38 AUROC. (ii) Score direction is not a property of the method but of its hyperparameters: the RIGID-style score is inverted (AUROC < 0.5) on SD1.5 and Wukong at noise level sigma=0.05, recovers to >0.5 for every generator at sigma=0.01, and collapses to 0.15 at sigma=0.3. (iii) Dataset format bias inflates robustness claims: without unified re-encoding, AUROC under JPEG-50 exceeds the clean condition for the AlexNet-backbone reconstruction score; after bias correction the residual anomaly localizes to a single generator (BigGAN). The audited scores have complementary per-generator failure sets, but naive z-score fusion does not beat the best single score, indicating that exploiting complementarity requires direction-aware combination.

AI.tldr: Across a 1,500-image GenImage benchmark (seven generators; JPEG Q70/50), the study audits two training-free detectors (AEROBLADE-style autoencoder reconstruction and RIGID-style noise-perturbation feature-similarity) plus a naive feature-kNN. They find: (1) implementation details (backbone, preprocessing) masquerade as method differences and can flip conclusions; (2) score direction depends on hyperparameters (RIGID-style inverted at some settings, recovers with others); (3) dataset-encoding bias inflates robustness claims; after correcting bias, robustness is mostly confined to one generator. Per-generator failure sets exist; naive z-score fusion against these methods fails to beat the best single score, indicating complementarity is direction-dependent and not automatically exploitable.
AI.motivation: There is a lack of controlled, protocol-consistent benchmarking for training-free detectors of AI-generated images. The paper aims to dissect robustness and failure modes across generators, preprocessing, encoding, and hyperparameters to distinguish genuine method strength from implementation artifacts and dataset biases.
AI.method: Evaluate two training-free detectors (AEROBLADE-style autoencoder reconstruction; RIGID-style noise-perturbation feature-similarity) and a naive feature-kNN control on a unified 1.5k GenImage-derived benchmark spanning seven generators and JPEG Q70/Q50. Systematically vary: (i) LPIPS backbone (AlexNet vs VGG-16), (ii) input preprocessing (resize-to-512 vs native-resolution), (iii) detector hyperparameters (noise levels sigma; specific datasets SD1.5/Wukong), (iv) dataset re-encoding to remove format bias; compute AUROC per generator; assess z-score fusion.
AI.result: Three cautionary findings: (i) Implementation details masquerade as method differences; changing LPIPS backbone or preprocessing flips per-generator AUROC conclusions; (ii) Score direction is hyperparameter-dependent (RIGID-style AUROC can be <0.5 at certain sigmas and >0.5 at others; extreme values 0.01–0.3 affect results); (iii) Dataset format bias inflates robustness claims (JPEG-50 performance for AlexNet-based reconstruction; bias-corrected results isolate anomaly to BigGAN). Per-generator failures are complementary but naive fusion (z-score) does not beat the best single score; direction-aware combination is needed to exploit complementarity.
AI.conclusion: Robust evaluation of training-free detectors requires controlling preprocessing, encoding, and hyperparameters; reported gains are fragile to artifacts and biases. A direction-aware fusion strategy is necessary to leverage complementary per-generator failures, and a unified benchmark protocol is essential to avoid overstating robustness.

title: Effective Dimension Governs Generalization in Quantum Kernel Vision Models
summary: Recent quantum vision models-quantum vision transformers and quantum convolutional networks-report two striking but unexplained empirical phenomena: (i) ansatze with more, or more uniformly distributed, entanglement generalize better, and (ii) injecting quantum noise can improve test accuracy rather than degrade it. These observations are currently treated as curiosities, discovered by grid search and explained, if at all, by hand. We show that both are manifestations of a single, measurable quantity: the \emph{effective dimension} $d_{\rm eff}$ of the (noise-shaped) quantum feature kernel. Working primarily with quantum-kernel vision models-a quantum feature map read out by a kernel classifier-we give a spectral account in which entanglement structure and quantum noise are two knobs that move $d_{\rm eff}$; in an overfitting regime, contracting $d_{\rm eff}$ acts as ridge-like regularization. We analyze the mechanism: an \emph{exact} decomposition of the depolarized kernel $K_p=(1-p)^2K+\tfrac{p(2-p)}{D}\mathbf{1}\mathbf{1}^\top$ with $d_{\rm eff}(K_p)\to1$, a contraction result (and its boundary) for amplitude damping, a kernel-machine capacity bound, and a capacity/alignment risk decomposition; the monotone contraction operative in our entangled experiments is verified empirically, not proven in general. Along the one-parameter depolarizing family the collapse is instead exact by construction; we use it only to confirm the kernel decomposition to machine precision and at up to $12$ qubits, not as evidence for $d_{\rm eff}$. Amplitude damping contracts $d_{\rm eff}$ and lifts test accuracy by up to $+13\%$ along an inverted-U sweet spot; the effect's sign flips between the over- and under-fitting regimes; noise injection matches an explicit spectral-filtering frontier. Our results organize two reported anecdotes into a single measurable principle for designing quantum-vision models.

AI.tldr: A single spectral quantity, the effective dimension d_eff of the quantum feature kernel, unifies two puzzling observations in quantum-vision models: (i) more or more uniformly distributed entanglement improves generalization, and (ii) injecting quantum noise can boost test accuracy. Through an exact kernel decomposition under depolarizing noise and partial results for amplitude damping, the authors show how entanglement structure and noise move d_eff, acting as ridge-like regularization in overfitting; they verify monotone contraction empirically up to 12 qubits and report up to ~13% accuracy gains in inverted-U regimes. This provides a design principle for building quantum-vision models by spectral control of the feature kernel.
AI.motivation: To explain two previously anecdotal phenomena in quantum-vision models—generalization linked to entanglement distribution and accuracy gains from noise—by identifying a single, measurable spectral quantity that governs behavior across models.
AI.method: Spectral analysis of quantum-kernel vision models (kernel classifiers on quantum feature maps). Derivation of an exact decomposition for the depolarized kernel K_p = (1-p)^2 K + [p(2-p)/D] 11^T, study of the d_eff(K_p) contraction to 1, exploration of amplitude-damping effects, derivation of a kernel-machine capacity bound, and a capacity/alignment-risk decomposition. Empirical verification of monotone contraction in entangled experiments; experiments extended to up to 12 qubits.
AI.result: d_eff captures the observed phenomena: depolarization shrinks d_eff toward 1; amplitude damping contracts d_eff and can lift test accuracy by up to 13% at an inverted-U sweet spot; noise injection acts as spectral-filtering that aligns with the predicted frontier. The depolarizing case yields an exact, construction-based collapse and a precise kernel decomposition, validated to machine precision up to 12 qubits. Overall, two anecdotes are unified under the d_eff framework and ridge-like regularization in overfitting regimes.
AI.conclusion: The reported anecdotes about entanglement-driven generalization and noise-induced accuracy improvements are manifestations of a single principle: controlling the effective dimension d_eff of the quantum feature kernel through entanglement structure and noise. This provides a practical design guideline for quantum-vision models: engineer the kernel spectrum to achieve desirable d_eff (neither too large nor too small) to balance generalization and learning capacity.

title: FreeStyle: Free Control of Style-Content Dual-Reference Generation from Community LoRA Mining
summary: Style-content dual-reference generation aims to synthesize an image that preserves the structure and semantics of a content reference while adopting the style of a separate style reference.Despite recent progress, this setting remains challenging because models must balance content fidelity, style alignment, and instruction following avoiding semantic leakage from the style reference.A key bottleneck is the lack of large-scale triplet data with clean content-style separation and broad long-tail style coverage.In this work, we propose FreeStyle, a scalable dual-reference generation framework based on community LoRA mining.We treat community LoRAs as compositional anchors for style and content, and design a rigorous generation and filtering pipeline to construct large-scale Style-Reference and Content-Reference triplets across multiple base models.To address content leakage, we adopt a two-stage curriculum with stage-specific disentanglement mechanisms: an attention-level enrichment constraint that suppresses style-reference leakage in the style-transfer stage, and a frequency-aware RoPE modulation strategy that targets positional-correspondence-based leakage in the harder dual-reference stage.We also introduce a benchmark covering both style-reference and dual-reference generation, with evaluations on style similarity, content preservation, aesthetics, instruction following, and leakage rejection. The benchmark incorporates a style-invariant Content Alignment Score (CAS) and introduces a calibrated VLM-based Rejection Score for evaluating generation reliability and leakage suppression.Extensive experiments show that our model achieves a strong balance among style alignment, content preservation, and leakage suppression.

AI.tldr: FreeStyle proposes scalable dual-reference generation using community LoRA mining to construct large-scale Style-Reference and Content-Reference triplets, with two-stage disentanglement (attention enrichment and frequency-aware RoPE modulation) to reduce leakage; includes a new CAS and VLM-based Rejection Score benchmark; shows balanced style alignment, content preservation, and leakage suppression.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of large-scale, clean content-style triplets and broad long-tail style coverage; prevent semantic leakage from the style reference; enable robust dual-reference generation.
AI.method: Utilizes community LoRAs as compositional anchors for style and content; a generation and filtering pipeline to construct Style-Reference and Content-Reference triplets across multiple base models; two-stage curriculum with stage-specific disentanglement: (1) attention-level enrichment constraint to suppress style-reference leakage in the style-transfer stage; (2) frequency-aware RoPE modulation to target positional-correspondence-based leakage in the harder dual-reference stage.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show a strong balance among style alignment, content preservation, and leakage suppression; introduces a benchmark covering style-reference and dual-reference generation, with Style similarity, Content preservation, aesthetics, instruction following, and leakage rejection; a style-invariant Content Alignment Score (CAS) and a calibrated VLM-based Rejection Score for reliability and leakage evaluation.
AI.conclusion: FreeStyle demonstrates scalable, effective dual-reference generation with reduced leakage and broad style coverage; provides a practical benchmark for evaluating style-content dual-reference tasks.

title: Learner-based Concept Drift Detection: Analysis and Evaluation
summary: Machine learning algorithms deployed for evolving streaming environments must handle the non-stationary data distributions, commonly referred to as concept drift. The presence of concept drift poses a major challenge for many real-world applications because it can severely degrade their predictive performance, hindering their ability to support robust decision-making. Consequently, the timely and efficient detection of drift events is critical for sustaining high accuracy over time. This study examines theoretically the concept drift characteristics and numerous drift detection algorithms across several categories. Furthermore, we evaluate their performance on both synthetic and real-world datasets exhibiting diverse streaming scenarios and drift characteristics, such as abrupt and gradual changes. This study aims to enhance understanding of the complex notion of concept drift characteristics and behavior of drift detectors, along with their applicability to diverse contexts.

AI.tldr: Comprehensive theoretical and empirical analysis of concept drift and drift detectors in streaming ML, comparing detectors across categories on synthetic and real data with abrupt and gradual drift to guide applicability and detector selection.
AI.motivation: Handle non-stationary data in streaming environments; drift can degrade predictive performance and decision-making, making timely drift detection critical.
AI.method: Theoretical examination of drift characteristics; survey and categorize drift detection algorithms; empirical evaluation on synthetic and real-world streams exhibiting diverse drift types (abrupt and gradual); analysis of detector behavior and applicability.
AI.result: The abstract indicates a dual approach: a theoretical characterization of concept drift and a broad empirical evaluation of drift detectors across categories, using varied datasets to assess performance and applicability.
AI.conclusion: Aims to deepen understanding of concept drift and detector behavior, guiding the selection and deployment of drift detectors across diverse streaming contexts.

title: S-Agent: Spatial Tool-Use Elicits Reasoning for Spatial Intelligence
summary: Real-world spatial intelligence requires reasoning over a continuous and evolving 3D world, yet existing VLMs and tool-augmented agents largely remain tied to static, stateless inference from isolated visual observations. We introduce \textbf{\textsc{S-Agent}}, a spatial tool-use agentic paradigm for understanding and reasoning over continuous multi-view images and videos. By formulating spatial reasoning as spatio-temporal evidence accumulation rather than isolated frame-level prediction, \textsc{S-Agent} reshapes spatial perception into scene-centric understanding beyond frame-centric recognition. Specifically, \textsc{S-Agent} casts the VLM as a semantic planner that decides what evidence is needed, while a hierarchy of spatial tools and experts grounds objects in 2D, lifts them into 3D geometric evidence, and aggregates this evidence into high-level spatial knowledge (\textit{e.g.}, counting, measurement, orientation, and relative position). Additionally, a temporal memory mechanism, including Scene Memory for maintaining the evolving scene state and Agent Memory for accumulating reasoning context, enables evidence integration across frames and reasoning steps. Comprehensive experiments on multi-view and video spatial reasoning benchmarks show that \textsc{S-Agent} consistently improves both open-source and closed-source VLMs in a training-free manner. Beyond inference-time augmentation, supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on \textsc{S-Agent}-generated spatial trajectories \textsc{S-300K} yields \textsc{S-Agent-8B}, a compact spatial agent that significantly surpasses similar-scale baselines (e.g., Qwen3-VL-8B) and performs comparably to advanced closed-source models (e.g., GPT-5.4 and Gemini 3).

AI.tldr: S-Agent is a spatio-temporal, tool-augmented agent that reason over continuous multi-view scenes, turning VLMs into semantic planners and grounding evidence with a hierarchy of spatial tools, memory, and 3D reasoning to achieve training-free gains and a compact, strong agent after SFT.
AI.motivation: Real-world spatial intelligence requires integrating information over time and from multiple views in a continuous 3D world. Existing VLMs are mostly static and frame-centric, limiting scene-centric understanding and long-horizon reasoning.
AI.method: Introduce S-Agent, a spatial tool-use paradigm where a VLM acts as a semantic planner to decide needed evidence. A hierarchy of spatial tools and experts ground 2D observations to 3D geometric evidence, which is accumulated into high-level spatial knowledge (counting, measurement, orientation, relative position). A temporal memory system (Scene Memory and Agent Memory) enables cross-frame evidence integration. Evaluation on multi-view/video spatial reasoning benchmarks shows training-free gains; SFT on S-Agent-generated spatial trajectories (S-300K) produces S-Agent-8B, a compact agent surpassing similar-scale baselines and rivaling advanced closed-source models.
AI.result: S-Agent yields consistent, training-free improvements for both open- and closed-source VLMs on multi-view and video spatial reasoning tasks. After SFT, S-Agent-8B significantly outperforms similar-scale baselines (e.g., Qwen3-VL-8B) and is competitive with larger closed-source models (GPT-5.4, Gemini 3).
AI.conclusion: Spatial reasoning benefits from spatio-temporal evidence accumulation and tool-based grounding, enabling scene-centric understanding beyond frame-level recognition. S-Agent demonstrates a scalable path to stronger, compact spatial agents via memory and evidence integration, with strong performance both in zero-shot and after SFT.

title: Boundary Embedding Shaping with Adaptive Contrastive Learning for Graph Structural Disentanglement
summary: Graph neural networks (GNNs) excel at aggregating neighbor information for classification, yet their performance is hindered by graph structural entanglement, where spurious correlations from semantically irrelevant neighbors contaminate node embeddings. This challenge is most acute for nodes near class boundaries in the embedding space, where amplified structural noise blurs decision boundaries and destabilizes predictions. Existing robust GNN methods largely treat all nodes uniformly, ignoring boundary vulnerabilities. In this paper, to improve classification performance, we tackle graph structural disentanglement by identifying boundary-region entanglement as the primary bottleneck and propose Boundary Embedding Shaping (BES), an adaptive contrastive learning GNN plug-in module that selectively suppresses spurious structural noise at decision boundaries with minimal model parameter perturbation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that BES consistently improves boundary discrimination and outperforms existing leading methods. Notably, BES boosts GCN performance by an average of 3.3% in node classification (up to 5.0% on WikiCS) and achieves superior accuracy in link prediction.

AI.tldr: BES is a boundary-focused adaptive contrastive learning plug-in for GNNs that suppresses boundary-region structural noise, improving node classification and link prediction with modest parameter changes.
AI.motivation: Graph structural entanglement causes spurious correlations from irrelevant neighbors to contaminate node embeddings, especially near class boundaries, degrading decision boundaries and predictions. Existing methods treat all nodes uniformly and do not address boundary vulnerabilities.
AI.method: Introduce Boundary Embedding Shaping (BES), an adaptive contrastive learning module that selectively suppresses spurious structural noise at decision boundaries. BES is a lightweight plug-in that can be integrated with standard GNNs to reweight or contrast embeddings near boundaries, reducing boundary-region entanglement with minimal parameter perturbation.
AI.result: BES consistently improves boundary discrimination and outperforms existing methods, achieving +3.3% average improvement in node classification (up to +5.0% on WikiCS) and strong gains in link prediction.
AI.conclusion: Focusing on boundary-region entanglement with an adaptive, minimally perturbative contrastive plug-in yields robust performance gains in GNNs for both node classification and link prediction.

title: HumanScale: Egocentric Human Video Can Outperform Real-Robot Data for Embodied Pretraining
summary: Embodied foundation models are expected to benefit from data scaling like large language models, but face a much tighter data bottleneck. Teleoperated real-robot trajectories remain the dominant pretraining source due to their precise action supervision and embodiment alignment, yet their scalability is limited by high collection cost, acquisition difficulty, and low behavioral and environmental diversity. These limitations have sparked interest in egocentric human video as a scalable, substantially lower-cost, and more diverse alternative for embodied model pretraining. However, its effectiveness compared to teleoperated real-robot data remains underexplored. To address this question, we conduct a systematic study comparing egocentric human video and teleoperated real-robot trajectories as pretraining data sources for embodied foundation models, under fixed post-training and validation protocols. Surprisingly, we find that egocentric data, when processed through a carefully designed filtering and labeling pipeline, is not merely a viable substitute for model pretraining but can lead to superior performance. With the same amount of pretraining data, models pretrained on egocentric data achieve a 24% lower validation loss on real-robot action prediction, as well as 52.5% and 90% higher success rates on in-distribution and out-of-distribution real-robot task execution, respectively. This finding verifies a scalable paradigm for embodied foundation models: pretrain on egocentric human video to learn diverse world representations, then adapt with a small amount of labeled real-robot data for action-space alignment. We hope this study encourages broader exploration of egocentric data and offers guidance for data quality assessment before costly robot data collection.

AI.tldr: Egocentric human video, with a carefully designed filtering and labeling pipeline, can outperform teleoperation data for pretraining embodied foundation models; with equal pretraining data, egocentric data yields 24% lower validation loss for real-robot action prediction and substantially higher task success rates (52.5% in-distribution, 90% out-of-distribution).
AI.motivation: Embodied foundation models face a data bottleneck: teleoperated real-robot trajectories offer precise supervision but are costly and diverse limits; egocentric video is scalable and diverse. The paper investigates whether egocentric data can match or exceed teleop data for pretraining under fixed post-training/validation protocols.
AI.method: Systematic comparison of egocentric human video and teleoperated real-robot trajectories as pretraining data sources for embodied foundation models, with a fixed training/validation regime. Egocentric data are processed through a carefully designed filtering and labeling pipeline to align with the robot action space. Evaluation uses real-robot action prediction and task execution, with the same amount of pretraining data.
AI.result: With the same data amount, models pretrained on egocentric data achieve a 24% lower validation loss for real-robot action prediction, and 52.5% and 90% higher success rates on in-distribution and out-of-distribution real-robot task execution, respectively.
AI.conclusion: Pretraining on egocentric videos, followed by adaptation with a small amount of labeled real-robot data for action-space alignment, offers a scalable paradigm for embodied foundation models. The study advocates broader exploration of egocentric data and emphasizes data quality assessment prior to costly robot data collection.

title: Integrating national forest inventory, airborne lidar, and satellite imagery for wall-to-wall mapping of forest structure with computer vision
summary: Remote sensing is increasingly relied upon to deliver actionable science for forest and wildfire risk management across large landscapes. Wall-to-wall, annually updated maps are a persistent need for effective forest management. Many planning systems and data collections combine disparate data sources with different purposes, vintages, and prediction quality, which leads to confounding behavior in operational planning systems. We introduce the VibrantForests framework, developed and applied to map forest attributes and provide a coherent foundation for effective forest and wildfire planning. VibrantForests includes a satellite-based forest structure model trained on lidar-derived samples and applied across the contiguous United States to concurrently generate estimates of canopy cover, canopy height, aboveground live tree biomass, basal area, and quadratic mean diameter at 10-meter resolution. We demonstrate predictive capability spanning the full spectrum of forest conditions ranging from sparse-canopy/low-biomass to dense-canopy/high-biomass. Results show that our model extends the range at which saturation is commonly encountered in comparable passive-sensor models, and reduces regression-to-mean behavior that commonly produces overestimation of forest attributes in small/sparse conditions and underestimation in large/dense conditions. The VibrantForests framework addresses a key limitation in large-area forest and wildfire planning by delivering coherent wall-to-wall estimates of management-relevant attributes at annual cadence and 10m resolution.

AI.tldr: VibrantForests provides wall-to-wall, 10 m maps of key forest attributes across the contiguous US using a satellite-based model trained on lidar samples, extending predictive range and reducing bias; enabling annual nationwide forest/wildfire planning.
AI.motivation: Need for consistent, up-to-date, high-resolution forest attribute maps across large landscapes; disparate data sources harm planning; existing passive-sensor models saturate and misestimate in sparse/dense conditions.
AI.method: Train a satellite-based forest structure model on lidar-derived samples; apply nationally to estimate canopy cover, canopy height, aboveground live tree biomass, basal area, and quadratic mean diameter at 10 m; enable concurrent estimates at annual cadence.
AI.result: Model demonstrates predictive capability across sparse to dense canopies; extends saturation range; mitigates regression-to-mean bias; provides coherent wall-to-wall estimates at 10 m annually.
AI.conclusion: Addresses a key limitation in large-area forest and wildfire planning by delivering coherent, annual, 10 m-resolution forest attribute maps.

title: SARLO-80: Worldwide Slant SAR Language Optic Dataset 80cm
summary: Multimodal foundation models have advanced rapidly thanks to large optical benchmarks, but comparable resources for synthetic aperture radar (SAR) remain limited. Existing SAR--optical datasets largely rely on low-resolution, intensity-only Ground Range Detected~(GRD) products and do not preserve complex-valued SAR measurements or native acquisition geometry, which restricts physically grounded multimodal learning. In particular, large-scale public datasets combining very-high-resolution (VHR) SAR SLC, aligned optical imagery, and natural-language descriptions are still lacking. We present a VHR SAR--optical--text dataset built from open-access Umbra spotlight acquisitions distributed as Sensor Independent Complex Data (SICD). From around 2,500 worldwide scenes (VV/HH, 20cm--2m native resolution), we standardize all SAR data to an 80cm slant-range grid via band-limited FFT resampling and tile the imagery into 1024 by 1024 patches. For each SAR patch, we retrieve a high-resolution optical tile and warp it into the SAR grid using local coordinate correspondences for local pixel-level alignment. We further generate three caption variants (SHORT/MID/LONG) per sample to support vision--language training and evaluation. Our dataset contains 119,566 triplets (complex and amplitude slant-range SAR patch, aligned optical patch, natural-language description) covering 257 locations across 72 countries and a broad range of land types and infrastructures. We release fixed train/validation/test splits and the full preprocessing and baseline code to enable reproducible benchmarks for multimodal alignment on cross-modal retrieval and conditional generation in native SAR geometry. The dataset is publicly available on the Hugging Face Hub at https://huggingface.co/datasets/ONERA/SARLO-80.

AI.tldr: A large, high-resolution SAR–optical–text dataset (SARLO-80) built from Umbra SICD, aligning complex-valued SAR with optical imagery on an 80 cm slant-range grid, with 119,566 triplets and three caption variants; released with train/val/test splits and baseline code to enable cross-modal learning in native SAR geometry.
AI.motivation: SAR multimodal learning resources are scarce, especially for complex-valued data and native acquisition geometry; existing datasets are low-resolution and largely intensity-only, hindering physically grounded multimodal modeling. The work fills a resource gap with wide geographic coverage.
AI.method: Use Umbra spotlight acquisitions in Sensor Independent Complex Data (SICD); standardize to an 80 cm slant-range grid via band-limited FFT resampling; tile into 1024×1024 patches; retrieve aligned high-resolution optical tiles and warp into the SAR grid using local coordinate correspondences; generate three caption variants (SHORT/MID/LONG) per sample.
AI.result: 119,566 triplets (complex and amplitude slant-range SAR patch, aligned optical patch, natural-language description) from 257 locations across 72 countries; 2,500+ scenes (VV/HH, 20 cm–2 m native resolution); fixed train/validation/test splits; preprocessing and baseline code publicly available; dataset on Hugging Face Hub at HuggingFace ONERA/SARLO-80.
AI.conclusion: The dataset enables reproducible benchmarks for multimodal alignment on cross-modal retrieval and conditional generation in native SAR geometry, expanding resources for multimodal learning with SAR data and broad land-cover/infrastructure coverage.

title: Shifting-based Optimizable Linear Relaxations for General Activation Functions
summary: The use of neural networks (NNs) is rapidly increasing, including in safety- and security-critical domains. To provide formal guarantees about NN behavior, many verification methods rely on optimizable linear relaxations of activation functions. However, existing techniques depend on hand-crafted relaxations for each activation function. Extension to state-of-the-art activation functions therefore requires substantial manual effort. In contrast, our approach SLiR (Shifting-based Linear Relaxations) is broadly applicable, requiring only a Lipschitz constant or a set of critical points. SLiR parameterizes relaxations by their slope and computes the corresponding offset via a shifting procedure that ensures sound upper and lower bounds over the input domain, enabling efficient optimization while maintaining correctness. Our experiments show that SLiR produces tight relaxations across a wide range of practical activation functions and enables verification of up to 7.8x more properties compared to state-of-the-art methods.

AI.tldr: SLiR (Shifting-based Linear Relaxations) provides a general, sound method to construct linear relaxations for activation functions used in neural networks by parameterizing with slope and shifting to set offsets, requiring only a Lipschitz constant or critical points.
AI.motivation: To enable formal verification of neural networks with a wide variety of activation functions without needing hand-crafted relaxations for each function, addressing the manual burden and limitations of existing methods.
AI.method: SLiR parameterizes linear relaxations by slope and determines the offset via a shifting procedure to guarantee sound upper and lower bounds over the input domain; the approach relies on a Lipschitz constant or a set of critical points and is designed for efficient optimization.
AI.result: Produces tight relaxations across many activation functions and enables verification of up to 7.8x more properties than state-of-the-art methods.
AI.conclusion: A general, scalable relaxation framework that reduces manual effort, broadens applicability to diverse activations, and improves verification efficacy.

title: VisDom: Sparse Novel View Synthesis with Visible Domain Constraint
summary: Sparse novel view synthesis (NVS) remains challenging due to the ambiguity of recovering 3D geometry from few input views. While NeRF- and Gaussian Splatting (GS)-based methods perform well with dense supervision, they often overfit in sparse settings, producing floating artifacts and inconsistent geometry. Silhouette consistency is commonly used as a regularizer, but it remains insufficient, as silhouette-consistent regions can extend beyond the true object geometry. We introduce VisDom, a learning-free geometric constraint that augments classical carving-based visual hull reconstruction by enforcing a minimum multi-view visibility requirement. Specifically, we define a visible domain as the subset of 3D space observed by at least $K$ views and use it as an additional filtering criterion on top of standard silhouette-based reconstruction. This provides a stronger spatial prior in sparse-view settings. We integrate VisDom into both implicit (NeRF) and explicit (GS) pipelines by restricting volumetric sampling and guiding Gaussian placement during optimization. Experiments on three challenging datasets show consistent improvements in sparse-view NVS, enabling high-quality object-centric reconstruction from as few as four input images. Our method is domain-agnostic, requires only silhouettes, and introduces no learned parameters, making it a simple complement to existing approaches. Applying VisDom on top of GaussianObject further improves performance on Omni3D and MipNeRF360, while matching or surpassing it at 22 $\times$ lower training cost.

AI.tldr: VisDom is a learning-free geometric constraint that strengthens sparse-view 3D reconstruction by enforcing a minimum multi-view visibility (K) to define a visible domain, augmenting silhouette-based carving without learned parameters and compatible with both NeRF and Gaussian Splatting; it yields consistent improvements and lower training cost.
AI.motivation: Sparse-view NVS suffers from severe geometric ambiguity and overfitting when using NeRF/GS; silhouette regularization alone is insufficient as silhouette-consistent regions can extend beyond the true object geometry.
AI.method: Define a visible domain as the region observed by at least K views. Use this as an additional filter on top of silhouette-based reconstruction to impose a stronger spatial prior. Integrate VisDom by restricting volumetric sampling in implicit NeRF pipelines and guiding Gaussian placement in explicit GS pipelines. No learned parameters; domain-agnostic and only requires silhouettes.
AI.result: Experiments on three datasets show consistent improvements in sparse-view NVS, enabling high-quality object-centric reconstruction from as few as four input images. When applied on top of GaussianObject, performance improves on Omni3D and MipNeRF360, achieving comparable or better results at ~22x lower training cost.
AI.conclusion: VisDom provides a simple, learning-free, domain-agnostic complement to existing NeRF/GS pipelines, leveraging multi-view visibility to strengthen geometry priors in sparse data settings and reducing training burden without introducing new parameters.

title: Recurrent neural networks approximate continuous functions
summary: Classical approximation theorems ask for a new neural network whenever the target accuracy is improved. This paper studies the opposite possibility: can the network be chosen once and for all, and can accuracy be bought only by letting it run longer? We prove that this is possible for every continuous function on [-1,1]. More precisely, each such function is uniformly approximated by the time evolution of a single ReLU recurrent neural network with fixed weights and fixed hidden dimension. The mechanism behind the construction is a new intermediate model, the Turing machine with neural units (TMNU). This model retains the algorithmic freedom needed to implement polynomial approximation schemes, while remaining rigid enough to be simulated by RNNs with explicit bounds on hidden dimension and weight magnitude. The resulting convergence rates reflect the underlying polynomial approximation rates. We complement the construction with minimax lower bounds showing that runtime is not merely a proof artifact, but an unavoidable resource in this fixed-network approximation paradigm.

AI.tldr: A fixed ReLU RNN of fixed size can uniformly approximate any continuous function on [-1,1] by increasing runtime, via a TMNU model; convergence rates match polynomial approximation, with minimax lower bounds showing runtime is an unavoidable resource.
AI.motivation: Standard approximation theorems require larger networks for higher accuracy; this work asks whether one can fix the network and gain accuracy solely by running it longer, tying approximation to computation time.
AI.method: Introduce the Turing machine with neural units (TMNU) as an intermediate model; show how it can be simulated by RNNs with explicit bounds on hidden dimension and weight magnitudes; leverage polynomial approximation schemes to construct the fixed-network computation; derive convergence rates.
AI.result: For every continuous function on [-1,1], the single fixed-architecture ReLU RNN's time evolution uniformly approximates it; rates correspond to polynomial approximation rates; provide minimax lower bounds establishing runtime necessity in fixed-network setting.
AI.conclusion: This work demonstrates a time-driven, fixed-network paradigm for universal approximation, provides a concrete computational model (TMNU) bridging algorithmic and neural computation, and shows that runtime is a fundamental resource in fixed-network approximation.

title: Quantum-classical physics-informed Kolmogorov-Arnold networks for PDEs
summary: We develop QCPIKAN, the first quantum-classical physics-informed Kolmogorov-Arnold network designed to solve partial differential equations (PDEs). Built upon Chebyshev-polynomial KAN layers and parameterized quantum circuits, this hybrid framework embeds physical constraints into the training loss to enforce physical consistency. Our theoretical investigations grounded in approximation theory prove that this design accelerates high-frequency error convergence to an exponential rate and effectively mitigates numerical dispersion. We validate the framework across three typical seepage scenarios in porous media, including single-phase flow, component transport and two-phase flow. Compared with existing quantum-classical physics-informed neural networks, QCPIKAN achieves superior performance in global prediction accuracy, local error control, dynamic evolution tracking and displacement front localization. This work provides a robust and efficient alternative for solving complex PDEs.

AI.tldr: QCPIKAN: a quantum-classical physics-informed Kolmogorov–Arnol’d network for PDEs, combining Chebyshev KAN layers with parameterized quantum circuits; shows exponential convergence of high-frequency errors and reduced dispersion; validated on three porous-media seepage problems; outperforms existing quantum-classical PINNs.
AI.motivation: address limitations of PINNs in PDE solving, especially high-frequency error convergence and numerical dispersion; leverage hybrid quantum-classical architecture and Kolmogorov–Arnol’d networks to embed physics and improve accuracy.
AI.method: construct QCPIKAN with Chebyshev-polynomial KAN layers and parameterized quantum circuits; physics-informed loss enforcing PDE constraints; theoretical analysis via approximation theory; empirical validation on single-phase flow, component transport, and two-phase flow in porous media; comparison to existing quantum-classical PINNs.
AI.result: theory shows exponential convergence of high-frequency errors and mitigated dispersion; empirical results show superior global accuracy, local error control, dynamic evolution tracking, and displacement-front localization vs baselines.
AI.conclusion: QCPIKAN offers a robust, efficient quantum-classical alternative for solving complex PDEs, advancing the state-of-the-art in physics-informed quantum-inspired neural networks.

title: CalTennis: Large Multi-View Tennis Video Dataset and Benchmark of Monocular-to-3D Pose Estimation
summary: The Caltech Tennis Dataset (CalTennis) is a large-scale video benchmark for evaluating monocular-to-3D pose estimation in the wild. CalTennis comprises over 11 million frames (51 hours) of tennis practice and match play from 40 players, captured with 2-6 synchronized cameras at 60 Hz. It is 10 times larger than existing in-the-wild human motion video datasets and 3 times larger than existing MOCAP-ground-truthed datasets, and it is the first large-scale benchmark to provide synchronized multi-view recordings of expert athletic motion. The multi-view setup enables inexpensive, label-free evaluation of monocular-to-3D pose estimation algorithms. We describe a simple, standardized protocol that enables data collection without specialized equipment or expertise, along with fully automated video calibration and synchronization. Benchmarking state-of-the-art monocular-to-3D pose methods on CalTennis, we find that while 3D joint angle recovery is now quite accurate, all models struggle to estimate depth and foot contact consistently. We further propose two novel performance metrics, footwork and stability, as well as qualitatively study body shape inconsistency. These metrics expose previously underexplored failure modes and point to concrete opportunities for improvement in pose estimation and action analysis.

AI.tldr: CalTennis is a large-scale, synchronized multi-view benchmark for monocular-to-3D pose estimation in tennis, enabling label-free evaluation and revealing depth and foot-contact challenges; it introduces a simple data-collection protocol, automated calibration, and new metrics (footwork, stability).
AI.motivation: There is a need for a large, real-world athletic motion dataset with synchronized multi-view data to robustly evaluate monocular-to-3D pose estimation, and to uncover failure modes (e.g., depth, foot contact) not captured by existing datasets.
AI.method: Collect ~11M frames (51 hours) from 40 players using 2–6 synchronized cameras at 60 Hz; automated, protocol-driven data collection with no specialized equipment; fully automatic video calibration and synchronization; benchmark state-of-the-art monocular-to-3D pose methods; introduce footwork and stability metrics and study body-shape inconsistency.
