AI.result: CalTennis is 10× larger than existing in-the-wild datasets and 3× larger than MOCAP-ground-truthed datasets; 3D joint angles are fairly accurate, but depth and foot contact are inconsistent; new metrics reveal previously underexplored failure modes and guide improvements in pose estimation and action analysis.
AI.conclusion: CalTennis provides a scalable, label-free evaluation platform for athletic motion capture that exposes concrete weaknesses in current monocular-to-3D methods and points to directions for methodological and metric improvements.

title: Constrained hybrid modelling to predict microbial dynamics and organic matter turnover in soil systems
summary: Soil microorganisms control organic matter cycling and largely determine how soil systems can cope with and mitigate climate change and environmental threats. Representing microbial dynamics in process-based soil models is therefore critical to predict carbon cycling in soils, albeit highly challenging to inform from data. One promising approach to improve their parametrisation is the integration of genomic data, yet modelling the complex and unknown relationship between genomes and the processes the microbes are driving is an unsolved problem. In this work, we present the first hybrid modeling framework for deriving biokinetic parameter values of a process-based soil organic matter turnover model from metagenome-inferred functional traits based on DNA sequencing data. Our model predicts biokinetic parameters of the process-based model from genomic trait data with a neural network and integrates constraints from ecological theory and literature to ensure realistic behavior, even of non-observed state variables. We evaluate our method on synthetic genomic trait datasets of varying complexity and on real data, showing that our approach improves performance over multiple baselines and learns the dynamics of unmeasurable components of the process-based model effectively, even for small training datasets.

AI.tldr: A neural-network-based hybrid framework maps metagenomic traits to biokinetic parameters of a soil organic matter turnover model, improving parameter inference and dynamics predictions, especially for unobserved components and small datasets.
AI.motivation: Soil process-based models require microbial dynamics; genomic data offers mechanistic detail but linking genomes to processes is challenging, hindering effective parameterization.
AI.method: Introduce the first hybrid framework that derives biokinetic parameters from metagenome-inferred functional traits using a neural network, constrained by ecological theory to ensure realistic behavior of observed and unobserved state variables; evaluate on synthetic and real datasets.
AI.result: The approach outperforms multiple baselines and effectively learns the dynamics of unmeasured model components, even with limited training data.
AI.conclusion: Genomics-guided parameterisation within a constrained hybrid framework is feasible and enhances predictive capability of process-based soil OM turnover models.

title: SSD: Spatially Speculative Decoding Accelerates Autoregressive Image Generation
summary: Autoregressive models excel in visual generation by treating images as 1D sequences of discrete tokens, mirroring language modeling. However, this flattening discards the intrinsic 2D spatial locality of visual signals, creating severe computational bottlenecks during inference. We introduce Spatially Speculative Decoding (SSD), a framework that aligns the predictive objective with the natural geometry of images. Rather than predicting only the immediate next token in a 1D sequence, our model simultaneously predicts the adjacent horizontal token and the token directly below it. By capitalizing on this 2D spatial correlation, spatially speculative decoding overcomes the memory wall in visual inference. Our approach accelerates autoregressive image generation by up to 13.3x while maintaining high fidelity on DPG-Bench and GenEval. Our results suggest that respecting the underlying geometry of vision unlocks massive computational efficiencies, paving the way for real-time, high-resolution autoregressive generative models.

AI.tldr: Spatially Speculative Decoding (SSD) accelerates autoregressive image generation by exploiting 2D image geometry: at each step, predict both the adjacent horizontal token and the token below it, leveraging 2D locality to overcome memory bottlenecks, achieving up to 13.3x speedups with maintained fidelity on standard benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Images exhibit 2D spatial locality; standard autoregressive decoding flattens images to 1D sequences, causing inefficiencies and memory bottlenecks during inference. There is a need to align predictive objective with image geometry to enable faster generation.
AI.method: SSD modifies the autoregressive decoding objective to predict two tokens per step: the next token horizontally adjacent and the token directly below the current token, leveraging 2D spatial correlations. This speculative decoding reduces dependency chains and memory traffic, enabling faster inference while preserving fidelity; evaluation on DPG-Bench and GenEval demonstrates speedups.
AI.result: Inference speed improved up to 13.3x compared to traditional autoregressive decoding; fidelity remains high on DPG-Bench and GenEval benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Respecting the inherent 2D geometry of vision can unlock substantial computational efficiencies for autoregressive image generation, enabling real-time, high-resolution models.

title: Critical Percolation as a Synthetic Data Model for Interpretability
comment: 21 pages, 10 figures, accepted to the Mechanistic Interpretability Workshop at ICML 2026
summary: Neural networks learn features that reflect the hierarchical, multi-scale structure of natural data. Synthetic datasets used to evaluate interpretability methods typically lack this structure, limiting their value as realistic toy models. To close this gap, we introduce a family of synthetic datasets consisting of hierarchical functions defined on critical mean-field percolation clusters embedded in a high-dimensional data space. The percolation data consists of sparse, low-dimensional fractal clusters with a power-law size distribution. Latent variables modeling a taxonomic hierarchy generate each data point's target value. The data model is analytically tractable with known critical exponents that fix its properties without requiring hyperparameter tuning. We leverage a mapping between percolation clusters, random trees, and additive coalescence to propose an almost linear-time algorithm to jointly sample a random tree and its hierarchical latent decomposition, enabling data generation at arbitrary scale. Using probing experiments, we find that the model's ground-truth latent variables can be linearly decoded from neural network activations. Together, sparsity, self-similarity, power-law statistics, and analytical tractability make critical percolation a principled testbed for interpretability research.

AI.tldr: Proposes a principled, scalable synthetic dataset family based on critical mean-field percolation clusters with hierarchical latent structure; analytically tractable and suitable for evaluating interpretability methods; latent variables are linearly decodable from NN activations.
AI.motivation: Interpretability benchmarks often lack realistic hierarchical, multi-scale structure; a dataset with known critical exponents and a tractable generative process provides a solid testbed to study explanations.
AI.method: Construct hierarchical functions on critical percolation clusters embedded in high-dimensional space; latent taxonomic hierarchy generates targets; map percolation clusters to random trees and use additive coalescence to sample a hierarchical latent decomposition in almost linear time, enabling scalable data generation; exponents fix properties without hyperparameter tuning.
AI.result: Probing experiments show that ground-truth latent variables can be linearly decoded from neural network activations; data exhibit sparsity, self-similarity, and power-law statistics, with analytical tractability.
AI.conclusion: Critical percolation offers a principled, scalable testbed for interpretability research, combining realistic hierarchical structure with analytic tractability for evaluating methods.

title: Current World Models Lack a Persistent State Core
summary: World models are increasingly regarded as a decisive step toward artificial general intelligence, yet modeling the physical world demands more than rendering convincing frames on demand: it requires an internal world state that keeps evolving over time, decoupled from observation, so that objects endure and events run to their conclusions whether or not a camera is watching, much as the moon holds to its orbit when no one is looking. This requirement is a blind spot of existing benchmarks, which reward surface properties such as fidelity, motion, and camera controllability while never asking whether a generated world keeps evolving once it is unobserved. We introduce \textbf{WRBench}, the first systematic diagnostic benchmark that treats camera motion as an intervention on observability and resolves evaluation into a human-calibrated chain that asks whether the camera executes the requested interaction, whether the scene stays continuous and identifiable while in view, and whether a returning target remains consistent with the event that was set in motion. Across 9{,}600 videos from 23 models spanning four control paradigms, one finding proves stubborn: current systems maintain the observed world as a tracking shot, resuming a returning target in the state at which it was abandoned rather than advancing the event while it went unseen. Because this failure recurs across control paradigms, model families, and increments of scale, robust world-state evolution does not follow from cleaner imagery, tighter control, richer geometric priors, or sheer parameter count We therefore argue that the stability of the physical state kernel and the consistency of worldlines under viewpoint intervention should become first-class objectives of world-model design, so that a world model captures how the world will unfold rather than how the next frame appears.

AI.tldr: WRBench introduces a diagnostic benchmark to test world-models on enduring, evolving world states under camera interventions, revealing that current systems preserve observed state rather than progress events unseen; calls for prioritizing stable world-state evolution over imagery fidelity.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks emphasize surface properties (fidelity, motion, controllability) and camera-facing observations, while real agents require a persistent internal world state that evolves independently of observation to support long-horizon reasoning.
AI.method: A systematic diagnostic benchmark where camera motion is an intervention on observability, with a human-calibrated evaluation chain: (1) the camera follows the requested interaction, (2) the scene remains continuous/identifiable while in view, (3) a returning target remains consistent with the set event. Evaluates 9,600 videos from 23 models across four control paradigms.
AI.result: Across all paradigms and model families, current systems tend to maintain the observed world as a tracking shot, resuming a returning target at the state it was abandoned rather than advancing the event when unseen.
AI.conclusion: Robust world-state evolution does not emerge from better imagery, stricter control, richer priors, or larger parameter counts; world-model design should prioritize a stable physical-state kernel and consistent worldlines under viewpoint intervention to capture how the world unfolds, not merely how the next frame looks.

title: On the Variance of Temporal Difference Learning and its Reduction Using Control Variates
summary: We analyze the variance of temporal difference (TD) learning using the phased setting with tabular representation, and show that one of the mechanisms behind its ability to reduce variance is by effectively aggregating over a larger number of independent trajectories. Based on this insight, we demonstrate that (1) the variance of TD is asymptotically bounded from above by Monte Carlo (MC) estimators, and (2) shorter horizon updates incurs less variance for a fixed number of samples. Beyond TD, we show that Direct Advantage Estimation (DAE), a method for estimating the advantage function, can be seen as a type of regression-adjusted control variate, which achieves a tighter bound on the variance compared to TD in the large-sample limit. Finally, we numerically illustrate the behaviors of these estimators with carefully designed environments.

AI.tldr: TD variance in phased tabular RL is asymptotically bounded by MC; longer trajectory aggregation reduces variance; Direct Advantage Estimation (DAE) acts as a regression-adjusted control variate with tighter variance bounds; experiments corroborate.
AI.motivation: Understanding why TD learning reduces variance and how it compares to Monte Carlo, including how horizon length and variance reduction techniques influence TD performance.
AI.method: Theoretical variance analysis in the phased setting with a tabular representation, deriving asymptotic upper bounds for TD variance relative to MC; analyzing horizon-length effects; reframing DAE as a regression-adjusted control variate; supported by carefully designed numerical experiments.
AI.result: TD variance is asymptotically bounded above by MC estimators; shorter horizon updates reduce variance for a fixed sample budget; DAE provides a tighter variance bound than TD in the large-sample limit; numerical experiments illustrate these variance behaviors in designed environments.
AI.conclusion: Variance reduction in TD arises from effectively aggregating over more independent trajectories; DAE serves as a variance-reduction mechanism via a regression-adjusted control variate, offering improved variance bounds and practical guidance on horizon choice and applying variance-reduction techniques.

title: Thinking in Boxes: 3D Editing in Real Images Made Easy
summary: Text and 2D-conditioning interfaces provide weak, ambiguous control over spatial transformations in image editing -- particularly under large object motions and camera changes. Prior work has used 3D primitives such as boxes, but only as loose conditioning signals indicating approximate object location rather than specifying the transformation. We instead use 3D boxes as structured specifications: the user provides the input and output boxes of the edit, casting editing as a well-posed geometry problem. This ``thinking in boxes'' interface, where each box face is color-coded to convey 3D orientation, gives precise control over translation, rotation, scaling, and viewpoint changes in real images while preserving scene and object identity, and recovering previously unseen object regions. To ground transformations in scene appearance, we introduce a depth-aligned planar floor as a global reference frame, shaded with depth-aware cues. Conditioned on this structure, an image generator produces consistent results under large transformations. Trained in two stages -- on synthetic multi-object scenes and a small set of real-world videos from Objectron -- the system generalizes to complex, in-the-wild real images. Our method operates directly on real photographs and substantially outperforms recent state-of-the-art methods on large 3D edits.

AI.tldr: A 3D-geometry editing framework that uses input/output 3D boxes as explicit transformation signals, with a depth-aligned floor reference, trained in two stages (synthetic scenes and Objectron videos) to realize stable, large 3D edits on real images and outperform current state-of-the-art.
AI.motivation: 2D conditioning and loose 3D cues poorly constrain large object motions and viewpoint changes; a structured 3D specification is needed to make edits well-posed, preserve identity, and allow substantial camera/object changes.
AI.method: Introduce 'thinking in boxes': user provides input and output 3D boxes; faces color-coded to convey orientation; depth-aligned planar floor as a global reference frame; train a generator conditioned on this structure; two-stage training on synthetic multi-object scenes and real-world Objectron videos; operates directly on real photographs and enables large transformations while preserving content.
AI.result: The approach yields consistent results under large transformations, preserves scene/object identity, recovers unseen regions, generalizes to in-the-wild real images, and substantially outperforms recent state-of-the-art methods on large 3D edits.
AI.conclusion: 3D box-based conditioning provides precise, robust control for 3D edits in real photographs, grounding transformations in scene appearance via depth cues and demonstrating strong generalization beyond synthetic data.

title: Train, Retrieve, or Both? A Four-Arm Head-to-Head for Correct Statutory Citation on the Ontario Residential Tenancies Act
summary: Self-represented tenants, landlords, and help-desk staff need to be pointed at the provision of law that actually governs a question, with a correct statutory citation. We study this task on the Ontario Residential Tenancies Act, 2006 (RTA) and its core regulation, asking the operator's question empirically: is fine-tuning enough, or is hybrid retrieval needed? We run a four-arm head-to-head on Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct (base zero-shot, LoRA SFT-only, RAG-only, and an SFT+RAG hybrid), scored on citation exact-match (section+subsection) over a small, human-verification-pending real eval set. The base model cannot cite the RTA and SFT-only mis-recalls sections; retrieval is essential and drives hallucination to zero by construction; and the SFT+RAG hybrid scores highest at 0.481 exact-match with zero hallucinated citations. Its edge comes from SFT making provision selection more robust to the higher-recall candidate sets that hurt zero-shot RAG. Notably, this cheap bge-small hybrid matches or beats a pipeline built on bigger, specialized retrieval models (a larger embedder and a cross-encoder reranker), and a larger/improved training set does not help either: strong statutory-citation performance here does not require specialized retrieval models or more data. The artifact zeroes hallucination and clears the lift-over-base bar but does not reach the aspirational 0.70 exact-match target. All results are on a small, human-verification-pending real eval set and are reported as preliminary.

AI.tldr: Hybrid SFT+RAG approach yields best statutory-citation accuracy for Ontario RTA in a four-arm eval, reducing hallucinations; retrieval is essential; base and SFT-only underperform; larger retrievers and more data do not necessarily help; score 0.481 exact-match; aims but not reach 0.70 target; results preliminary.
AI.motivation: Assess whether fine-tuning alone suffices for legal citation tasks or whether retrieval-augmented approaches are necessary to provide correct statutory citations to legal staff and self-represented tenants.
AI.method: Four-arm head-to-head evaluation on Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct: base zero-shot, LoRA SFT-only, RAG-only, and SFT+RAG hybrid; evaluate exact-match (section+subsection) citations on a small, human-verification-pending real eval set.
AI.result: SFT+RAG hybrid achieves highest exact-match score (0.481) with zero hallucinated citations; base model cannot cite RTA; SFT-only mis-recalls sections; retrieval essential and drives hallucination to zero by construction; hybrid matches/beats a pipeline built on larger retrieval models; larger training data does not improve performance in this task; preliminary results from a small eval set.
AI.conclusion: Retrieval-augmented fine-tuning reduces hallucinations and improves citation accuracy without needing larger retrievers or more data, but the aspirational 0.70 exact-match target is not yet reached; findings are preliminary due to the small eval set.

title: UNIEGO: Proxies as Mediators for Unified Egocentric Video Representation Learning
summary: Egocentric video understanding is inherently limited by the narrow perspective of wearable cameras: a single viewpoint, a single modality, a single model cannot capture the full richness of human action. We argue that a truly expressive egocentric representation must subsume complementary knowledge across viewpoints, modalities, and foundation model representations, yet remain deployable from egocentric video alone. To this end, we introduce a hierarchical multi-teacher distillation framework that produces UNIEGO, a unified egocentric encoder trained with nine teachers spanning ego-exo viewpoints, RGB, depth, and skeleton modalities, and four foundation models. Rather than distilling directly from heterogeneous teachers whose incompatible architectures and feature geometries induce conflicting gradients, our framework interposes a layer of representation-specific Proxy models that translate diverse teacher knowledge into a homogeneous egocentric space. A second distillation stage, Selective Proxy Distillation (SPD), then adaptively selects, for each training sample, the subset of proxies that are both correct and confident, distilling exclusively from reliable supervision and suppressing erroneous signals. SPD is further stabilized by initializing UNIEGO as a learned convex combination of proxy parameters, placing the unified model in a well-conditioned region of the loss landscape before distillation begins. UNIEGO achieves state-of-the-art performance across three egocentric video understanding tasks - action recognition, video retrieval, and action segmentation on three challenging ego-exo benchmarks, outperforming naive multi-teacher distillation baselines and demonstrating that structured, proxy-mediated knowledge transfer yields richer and more discriminative egocentric representations.

AI.tldr: UNIEGO: a hierarchical multi-teacher distillation framework for egocentric video understanding that uses nine teachers across ego-exo viewpoints, RGB/depth/skeleton modalities, and four foundation models; introduces Proxy models to unify heterogeneous knowledge into an egocentric space, followed by Selective Proxy Distillation (SPD) to weed out unreliable signals, initialized as a convex combination for stability; achieves state-of-the-art on action recognition, video retrieval, and action segmentation across three ego-exo benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Egocentric video understanding is limited by single-view, single-modality viewpoints and cannot capture the full spectrum of human action. A unified representation that fuses complementary knowledge from multiple viewpoints, modalities, and foundation models, while remaining deployable from egocentric video alone, is desirable.
AI.method: A hierarchical distillation pipeline: (1) Proxy models translate knowledge from nine heterogeneous teachers (ego/exo viewpoints; RGB, depth, skeleton) and four foundation models into a homogeneous egocentric representation; (2) Selective Proxy Distillation (SPD) adaptively selects the subset of proxies that are correct and confident for each training sample, distilling only reliable supervision; (3) Initialize UNIEGO as a learned convex combination of proxy parameters to stabilize training before distillation.
AI.result: UNIEGO achieves state-of-the-art performance on three egocentric video understanding tasks—action recognition, video retrieval, and action segmentation—across three ego-exo benchmarks, outperforming naive multi-teacher distillation baselines and demonstrating that proxy-mediated knowledge transfer yields richer, more discriminative egocentric representations.
AI.conclusion: Structured, proxy-mediated, hierarchical knowledge transfer enables a richer, robust egocentric encoder that leverages diverse viewpoints, modalities, and foundation-model knowledge while remaining deployable from egocentric video alone; SPD effectively filters unreliable cues and stabilizes learning.

title: Judging to Improve: A De-biased VLM-as-3D-Judge Protocol for Single-Image 3D Generation
summary: A companion study established a de-biased, cross-model VLM-as-3D-judge that reliably ranks single-image-to-3D mesh quality where cheap geometry and CLIP proxies fall short. This paper asks: can that judge's preferences specialize a strong open generator, TRELLIS, on one asset class (furniture), cheaply and without human labels? Taking the judge from ranking to optimization is where the work lives. Pushing a VLM judge into the training and evaluation loop exposes failure modes ranking never triggered, so our contribution is an optimization-grade hardening of the judge: a training judge (Qwen2.5-VL-7B) held distinct from an evaluation judge (InternVL3-8B) to break circularity; position-bias correction; and fixes for three failure modes (image overload, geometry-hiding splat renders, and reference-free judging that rewards clean-but-wrong outputs), with calibration evidence (clear-gap win-rate 0.83-1.0; base-vs-base ~0.5). Using this protocol as an independent evaluator, and working only from public models and data with lightweight parameter-efficient adaptation, we find our methods match the strong base rather than exceed it. Independent base samples carry essentially no learnable preference (0.94 order-flip rate), so signal must be engineered by quality-contrastive construction. Across six adaptation methods, two input regimes, and a severity sweep, the most targeted - conditioner repair under severe degradation - reaches parity (0.50) with the base, while no method clears the >=65% win-rate target. The result is mechanistic: clean inputs saturate the judge, flow-DIT fine-tuning washes out through the sampler, and conditioning repair is the locus that moves geometry. Win-rates are directional at n=8 objects. Matching a strong public-data base with cheap adaptation is itself informative: exceeding it needs more than lightweight PEFT on public data, and the judge protocol is reusable.

AI.tldr: A study probes whether a cross-model VLM-based 3D judge can be specialized to optimize a public asset-class generator (TRELLIS for furniture) with minimal supervision, using separate training/evaluation judges and calibration; results show only parity with a strong base under cheap adaptation, with no method exceeding a 65% win-rate, suggesting that lightweight adaptation on public data is insufficient to exceed the base and that the protocol is reusable for evaluation in similar setups.
AI.motivation: To assess whether the ranking preferences learned by a debiased cross-model VLM can be specialized to optimize a specific asset class (furniture) with cheap, label-free adaptation, and to understand failure modes when a judge is used within optimization.
AI.method: Use a training judge (Qwen2.5-VL-7B) distinct from an evaluation judge (InternVL3-8B) to avoid circularity; apply position-bias correction; fix three failure modes (image overload, geometry-hiding splat renders, and reference-free judging that favors clean-but-wrong outputs); calibrate with evidence (clear-gap win-rate 0.83–1.0; base-vs-base ~0.5); perform optimization using lightweight parameter-efficient adaptation on public models/data; evaluate across six adaptation methods, two input regimes, and a severity sweep.
AI.result: Across adaptations and regimes, the most targeted conditioner repair under severe degradation reaches parity with the base (0.50); no method clears the >=65% win-rate target; independent base samples show essentially no learnable preference (0.94 order-flip rate); calibration indicates signal must be engineered via quality-contrastive construction; win-rates are directional at n=8 objects.
AI.conclusion: Matching a strong public-base with cheap adaptation is informative but insufficient to exceed it; more than lightweight PEFT on public data is needed to beat strong baselines; the judge protocol is reusable for independent evaluation in similar contexts.

title: CRAX: Fast Safe Reinforcement Learning Benchmarking
summary: Safety is a core concern for deploying reinforcement learning (RL) agents in real-world domains such as robotics and autonomous driving. While benchmarks have been central to progress in RL, existing safety benchmarks with high-fidelity 3D physics remain computationally slow, limiting large-scale experimentation and rapid prototyping. To address this gap, we propose CRAX (Constrained RL Accelerated with JAX). Built on top of the MuJoCo XLA (MJX) physics engine with realistic 3D dynamics, CRAX leverages vectorized operations and hardware acceleration, yielding up to ~100x speedups over comparable CPU-based safety benchmarks. The benchmark features six environment suites and three agent-specific tasks, each spanning three difficulty levels. Evaluating six popular safe RL methods shows that no single approach dominates across all tasks, and reveals the trade-offs between performance and safety. We find that curriculum learning across difficulty levels and safety transfer can improve performance over direct training in harder settings.

AI.tldr: CRAX accelerates safe RL benchmarking on MuJoCo XLA using JAX, delivering ~100x speedups; six env suites and three tasks across three difficulties; six safe RL methods show no dominant approach; curriculum learning and safety transfer help.
AI.motivation: High-fidelity, safety-critical RL domains require fast, scalable benchmarks to enable rapid experimentation and prototyping.
AI.method: Develop CRAX on the MJX physics engine with vectorized operations and hardware acceleration; provide six environment suites and three agent-specific tasks across three difficulty levels; evaluate six safe RL methods.
AI.result: Achieves up to ~100x speedups over CPU-based safety benchmarks; reveals trade-offs between performance and safety; curriculum learning across difficulties and safety transfer improve performance in harder settings.
AI.conclusion: CRAX enables rapid prototyping of safe RL, provides insights on method trade-offs, and highlights effective curriculum and transfer strategies; there is no single dominating safe RL method across tasks.

title: JanusMesh: Fast and Zero-Shot 3D Visual Illusion Generation via Cross-Space Denoising
summary: Creating 3D visual illusions, a single 3D mesh that reveals entirely different semantics from various viewing angles, is a fascinating but tough challenge. Existing optimization-based methods are slow and can produce oversaturated colors. In contrast, naive stitching approaches fail to produce geometrically coherent objects. This results in visible unnatural seams and semantic leaks. In this paper, we present a fast and training-free framework for generating text-driven 3D visual illusions. Our approach decouples the generation into two stages. First, we propose a cross-space dual-branch denoising process. This process dynamically decodes 3D latents into voxel space for CLIP-guided orientation alignment and Signed Distance Field (SDF) blending, which ensures seamless geometric fusion. Second, we introduce a view-conditioned texture synthesis module that projects and aggregates view-specific 2D diffusion priors onto the fused geometry. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method generates highly realistic, dual-semantic 3D illusions in just 3-5 minutes. It significantly outperforms existing methods in geometric integrity, semantic recognizability, and efficiency. Project page: https://siang1105.github.io/JanusMesh.github.io/

AI.tldr: A fast, training-free framework (JanusMesh) to create 3D visual illusions with dual semantics from multiple views by decoupling geometry and texture into two stages.
AI.motivation: Existing approaches trade off geometry quality, aesthetics, or speed: optimization-based methods are slow and oversaturated; naive stitching yields visible seams and semantic leaks. A need for a fast, training-free pipeline that preserves geometry and enables view-conditioned semantic variation.
AI.method: Stage 1: cross-space dual-branch denoising that decodes 3D latents into voxel space for CLIP-guided orientation alignment and SDF blending to ensure seamless fusion. Stage 2: view-conditioned texture synthesis projecting and aggregating view-specific 2D diffusion priors onto the fused geometry.
AI.result: Demonstrates highly realistic dual-semantic 3D illusions in 3–5 minutes, with superior geometric integrity, semantic recognizability, and efficiency compared with existing methods.
AI.conclusion: The proposed two-stage, training-free framework offers fast, coherent, dual-semantic 3D illusion generation by separating geometry and texture optimization and leveraging CLIP guidance and diffusion priors.

title: Towards Modality-imbalanced Federated Graph Learning: A Data Synthesis-based Approach
summary: MultiModal Federated Graph Learning (MM-FGL) offers a natural collaborative training paradigm, but its practical deployment is challenged by two granularities of modality imbalance. Client-level imbalance occurs when certain clients lack entire modalities, while node-level imbalance occurs when individual nodes exhibit missing visual or textual attributes. While several relevant studies exist, our investigation reveals that they predominantly target graph-agnostic or centralized scenarios, rendering them difficult to adapt directly. To address these challenges, we formalize modality-imbalanced MM-FGL as an implicit graph-aware latent semantic representation synthesis problem. This paradigm recovers missing modal semantics directly within the representation space, thereby maximizing alignment with the original data's semantic distribution and mitigating the high variance induced by missing modalities. To this end, we propose FedMGS (Federated Modality-aware Graph Synthesis), which integrates three core components. The availability-aware graph encoder prevents missing modalities from contaminating local structural propagation. The prototype-guided latent semantic synthesizer establishes cross-client semantic anchors for unavailable modalities. The reliability-calibrated semantic fusion mechanism regulates the impact of recovered latent representations prior to predictive readout. Extensive experiments on four tasks show that FedMGS consistently outperforms competitive baselines with gains up to 17.41% with best efficiency-performance tradeoff.

AI.tldr: FedMGS tackles modality-imbalanced MM-FGL by synthesizing missing modal semantics in latent space, using a graph-aware encoder, prototype-guided synthesizer, and reliability-calibrated fusion. It yields substantial gains (up to 17.41%) with favorable efficiency-accuracy tradeoffs across four tasks.
AI.motivation: Modality imbalance in multimodal federated graph learning causes high variance and misalignment when clients/nodes lack certain modalities. Existing graph-agnostic or centralized methods are ill-suited for federated graph settings; a graph-aware, latent semantic synthesis approach is needed to recover missing semantics while preserving privacy and efficiency.
AI.method: Introduce FedMGS with three components: (1) availability-aware graph encoder to isolate missing modalities from local propagation; (2) prototype-guided latent semantic synthesizer to create cross-client anchors for unavailable modalities; (3) reliability-calibrated semantic fusion to regulate recovered latent representations before prediction.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on four tasks show FedMGS consistently outperforms competitive baselines, achieving gains up to 17.41% with the best efficiency-performance tradeoff.
AI.conclusion: FedMGS effectively addresses modality-imbalanced MM-FGL by synthesizing latent semantic representations in a privacy-preserving, graph-aware manner, improving predictive performance while maintaining efficiency. The framework offers a robust, scalable solution for real-world deployment and suggests potential applicability to broader multimodal federated learning challenges.

title: The Significance of Style Diversity in Annotation-Free Synthetic Data Generation
summary: Generating high-utility synthetic data for intent classification typically requires human-annotated seed data, which is often unavailable in fast-paced industrial settings. In this paper, we propose a framework for synthetic dialogue generation that works entirely without human-annotated data, relying solely on intent definitions. Our proposed dialogue generation framework utilizes two different types of topic and style attributes to improve data diversity. Also, we propose two novel post-hoc stylization models called Univ and Exam to transform synthetic LLM-generated utterances into more varied, human-like linguistic styles. To enhance data quality, we utilize an LLM-as-a-judge filtering process. Experimental results on both industrial and public datasets demonstrate that the proposed approach achieves up to 93.3% of the performance obtained using human-annotated training data. Crucially, the findings reveal that style diversity is more critical than topic diversity for synthetic data utility, as it prevents models from learning spurious stylistic correlations. Furthermore, the study shows that incorporating style attributes during the generation process is more effective than post-hoc style adaptation.

AI.tldr: Fully automated synthetic data generation for intent classification using only intent definitions; introduces two types of topic/style attributes and two post-hoc stylizers (Univ, Exam); uses LLM-as-judge filtering; yields up to 93.3% of supervised performance; style diversity is key and more impactful than topic diversity; generation-time style control beats post-hoc adaptation.
AI.motivation: In fast-paced industrial contexts, labeled seed data is scarce or unavailable. There is a need to generate high-quality, diverse synthetic data for intent classification without human annotation, while avoiding spurious correlations between style and topic.
AI.method: Proposes a framework that (i) encodes two categories of topic attributes and two categories of style attributes to drive data generation, (ii) uses two post-hoc stylization models (Univ, Exam) to diversify style of LLM-generated utterances, (iii) applies LLM-as-a-judge as a filtering step to ensure data quality, and (iv) evaluates on industrial and public datasets.
AI.result: Achieves up to 93.3% of performance compared to models trained with human-annotated data. Key finding: style diversity is more critical than topic diversity for synthetic data utility, as it prevents learning spurious stylistic correlations. Also, incorporating style attributes during generation is more effective than post-hoc adaptation.
AI.conclusion: A fully annotation-free synthetic data generation pipeline that explicitly models style diversity and leverages LLM-based filtering can approach supervised performance, with style-aware generation outperforming post-hoc stylization and style-diversity being the main driver of data utility.

title: Direct Advantage Estimation for Scalable and Sample-efficient Deep Reinforcement Learning
summary: Direct Advantage Estimation (DAE) has been shown to improve the sample efficiency of deep reinforcement learning algorithms. However, its reliance on full environment observability limits its applicability in realistic settings, and its requirement to model transition probabilities incurs substantial computational overhead for high-dimensional observations. In the present work, we address both limitations. First, we extend the theoretical framework of DAE to partially observable domains with minimal modifications. Second, we reduce its computational complexity by introducing discrete latent dynamics models that efficiently approximate transition probabilities. We evaluate our approach on the Arcade Learning Environment and find that DAE scales effectively with function approximator capacity while retaining high sample efficiency.

AI.tldr: Extends Direct Advantage Estimation to partially observable domains and introduces discrete latent dynamics to reduce complexity; demonstrates maintained sample efficiency on Atari with scalable function approximation.
AI.motivation: DAE currently assumes full observability and incurs high computational cost for transition modeling in high-dimensional observations. The work aims to make DAE applicable to realistic, partially observable settings while reducing computation.
AI.method: Theoretical extension of DAE to partially observable (POMDP-like) domains with minimal modifications, plus introduction of discrete latent dynamics models to efficiently approximate transition probabilities.
AI.result: DAE remains sample-efficient and scales with the capacity of function approximators; evaluated on the Arcade Learning Environment, showing effective performance in Atari benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The approach broadens DAE applicability to realistic, partially observable tasks and reduces computational overhead via discrete latent dynamics, with performance improving as model capacity grows.

title: Pseudo-Feature Padding: A Lightweight Defense Against False Data Injection in Power Grids
summary: Deep Neural Networks DNNs have achieved remarkable accuracy in various tasks including their application in CyberPhysical Systems CPS for detecting False Data Injection Attacks FDIA during critical operations However the unique infrastructure of CPS makes DNNs vulnerable to exploitation by attackers aiming to evade detection Additionally the distinct nature of CPS presents challenges for conventional defense mechanisms against FDIA This paper proposes an innovative defense framework that strengthens DNNs against such attacks by introducing an additional input layer that performs padding in the input samples using pseudofeature values derived from the inputs statistical distribution This padding increases the input dimensionality in a randomized and dataaware manner making adversarial attacks computationally infeasible due to the nontransferable nature of crafted perturbations and the unpredictability of the padded structure Our method is lightweight modelagnostic and requires no modifications to the core architecture making it highly deployable in realworld CPS settings We evaluated our framework on critical power grid applications such as state estimation using the IEEE 14bus 30bus 118bus and 300bus systems Experiments under adversarial settings demonstrate that our padding strategy significantly improves model robustness with negligible impact on performance and effectively mitigates attacks that would otherwise bypass conventional defenses

AI.tldr: A lightweight, model-agnostic defense for CPS DNNs against FDIA by adding a randomized input padding layer that uses pseudofeatures from input statistics, increasing input dimensionality to hinder attack transferability; evaluated on IEEE power-grid benchmarks with robust performance gains.
AI.motivation: FDIA poses a serious threat to DNN-based CPS. CPS infrastructure challenges reduce effectiveness of conventional defenses, necessitating deployable, architecture-agnostic approaches that preserve performance.
AI.method: Introduce an extra input padding layer that augments samples with pseudofeature values derived from the inputs' statistical distribution. Padding is randomized and data-aware, increasing dimensionality without modifying core architecture; lightweight and model-agnostic.
AI.result: On IEEE 14/30/118/300-bus systems, the padding strategy enhances robustness against adversarial attempts under realistic attack scenarios, with negligible degradation in nominal performance.
AI.conclusion: The proposed padding-based defense offers a practical, deployable CPS protection for DNNs against FDIA, scalable to large power-grid benchmarks while preserving core model accuracy.

title: On the Redundancy of Timestep Embeddings in Diffusion Models
summary: Diffusion models rely heavily on explicit timestep embeddings to modulate the denoising process across various noise scales. In this work, we challenge the necessity of these temporal signals by analyzing their impact on U-Net and Diffusion Transformer architectures. Beyond empirical evidence, we provide a theoretical framework demonstrating that, under certain conditions, the global minimizer of the diffusion training objective can be achieved without explicit timestep conditioning. Our findings reveal a surprising robustness when timestep embeddings are completely removed. Extensive ablation studies on the CelebA and CIFAR-10 datasets show that these time-agnostic models can maintain high structural fidelity and even surpass their conditioned counterparts in competitive metrics, including FID, precision, and recall. Our analysis suggests these architectures can implicitly infer noise scales from the corrupted input under specific assumptions, rendering explicit temporal conditioning redundant. This study challenges long-standing temporal conditioning paradigms and paves the way for more efficient and structurally focused generative architectures.

AI.tldr: Timestep embeddings may be unnecessary for diffusion models; time-agnostic architectures can achieve competitive or superior results with a theoretical guarantee for a global minimizer without explicit timestep conditioning.
AI.motivation: Reevaluate the necessity of explicit temporal conditioning to improve efficiency and robustness in diffusion models.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis showing conditions under which the diffusion objective's global minimizer is achieved without timestep conditioning; comprehensive ablations on CelebA and CIFAR-10; evaluation of U-Net and Diffusion Transformer with and without timestep embeddings across FID, precision, and recall.
AI.result: Time-agnostic models maintain high structural fidelity and can even surpass conditioned models on key metrics; architectures can implicitly infer noise scales from corrupted inputs, reducing reliance on explicit temporal signals.
AI.conclusion: Temporal conditioning in diffusion models may be redundant under certain assumptions, enabling simpler, more efficient architectures and guiding future design toward time-agnostic diffusion frameworks.

title: Neural network surrogates with uncertainty quantification for inverse problems in partial differential equations
summary: Inverse problems for differential equations arise throughout science and engineering, where one seeks to infer unknown model parameters from noisy or incomplete observations. Traditional numerical methods for these problems are often computationally expensive, particularly in Bayesian settings where evaluating the likelihood becomes costly for complex forward models and high-dimensional parameter spaces. To address this challenge, we introduce DeepGaLA, a neural-network surrogate for differential equation solvers that provides uncertainty-aware predictions, reducing overconfident inference when training data are limited. To evaluate the fidelity of the surrogate-induced posterior approximations in practice, we show that a short run of delayed-acceptance Markov chain Monte Carlo can serve as an effective diagnostic. Across a range of numerical experiments, DeepGaLA delivers forward-model approximations with accuracy comparable to established Gaussian-process surrogates, while better maintaining efficiency as parameter dimension grows. Moreover, it can incorporate differential-equation constraints, including in nonlinear settings. Overall, these results indicate that uncertainty-quantified neural surrogates can enable scalable and reliable Bayesian inference for inverse problems in complex systems.

AI.tldr: Neural-network surrogate DeepGaLA with uncertainty quantification enables scalable Bayesian inference for inverse problems; uses delayed-acceptance MCMC as diagnostic; competitive with Gaussian-process surrogates and scales better with dimensionality; can enforce differential-equation constraints.
AI.motivation: Computational bottlenecks in Bayesian inverse problems due to expensive forward models and high-dimensional parameter spaces; need uncertainty-aware surrogates to avoid overconfident inferences with limited data.
AI.method: Introduce DeepGaLA as a neural-network surrogate for differential equation solvers with uncertainty quantification; incorporate differential-equation constraints (including nonlinear) into the surrogate; use delayed-acceptance MCMC as a practical diagnostic to evaluate surrogate-induced posterior fidelity; compare performance against Gaussian-process surrogates across numerical experiments.
AI.result: Forward-model approximations by DeepGaLA match GP surrogates in accuracy while maintaining greater efficiency as parameter dimension increases; uncertainty quantification reduces overconfident inferences when training data are limited; the method accommodates DE constraints, enhancing applicability to nonlinear settings.
AI.conclusion: Uncertainty-quantified neural surrogates like DeepGaLA provide scalable and reliable Bayesian inference for inverse problems in complex systems, combining competitive accuracy with better dimensional scalability and the ability to enforce differential-equation constraints.

title: Sparsity, Superposition, and Forgetting: A Mechanistic Study of Representation Retention in Continual Learning
summary: Continual learning (CL) systems often forget previously acquired knowledge, yet the mechanisms driving forgetting remain hard to isolate in practice because real datasets entangle many factors. We present a controlled, toy-world framework that makes these mechanisms observable and testable. Using a synthetic generator-separator pipeline, we define ground-truth latent features, build tasks with tunable sparsity and overlap, and introduce measurable quantities for representation strength and superposition (directional overlap among features). We then study retention dynamics-the temporal change of representation strength by fitting sparse dynamical relations (via SINDy) between retention, superposition, and exposure history. A complementary task-level analysis based on effective rank characterizes how representational capacity is allocated across tasks. Our controlled experiments yield three takeaways. (1) Superposition tends to increase over time with transient dips at task boundaries, suggesting boundary-specific interference rather than steady drift. (2) Higher feature sparsity induces more superposition yet does not inevitably cause forgetting; when representations remain strong, forgetting can be reduced despite overlap. (3) Task-level effective rank grows with sparsity, indicating broader capacity usage under sparse regimes. Together, these results nuance the common intuition that more superposition leads to more forgetting by showing that overlap interacts with representation strength and capacity allocation. Our toy analysis provides falsifiable hypotheses and diagnostic tools for CL.

AI.tldr: A synthetic, controllable continual learning framework reveals how representation strength, sparsity, and feature superposition interact to affect forgetting, challenging the view that more overlap inherently harms retention.
AI.motivation: To isolate and study forgetting mechanisms in continual learning by creating a fully controllable toy-world where ground-truth features, task sparsity/overlap, and representation dynamics can be measured and tested.
AI.method: A synthetic generator-separator pipeline defines ground-truth latent features; tasks with tunable sparsity and overlap; measures representation strength and directional overlap (superposition); retention dynamics are modeled by sparse identification of nonlinear dynamics (SINDy) relating retention, superposition, and exposure history; task-level effective rank characterizes capacity allocation across tasks.
AI.result: Three main findings: (1) Superposition tends to increase over time with transient dips at task boundaries, suggesting boundary-specific interference rather than steady drift. (2) Higher feature sparsity increases superposition but does not inevitably cause forgetting; when representations remain strong, forgetting can be reduced despite overlap. (3) Task-level effective rank grows with sparsity, indicating broader capacity usage under sparse regimes.
AI.conclusion: Overlap interacts with representation strength and capacity allocation; the toy framework yields falsifiable hypotheses and diagnostic tools for continual learning, offering a more nuanced understanding of forgetting beyond the simple overlap-accumulation intuition.

title: Evolutionary Two-Stage Hyperparameter Optimization Strategies for Physics-Informed Neural Networks
comment: Equal advising: Daria Pugacheva and Fedor Ratnikov. Accepted to the ICLR 2026 Workshop on AI and PDEs
summary: Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) solve Partial Differential Equations (PDEs) by embedding physical laws into neural network training. However, their performance suffers from unstable convergence, training plateaus, and strong sensitivity to architectural and optimization hyperparameters due to the highly non-convex and multi-term structure of the physics-informed loss. In this setting, the outer-loop hyperparameter search is a noisy and black-box optimization problem over heterogeneous parameters, where classical local or gradient-based strategies are easily trapped in suboptimal regions. Evolutionary algorithms, with their population-based exploration and ability to handle mixed, non-differentiable search spaces, provide a more robust mechanism for discovering promising configurations. We propose and investigate a two-stage approach based on evolutionary algorithms that combines exploration and exploitation parts of PINNs training to improve solution accuracy and robustness under fixed computational budgets. In the first stage, we perform low-fidelity training runs with truncated epochs to rapidly screen candidate configurations, treating hyperparameter selection as a black-box outer-loop problem. In the second stage, only the most promising candidates are fully trained with standard gradient-based optimizers to refine the solution. Evaluated on three popular problems, namely Advection, Klein-Gordon and Helmholtz equations, our method consistently outperforms standard training and achieves significantly lower mean error within constrained computational resources.

AI.tldr: Two-stage evolutionary search for PINNs hyperparameters improves robustness under fixed budgets by first low-fidelity screening of candidates, then full training of the best ones; tested on Advection, Klein-Gordon, and Helmholtz with superior accuracy.
AI.motivation: PINNs exhibit unstable convergence and strong sensitivity to hyperparameters due to the non-convex, multi-term physics-informed loss. Outer-loop hyperparameter optimization is noisy and black-box; evolutionary algorithms offer robust, population-based search over heterogeneous parameter spaces.
AI.method: A two-stage procedure: (1) low-fidelity stage using truncated epochs to quickly evaluate and screen candidate hyperparameters via an evolutionary algorithm; (2) high-fidelity stage where the most promising candidates are fully trained with gradient-based optimizers to refine solutions; the approach aims to balance exploration and exploitation within a fixed computational budget.
AI.result: The proposed two-stage EA-guided training consistently outperforms standard PINN training, achieving significantly lower mean errors under constrained computational resources across Advection, Klein-Gordon, and Helmholtz problems.
AI.conclusion: A hybrid exploration-exploitation strategy with evolutionary search for the outer-loop hyperparameters enhances PINN robustness and accuracy within fixed budgets, suggesting EAs effective for outer-loop optimization in physics-informed learning.

title: Data Bias Mitigation under Coverage Constraints & The Price of Fairness
summary: Machine learning models have been shown to exhibit discriminatory outcomes or degraded performance for individuals at the intersection of multiple sensitive attributes, such as race and gender. This stems in part from two interrelated challenges: the lack of principled measures for quantifying bias (potentially intersectional), and insufficient representation of intersectional subgroups in training data. We extend a recent bias mitigation framework to incorporate coverage constraints that enforce sufficient representation across groups, including intersectional subgroups. Since achieving exactly zero bias for all groups may not be data efficient (meaning it may require large amounts of data), our solution trades small approximation errors in bias for greater data efficiency while satisfying coverage constraints. We also formulate bias mitigation as an integer linear program that optimizes over all mitigation strategies, and characterize the price of fairness, the minimum data modification cost, as a function of fairness tolerance. This is essential both for legal compliance, where regulations may mandate specific fairness thresholds, and for data governance, enabling practitioners to make informed trade-offs between bias reduction and data modification (particularly, data purchasing) costs. We evaluate our techniques on publicly available datasets, demonstrating that bias mitigation via our framework preserves predictive accuracy across multiple classifiers, and that coverage constraints, while motivated by statistical considerations, are essential for preserving downstream ML performance.

AI.tldr: Extends a bias mitigation framework by adding coverage constraints to ensure representation of intersectional groups (including subgroups) and frames bias mitigation as an integer linear program, balancing fairness with data efficiency. It introduces the price of fairness as a function of tolerance and demonstrates accuracy retention when enforcing coverage constraints on public datasets.
AI.motivation: Address two core issues in intersectional fairness: (1) lack of principled, quantitative measures for intersectional bias, and (2) underrepresentation of intersectional subgroups in training data, which degrade performance and fairness.
AI.method: Extend existing bias mitigation framework with coverage constraints to guarantee sufficient representation across groups, including intersectional subgroups. Accept approximate bias removal for data efficiency. Reformulate mitigation as an integer linear program that searches over all mitigation strategies. Analyze the price of fairness as a function of fairness tolerance. Evaluate on public datasets, comparing accuracy with and without coverage constraints across multiple classifiers.
AI.result: Coverage constraints help preserve downstream performance and maintain predictive accuracy across classifiers. The approach achieves bias reduction with data efficiency, and the ILP formulation enables explicit trade-offs between bias and data modification costs (data purchasing) under regulatory-like fairness thresholds.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating coverage constraints yields practical, data-efficient intersectional bias mitigation that supports legal compliance and governance needs. The framework provides a tunable fairness-accuracy-cost trade-off and demonstrates that coverage constraints are essential for maintaining performance while mitigating bias.

title: Agentic Symbolic Search: Characterizing PDEs Beyond Hand-crafted Expressions, Meshes, and Neural Networks
summary: Mathematicians understand a PDE solution through mathematical structures rather than tables of computed values. Historically, this has been the product of mathematical analysis, carried out by hand for each problem individually. Neither numerical simulation nor neural networks produce those structures directly. We propose Agentic Symbolic Search (ASYS), a prior-guided framework in which an agent translates PDE theory, public problem constraints, and accumulated search experience into testable differentiable symbolic programs. The mathematical forms are refined under evolutionary search, while their continuous parameters are fit by gradient-based optimization. This makes the search an automated form of inductive-bias injection rather than blind symbolic regression. For problems with known analytical forms, ASYS recovers these forms naturally; for other problems, ASYS constructs analytical approximations which can guide mathematicians toward further analysis. In our experiments, across five problems spanning bounded dynamics, finite-time blow-up, and free-boundary focusing, ASYS produces interpretable representations, including a geometric interface formula for Allen-Cahn 2D dynamics and a nine-parameter contraction law for Keller-Segel chemotactic blow-up, in settings where no closed-form description was previously available. ASYS shows the possibility of a new paradigm for characterizing PDE solutions, beyond handcrafted analytical solutions, mesh-based numerical solutions, and neural network approximations.

AI.tldr: ASYS introduces a prior-guided, differentiable symbolic search to derive analytical PDE representations, blending theory, constraints, and search experience. It yields interpretable analytical forms (when known) and approximations (when not) across diverse PDE problems, offering a new paradigm beyond numerical/NN approaches.
AI.motivation: To recover mathematical structures of PDEs, not just numerical solutions, addressing the gap between handcrafted analysis and black-box computation. Numerical simulators and neural nets excel at data fitting but struggle to produce clean, interpretable governing equations. ASYS seeks to automate inductive bias toward symbolic, analytical forms.
AI.method: Agentic Symbolic Search (ASYS): an agent that encodes PDE theory, public constraints, and accumulated search experience into differentiable symbolic programs. The forms are refined by evolutionary search (structure) while continuous parameters are optimized by gradient methods (parameters). This inductive-bias-guided search aims to recover existing analytical forms or construct new analytical approximations.
AI.result: Applied to five PDE problems (bounded dynamics, finite-time blow-up, free-boundary focusing). ASYS yields interpretable representations, including a geometric interface formula for Allen-Cahn 2D dynamics and a nine-parameter contraction law for Keller-Segel blow-up, in cases lacking closed-form descriptions. The framework demonstrates a potential paradigm shift toward automatic discovery of PDE-informed analytical structures rather than purely numerical or NN-based solutions.
AI.conclusion: ASYS shows promise as a new method for PDE solution characterization, enabling interpretable, analytical approximations where closed forms are unknown, and complementing traditional analytic and numerical approaches. It suggests directions for broader applicability, refinement, and integration into mathematical practice.

title: UltraQuant: 4-bit KV Caching for Context-Heavy Agents
summary: Context-heavy agents place unusual pressure on the key-value (KV) cache: long prefixes are reused across many short turns, while concurrency determines whether the serving system can keep GPUs utilized. We study 4-bit KV-cache compression for this setting, using TurboQuant-style rotation and codebook quantization as a quality anchor and vLLM FP8 KV caching as the deployment anchor. We report three contributions. First, we frame 4-bit KV caching around multi-round agent workloads where task quality, cache residency, and serving throughput must be measured jointly. Second, we describe the practical design choices needed to make the 4-bit path robust, including asymmetric K/V treatment, Walsh-Hadamard rotation, QJL removal, and block-scale variants. Third, we present serving optimizations on AMD GPUs, including optimized decode-attention kernels and UltraQuant, an FP4 approximation path that uses FP8 queries, FP4 KV tensors, UE8M0 group scales, and native scaled-MFMA support on CDNA4. On a long-context, multi-turn agentic workload, UltraQuant cuts P50 time-to-first-token by 3.47x in the cache-pressured late rounds (2.3x across all rounds) and raises output throughput by 1.63x over the FP8 KV baseline.

AI.tldr: 4-bit key-value cache compression (UltraQuant) for context-heavy, multi-round agents yields substantial latency and throughput gains on AMD GPUs using rotation, codebook quantization, and FP8/FP4 pathways, achieving ~3.5x P50 speedup in late rounds and ~1.6x throughput improvement over FP8 baseline.
AI.motivation: Context-heavy, long-prefix workloads place heavy pressure on KV caches; efficient caching must balance task quality, cache residency, and serving throughput, motivating compact 4-bit KV representations.
AI.method: Frame 4-bit KV caching around multi-round agent workloads. Implement practical design choices (asymmetric K/V treatment, Walsh-Hadamard rotation, QJL removal, block-scale variants). Deploy on AMD GPUs with optimized decode-attention kernels and UltraQuant FP4/FP8 paths (FP8 queries, FP4 KV, UE8M0 group scales) leveraging native MFMA on CDNA4.
AI.result: UltraQuant reduces P50 time-to-first-token by 3.47x in cache-pressured late rounds (2.3x overall) and increases output throughput by 1.63x over the FP8 KV baseline on long-context, multi-turn workloads.
AI.conclusion: 4-bit KV caching, when paired with careful design (rotation, asymmetric K/V, QJL removal) and GPU-optimized FP4/FP8 paths, can deliver substantial latency reductions and higher throughput in persistent, long-context agent workloads.

title: Marginal Advantage Accumulation for Memory-Driven Agent Self-Evolution
summary: In batch-style trace distillation, the same memory operation may receive contradictory feedback across different batches. Existing methods lack a cross-batch, operation-level evidence accumulation mechanism, making it impossible to distinguish stably effective operations from accidental hits. This paper formalizes the requirement as two structural conditions, alignability and comparability, and proposes Marginal Advantage Accumulation (MAA). MAA constructs differential signals to make them comparable across batches, accumulates signed evidence per operation via EMA, and ensures cross-batch traceability through semantic identity merging. As a post-processing architecture, MAA achieves the best results in 14 out of 16 settings across 4 benchmarks and 4 target models, consistently outperforming existing batch-level distillation baselines and matching or surpassing online alternatives in most settings, while reducing optimization-phase token consumption by approximately 75%.

AI.tldr: MAA introduces cross-batch, operation-level evidence accumulation for batch-style trace distillation, addressing alignability and comparability. It uses differential signals and EMA-based accumulation with semantic identity merging, offering stable, cross-batch traceability and large efficiency gains.
AI.motivation: Batch-style trace distillation suffers from inconsistent (contradictory) feedback for the same memory operation across different batches. Without cross-batch evidence accumulation, it’s hard to distinguish truly effective operations from noise or accidental hits, hindering stable optimization.
AI.method: MAA constructs differential signals to align signals across batches (alignability) and compare them (comparability). It accumulates signed evidence per operation via an EMA, enabling cross-batch traceability through semantic identity merging. It is framed as a post-processing architecture, enabling integration with existing pipelines.
AI.result: In 4 benchmarks across 4 target models, MAA achieves the best results in 14 of 16 settings, consistently outperforms batch-level distillation baselines, and matches or surpasses online alternatives in most settings. It reduces optimization-phase token consumption by ~75%.
AI.conclusion: MAA provides a principled, cross-batch, operation-level evidence accumulation framework that satisfies the alignability and comparability conditions, yielding stable performance gains and significant efficiency improvements over existing batch-level and online methods.

title: Contagion Networks: Evaluator Bias Propagation in Multi-Agent LLM Systems
summary: When large language models serve as evaluators in multi-agent systems, their systematic evaluation biases propagate through the agent network. We introduce Contagion Networks, a formal framework for measuring how evaluator biases spread across interacting LLM agents. In a controlled 3-agent experiment using DeepSeek-chat with three distinct evaluator bias profiles (structured, balanced, evidence-based), we measure the Cross-Agent Contagion Matrix Gamma_3 and find that evaluator biases consistently propagate between agents (gamma in [0.157, 0.352]), even within the same underlying model. We identify three propagation regimes governed by the spectral radius rho(Gamma_N), and demonstrate that homogeneous-model agents produce contagion coefficients 3-5x weaker than cross-model coefficients observed in prior work (MM-EPC: gamma approx 0.85-1.3), placing them in the suppression regime. We show that increasing evaluator committee size from k=1 to k=3 reduces effective contagion by 72.4%, providing an actionable mitigation strategy. We release the open-source Contagion Network experimental framework.

AI.tldr: Contagion Networks quantify and mitigate propagation of evaluator biases across LLM agents. Gamma_3 values ~0.16–0.35 show biases spread; propagation regimes are classified by spectral radius; homogeneous-model contagion is weaker than cross-model; increasing evaluator committee size (k) from 1 to 3 reduces contagion by 72.4%; code is open-source.
AI.motivation: Understand how evaluator biases propagate in multi-agent LLM systems and provide actionable strategies to reduce bias contagion, filling the gap in formal measurement and mitigation of cross-agent evaluation biases.
AI.method: Controlled 3-agent experiment using DeepSeek-chat with three evaluator bias profiles (structured, balanced, evidence-based). Compute Cross-Agent Contagion Matrix Gamma_3. Analyze spectral radius rho(Gamma_N) to identify propagation regimes. Compare contagion across homogeneous-model vs cross-model settings (MM-EPC baseline). Vary evaluator committee size from k=1 to k=3 to assess mitigation. Report results and release open-source framework.
AI.result: Gamma_3 values in the range [0.157, 0.352], indicating consistent evaluator bias propagation across agents. Three propagation regimes governed by the spectral radius rho(Gamma_N). Homogeneous-model agents exhibit contagion coefficients 3–5x weaker than cross-model coefficients (MM-EPC: gamma ~0.85–1.3), placing them in the suppression regime. Increasing evaluator committee size from k=1 to k=3 reduces effective contagion by 72.4%. Open-source Contagion Network experimental framework released.
AI.conclusion: The Contagion Networks framework provides a formal, scalable approach to measure, classify, and mitigate evaluator bias contagion in multi-agent LLM systems. Key mitigation is increasing evaluator committee size, and the framework enables replication and extension.

title: Execution-State Capsules: Graph-Bound Execution-State Checkpoint and Restore for Low-Latency, Small-Batch, On-Device Physical-AI Serving
summary: Mainstream LLM serving systems reuse prefix work mainly through paged or radix key-value (KV) caches. This is highly effective for high-throughput, high-concurrency serving, but it manages only one positional fragment of execution state: the KV cache. We study the opposite regime: low-latency, small-batch, on-device physical-AI serving, where interactive LLM agents, speech systems, and robot policies repeatedly branch, reset, interrupt, and re-enter under tight responsiveness budgets. We introduce execution-state capsules, a graph-bound checkpoint and restore mechanism for the complete restorable state at a committed boundary. FlashRT is a white-box, backend-facing kernel runtime whose evaluated NVIDIA CUDA backend runs captured graph plans over contiguous static buffers with no block-table indirection. Because the live state is a closed set of named buffers, a capsule can snapshot, restore, fork, or roll back the whole execution boundary, including KV, recurrent state, convolution state, MTP state, and metadata. This moves reuse from token-addressed KV fragments to graph-bound execution-state boundaries. On an RTX 5090, capsule restore is byte-exact at the stored-state level and token-identical under greedy decode. A KV-only ablation diverges, showing that recurrent state is load-bearing. GPU-resident snapshot and restore are sub-millisecond, and TTFT speedup over cold prefill grows from 3.9x at 2k tokens to 27x at 16k tokens. On Jetson AGX Thor and DGX Spark, the same correctness and structural properties hold. Capsules are not a replacement for high-throughput KV-cache serving; they define a complementary latency-first serving point for explicit execution-state reuse.

AI.tldr: Introduces execution-state capsules and FlashRT for low-latency, on-device LLM serving by snapshotting/restoring complete execution state at graph-bound boundaries, enabling sub-millisecond GPU state restore and substantial TTFT speedups, as a complement to traditional KV caches.
AI.motivation: Addresses latency-sensitive, on-device LLM deployment where interactive agents and systems repeatedly branch, reset, and re-enter within tight responsiveness budgets, requiring fine-grained state reuse beyond token-addressed KV caches.
AI.method: Proposes execution-state capsules as graph-bound checkpoints; FlashRT is a white-box kernel runtime executing captured graph plans on contiguous static buffers with no block-table indirection. The live state is a closed set of named buffers, allowing capsule snapshot/restore/fork/rollback of the entire execution boundary, including KV, recurrent state, convolution state, MTP state, and metadata. Evaluations on RTX 5090, Jetson AGX Thor, and DGX Spark show byte-exact restores and token-identical decoding under greedy decoding; GPU-resident snapshot/restore is sub-millisecond; TTFT speedup over cold prefill grows with token count.
AI.result: Capsules enable precise, graph-bound restoration of complete execution state, achieving byte-exact restoration and token-identical decoding; TTFT speedups scale with token length (3.9x at 2k tokens to 27x at 16k tokens); correctness holds across hardware (RTX 5090, Jetson AGX Thor, DGX Spark); not a replacement for KV caches but provides a complementary, latency-first path for on-device serving.
AI.conclusion: Execution-state capsules and the FlashRT kernel present a new approach to low-latency, on-device LLM serving by snapshotting/restoring complete execution state at graph boundaries. This complements traditional KV-based reuse and enables sub-millisecond restores and scalable TTFT speedups, suitable for interactive agents, robotics, and other latency-sensitive deployments; further integration with broader serving stacks could be explored.

title: Multi-Task Bayesian In-Context Learning
summary: Bayesian predictive inference provides a principled framework for uncertainty quantification, data efficiency, and robust generalization. However, exact inference is often intractable, and scalable approximations may remain computationally expensive or require restrictive modeling assumptions that degrade predictive performance. Prior-Data Fitted and in-context models have recently emerged as an amortized alternative by learning to map datasets directly to predictive distributions, but existing approaches are tightly coupled to the support of the training prior and lack explicit mechanisms for adapting to new priors at test time, resulting in limited robustness under distribution shift. We introduce a multi-task in-context learning framework for amortized hierarchical Bayesian predictive inference that explicitly represents prior information as a prefix of in-context datasets. A transformer trained on sequences of prior and target tasks learns to adapt its predictions across families of priors. On a suite of evaluations with increasing difficulty, including out-of-meta-distribution priors and priors with high-dimensional latent structures, our method matches oracle Bayesian predictors while being orders of magnitude faster. We further demonstrate its practical relevance on a real-world spatiotemporal temperature prediction benchmark. Code is available at https://github.com/martianmartina/multi-task-bayesian-icl/.

AI.tldr: A multi-task in-context learning framework for amortized hierarchical Bayesian predictive inference that treats priors as in-context prefixes, enabling fast adaptation to new priors and matching oracle Bayesian predictors while scaling to challenging priors and real-world data.
AI.motivation: Exact Bayesian inference is often intractable; existing amortized methods are restricted by training priors and struggle under distribution shift; there is a need for an approach that explicitly encodes and adapts to priors at test time to improve robustness and data efficiency.
AI.method: Train a transformer on sequences of prior and target tasks, where priors are encoded as in-context prefixes; the model learns to adapt predictive distributions across a family of priors, enabling amortized hierarchical Bayesian inference.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations show the method matches oracle Bayesian predictors across increasing difficulty, including out-of-distribution priors and high-dimensional latent structures, with orders of magnitude faster performance; validated on a real-world spatiotemporal temperature benchmark.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates practical relevance of multi-task in-context Bayesian inference, offering a scalable, robust alternative that can adapt to new priors at test time; code available.

title: Predictability as a Fine-Grained Measure for Privacy
summary: Differential privacy (DP) ensures rigorous individual-level privacy guarantees against even the most knowledgeable attackers, but its worst-case nature can impose a costly privacy-accuracy tradeoff. We introduce privacy via predictability, a fine-grained framework that explicitly incorporates the attacker's core knowledge, a compromised portion of the dataset generated by a stochastic process, and a specified family of queries. Predictability measures privacy leakage as the incremental gain in an attacker's ability to predict sensitive information about unknown individuals after observing the algorithm's output, beyond what can already be inferred from the compromised data. We show that predictability and DP are generally incomparable: each can be small while the other is large. However, in the worst-case regime where all but one individual is compromised, and all binary queries are considered sensitive, predictability implies mutual-information DP. More generally, predictability provides a finer-grained privacy metric tailored to specific sensitive information and specific attacker models. We introduce a general framework, using the generalized method of moments (GMM), to analyze asymptotic predictability when the compromised data is generated by a stationary, ergodic, mixing process. Using this analysis, we derive a predictability-calibrated output perturbation scheme for ERM. Our approach is complementary to DP and can be used alongside DP to provide fine-grained privacy control.
