AI.tldr: A fine-grained privacy framework called 'privacy via predictability' measures leakage as the incremental gain an attacker gains in predicting sensitive information after observing the algorithm's output, revealing incomparability with differential privacy (DP); in a worst-case compromised setup it implies mutual-information DP; the framework employs the generalized method of moments (GMM) to analyze asymptotic predictability for stationary, ergodic, mixing processes and yields a predictability-calibrated output perturbation for empirical risk minimization (ERM); it complements DP and can be used alongside it.
AI.motivation: DP is a worst-case, one-size-fits-all privacy standard that can impose stringent privacy-utility tradeoffs. The work proposes a more nuanced, attacker-model–driven privacy notion (predictability) that accounts for known compromised data and targeted queries, enabling fine-grained control over privacy leakage.
AI.method: Introduce a formal framework for privacy via predictability by modeling attacker knowledge as a compromised portion of data generated by a stochastic process and focusing on a specified family of queries. Analyze the relationship to DP (incomparability; MI-DP in a strong worst-case). Develop a general GMM-based analysis to characterize asymptotic predictability under stationary, ergodic, mixing data. Propose a predictability-calibrated output perturbation scheme for ERM. Argue that this metric is complementary to DP and can be used alongside it.
AI.result: Demonstrate that, generally, predictability and DP are incomparable, and that in a strong worst-case (all but one individuals compromised, binary queries sensitive) predictability implies mutual-information DP. Provide a framework to analyze asymptotic predictability via GMM and derive a perturbation mechanism calibrated to predictability for ERM. Position the approach as a flexible, attacker-model–aware privacy metric that complements DP.
AI.conclusion: Predictability offers a finer-grained privacy metric tailored to specific sensitive information and attacker models. It extends privacy analysis beyond DP and can be integrated with DP to achieve customized privacy-utility tradeoffs.

title: Optimal Deterministic Multicalibration and Omniprediction
summary:
A model is multicalibrated on a collection of group weights $G$ if it is calibrated -- i.e. unbiased even conditional on its prediction -- not just overall, but also after reweighting contexts by each $g \in G$. It is a useful property for many downstream applications and is a basic desideratum of trustworthy machine learning. Before this work, all predictors known to attain the minimax-optimal $\widetilde O(\varepsilon^{-3})$ sample complexity rate for $\varepsilon$-multicalibration were randomized, while deterministic predictors were known only with substantially worse sample complexity. Whether randomization is necessary for optimal sample complexity in multicalibration was explicitly asked by [CLNR26] and implicitly in several prior works.
  We resolve this open problem by giving a minimax-optimal multicalibration algorithm that outputs a deterministic predictor. We then generalize the algorithm to produce optimal deterministic predictors that satisfy outcome indistinguishability (OI) with respect to finite or finitely covered collections of tests. As an application, this also gives deterministic omnipredictors and panpredictors with optimal sample complexity, resolving open problems posed by [OKK25] and [BHHLZ25].

AI.tldr: A deterministic minimax-optimal multicalibration algorithm is introduced, matching prior randomized methods’ O~(ε^{-3}) sample complexity; the approach extends to outcome indistinguishability and yields deterministic omnipredictors/panpredictors, resolving open problems about the necessity of randomness.
AI.motivation: Determine whether randomness is necessary to achieve minimax-optimal sample complexity in multicalibration and related concepts; fill gaps left by prior work and answer open questions in the literature.
AI.method: Develop a deterministic predictor achieving minimax-optimal multicalibration; generalize to outcome indistinguishability for finite or finitely covered test collections; derive deterministic omnipredictors and panpredictors with optimal sample complexity.
AI.result: Provides a minimax-optimal deterministic multicalibration algorithm with the same sample complexity as previous randomized methods (~O~(ε^{-3})); extends to deterministic outcome indistinguishability; yields deterministic omnipredictors/panpredictors and resolves open problems posed in OKK25 and BHHLZ25.
AI.conclusion: Randomization is not necessary for optimal sample complexity in multicalibration; deterministic predictors suffice and enable robust predictions alongside broader notions like outcome indistinguishability and omniprediction.

title: How Transparent is DiffusionGemma?
comment: 20 main text pages and 6 pages of references and appendices
summary: LLM reasoning transparency is a critical affordance for understanding model decisions, mitigating misuse and misalignment, and debugging surprising model behaviors. However, DiffusionGemma performs a larger fraction of its computation in a continuous latent space; does this make its reasoning less transparent? We study this question by decomposing transparency into two components: variable transparency, whether we understand intermediate snapshots of a model's computational state; and algorithmic transparency, whether we can use these snapshots to reconstruct the process by which the model arrived at its outputs. Naively, DiffusionGemma has poor variable transparency: its opaque serial depth, the amount of serial computation that occurs in between interpretable model states, seems at first 28.6X higher than the corresponding autoregressive Gemma 4 model. However, we show that we can map the information flowing between denoising steps through an interpretable token bottleneck with no decrease in downstream performance. Treating these intermediate states as interpretable reduces the opaque serial depth to just 1.1X that of Gemma 4. Algorithmic transparency is harder for diffusion models than for autoregressive models because all token predictions in the canvas can change at every denoising step, giving the model the power to implement complicated distributed algorithms during the denoising process. To begin bridging this gap, we conduct a suite of interpretability case studies, uncovering initial evidence of novel diffusion-specific phenomena such as non-chronological reasoning, token and sequence smearing, and intermediate-context reasoning. Finally, we test monitorability, a key application of transparency that measures whether model outputs are useful for downstream tasks. We find that DiffusionGemma is similarly monitorable to Gemma 4.

AI.tldr: DiffusionGemma's diffusion-based reasoning can reach parity in variable transparency with an interpretable bottleneck; algorithmic transparency remains harder; diffusion-specific interpretability phenomena observed; monitorability similar to Gemma 4.
AI.motivation: Assess if diffusion-based LLM reasoning is inherently less transparent and whether intermediate latent steps can be made interpretable, to improve safety, debugging, and alignment.
AI.method: Decompose transparency into variable and algorithmic components. Measure opaque serial depth. Introduce an interpretable token bottleneck to map information flow between denoising steps. Conduct interpretability case studies for diffusion-specific phenomena. Assess monitorability for downstream tasks.
AI.result: Variable transparency greatly improved by the token bottleneck; opaque depth reduced to 1.1x of Gemma 4 with no loss in downstream performance. Algorithmic transparency remains harder due to distributed, per-step token changes. Observed diffusion-specific phenomena: non-chronological reasoning, token/sequence smearing, intermediate-context reasoning. Monitorability of DiffusionGemma is comparable to Gemma 4.
AI.conclusion: An interpretable bottleneck can substantially close the gap in variable transparency for diffusion models without sacrificing performance, while algorithmic transparency remains a challenge. Early diffusion-specific interpretability signals emerge, and practical monitorability matches autoregressive baselines, supporting safer deployment and debugging of diffusion-based LLMs.
